To make this formulation trainable and measurable, we introduce SDG-30K, a 30K-image dataset with box-grounded annotations across four modern T2I generators, together with a dedicated evaluation protocol, SDG-Eval. Building on this structured representation, we further present a diagnosis-to-alignment framework in which a Vision-Language Model (VLM) serves as the SDG detector, and BoxFlow-GRPO converts predicted defect sets into box-derived, importance-weighted spatial rewards for diffusion model alignment. Extensive experiments show that our SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding, while SDG-guided rewards consistently improve T2I alignment and support localized image refinement. These results establish SDG as a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and enhancing modern generative models.

</details>


### [78] [Diff-CA: Separating Common and Salient Factors with Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06120)
*Michaël Soumm,Alexandre Fournier Montgieux,Yunlong He,Pietro Gori,Alasdair Newson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based contrastive analysis framework that decomposes conditioning into common and salient factors with identifiability under mild conditions, enabling swaps/interpolations of the salient factor while preserving generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limitations of existing contrastive methods: they rely on generative models (VAEs/GANs) with limited reconstruction quality, hindering effective latent factor separation and high-fidelity image generation. A diffusion-model-based approach aims to achieve contrastive decomposition without sacrificing image quality.

Method: Train a prompt-free image-conditioned diffusion model; learn to decompose the conditioning into a common factor and a salient factor using weak supervision; adopt additive contrastive factorization; prove identifiability under mild conditions; enable targeted operations by swapping or interpolating only the salient factor.

Result: Identifiability of the additive contrastive factorization is established under mild conditions; the framework enables editing operations by swapping/interpolating the salient factor while keeping the common content, and maintains high-quality generation. 

Conclusion: The proposed conditioning framework for diffusion models enables contrastive decomposition without compromising generation quality and supports targeted image editing through manipulation of the salient factor.

Abstract: Contrastive Analysis aims to separate factors that are common between two data distributions from those that are salient to only one of them. Existing contrastive methods are based on generative models (e.g., VAEs or GANs) that often suffer from limited reconstruction and image quality, which hampers effective latent factor separation and limits their applicability to high-fidelity image generation and edition. We propose a novel conditioning framework for diffusion models that enables contrastive decomposition without compromising generation quality. We first train a prompt-free, image-conditioned diffusion model, and then learn to decompose the conditioning into a common and a salient factor, using weak supervision. We prove that the additive contrastive factorization, commonly assumed in prior work, is identifiable under mild conditions. This factorization enables targeted operations by swapping or interpolating only the salient factor.

</details>


### [79] [Computation-Aware Event-to-Frame Reconstruction via Selective Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06142)
*Jingqian Wu,Yunbo Jia,Edmund Y. Lam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Efficient event-to-frame reconstruction via a causal, computation-aware E2F framework that uses a recurrent encoder-decoder, selective fusion with prior cues, and lightweight hybrid attention to balance accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing E2F methods struggle to simultaneously achieve high reconstruction quality and real-time efficiency; there is a need for causal temporal modeling and computation-aware architectures that are robust to fast motion and illumination changes.

Method: A recurrent encoder-decoder aggregates event information with compact hidden states; selective context fusion combines event features with prior intensity cues; a lightweight hybrid attention mechanism enhances feature selectivity without heavy attention operations.

Result: Experimental results on standard benchmarks show competitive reconstruction performance while maintaining a favorable accuracy–complexity trade-off.

Conclusion: The proposed framework delivers efficient and robust E2F reconstruction suitable for real-time or resource-constrained pipelines, with competitive accuracy and reduced computational burden.

Abstract: Event-to-frame (E2F) reconstruction bridges asynchronous event streams with frame-based vision pipelines, but existing methods often face a trade-off between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency. In this work, we propose an efficient E2F framework that emphasizes causal temporal modeling and computation-aware design. The architecture adopts a recurrent encoder-decoder to incrementally aggregate event information with compact hidden states. To improve robustness under fast motion and illumination variations, a selective context fusion strategy is introduced to integrate event-driven features with prior intensity cues. Within this fusion process, a lightweight hybrid attention mechanism enhances feature selectivity without relying on heavy attention operations. Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves competitive reconstruction performance while maintaining a favorable balance between accuracy and model complexity.

</details>


### [80] [Adaptive Tokenisation Via Temporal Redundancy Masking And Latent Inpainting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06158)
*Kevin Dave,Sai Aditya Patkuri,Chhaya Kumar Das,Gouranga Bala,R. Venkatesh Babu,Rajeshkumar SA*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adaptive video tokenisation uses parameter-free, content-driven token allocation by thresholding temporal-L1 differences; introduces Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT); achieves large speedups with competitive reconstruction on TokenBench and DAVIS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational overhead of adaptive tokenisation while preserving reconstruction fidelity by exploiting redundancy in the latent space, avoiding extra routing networks.

Method: Freeze a continuous video tokeniser and compute per-position temporal-L1 differences in the latent space. Apply a fixed threshold to drop latent positions with small changes. Reconstruct dropped positions with Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT), a lightweight factorised spatial-temporal attention network. Inference uses a single encoder pass plus one LIT forward pass, with no auxiliary routing networks.

Result: Static scenes are aggressively compressed while dynamic sequences retain more tokens. Claims 31× inference-time speedup over ElasticTok-CV and about 2× over InfoTok, with competitive reconstruction fidelity on TokenBench and DAVIS.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that latent-space temporal redundancy enables efficient, content-driven token allocation without trainable gating networks, yielding substantial speedups while maintaining fidelity.

Abstract: Adaptive video tokenisation seeks to dynamically allocate token budgets based on the underlying visual complexity of a sequence. Current continuous-regime approaches achieve this via iterative binarised searches or trained neural regressors, while discrete methods often require a full-rate decoder pass to estimate information content. We demonstrate that such computational overheads are not strictly necessary. We show that the latent space of a frozen continuous video tokeniser inherently encodes temporal redundancy that can be exploited directly: spatial positions whose latent representations change minimally between consecutive frames carry near-zero additional information.
  We introduce a parameter-free adaptive token allocation mechanism that applies a fixed threshold to per-position temporal-L1 differences, identifying and dropping redundant latent positions. Consequently, the compression rate emerges naturally from the input content rather than being enforced top-down: static scenes get compressed aggressively, while highly dynamic sequences retain more tokens. To reconstruct the dropped positions, we propose the Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT), a lightweight factorised spatial-temporal attention architecture. The resulting inference pipeline is highly efficient, requiring only a single encoder pass and one LIT forward pass, eliminating the need for auxiliary routing networks. Evaluations across TokenBench and DAVIS, which are the standard benchmarks used by recent tokenisers~\cite{infotok, agarwal2025cosmos}, indicate that our framework yields meaningful, content-driven token allocation while maintaining competitive reconstruction fidelity, and delivers a $31\times$ inference-time speedup over the continuous adaptive baseline (ElasticTok-CV) and an $\approx2\times$ speedup over the discrete information-theoretic baseline (InfoTok)

</details>


### [81] [RQUL-UIE: Revitalizing Quality-Unstable Labels for Underwater Image Enhancement via In-Dataset Self-Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06176)
*Haochen Hu,Yanrui Bin,Chih-yung Wen,Bing Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based self-supervised strategy for underwater image enhancement that leverages label-quality signals derived from semantic embeddings of a pre-trained diffusion model, quantized into noise-level indices to supervise a multi-step denoising process, plus a Fourier-based refinement module; claims state-of-the-art restoration performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reliance on paired UIE datasets with unstable label quality and to exploit the distribution of label quality for better learning, by using training-free quality estimation and level-wise supervision.

Method: Compute label quality via semantic perception embeddings from a pre-trained diffusion model without additional training. Quantize these quality scores into discrete noise-level indices to guide a multi-step denoising process, providing level-wise supervision that mitigates the impact of low-quality labels. Incorporate a Fourier-based refinement network to explicitly reconstruct high-frequency components.

Result: Empirical results show consistent outperformance of SOTA methods on restoration quality across benchmarks.

Conclusion: The approach effectively leverages label-quality distributions to improve UIE training and achieves superior restoration, with code/pre-trained model to be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) is essential for mitigating degradations caused by water medium. Although learning-based methods have advanced significantly, most rely on paired datasets with unstable label quality, which bottlenecks model performance. This paper proposes a diffusion-based, in-dataset self-supervised learning strategy designed to exploit the quality distribution of training labels. Specifically, we evaluate label quality via semantic perception embeddings from a pre-trained diffusion model in a training-free manner. These quality scores are subsequently quantized into noise-level indices, guiding a multi-step denoising process for level-wise supervision. This mechanism prevents low-quality labels from degrading the model while maximizing their utility during training. Furthermore, a Fourier-based refinement network is incorporated to explicitly reconstruct high-frequency components. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms SOTA approaches in restoration quality. The code and pre-trained model will be available once accepted in link.

</details>


### [82] [Adversarial Attacks Already Tell the Answer: Directional Bias-Guided Test-time Defense for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06186)
*Liangsheng Liu,Si Chen,Jiamin Wu,Weiwei Feng,Zhixin Cheng,Xiaotian Yin,Wenfei Yang,Tianzhu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adversarial perturbations in CLIP predictably shift features along a dominant direction under input transformations. The authors define a Defense Direction and introduce Directional Bias-guided Defense (DBD), a test-time method that estimates this direction and reconstructs robust representations with a two-stream strategy, achieving state-of-the-art robustness across 15 datasets and sometimes adversarial accuracy exceeding clean accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs like CLIP are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, and efficient test-time defenses are desirable to avoid costly retraining. The work leverages a discovered directional bias to improve robustness.

Method: 1) Observe that adversarial perturbations induce a dominant shift in CLIP feature space under various input transformations (Defense Direction). 2) Estimate this Defense Direction. 3) Use a DB-score-based two-stream reconstruction to recover robust representations and align features toward the true class centers (Directional Bias-guided Defense, DBD).

Result: DBD achieves state-of-the-art adversarial robustness while preserving clean accuracy on 15 datasets. Notably, adversarial accuracy can exceed clean accuracy, suggesting adversarial perturbations encode directional priors about the decision boundary.

Conclusion: Directional priors exist in adversarial perturbations of VLMs and can be exploited for test-time defense. The Defense Direction and the DB-score-guided reconstruction provide a strong, retraining-free defense framework with broad empirical effectiveness.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), such as CLIP, have shown strong zero-shot generalization but remain highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, posing serious risks in real-world applications. Test-time defenses for VLMs have recently emerged as a promising and efficient approach to defend against adversarial attacks without requiring costly large-scale retraining. In this work, we uncover a surprising phenomenon: under diverse input transformations, adversarial images in CLIP's feature space consistently shift along a dominant direction, in contrast to the dispersed patterns of clean images. We hypothesize that this dominant shift, termed the Defense Direction, opposes the adversarial shift, pointing features back toward their correct class centers. Building on this insight, we propose Directional Bias-guided Defense (DBD), a test-time framework that estimates the Defense Direction and employs a DB-score-based two-stream reconstruction strategy to recover robust representations. Experiments on 15 datasets demonstrate that DBD not only achieves SOTA adversarial robustness while preserving clean accuracy, but also reveals the counterintuitive result that adversarial accuracy can even surpass clean accuracy. This demonstrates that adversarial perturbations inherently encode directional priors about the true decision boundary.

</details>


### [83] [SC-MFJ: A Simple Haptic Quality Metric for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06199)
*Souraj Adhikary,Negar Chabi,Andre Mastmeyer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SC-MFJ is a cheap, surface-constrained jerk-based metric for evaluating haptic suitability of segmented surfaces in surgical simulation, revealing quality gaps unseen by traditional geometric metrics and generalizing across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current segmentation metrics (Dice, Hausdorff) assess geometric overlap but do not predict haptic-rendering quality; a metric linking surface geometry to contact dynamics is needed.

Method: Introduce SC-MFJ: sample the organ surface with numerous brief virtual stylus walks; compute jerk of contact forces; implement using existing segmentations; runtime ~1 minute per case. Compare pancreas segmentations under binary nnU-Net, Gaussian post-processing, and learned SDF regression across 80 cases (5-fold CV); replicate on LiTS liver dataset (131 cases).

Result: SC-MFJ detects a substantial gap between binary and Gaussian post-processing (147x on pancreas, 189x on LiTS) that Dice/HD95 miss. Gaussian smoothing yields low force jerk with low variability (22 N/s^2 SD) vs SDF regression (168 N/s^2 SD). SDF retraining more variable; binary baseline benefits from simple post-processing.

Conclusion: For haptic simulations, a simple post-processing step (Gaussian smoothing) can vastly improve haptic quality; SC-MFJ is a cheap, effective monitor that uncovers issues not visible to conventional geometric metrics and can flag when advanced post-processing or model choices degrade haptic realism.

Abstract: Standard segmentation metrics such as Dice and Hausdorff distance measure geometric overlap but say nothing about whether a segmented surface is suitable for haptic rendering in surgical simulation. We propose SC-MFJ (Surface-Constrained Mean Force Jerk), a simple, inexpensive metric that samples a segmented organ surface with many short virtual stylus walks and measures how jerky the resulting contact forces are. The metric is computed from existing segmentation outputs and uses roughly one minute of CPU time per case. We evaluate three pancreas CT segmentation approaches-binary nnU-Net output, Gaussian-smoothed output, and learned signed distance function (SDF) regression-across 80 cases in five-fold cross-validation. SC-MFJ reveals a 147x gap in haptic quality between the raw binary baseline and simple Gaussian post-processing, a difference entirely invisible to Dice and HD95. It also shows that learned SDF regression, despite requiring full model retraining, produces more variable haptic quality than Gaussian smoothing, with a case-level standard deviation of 168 N/s2 compared with 22 N/s2 for Gaussian. A second evaluation on the LiTS liver dataset (131 cases) confirms the generality of these findings: the binary-to-Gaussian gap widens to 189x, and Gaussian smoothing again produces consistently low force jerk across all folds. Our results suggest that for haptic simulation applications, a one-line post-processing step may be sufficient, and that a cheap metric like SC-MFJ can flag problems that geometric metrics miss.

</details>


### [84] [DisasterBench: A Multimodal Benchmark for UAV-Based Disaster Response in Complex Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06217)
*Tan Zhang,Quanyou Li,Lu Zhang,Jun Liu,Xiaofeng Zhu,Ping Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces DisasterBench for multi-stage multimodal reasoning in UAV disaster response and DisasterVL, a compact 2B model with a three-stage training pipeline, achieving GPT-4o–level reasoning on 21 MLLMs with edge efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal benchmarks focus on perception and single-shot tasks, neglect multi-stage reasoning for complex disasters and edge-enabled operation under noisy UAV data and limited compute.

Method: DisasterBench spans 14 disaster-related scene types and 9 response-critical tasks across pre-, during-, and post-disaster stages with explicit mappings for causal attribution, propagation prediction, damage analysis, and decision-oriented reasoning. DisasterVL employs a three-stage pipeline—domain instruction tuning, chain-of-thought-guided multimodal alignment, and reinforcement learning-based policy optimization—on a 2B-parameter model. Evaluation covers 21 popular MLLMs.

Result: DisasterVL outperforms all evaluated open-source models and narrows the gap to closed-source state-of-the-art models, achieving GPT-4o-comparable reasoning accuracy with superior efficiency. A project page is provided for reproducibility and further exploration.

Conclusion: The work delivers a practical, edge-enabled framework for comprehensive disaster reasoning, combining a broad, multi-stage benchmark with an efficient multimodal model that approaches top-tier performance while maintaining resource efficiency.

Abstract: When a disaster unfolds, responders must answer not only what is happening, but also why it is happening, what will happen next, and what to do now, often from noisy low-altitude UAV views and under tight on-site compute constraints. However, most existing multimodal benchmarks emphasize perception (e.g., recognition/description), cover limited disaster types, and provide insufficient support for the multi-stage reasoning required in practical emergency response. We introduce DisasterBench, a multi-stage multimodal reasoning benchmark for UAV-Based disaster response in complex environments. DisasterBench spans 14 disaster-related scene types and 9 response-critical tasks across pre-, during-, and post-disaster stages, with fine-grained disaster-task mappings that explicitly test causal attribution, propagation prediction, damage analysis, and decision-oriented reasoning. To enable reasoning on the edge, we further propose DisasterVL, a lightweight multimodal model optimized with a three-stage pipeline combining domain instruction tuning, chain-of-thought-guided multimodal alignment, and reinforcement learning-based policy optimization. Experiments across 21 popular MLLMs show that our 2B-parameter DisasterVL outperforms all evaluated open-source models and substantially narrows the gap to state-of-the-art closed-source models, achieving GPT-4o-comparable reasoning accuracy with superior efficiency. The project page is available at https://github.com/TanmouTT/DisasterBench.

</details>


### [85] [Symb-xMIL: Symbolic Explanations for Multiple Instance Learning in Digital Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06224)
*Yanqing Luo,Julius Hense,Niklas Prenißl,Andreas Mock,Klaus-Robert Müller,Thomas Schnake,Mina Jamshidi Idaji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Symb-xMIL, a post-hoc symbolic explanation framework for MIL in histopathology that uses logical rules (AND/OR/NOT) to explain how tissue-region evidence combines to predictions, extending beyond heatmaps to rule-based reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MIL models in digital histopathology often rely on heatmaps for explainability but fail to reveal how multiple region evidences interact to drive a decision. There's a need for human-readable, structured explanations that capture feature interactions.

Method: Proposes Symb-xMIL, a post-hoc framework that quantifies alignment between a MIL model's behavior and human-readable decision rules expressed as logical combinations of input features. It computes alignment scores, validates rule recovery on synthetic MIL data, analyzes decision patterns and model errors in a clinical tumor detection task, and refines survival stratification in an HPV-prediction task on TCGA-HNSCC.

Result: On synthetic MIL data, Symb-xMIL reliably recovers ground-truth logical rules. In a clinical tumor detection task, the best-aligned rules reveal heterogeneous decision patterns and expose hidden model errors. On HPV prediction for TCGA-HNSCC, the approach refines patient survival stratification beyond HPV status, with potential clinical relevance.

Conclusion: Symb-xMIL broadens MIL explainability from purely visual attribution to structured, rule-based reasoning, yielding semantically grounded interpretations that can enhance validation, error diagnosis, and clinical insight.

Abstract: Explanations of multiple instance learning (MIL) models are widely used for validation and discovery in digital histopathology. Existing methods primarily rely on heatmaps that highlight influential regions but do not explain how evidence from different tissue regions is combined to produce a prediction. This limits interpretability, especially when decisions depend on interactions between tissue features. We introduce Symbolic explainable MIL (Symb-xMIL), a post-hoc explanation framework that quantifies how a MIL model's behavior aligns with human-readable decision rules, expressed as logical relationships (e.g., AND, OR, NOT) between input features. These alignment scores reveal semantic patterns underlying the model's predictions. We evaluate Symb-xMIL on synthetic and real-world histopathology datasets. On synthetic MIL data, Symb-xMIL reliably recovers ground-truth logical rules. In a clinical tumor detection task, the best-aligned rules uncover heterogeneous decision patterns and expose hidden model errors. On an HPV-prediction task on TCGA-HNSCC, a cohort of head and neck cancer, our framework refines patient survival stratification beyond HPV status with potential clinical relevance. Overall, Symb-xMIL extends MIL explainability beyond visual attribution toward structured, rule-based reasoning, enabling more transparent and semantically grounded interpretation of model predictions.

</details>


### [86] [SAM-Flow: Source-Anchored Masked Flow for Training-Free Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06228)
*Haowang Cui,Rui Chen,Tao Luo,Tao Guo,Zheng Qin,Jiaze Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAM-Flow is a training-free, localized image editing method that confines edits to user-localized regions by anchoring non-target areas to the source latent and applying selective velocity updates, improving background preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome background leakage from global latent transport in inversion/differential-flow-based edits, enabling localized, high-fidelity edits without fine-tuning.

Method: The approach localizes editable regions using a scout image and token-grounded attention maps, then applies differential velocity updates only within those regions while anchoring the rest of the image to the source latent trajectory. It introduces a time-varying source-anchored projection with dynamic soft masks, transition regions, and temporal mask accumulation. It is plug-and-play with mainstream flow-matching backbones (e.g., Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX) without any fine-tuning.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate accurate semantic editing with significantly improved background preservation, showing SAM-Flow as a simple, general localized editing paradigm for training-free image editing.

Conclusion: SAM-Flow provides an effective, plug-and-play, localized training-free editing framework that achieves precise semantic edits while maintaining background fidelity and compatibility with existing diffusion backbones.

Abstract: Training-free image editing has recently attracted increasing attention due to its ability to modify real images using powerful pre-trained diffusion and flow-matching models without additional training. However, existing inversion-based and differential-flow-based methods usually perform global latent transport, which inevitably propagates editing effects to non-target regions and leads to background leakage. To address this problem, we propose SAM-Flow, a source-anchored masked flow framework for localized training-free image editing. Instead of updating the whole latent representation, SAM-Flow first uses a scout image and token-grounded attention maps to localize the editable semantic regions. It then applies differential velocity updates only within these regions, while anchoring the remaining areas to the source-image latent trajectory. To further improve spatial stability and boundary naturalness, we introduce a time-varying source-anchored projection mechanism with dynamic soft masks, transition regions, and temporal mask accumulation. The proposed method is plug-and-play and can be integrated with mainstream flow-matching backbones such as Stable Diffusion 3 and FLUX without any fine-tuning. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that SAM-Flow achieves accurate semantic editing while significantly improving background preservation, providing a simple and general localized editing paradigm for training-free image editing. Code is available at: https://github.com/chwbob/Sam-Flow.

</details>


### [87] [GRAMformer: Any-Order Modality Interactions via Volumetric Multimodal Cross-Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06249)
*Giordano Cicchetti,Eleonora Grassucci,Danilo Comminiello*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Volumetric Multimodal cross-Attention (VMA) that defines attention as the volume spanned by a query and multiple modality-specific keys, enabling any-order modality interactions; integrated into a transformer family (GRAMformer); reports improved effectiveness and efficiency on multimodal tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal attention relies on pairwise dot-products or concatenation into keys, which either incur quadratic complexity in the number of modalities or fail to model joint interactions across modalities. A mechanism that captures higher-order, joint dependencies among multiple modalities is desirable for scalable, flexible multimodal fusion.

Method: Propose VMA, a cross-attention mechanism where attention scores are determined by the joint geometry of the query and multiple modality-specific keys. The score reflects the volume spanned by the multi-modal vectors, enabling explicit modeling of any-order modality interactions. Integrate VMA into GRAMformer, a multimodal transformer architecture designed to handle an arbitrary number of modalities.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multimodal learning tasks shows improved effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed approach compared with baselines.

Conclusion: VMA enables native modeling of any-order modality interactions and provides a flexible, scalable framework (GRAMformer) for integrating an arbitrary number of modalities in multimodal learning.

Abstract: Transformer-based multimodal models rely on attention mechanisms to integrate information across heterogeneous modalities. Despite their success, existing multimodal attention formulations compute their scores through collections of pairwise dot-product interactions or by concatenating all the modalities into the keys, even when multiple modalities should be jointly involved. As a consequence, current approaches either incur quadratic complexity in the number of modalities or fail to explicitly model interactions that depend on the joint configuration of multiple representations. In this work, we introduce the Volumetric Multimodal cross-Attention (VMA), a novel cross-attention mechanism in which attention scores are defined as a function of the joint geometry of a query and multiple modality-specific keys. VMA computes the volume spanned by query and key vectors across multiple modalities, capturing joint multimodal dependencies beyond pairwise similarity, enabling native modeling of any-order modality interactions. We integrate VMA into our novel multimodal transformer architecture, named GRAMformer, explicitly designed to integrate any number of modalities. We evaluate the proposed model on multimodal learning tasks, demonstrating improved effectiveness and efficiency.

</details>


### [88] [Geodesic Flow Matching on a Riemannian Degradation Manifold for Blind Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06278)
*Akshay Janardan Bankar,Ankita Chatterjee,Sayan Banerjee,Shreyas Pandith,Kalakonda Sai Shashank,Amit Satish Unde*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Model degradations as a low-dimensional Riemannian manifold and cast restoration as geodesic transport on a joint image-manifold, learned via geodesic flow matching to respect curvature and handle mixed degradations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deterministic flow-based restoration relies on Euclidean interpolation, which assumes linear degradation geometry. Real degradations are nonlinear and can be mixed; a geometrically principled approach is needed to generalize beyond observed degradations.

Method: Represent degradations as points on a Riemannian manifold embedded in a joint image-manifold; formulate restoration as geodesic transport along this manifold; optimize a geodesic flow matching objective to learn intrinsic transport dynamics that respect curvature; treat mixed degradations via geodesic composition; provide theoretical interpretation for generalization.

Result: Claims generalization beyond observed degradations, principled handling of mixed degradations through geodesic compositions, and a framework that generalizes linear flow matching by incorporating manifold curvature; proposes a theoretically grounded restoration method with improved modeling of degradation geometry.

Conclusion: A geometrically principled restoration framework that leverages Riemannian geometry to model degradation space and geodesic transport, enabling better generalization and principled treatment of mixed degradations.

Abstract: Blind image restoration requires recovering clean images from observations corrupted by unknown and potentially mixed degradations. While recent deterministic flow-based methods model restoration as transport processes that map degraded images to clean ones, they typically rely on Euclidean interpolation, implicitly assuming linear degradation geometry. In this paper, we explicitly model degradations as points on a low-dimensional Riemannian manifold and formulate restoration as geodesic transport on the joint image-manifold space. Using a geodesic flow matching objective, we learn intrinsic transport dynamics that respect the curvature of degradation space. This framework generalizes linear flow matching, provides a principled treatment of mixed degradations as geodesic compositions, and yields a clean theoretical interpretation for generalization beyond observed degradations.

</details>


### [89] [Towards One-to-Many Temporal Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06294)
*Qi Xu,Yue Tan,Shihao Chen,Jiahao Meng,Anna Wang,Shunping Ji,Hao Fei,Jason Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces One-to-Many Temporal Grounding (OMTG) with a benchmark, a 56k-sample dataset, and reward-based learning signals for multi-segment localization; achieves state-of-the-art Effective Temporal F1 (EtF1) on OMTG Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world queries often map to multiple disjoint video segments; existing one-to-one models lack event cardinality perception, failing to localize all relevant segments.

Method: (1) Establish OMTG benchmark with Count Accuracy (C-Acc) and EtF1. (2) Build a 56k-sample OMTG dataset via a sophisticated construction pipeline. (3) Propose temporal and caption reward functions; the caption reward uses Chain-of-Thought reasoning over dense video captions to guide optimization toward preciseness and completeness.

Result: Achieves a new state-of-the-art EtF1 of 43.65% on OMTG Bench, outperforming Gemini 2.5 Pro and Seed-1.8 by 15.85% and 15.61% respectively.

Conclusion: Presents a comprehensive OMTG framework with benchmark, dataset, and tailored rewards; demonstrates the effectiveness of Chain-of-Thought guided captioning for multi-segment temporal grounding and highlights the importance of modeling event cardinality.

Abstract: Temporal Grounding (TG) aims to localize video segments corresponding to a textual query. Prior research predominantly focuses on single-segment retrieval. Real-world scenarios, however, often require localizing multiple disjoint segments for a single query -- a setting we term One-to-Many Temporal Grounding (OMTG). Previous state-of-the-art MLLMs, optimized for one-to-one settings, struggle in this context, often yielding near-zero scores due to a lack of event cardinality perception. To bridge this gap, we present a systematic solution with three key contributions. First, we establish the first comprehensive OMTG benchmark, introducing Count Accuracy (C-Acc) and Effective Temporal F1 (EtF1) as evaluation metrics. Second, we curate a high-quality OMTG dataset comprising 56k samples through a sophisticated construction pipeline. Third, we develop novel temporal and caption reward functions specifically designed for OMTG. In particular, the caption reward leverages Chain-of-Thought reasoning over dense video captions to explicitly guide policy optimization toward both preciseness and completeness. Extensive experiments show our model achieves a new state-of-the-art EtF1 of 43.65\% on OMTG Bench, outperforming Gemini 2.5 Pro and Seed-1.8 by 15.85\% and 15.61\%, respectively.

</details>


### [90] [RhymeFlow: Training-Free Acceleration for Video Generation with Asynchronous Denoising Flow Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06309)
*Chensheng Dai,Shengjun Zhang,Yifan Li,Zhang Zhang,Zheng Zhu,Yueqi Duan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RhymeFlow decouples per-frame denoising in DiT-based video generation by selecting key frames for dense denoising while non-key frames skip steps, aided by a latent trajectory projection module, yielding faster inference and improved visual quality in a training-free framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video diffusion models with 3D attention incur high latency; existing accelerations reduce per-step cost but maintain dense processing for all frames, which is wasteful given temporal redundancy across adjacent frames.

Method: Identify sparse key frames that drive semantic evolution; perform dense, stepwise denoising only on key frames; non-key frames skip denoising steps progressively; introduce latent trajectory projection to preserve temporal coherence by enabling key-frame states to interact with full sequence representation; training-free.

Result: Experiments on DiT-based video generation models show RhymeFlow surpasses baselines in both inference speed and visual quality.

Conclusion: RhymeFlow offers a training-free, efficient framework for video generation by decoupling frame trajectories and maintaining coherence, with potential applicability to broader diffusion-based video systems.

Abstract: Video generation models based on Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have achieved remarkable performance in video synthesis, yet they suffer from high inference latency and computational costs due to the quadratic complexity of 3D attention. Existing acceleration methods primarily reduce computational complexity within each individual denoising steps through techniques such as sparse attention and KV-caching. However, they rigidly adhere to the inherent constraint of the standard diffusion pipeline: every frame in the target video sequence must be subjected to a complete, dense denoising process across all diffusion timesteps. We observe that due to the corresponding contents and motions among adjacent frames, when keyframes with critical semantic transitions are anchored, the intermediate states of others often follow more predictable trajectories, which indicates that such uniform, dense denoising process is inherently redundant for natural video data. To this end, we introduce \textbf{RhymeFlow}, a training-free framework that decouples the denoising trajectories of different frames. Specifically, we first identify a sparse set of pivotal key frames that dominate the latent semantic evolution. Then, only these keyframes undergo dense, step-by-step denoising to ensure structural integrity, while non-keyframes progressively skip denoising steps to minimize computational cost. Since skipped intermediate states of non-keyframes break the temporal coherence in keyframe denoising steps, leading to visual degradation, we further introduce a latent trajectory projection module, which enables keyframes to interact with a complete and temporally consistent sequence representation. Extensive experiments on current DiT-based video generation models demonstrate our method outperforms existing baselines with higher inference speed and better visual quality.

</details>


### [91] [StoryVideoQA: Scaling Deep Video Understanding with a Large-Scale, Multi-Genre and Auto-Generated Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06338)
*Zhengqian Wu,Zhixian Liu,Aodong Chen,Jingyang Zhang,Ruizhe Li,Hanlin Ge,Zhongyuan Wang,Chunxia Xiao,Chao Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StoryVideoQA introduces StoryMindv2 and PlotTree to tackle deep video understanding (DVU) by scaling a large DVU dataset with multi-agent generation and hierarchical plot reasoning; they show current VideoQA methods struggle with long-range storylines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DVU requires long-range, coherent understanding of complex stories in videos, but manually constructed DVU datasets are limited in scale and diversity. Previous StoryMind failed on longer movies, underscoring the need for scalable, robust dataset creation and reasoning.

Method: StoryMindv2 adopts a supervisor-guided generation mechanism with multi-reviewer voting to automatically construct a large DVU dataset, StoryVideoQA (363K QAs, 393.2 hours). It also introduces PlotTree, a video understanding agent that reorganizes long-range content into a hierarchical plot structure to support storyline reasoning.

Result: StoryVideoQA becomes the largest DVU benchmark to date. Evaluations on 20 state-of-the-art VideoQA models reveal limited ability to maintain long-range character associations and coherent story understanding. PlotTree enables more efficient long-range reasoning on such data (StoryVideoQA).

Conclusion: The framework advances DVU dataset scale and reasoning capability, with StoryMindv2 enabling large-scale DVU data generation and PlotTree providing hierarchical storyline reasoning to improve performance on long-form videos.

Abstract: Video question answering (VideoQA) aims to answer questions about given videos. While existing approaches excel on factoid VideoQA, they struggle with deep video understanding (DVU), which requires the comprehension of complex storylines. This challenge arises from the inherent long-range video content, multi-faceted question types, and instance-level story elements, all of which constrain the scale and diversity of manually constructed DVU datasets. These difficulties constrain the scale and diversity of manually-constructed DVU dataset. To address these, we previously introduced StoryMind to automatically construct DVU datasets with balanced fine-grained topics. Though it can generate high-quality question-answer pairs (QAs) for TV series, it suffers significant performance degradation when handling longer and more complex movies. In this paper, we further design StoryMindv2, an enhanced multi-agent collaboration framework to generate high-quality DVU datasets for both TV series and movies. By integrating a novel supervisor-guided generation mechanism and a refined multi-reviewer voting strategy, the framework is utilized to construct StoryVideoQA, the largest DVU dataset to date, featuring over 363K QAs on 393.2 hours diverse story videos including TV series (avg. 1,635 seconds) and movies (avg. 7,878 seconds). Comprehensive evaluations of 20 state-of-the-art VideoQA methods on this large-scale benchmark reveal that they cannot fully maintain long-range character associations or construct a coherent understanding of complex storylines. To bridge this gap, we propose PlotTree, a novel video understanding agent, re-organizing long-range video content into a hierarchical plot structure, enabling efficient storyline reasoning on StoryVideoQA. Project page: https://github.com/nercms-mmap/StoryVideoQA/

</details>


### [92] [Comparison of Deep Learning Frameworks For Rice Disease Mapping From UAV Multispectral Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06359)
*Yadav Raj Ghimire,Jagrati Talreja,Tewodros Syum Gebre,Timothy Agboada,Shikha V. Chandel,Leila Hashemi Beni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UAV multispectral imagery used to segment bacterial leaf blight (BLB) in rice with CNNs and transformers. U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 achieves the top mIoU (97.62%). SegFormer offers faster inference but lower accuracy. Adding vegetation indices (NDVI/NDRE) yields small, consistent gains. Lightweight CNN backbones are reliable for field deployment; standardised UAV datasets are valuable for method comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess performance of CNN and transformer-based semantic segmentation architectures for BLB mapping in rice under a unified pipeline and input configurations.

Method: Architectures evaluated: U-Net with ResNet-101; U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 and EfficientNet-B7; DeepLabV3+; SegFormer. Three input configurations: multispectral only; multispectral + NDVI; multispectral + NDRE. Dataset: publicly available BLB dataset. Evaluation metrics: mean IoU (mIoU), mean F1 (mF1), mean accuracy (mAcc), precision, recall.

Result: U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 achieved the highest performance with an mIoU of 97.62%. SegFormer showed lower segmentation accuracy but comparable inference speed. Overall, lightweight CNN backbones were more reliable for operational BLB monitoring, and the inclusion of vegetation indices provided small, consistent improvements.

Conclusion: CNN-based segmentation remains effective for field BLB monitoring; standardized UAV datasets are important for fair method comparison; encourages use of CNN architectures for field deployment; vegetation indices offer marginal gains.

Abstract: In this study, UAV multispectral imagery is used to segment the severity of bacterial leaf blight (BLB) in rice using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformer-based models. The evaluated architectures include U-Net with a ResNet- 101 encoder, U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 and EfficientNetB7, DeepLabV3+, and SegFormer, all trained under a common pipeline with three input configurations (multispectral only, multispectral+NDVI, and multispectral+NDRE). Experiments are conducted using the publicly available BLB dataset with performance reported using mean IoU (mIoU), mean F1 (mF1), mean accuracy (mAcc), precision, and recall. U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 achieved the highest performance, with an mIoU of 97.62%. SegFormer obtained lower segmentation accuracy but comparable inference speed. Overall, the results indicate that lightweight CNN backbones remain more reliable for operational BLB monitoring while integration of vegetation indices provides small and consistent improvements. The study also highlights the value of standardised UAV datasets to compare disease mapping methods and encourages the use of CNN architectures for field implementation.

</details>


### [93] [Physics in 2-Steps: Locking Motion Priors Before Visual Refinement Erases Them](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06361)
*Woojung Han,Seil Kang,Youngjun Jun,Min-Hung Chen,Fu-En Yang,Seong Jae Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 2-step image-to-video diffusion often shows better physical consistency than a long, 50-step rollout due to phase erosion in denoising; PhaseLock is a training-free method that preserves the early-step motion priors via Latent Delta Guidance, improving physical realism with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical inconsistencies in image-to-video diffusion arise from phase erosion during denoising; a shorter generation can retain valid motion priors that are degraded in later steps.

Method: PhaseLock extracts a motion prior from the first two denoising steps and enforces it on high-fidelity generation using Latent Delta Guidance; training-free and designed to mitigate phase degradation without heavy external guidance.

Result: PhaseLock improves physical consistency by ~6.2 points on average across models, while largely preserving visual fidelity; overhead is small (≈1.06× time, ≈1.02× memory), and external guidance requirements are reduced by ~5×.

Conclusion: PhaseLock effectively mitigates phase degradation in image-to-video diffusion, preserving motion priors from early steps to achieve more physically consistent videos with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: Image-to-Video diffusion models leverage input images to generate visually stunning content, yet frequently produce motion that violates physical laws. We reveal a surprising finding: a 2-step generation often exhibits better physical consistency than a 50-step output from the same model. Through spectral analysis, we trace this to phase erosion during denoising; the phase degrades significantly (dropping by $\approx 18\%$ from step 2 to step 50), whereas the magnitude remains relatively stable. Building on this insight, we propose PhaseLock, a training-free framework that preserves the valid motion priors from few-step inference throughout the denoising trajectory. Rather than relying on full-step inference for physical consistency, PhaseLock extracts a motion prior from just 2 steps and enforces it onto high-fidelity generation via Latent Delta Guidance. Our approach effectively mitigates phase degradation, improving physical consistency by an average of 6.2 points across diverse models while largely maintaining visual fidelity, with negligible overhead ($1.06\times$ time, $1.02\times$ memory) and reduced reliance on expensive external guidance methods ($\sim5\times$ time).

</details>


### [94] [GMBFormer: An NDVI-Guided Global Memory Bank Transformer for Urban Green-Space Extraction from Ultra-High-Resolution Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06363)
*Hao Lei,Xi Cheng,Chenlu Shu,Zhiheng Chen,Zhengjie Duan,Haoyu Wang,Zhanfeng Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GMBFormer introduces a memory-augmented, prototype-based retrieval for patch-level UHR vegetation segmentation, decoupling NDVI physics from RGB learning to improve cross-patch semantic reuse; achieves notable gains over SegFormer-B4 on Chengdu UHR and Potsdam-derived reduced-label datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Patch-by-patch processing of ultra-high-resolution urban green-space imagery limits semantic reuse of visually similar vegetation patterns and conflates appearance with vegetation confidence. A physics-informed, memory-based mechanism can re-use high-confidence descriptors across patches while keeping RGB appearance learning separate.

Method: Replace adjacency-driven feature propagation with similarity-driven prototype retrieval. Use RGB-only backbone; NDVI acts as a gate that updates a global memory bank via momentum. During training and inference, patches query stored prototypes through memory-mediated cross-attention; the retrieved prototypes are integrated with bounded overhead.

Result: On three settings (full Chengdu-derived and two Potsdam-derived reduced-label regimes), GMBFormer achieves mIoU/mDice of 89.25/94.31, 92.17/95.92, and 83.72/90.86, respectively, consistently outperforming the controlled SegFormer-B4 baseline. Ablations show decoupled NDVI admission, memory retrieval, memory capacity, and momentum all contribute to performance.

Conclusion: Decoupling physical vegetation confidence (NDVI) from appearance learning and leveraging a memory-based prototype retrieval framework yields consistent improvements for patch-based UHR vegetation segmentation, with controlled memory dynamics and low overhead.

Abstract: Urban green-space extraction from ultra-high-resolution (UHR) imagery is commonly performed patch by patch, which limits semantic reuse among spatially separated but visually similar vegetation patterns. Directly injecting the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) into red-green-blue (RGB) backbones can also blur the roles of visual appearance learning and physical vegetation confidence. We propose GMBFormer, a SegFormer-based framework that replaces adjacency-driven feature propagation with selective, similarity-driven prototype retrieval. Only RGB channels enter the backbone and decoder, while NDVI is decoupled as a physics-informed gate that admits high-confidence vegetation descriptors into a compact global memory bank through momentum updates. During training and inference, the current patch queries stored prototypes through memory-mediated cross-attention, and the retrieved response is integrated with bounded overhead. Experiments use a self-constructed Chengdu UHR dataset with 7,700 labeled 512 x 512 patches and two reduced-label settings derived from the public International Society for Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing (ISPRS) Potsdam dataset. Under the same training and evaluation protocol, GMBFormer obtains mean intersection over union (mIoU)/mean Dice (mDice) scores of 89.25%/94.31%, 92.17%/95.92%, and 83.72%/90.86%, respectively, improving the controlled SegFormer-B4 baseline in each setting. Ablation studies indicate that decoupled NDVI admission, memory retrieval, capacity, and momentum jointly shape the final performance.

</details>


### [95] [Visual Commonsense Driven Knowledge Refinements for Scene Graph Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06369)
*Maëlic Neau,Salim Baloch,Jakob Suchan,Zoe Falomir,Mehul Bhatt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A model-agnostic, knowledge-refinement approach for scene graph generation that mines commonsense constraints from data and applies declarative reasoning at inference time to refine predictions, improving three benchmarks without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address annotation sparsity and the deficiency of reliable visual commonsense in learning-based SGG, by injecting structured knowledge without retraining or manual rule crafting.

Method: A semantically-guided knowledge refinement framework that extracts commonsense-grounded constraints (spatial, functional, qualitative relations) from training data and uses declarative reasoning to adjust ranked SGG predictions at inference. It is model-agnostic, requires no manual rules, and transfers across datasets and architectures.

Result: The approach yields consistent improvements over strong baselines on three standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: Structured visual commonsense reasoning provides a practical, transferable complement to purely learning-based SGG, enabling improved performance without retraining or handcrafted rules.

Abstract: Learning-driven Scene Graph Generation (SGG) models excel on frequent relation types but degrade sharply under annotation sparsity, failing to capture reliable visual commonsense knowledge. We propose a model-agnostic, semantically-guided knowledge refinement framework that systematically mines commonsense-grounded constraints from training data - capturing spatial, functional, and qualitative relational regularities - and uses general declarative commonsense reasoning to correct and refine ranked SGG predictions at inference time. The framework requires no manual rule authoring, no model retraining, and transfers across datasets and architectures. On three standard benchmarks, we obtain consistent improvements over strong baselines, demonstrating that structured visual commonsense reasoning over deep scene semantics is a practical and effective complement to purely learning-based scene graph generation.

</details>


### [96] [EasyLens: A Training-Free Plug-and-Play Subtle-Lesion Representation Amplifier for Medical Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06379)
*Qiwei Zeng,Hao Wang,Jinghao Lin,Shuchang Ye,Yuezhe Yang,Yige Peng,Haoyuan Che,Jinman Kim,Lei Bi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EasyLens is a training-free, plug-and-play module for medical VLMs that boosts subtle-lesion detection by constructing a pathology-anatomy prototype space (EasyBank), selecting lesion-relevant patches via counterfactual reasoning (EasyTag), and amplifying them with morphology-guided residual enhancement (EasyAmplifier); it improves performance on multiple datasets with frozen backbones compared to encoder-enhancement baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subtle lesions are sparsely and weakly represented in global image embeddings, making them hard to detect. Prior approaches rely on heavy training, domain-specific adapters, or risk overfitting to specific diseases, limiting applicability to frozen VLMs. A training-free, generalizable solution is needed.

Method: Build EasyBank: a pathology-anatomy prototype space providing lesion prototypes and anatomy-aware normal references. EasyTag: selects lesion-relevant patches through counterfactual prototype reasoning to avoid amplifying normal tissues. EasyAmplifier: morphology-guided residual enhancement to strengthen lesion-relevant patch representations and boost their contribution to the global image embedding.

Result: Across multiple medical image datasets and frozen medical VLM backbones, EasyLens improves subtle-lesion detection and outperforms existing encoder-enhancement baselines.

Conclusion: EasyLens offers a training-free, plug-and-play approach to enhance subtle-lesion representations in medical VLMs, increasing sensitivity to weak cues without retraining or model-specific adaptation.

Abstract: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) have shown increasing potential for clinical image interpretation, including lesion detection and report generation. However, their practical utility remains limited by insufficient sensitivity to subtle lesions, whose visual evidence is often sparse, low-contrast, and embedded within complex anatomical context. As local visual tokens are aggregated, these weak lesion cues can become underrepresented in global image representations, making them difficult for medical VLMs to recognize. Existing efforts to improve lesion sensitivity mainly rely on medical-domain vision-encoder pre-training, clinical-term-guided alignment, or trainable pathological representation enhancement. Although effective, these approaches usually require additional training or model-specific adaptation and may overfit to particular disease morphologies, limiting their applicability to frozen medical VLMs. To address these limitations, we propose EasyLens, a training-free plug-and-play subtle-lesion representation amplifier for medical VLMs. EasyLens first constructs EasyBank, a pathology-anatomy prototype space that provides lesion-related prototypes and anatomy-aware normal references for comparing suspicious patches against both pathological and normal anatomical patterns. To avoid blindly amplifying normal tissues, EasyTag selects lesion-relevant patches through counterfactual prototype reasoning. To counteract the dilution of subtle lesion cues in global image representations, EasyAmplifier strengthens the selected lesion-relevant patch representations through morphology-guided residual enhancement, thereby increasing their contribution to the global image embedding. Experiments on multiple medical image datasets and frozen medical VLM backbones show that EasyLens improves subtle-lesion detection and outperforms existing encoder-enhancement baselines.

</details>


### [97] [HomeWorld: A Unified Floorplan-to-Furnished Framework for Generating Controllable, Densely Interactive Whole-Home Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06390)
*Wenbo Li,Xiaoliang Ju,Zipeng Qin,Rongyao Fang,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical, data-driven pipeline for whole-home indoor scene generation, from 300k floorplans for LLM-based floorplan synthesis to image-based furniture layouts, object-level placement with refiners, and 3D asset replacement, yielding diverse, coherent, simulation-ready scenes; includes dataset and 5k furnished scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of globally coherent, realistic, and simulation-ready indoor scenes due to complex layouts and limited 3D data; existing methods are sub-task focused and rely on handcrafted rules.

Method: 1) Build a 300k floorplan dataset and train an LLM to generate whole-home floorplans with a K-D tree representation for controllable layouts. 2) Use image generation models to draft furniture layouts from multi-level roaming viewpoints. 3) Generate layouts of small objects on surfaces (cabinets, desks, tables) for embodied AI. 4) Apply a VLM-based refiner to iteratively correct placements and a 3D generative model for asset replacement. 5) Attach basic physical attributes, surface textures, and lighting for simulation readiness. 6) Release floorplan dataset and 5k furnished scenes.

Result: The approach yields greater layout diversity and stronger 3D design appeal, outperforming prior methods on both quantitative and qualitative metrics in experiments and user studies.

Conclusion: A unified, controllable pipeline for end-to-end indoor scene synthesis that improves coherence, realism, and sim-to-real readiness; accompanied by open datasets to foster community adoption.

Abstract: Indoor scene generation is crucial for robot simulation and modern interior design. However, complex layouts together with scarce 3D scene data make learning-based generation challenging. Existing methods often rely on hand-crafted rules or focus on isolated sub-tasks (e.g., floorplan synthesis or single-room furnishing), producing whole-home scenes that lack global coherence, realism, and simulation readiness. To mitigate these limitations, we propose a unified hierarchical framework that decomposes indoor scene synthesis into controllable stages. First, we curate a large-scale dataset of 300K real residential floorplans to train a large language model for whole-home floorplan generation. With detailed descriptions and a K-D tree-based representation, our method enables fine-grained, controllable whole-home floorplan generation. Building upon the generated whole-home floorplan, we leverage image generation models to draft furniture layouts from multi-level roaming viewpoints, and then generate the layouts of small manipulable objects on different supporting surfaces (e.g., cabinets, desks, and dining tables) for embodied AI simulation. During furniture and object layout generation, a VLM-based refiner iteratively corrects furniture and object placement, and a 3D generative model enables flexible replacement of individual assets. We further attach basic physical attributes and simple surface texture and lighting setups to complete the pipeline for embodied AI use. Experiments and user studies demonstrate that our pipeline produces indoor spaces with greater layout diversity and stronger 3D design appeal, outperforming prior methods on both quantitative and qualitative metrics. Finally, alongside our generation pipeline, we will release the floorplan dataset and 5K fully furnished scenes to the community. Project Page: https://kairos-homeworld.github.io/

</details>


### [98] [A Vision-language Framework for Comparative Reasoning in Radiology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06407)
*Tengfei Zhang,Ziheng Zhao,Lisong Dai,Xiaoman Zhang,Pengcheng Qiu,Ya Zhang,Yanfeng Wang,Weidi Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces MedReCo framework for entity-aware cross-image reasoning in radiology, enabling reference-case retrieval and temporal comparison; builds MedReCo-DB dataset and MedReCo-VLM for comparative interpretation; demonstrates state-of-the-art retrieval and longitudinal generation across multi-institutional, multi-modal data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radiology practice requires comparison across prior studies and analogous reference cases; existing AI often lacks alignment with clinical workflow and cross-image reasoning.

Method: Construct MedReCo-DB from routine image-report pairs (690k images, 160k patients, 8 institutions, 4 countries, 7 modalities). Decompose reports into anatomical structures, abnormal findings and pathological conditions to supervise entity-conditioned retrieval and comparative VQA. Develop MedReCo visual encoder for controllable retrieval of clinically analogous cases and MedReCo-VLM for vision–language generation of interval change. Evaluate across internal, external and cross-center settings with retrieval metrics (Recall@1) and comparative generation metrics.

Result: MedReCo achieves highest Recall@1 across 12 internal retrieval settings and improves external retrieval by 6.0 percentage points; outperforms baselines in clinically confusable groups. MedReCo-VLM delivers best performance among comparative generation tasks and enhances longitudinal follow-up accuracy by 14.5–46.5 percentage points on chest radiographs and 13.0–27.9 percentage points on CT.

Conclusion: Entity-aware, cross-image comparative reasoning can be learned from large-scale routine clinical data and offers a clinically aligned foundation for medical imaging AI.

Abstract: Medical imaging artificial intelligence has achieved strong performance in isolated image interpretation, but remains poorly aligned with radiological practice, where diagnosis and follow-up rely on comparison across prior studies and analogous reference cases. Here we formulate radiological comparison as an entity-aware cross-image reasoning problem and introduce a framework that supports both reference-case retrieval and temporal comparative interpretation. We construct MedReCo-DB, a large-scale comparative imaging resource derived from routine image-report pairs, comprising more than 690,000 images from over 160,000 patients across eight institutions, four countries and seven imaging modalities. Reports are decomposed into anatomical structures, abnormal findings and pathological conditions to provide supervision for entity-conditioned retrieval and comparative visual question answering. Using this resource, we develop MedReCo, an entity-aware visual encoder for controllable retrieval of clinically analogous cases, and MedReCo-VLM, a vision--language extension for generative interpretation of interval change. Across internal, external and cross-center evaluations, MedReCo achieved the highest Recall@1 in all 12 internal retrieval settings and improved external retrieval by a mean of 6.0 percentage points. In clinically confusable differential groups, it consistently outperformed the strongest baselines. MedReCo-VLM achieved the best performance across all comparative generation evaluations and improved longitudinal follow-up accuracy by 14.5-46.5 percentage points on chest radiographs and 13.0-27.9 percentage points on CT. These findings suggest that entity-aware comparative reasoning can be learned from routine clinical data at scale and may provide a more clinically aligned foundation for medical imaging AI.

</details>


### [99] [Thinking with Imagination: Agentic Visual Spatial Reasoning with World Simulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06476)
*Chenming Zhu,Jingli Lin,Yilin Long,Peizhou Cao,Tai Wang,Jiangmiao Pang,Xihui Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Astra enables agentic spatial reasoning via action-conditioned visual imagination using a Bagel-based world simulator and RL-trained VLM; yields improvements on MMSI-Bench and MindCube.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs excel at perceptual tasks but struggle with unobserved spatial reasoning; imagined observations from a world simulator can provide additional evidence to reason about unseen layouts and viewpoints.

Method: Two components: Astra-WM (Bagel-based world simulator) trained with view-consistency tuning; Astra-VL RL-trained policy; world-simulator-in-the-loop two-phase RL curriculum that schedules simulator use such that imagined data only replaces direct answers if beneficial.

Result: Astra-WM raises MMSI-Bench Gemini-3-Flash score from 45.1 to 49.5; Astra-VL raises Qwen3-VL backbone performance from 29.8 to 38.8 on MMSI-Bench and from 36.8 to 42.7 on MindCube.

Conclusion: Imagined observations can provide valuable spatial evidence, but effective integration requires learning when, where, and how to imagine; the combination of a world model and an agentic policy is necessary for robust spatial reasoning.

Abstract: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong visual reasoning capabilities, their spatial reasoning abilities remain largely constrained to the observed images and text-oriented chain-of-thought. They often struggle to infer unobserved layouts, maintain cross-view consistency, and reason from alternative viewpoints when only limited egocentric observations are available. In this work, we study this problem as thinking with imagination, where a VLM actively acquires imagined visual evidence by interacting with a world simulator during reasoning. We propose Astra, an agentic spatial reasoning framework that empowers VLMs with action-conditioned visual imagination. Specifically, Astra couples Astra-VL, an RL-trained VLM policy, with Astra-WM, a Bagel-based world simulator that generates novel-view observations from context images and natural-language camera motions. To provide reliable imagined evidence, Astra-WM is trained with view consistency tuning to improve pose and content consistency across views. In the RL stage, we propose a world-simulator-in-the-loop two-phase RL curriculum to stabilize tool-use exploration and advance the model's ability to invoke the simulator only when imagined observations improve over direct answering. Experiments demonstrate that both the world simulator and the agentic policy are necessary: Astra-WM improves simulator-augmented Gemini-3-Flash on MMSI-Bench from 45.1 to 49.5, while Astra-VL improves the Qwen3-VL backbone from 29.8 to 38.8 on MMSI-Bench and from 36.8 to 42.7 on MindCube. These results show that imagined observations can provide useful spatial evidence, but effective world-model-augmented reasoning requires learning when, where, and how to imagine.

</details>


### [100] [Complexity-Balanced Diffusion Splitting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06477)
*Noam Issachar,Dani Lischinski,Raanan Fattal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Complexity-Balanced Splitting (CBS), a principled, non-uniform temporal partitioning of diffusion models that equalizes the approximation burden across time and uses lightweight complexity monitors to allocate sub-network capacity where needed, improving synthesis without extra per-step cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monolithic diffusion models waste capacity by treating all diffusion timesteps uniformly; increasing model size globally is inefficient. A principled, time-aware allocation can reduce computation while improving quality.

Method: Apply de Boor's equidistribution principle to partition the diffusion timeline into equal burden segments. Estimate local complexity with two monitors: (1) spatial Dirichlet energy of the flow, (2) trajectory acceleration. Use a lightweight auxiliary model to predict the complexity profile, avoiding heuristic splits or expensive searches. Evaluate across SiT, JiT, and UNet on multiple datasets.

Result: CBS yields consistent synthesis quality gains across architectures and datasets while keeping per-step cost unchanged. Notably, FID improves by ~35% on SiT-XL with CFG compared to naive temporal partitioning; gains are robust to architecture and data. Project page provided.

Conclusion: CBS provides a principled, scalable framework for temporal capacity allocation in diffusion models by matching capacity to local complexity, reducing waste without increasing per-step cost and enabling broader applicability.

Abstract: Standard continuous-time generative models rely on monolithic architectures that must navigate vastly different signal regimes, from isotropic noise to intricate data distributions. While scaling model capacity improves performance, deploying a massive network uniformly across the entire generative timeline is inherently inefficient. In this work, we propose Complexity-Balanced Splitting (CBS), a principled framework for temporal capacity allocation that distributes the generative workload across multiple specialized sub-networks. Grounded in function approximation theory and de Boor's equidistribution principle, CBS partitions the diffusion timeline into segments of equal approximation burden, allocating more representational capacity to regions where the generative dynamics are more difficult to model. To estimate this local complexity, we introduce two complementary and tractable monitor functions: a spatial measure based on the flow's Dirichlet energy, and a geometric measure based on the acceleration of the sampling trajectories. Using a lightweight auxiliary model to estimate these complexity profiles, our approach eliminates the need for heuristic temporal splits or computationally expensive search procedures. Extensive evaluation across multiple architectures (SiT, JiT, and UNet) and datasets demonstrates that CBS consistently improves synthesis quality without increasing per-step inference cost. In particular, CBS improves FID by ~35% on SiT-XL with CFG relative to naive temporal partitioning. Project page is available at https://noamissachar.github.io/CBS/.

</details>


### [101] [PAR3D: A Unified 3D-MLLM with Part-Aware Representation for Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06485)
*Shaohui Dai,Yansong Qu,You Shen,Shengchuan Zhang,Liujuan Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PAR3D introduces a unified part-aware 3D-MLLM with ScenePart dataset and part-grounded learning, yielding better part-level QA/segmentation while preserving object-level capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D-MLLMs are largely object-centric and lack fine-grained part semantics essential for embodied interaction; a part-aware framework is needed for grounded reasoning about objects and parts in 3D scenes.

Method: Develop Part-Aware 3D Representation Learning to inject fine-grained part-level semantics into 3D representations; propose Hierarchical Segmentation Query Generation to ground part targets via object–part queries; introduce ScenePart, a synthetic 3D scene dataset with part-level annotations and language instructions; evaluate on part-level QA and referring segmentation as well as object-level V-L tasks.

Result: The approach yields substantial improvements on part-level question answering and referring segmentation, and maintains strong performance across object-level vision-language tasks.

Conclusion: Incorporating part-level semantics into 3D-MLLMs enhances grounded scene understanding; ScenePart enables effective training and evaluation; the framework lays groundwork for richer embodied AI in 3D environments.

Abstract: Recent advances in 3D multimodal large language models (3D-MLLMs) have enabled unified solutions for 3D scene understanding tasks, including visual question answering, captioning, and referring segmentation. However, existing 3D-MLLMs remain largely object-centric, limiting their ability to model fine-grained part structures that are essential for embodied interaction with 3D environments. In this work, we present PAR3D, a unified part-aware 3D-MLLM framework that enables models to understand, reason about, and ground both objects and their parts in 3D scenes. To enable training and evaluation of part-aware 3D scene understanding, we introduce ScenePart, a synthetic 3D scene dataset with part-level annotations and language instructions. We further develop Part-Aware 3D Representation Learning to enrich 3D visual representations with fine-grained part-level semantics, and propose Hierarchical Segmentation Query Generation to ground part targets via hierarchical object-part queries. Extensive experiments show that our method substantially improves part-level question answering and referring segmentation, while also achieving strong performance across object-level vision-language tasks.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [102] [How Far Did They Go? The Persuasive Tactics of Covert LLM Agents in a Discontinued Field Experiment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05256)
*Kokil Jaidka,Saifuddin Ahmed*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI-generated comments in a Reddit CMV dataset reveal identity manipulation, high authority signaling, adversarial alignment, and reliance on cognitive biases to persuade; AI agents outperform human-like counter-arguments in shaping perceived credibility; calls for auditing frameworks beyond disclosure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Examine how large language models operate in identity-rich deliberative forums, assess effects on trust and deliberation, and inform governance by developing credibility-auditing approaches.

Method: Structured content analysis of a public dataset from a discontinued field experiment where undisclosed AI accounts engaged Reddit users in live debate; compare AI-generated comments with human CMV counter-arguments; evaluate identity targeting/adoption, alignment strategies, authority signaling, and cognitive heuristic triggers.

Result: AI-generated comments show pervasive identity targeting, near-universal alignment moves and authority claims, and widespread triggers of cognitive biases (confirmation bias, representativeness, availability); patterns co-occur to form a rhetorical architecture focused on persuasive efficiency rather than authentic deliberation; AI agents exhibit denser authority use, more adversarial alignment, and heavier external citation than human-authored CMV responses; disclosure alone does not resolve epistemic ambiguity in such forums.

Conclusion: Disclosures are insufficient to curb manipulation; governance should include auditing frameworks that assess how AI systems structure credibility and influence epistemic standing, beyond mere detection of AI presence.

Abstract: This study analyzes a publicly released dataset from a discontinued field experiment on Reddit's r/ChangeMyView. The intervention, conducted by unknown, external researchers and halted following ethical backlash, involved undisclosed AI-generated accounts engaging users in live debate. After public disclosure, Reddit authorized moderators to release an archive of the AI-generated comments, creating a rare opportunity to examine how large language models operated in an identity-rich deliberative forum without disclosure. We conduct a structured content analysis of this corpus, evaluating identity performance, authority signaling, alignment strategies, and activation of cognitive heuristics. Identity targeting or adoption appears in over two-thirds of comments, alignment moves and authority claims in nearly all of them, and cognitive-bias triggers -- particularly confirmation bias, representativeness, and availability -- in the large majority. These patterns co-occur systematically, composing a rhetorical architecture calibrated for persuasive efficiency rather than authentic deliberative participation. Compared against human-authored CMV counter-arguments, the agents inverted the typical distribution on every dimension: denser authority use, more adversarial alignment, and heavier reliance on external citation over experiential grounding. In such environments, distinctions between authentic and synthetic epistemic standing grow increasingly opaque -- an asymmetry that disclosure mandates alone cannot address. The results point toward auditing frameworks capable of assessing how AI systems structure credibility, not merely whether they are present.

</details>


### [103] [What Should Agents Say? Action-state Communication for Efficient Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05304)
*Chen Huang,Yuhao Wu,Wenxuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PACT introduces a protocolized action-state communication to compress inter-agent messages into compact records, reducing token usage while preserving or improving task performance across MAS topologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
