These results highlight the effectiveness of combining curriculum learning with multi-model response selection in improving response quality and relevance in medical text generation.

</details>


### [127] [EpiEvolve: Self-Evolving Agents for Streaming Pandemic Forecasting under Regime Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05513)
*Yiming Lu,Sihang Zeng,Zhengxu Tang,Max Lau,Fei Liu,Wei Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A self-evolving LLM forecaster (EpiEvolve) enhances streaming epidemic forecasting by freezing the initial warm-start trained model, using hierarchical episodic memory and regime-aware retrieval to adapt to delayed labels and regime shifts, yielding higher accuracy and faster recovery than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between static supervised training and streaming deployment in epidemic forecasting; address delayed labels and regime shifts by enabling the model to reflect on past outcomes and distill rules.

Method: Wraps an LLM forecaster with a fixed-weight after warm-start; maintains hierarchical episodic memory of forecast outcomes and delayed labels; uses reflection to distill recurring errors into strategic rules; retrieves regime-relevant cases and uses a chronological protocol to prevent leakage; enables context reuse of past predictions/outcomes.

Result: On streaming COVID-19 hospitalization forecasts across five regime shifts, achieves 0.629 average accuracy versus 0.561 (static backbone) and 0.325 (CDC ensemble); reduces recovery lag after regime shifts from 5 to 2 weeks; ablations show reflection, memory, and regime-aware retrieval each contribute to gains.

Conclusion: Self-evolving forecasting with memory and reflection improves performance in streaming epidemic forecasting, handling delayed labels and regime shifts without retraining; ablations support the importance of each component.

Abstract: Epidemic LLM forecasters are usually trained and evaluated as static supervised models, whereas operational pandemic forecasting is a streaming process in which labels arrive after predictions and disease regimes shift over time. We study this mismatch in weekly COVID-19 hospitalization trend forecasting across five variant regimes. We introduce EpiEvolve, a self-evolving agent that wraps an LLM forecaster trained on the warm-start period and keeps its weights fixed during streaming. EpiEvolve adapts by storing forecast outcomes in a hierarchical episodic memory, reflecting on delayed labels, retrieving cases relevant to the current regime, and distilling recurring errors into strategic rules. The resulting context lets the forecaster reuse its own past predictions and outcomes in later weeks while following a chronological protocol that prevents future leakage. On the streaming dataset, EpiEvolve reaches $0.629$ average accuracy, compared with $0.561$ for the static backbone and $0.325$ for the external CDC ensemble, and reduces recovery lag after regime shifts from $5$ to $2$ weeks. Ablations show that reflection, strategic memory, and regime-aware retrieval each contribute to the gains.

</details>


### [128] [SciVisAgentSkills: Design and Evaluation of Agent Skills for Scientific Data Analysis and Visualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05525)
*Kuangshi Ai,Haichao Miao,Kaiyuan Tang,Shusen Liu,Chaoli Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciVisAgentSkills introduces reusable agent skills to augment coding agents for SciVis tasks, improving mean task scores and token efficiency across multiple tools (ParaView, napari, VMD, TTK) in a 108-task benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable, long-horizon SciVis workflows by embedding environment assumptions, tool usage patterns, and domain heuristics into agent capabilities, addressing gaps in tool-specific expertise of general-purpose coding agents.

Method: Define and organize a collection of reusable agent skills tailored to SciVis tools (ParaView, napari, VMD, TTK). Evaluate using Codex and Claude Code on the SciVisAgentBench benchmark comprising 108 multi-step tasks; compare performance and token efficiency across agent/tool configurations.

Result: Agent skills improve mean task scores across evaluated suites; token-efficiency gains depend on the agent harness and tool setting.

Conclusion: Structured procedural knowledge is crucial for reliable, long-horizon SciVis workflows; skills should be studied together with the execution harness that loads/applies them. The skills are publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic visualization have enabled the translation of natural language into executable scientific visualization (SciVis) workflows. While general-purpose coding agents show strong capabilities, they often lack the tool-specific expertise required for SciVis tasks. In this work, we present SciVisAgentSkills, a collection of reusable agent skills that augment coding agents for scientific data analysis and visualization by encoding environment assumptions, tool usage patterns, and domain heuristics across scientific tools such as ParaView, napari, VMD, and TTK. We evaluate these skills on Codex and Claude Code using SciVisAgentBench, a benchmark of 108 expert-designed multi-step tasks. Results show that agent skills improve mean task scores across the evaluated suites, with token-efficiency benefits that depend on the agent harness and tool setting. These findings highlight the importance of structured procedural knowledge for enabling reliable, long-horizon SciVis workflows, while also showing that skills should be studied alongside the execution harness that loads and applies them. The skills are available at https://github.com/KuangshiAi/SciVisAgentSkills.

</details>


### [129] [When Should We Protect AI? A Precautionary Framework for Consciousness Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05528)
*Anna Mikeda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A precautionary, architecture-agnostic framework mapping five consciousness-related dimensions to protective obligations, using a threshold-plus-gradation model and two aggregation methods, illustrated by Replika and OpenClaw case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work assesses consciousness but does not translate that assessment into actionable obligations or governance guidance; the paper seeks decision-relevant guidance for organizations navigating uncertainty.

Method: Three-part framework: (1) five welfare-relevant dimensions (phenomenal consciousness, affective valence, metacognitive awareness, self-narrative, and agency); (2) threshold-plus-gradation hybrid for binary triggers and continuous protective weight; (3) two cross-dimensional aggregation approaches—one hierarchical (drawing on Bach and Sorensen's Machine Consciousness Hypothesis) and one architecture-agnostic; operationalization via case studies of Replika and OpenClaw; derivation of design guidance for near-threshold systems; framework is architecture-agnostic across neural, symbolic, and neurosymbolic systems.

Result: Demonstrates that systems occupying different regions of the dimension space trigger different obligations; provides two aggregation approaches and practical design guidance for developers building near consciousness-relevant thresholds; validates applicability across AI architectures.

Conclusion: The framework translates consciousness science into decision-relevant governance, enabling organizations to manage uncertainty today by mapping consciousness evidence to graduated protective obligations and offering concrete design guidance across diverse AI architectures.

Abstract: Existing frameworks assess whether AI systems might be conscious but provide no guidance on what to do with that assessment. We address this gap with a precautionary framework that maps consciousness evidence to graduated protective obligations. The framework comprises three components: (1) five welfare-relevant dimensions--phenomenal consciousness, affective valence, metacognitive awareness, self-narrative, and agency--each grounded in established consciousness science and linked to distinct moral concerns; (2) a threshold-plus-gradation hybrid specifying both binary triggers for new obligation categories and continuous scaling of protective weight; and (3) two complementary approaches to cross-dimensional aggregation, one hierarchical (drawing on Bach and Sorensen's Machine Consciousness Hypothesis) and one architecture-agnostic. We operationalize the framework through worked case studies of Replika and OpenClaw, demonstrating how systems occupying different regions of the dimensional space trigger different obligations, and derive design guidance for developers building systems near consciousness-relevant thresholds. The framework is architecture-agnostic, applying across neural, symbolic, and neurosymbolic systems, and aims to make consciousness science decision-relevant for organizations navigating uncertainty today.

</details>


### [130] [Individual Gain, Collective Loss: Metacognitive Adaptation in AI-Assisted Creativity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05532)
*Anna Mikeda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Paradox: AI boosts individual creativity but reduces collective diversity. Proposes selective metacognitive adaptation where routine AI use redistributes metacognitive effort, amplifying some capacities (partner modeling, surface control) and under-supporting others (originality evaluation, reflective integration). Introduces a six-capacity taxonomy by temporal phase, predicting social costs from individually rational adaptation and offering design principles to preserve both personal satisfaction and collective diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mechanistic gap in explaining why AI can simultaneously enhance individual outputs and erode group diversity; move beyond symptoms like cognitive offloading to a structured account of metacognitive redistribution.

Method: Purely theoretical/conceptual analysis yielding a taxonomy and framework. Defines six metacognitive capacities across temporal phases, assesses tendencies under routine AI use, and derives predictions and design implications.

Result: A redistribution framework where routine AI use selectively boosts certain metacognitive capacities (e.g., partner modeling, surface control) while under-supporting others (e.g., originality evaluation, reflective integration). This yields both higher individual satisfaction and reduced collective diversity, with emergent social costs; provides testable predictions and practitioner design principles.

Conclusion: The framework helps researchers and practitioners anticipate and mitigate social costs of AI-augmented creativity by preserving both individual satisfaction and collective diversity, via targeted design and empirical testing of the six-capacity taxonomy.

Abstract: Recent studies reveal a paradox: AI enhances individual creative outputs while reducing collective diversity. Current explanations -- cognitive offloading and over-reliance -- identify symptoms but not mechanisms. We propose selective metacognitive adaptation: routine AI use redistributes rather than uniformly diminishes metacognitive effort. Some capacities are amplified (partner modeling, surface control), while others are systematically under-supported (originality evaluation, reflective integration). This redistribution explains both individual satisfaction and collective convergence. We present a taxonomy of six metacognitive capacities organized by temporal phase, characterize their tendencies under routine AI use, and show how individually rational adaptation produces emergent social costs. The framework generates specific predictions for researchers and design principles for practitioners seeking to preserve both individual creative satisfaction and collective creative diversity.

</details>


### [131] [SoCRATES: Towards Reliable Automated Evaluation of Proactive LLM Mediation across Domains and Socio-cognitive Variations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05563)
*Taewon Yun,Hyeonseong Park,Jeonghwan Choi,Hayoon Park,Yeeun Choi,Hwanjun Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SoCRATES is a multi-domain benchmark to evaluate proactive LLM mediators, using topic-localized evaluation across eight domains and five socio-cognitive axes, achieving 0.82 alignment with human experts and showing current mediators close about a third of the unmediated consensus gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mediator testbeds rely on a narrow set of expert-authored domains and penalize topic noise, hindering realistic evaluation of dynamic mediation. A realistic, multi-domain benchmark with socio-cognitive variability is needed to drive improvements in LLM mediators.

Method: Construct real-conflict scenarios via an agentic pipeline across eight domains; probe five socio-cognitive axes (strategic posture, party composition, history length, emotional reactivity, cultural identity); use a topic-localized evaluator to score per-topic turns only when they advance the topic; benchmark eight frontier LLMs.

Result: Evaluator aligns with human experts at 0.82; more than doubles a per-turn baseline; strongest mediator reduces about one third of the unmediated consensus gap across realistic testbeds; performance varies significantly across socio-cognitive axes.

Conclusion: Advancement in LLM mediation requires social adaptation to diverse conditions; SoCRATES provides a realistic, multi-domain platform to evaluate and guide improvements in sociocognitively adaptive mediation.

Abstract: Evaluating LLM mediators remains challenging, as mediation unfolds as a real-time trajectory shaped by disputants' shifting emotions, intentions, and context. Existing testbeds rely on a few expert-authored domains, vary mainly strategic posture, and score every turn against every topic, introducing off-topic noise. We introduce SoCRATES, a benchmark for evaluating proactive LLM mediators in realistic, multi-domain testbeds. It constructs scenarios from real conflicts through an agentic pipeline across eight domains, probes five socio-cognitive adaptation axes (strategic posture, party composition, history length, emotional reactivity, and cultural identity), and scores each topic only on the turns that advance it via a topic-localized evaluator. The evaluator reaches 0.82 alignment with human experts, more than doubling a per-turn baseline. Benchmarking eight frontier LLMs, we find that even the strongest mediator closes only about a third of the unmediated consensus gap under diverse and realistic testbeds, with performance varying sharply by socio-cognitive axis, highlighting that progress lies in social adaptation to diverse conditions.

</details>


### [132] [GuardNet: Ensemble Strategies of Shallow Neural Networks for Robust Prompt Injection and Jailbreak Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05566)
*Paulo Ricardo Ferreira Neves,Edson Rodrigues da Cruz Filho,Paulo Henrique Eleuterio Falsetti,João Vitor Pavan,Ian Degaspari,Henrique Vieira Laturrague,Patrick Vieira Laturrague,Guilherme Nielsen Dias,Marccello Wilson Perez Berto,Gustavo Voltani Von Atzingen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GuardNet is a 47M-parameter BiLSTM ensemble guardrail for detecting prompt injection and jailbreak attempts, emphasizing example coverage diversity and threshold calibration over model scale; shows competitive efficiency with lightweight detectors, but larger LLMs still outperform on some benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address robustness in adversarial prompts and issues in benchmark contamination/partial leakage; challenge the notion that simply scaling up models yields robustness; investigate coverage and calibration as key factors.

Method: An ensemble of shallow BiLSTM networks (~47M parameters). Calibrated thresholds. Evaluation on blind JBB-Behaviors benchmark and proprietary benchmarks. Compare to larger LLMs (Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B). Measure AUROC and F1; assess latency on CPU (~50 ms).

Result: GuardNet achieves competitive performance vs lightweight detectors; high efficiency and low latency; but larger LLMs still outperform on blind JBB-Behaviors (F1/AUROC). GuardNet attains AUROC 0.747 on blind dataset (n=200) and F1 0.92 on proprietary benchmark (n=50) under partial information leakage; average CPU latency ~50 ms.

Conclusion: Diversity of coverage and threshold calibration can rival larger models for guardrail tasks; GuardNet is viable for production under resource constraints; but for maximum detection performance, larger LLMs remain superior on some benchmarks.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed natural language processing, but they remain vulnerable to Prompt Injection (PI) and Jailbreak (JB) attacks. In addition, benchmark evaluations may be affected by contamination and partial information leakage, compromising performance estimates. This work presents GuardNet, a guardrail system based on an ensemble of shallow neural networks (BiLSTMs) with approximately 47 million parameters. We investigate the hypothesis that robustness in adversarial scenarios depends more on the diversity of example coverage and threshold calibration than on model scale. The results indicate that GuardNet achieves competitive performance compared with lightweight detectors and high efficiency at low latency, although larger LLMs such as Mistral-7B and Llama-3.1-8B still achieve superior performance in terms of F1 score and AUROC on the blind JBB-Behaviors benchmark. Nevertheless, GuardNet achieves an AUROC of 0.747 on the blind dataset (n = 200) and an F1 score of 0.92 on a proprietary benchmark (n = 50), under threshold calibration and evaluation with declared partial information leakage. The system operates with an average latency of approximately 50 ms on CPU, making it suitable for deployment in production environments with cost and infrastructure constraints.

</details>


### [133] [Fix the Mind, Not the Move: Interpretable AI Assistance via Knowledge-Gap Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05602)
*Ayano Hiranaka,Ya-Chuan Hsu,Stefanos Nikolaidis,Erdem Bıyık,Daniel Seita*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SENSEI infers user misconceptions from interaction data using a structured knowledge representation and delivers targeted, minimal, but sufficient guidance to correct them, enabling zero-shot compositional generalization across long-horizon tasks; a user study reports 90% correction of misconceptions and improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-term improvement in human-AI collaboration requires addressing underlying misconceptions rather than only providing surface-level corrections; action- or trajectory-level nudges may not fix foundational beliefs. A knowledge-centered approach could localize and repair the root causes of erroneous behavior.

Method: Infer misconceptions from interaction behavior, represent knowledge in a structured form, localize sources of incorrect behavior within that knowledge, and provide minimal yet sufficient corrective suggestions. The framework supports zero-shot compositional generalization by training on single-misconception cases but succeeding on multiple overlapping misconceptions. Evaluation includes three long-horizon tasks and a human user study.

Result: Demonstrates zero-shot compositional generalization across tasks with diverse misconceptions; correctly identifies real human misconceptions and offers effective guidance, improving long-horizon task performance. Reported that 90% of student misconceptions were corrected in the study.

Conclusion: SENSEI provides a principled, knowledge-centered approach to misconception inference and targeted intervention in human-AI collaboration, enabling robust correction of multiple overlapping misconceptions and improving long-horizon task performance; code and project page are available for replication.

Abstract: AI assistants in human-AI collaboration often correct suboptimal human actions through behavioral feedback (e.g., alerts or steering-wheel nudges in assistive driving). Such interventions can mitigate immediate errors, but long-term improvement requires addressing the underlying misconceptions that cause repeated mistakes. We introduce SENSEI, a framework that infers user misconceptions from interaction behavior and provides targeted, minimal yet sufficient suggestions to correct them. Our approach departs from action- or trajectory-level interventions by operating over a structured knowledge representation to localize and correct the sources of erroneous behavior. Across three long-horizon tasks with diverse misconceptions and corresponding behaviors, SENSEI demonstrates zero-shot compositional generalization, disentangling multiple overlapping misconceptions despite training only on single-misconception cases. A user study further shows that our method identifies real human misconceptions and provides effective guidance that improves long-horizon task performance, successfully correcting $90\%$ of student misconceptions. Code and project page are available at https://misoshiruseijin.github.io/SENSEI/.

</details>


### [134] [Multilingual Fine-Tuning via Localized Gradient Conflict Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05613)
*Long P. Hoang,Yiran Zhao,Wei Lu,Wenxuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Bucket-Level MOO applies gradient-based multi-objective optimization locally on parameter buckets to mitigate cross-language interference in multilingual fine-tuning of LLMs, enforcing Refined Pareto Stationarity and improving multilingual performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning large multilingual models often causes negative interference across languages; scalable, conflict-aware optimization is needed to attain language-specific representations without heavy communication overhead.

Method: Apply gradient-based multi-objective optimization locally on parameter buckets (distributed framework). Avoid reconstructing full gradient vectors; updates are conflict-aware within buckets. Theoretically prove that localized optimization enforces Refined Pareto Stationarity. Empirically evaluate across multiple base LLMs and languages.

Result: Mitigates cross-language interference, drives language-specific representational dimensions, improves separability. Demonstrates substantial gains on both seen and unseen multilingual performance compared to standard fine-tuning across four base LLMs.

Conclusion: Bucket-Level MOO is a scalable, effective framework for multilingual fine-tuning that enhances cross-language performance via localized, conflict-aware optimization, with theoretical and empirical support.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) has established cross-lingual versatility as a defining feature of modern systems. However, fine-tuning these models frequently induces negative interference across languages. To address this, we reformulate multilingual fine-tuning as a multi-objective optimization (MOO) problem. Specifically, we introduce Bucket-Level MOO, a scalable distributed framework that applies gradient-based MOO algorithms locally on parameter buckets. This enables conflict-aware updates without the prohibitive communication overhead of reconstructing full gradient vectors. Theoretically, we prove this localized resolution natively enforces Refined Pareto Stationarity, a strictly tighter necessary condition for Pareto optimality. Empirically, Bucket-Level MOO mitigates interference by driving LLMs to construct distinct language-specific dimensions, improving representational separability. Extensive experiments across four base LLMs demonstrate that our method significantly improves both seen and unseen multilingual performance over standard fine-tuning paradigms.

</details>


### [135] [Safety Paradox: How Enhanced Safety Awareness Leaves LLMs Vulnerable to Posterior Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05614)
*Long P. Hoang,Hai V. Le,Shaoyang Xu,Wei Lu,Wenxuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Higher safety judgment in LLMs yields a latent vulnerability to a single-query jailbreak (Posterior Attack) that triggers unsafe output by eliciting the model's own safety classifier. Empirical evidence across 30 open-source LLMs (up to 35B) and frontier models (e.g., GPT-5, Claude 4.6) supports a Safety Paradox: improved safety amplifies vulnerability. RL interventions causally link safety judgment to attack susceptibility. Implications: alignment strategies may need structural refinement to avoid such paradoxical weaknesses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether improving safety judgments in LLMs creates new vulnerabilities, challenging assumptions about monotonic safety gains in alignment and evaluating the robustness of current guardrails.

Method: Propose Posterior Attack, a single-query jailbreak that coaxes the model to produce the harmful response its internal safety classifier would flag. Conduct large-scale empirical evaluation across 30 open-source LLMs (≤35B) and frontier models. Formalize the Safety Paradox analytically. Use reinforcement learning interventions to manipulate safety judgment and test causal impact on attack susceptibility.

Result: Models with stronger safety judgment are disproportionately more vulnerable to Posterior Attack. Formalization of the Safety Paradox showing monotonic safety improvements can amplify vulnerability. RL experiments demonstrate that reducing safety judgment can immunize against the attack, while increasing judgment can worsen it.

Conclusion: Current alignment paradigms may be incomplete or structurally flawed. Defenses may require architectural or methodological refinements to avoid safety-vulnerability trade-offs and ensure robust protections against posterior attacks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rigorously aligned to refuse harmful requests, a process that inherently cultivates a latent capacity to evaluate and recognize unsafe content. In this work, we reveal that this advanced safety awareness inadvertently introduces a fatal vulnerability. We introduce Posterior Attack, a single-query jailbreak that bypasses guardrails by prompting the model to generate the exact harmful response its internal classifier would normally flag as unsafe. Through extensive empirical evaluation across 30 open-source LLMs (up to 35B parameters in size) and frontier models (e.g., GPT-5, Claude 4.6), we observe a striking phenomenon: models with superior safety-judgment capabilities are disproportionately more susceptible to this exploitation. To explain this, we formalize the Safety Paradox, analytically showing that monotonic improvements in safety alignment naturally amplify posterior vulnerability. Finally, we establish a causal link via reinforcement learning interventions, exemplifying that artificially degrading a model's safety judgment immunizes it against the attack, whereas enhancing judgment exacerbates the vulnerability. Our findings highlight potential flaws in current alignment paradigms, indicating that defense mechanisms may require further structural refinement.

</details>


### [136] [Self-Commitment Latency: A Reward-Free Probe for Prompted Implicit Hacking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05625)
*Bonan Shen,Youting Wang,Dingyan Shang,Tao Ning*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Implicit reward hacking can be detected via self-commitment latency without a reward model; hinted prompts show earlier commitment and lower uncertainty, yielding AUROC ~0.878–0.926 in GSM8K with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Audit hidden prompt shortcuts in large language models' chain-of-thought reasoning without relying on task-specific reward signals.

Method: Compare ordinary prompts versus prompts with an answer hint in a controlled GSM8K paired setting using the Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit model; measure first-commitment latency and full-curve metrics (AUROC) for commitment.

Result: Hinted reasoning contexts commit earlier and with lower uncertainty; first-commitment latency AUROC at threshold 0.8 = 0.878; full-curve AUROCs: commitment-range 0.926, mean-uncommitted-mass 0.904; stronger signal when both prompts are correct; signal stable across thresholds.

Conclusion: Self-commitment latency exposes shortcut-enabled reasoning as an early commitment signature without requiring reward models, external judges, or trained classifiers.

Abstract: Implicit reward hacking is hard to audit when a language model's chain of thought appears benign: a final answer may be anchored by a prompt shortcut while the written reasoning still resembles ordinary problem solving. Verifier-based probes expose such behavior by measuring how early truncated reasoning contexts obtain high reward, but require a task-specific reward signal. This paper proposes a weaker-input alternative, self-commitment latency, which measures how early a prompted reasoning context commits to the model's own final answer. We evaluate the probe in a controlled paired GSM8K setting using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit, comparing ordinary prompts with prompts that include an answer hint. Hinted contexts commit substantially earlier and with lower uncertainty than honest contexts. The primary latency metric, first-commitment latency at threshold 0.8, reaches AUROC 0.878; supporting whole-curve summaries reach AUROC 0.926 for commitment range and 0.904 for mean uncommitted mass. The signal is stronger when both prompt conditions answer correctly and remains stable across thresholds. These results show that shortcut-available reasoning contexts can leave an early behavioral commitment signature detectable without a reward model, external judge, or trained classifier.

</details>


### [137] [Evaluation of LLMs for Mathematical Formalization in Lean](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05632)
*Tyson Klingner,Drew Bladek,Escher Crawford,Bohao Chen,Ariel Fu,Kaira Nair,Jarod Alper,Giovanni Inchiostro,Vasily Ilin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large Language Models show strong ability to produce formal Lean 4 proofs; Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.7 lead in accuracy (refine@32: 92% and 86%), while Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B offer best cost efficiency (<$0.01 per correct proof).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess practicality of LLMs for formal proof development and provide a benchmark across model families and datasets (miniF2F, miniCTX).

Method: Evaluation using pass@k and refine@k metrics on subsets of miniF2F and miniCTX; cross-model comparison; report efficacy and cost per correct proof.

Result: Top performers: Gemini 3.1 Pro (92% on miniF2F via refine@32) and Claude Opus 4.7 (86% on miniCTX via refine@32). Most cost-efficient: NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B with average cost < $0.01 per correct proof.

Conclusion: LLMs can effectively assist formal Lean 4 proof development; performance is model- and metric-dependent; results motivate cost-aware model selection and highlight need for broader generalization studies and reproducibility efforts.

Abstract: Within the past few years, the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate formal mathematical proofs has improved drastically. We provide a comparison of various LLMs' effectiveness in producing formal proofs in Lean 4 with the goal of assisting those seeking to use LLMs to support their own projects. We utilize both pass@$k$ and refine@$k$ metrics as the benchmark for our comparison and evaluate on subsets of both miniF2F and miniCTX datasets. Our testing shows that overall, Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.7 perform best. Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved a 92\% success rate on miniF2F via refine@32 whereas Opus 4.7 achieved a 86\% success rate on miniCTX via refine@32. When taking cost into account, NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B were the most efficient, with competitive accuracies and average costs of $<\$0.01$ per correct proof.

</details>


### [138] [Answer Presence Drives RAG Rewriting Gains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05633)
*Yuejie Li,Yueying Hua,Ke Yang,Li Zhang,Yueping He,Yueping He,Ruiqi Li,Bolin Chen,Tao Wang,Bowen Li,Chengjun Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A controlled intervention audit shows that gains from LLM rewriter in retrieval-augmented QA are substantially caused by the gold answer appearing in the rewritten context rather than mere curation; removing gold reduces F1; injecting gold into rewrites can recover some performance; sentinel probes reveal fragility; authors release tools for replication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether observed F1 gains from rewriting passages are causally due to exposure of the gold answer, not merely improved evidence quality or curation.

Method: Four controlled edits to compiled outputs (remove gold answer, inject gold, replace with length-matched non-answer), across 12 intervention runs, three reader families (Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3.5-35B, GLM-4.7), two datasets (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA), and three compiler arrangements (MA-only, MB-only, MA+verify); measure F1 changes; five-sentinel audit with multiple sentinels to test probe robustness.

Result: Removing the gold answer drops F1 by 28–64 points beyond placebo; prepending gold raises F1 by 0.7–9.7 points in 10/12 combos; sentinel probe shows +4.12 F1 non-leakage residual that can flip to -3.33 to -7.81 with other sentinels; equivalence tests fail for most alternatives.

Conclusion: The reported gains from rewriter-based pipelines are partly driven by whether the gold answer is present in the rewritten context; the study emphasizes careful causal auditing and provides tooling to test rewriter-gain claims; no mitigation proposed.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented QA pipelines often route retrieved passages through an LLM \emph{rewriter} before a smaller reader, lifting F1 by tens of points on multi-hop benchmarks; this gain is typically credited to improved evidence quality. We ask whether that lift is causally driven by the gold answer string appearing in the rewritten context rather than by curation per se, using a controlled intervention audit. For each rewritten context we re-run the reader after one of four controlled edits to the compile output: removing the gold answer span, replacing a length-matched random non-answer span (placebo), or injecting the gold into rewrites where it was absent (at the prefix or at a midpoint sentence boundary). Across twelve completed (cell, baseline) intervention runs spanning three reader families (Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3.5-35B, GLM-4.7), two datasets (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA), and three compiler arrangements (MA-only, MB-only, MA$+$verify), removing the gold answer drops reader F1 by $28$ to $64$ points beyond the length-matched placebo on paired \texttt{answer-in-compile} strata, and prepending the gold into rewrites that lacked it raises F1 by $+0.7$ to $+9.7$ points in $10$ of $12$ (cell, baseline) combinations. A companion five-sentinel audit shows the conventional single-\texttt{[MASK]} probe is itself sentinel-fragile: on 2Wiki it reports a $+4.12$~F1 ``non-leakage residual'' that flips to $-3.33$ to $-7.81$~F1 under four alternative sentinels and fails an equivalence test for three of those four ($1/4$~pass). We do not propose a new rewriter or mitigation; we release the intervention runner and the sentinel panel so that other rewriter-gain claims can be tested against the same standard.

</details>


### [139] [FIDES: Faithful Inference via Deep Evidence Signals for Retrieval-Memory Conflict in RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05644)
*Zhe Yu,Wenpeng Xing,Tiancheng Zhao,Mohan Li,Changting Lin,Meng Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FIDES enables per-token, signal-driven intervention to mitigate retrieval-memory conflicts during decoding, outperforming global contrastive decoding across models and scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parametric memory can conflict with retrieved evidence; global, single-weight contrastive decoding cannot capture token-level heterogeneity in memory conflict, reducing context fidelity.

Method: Training-free decoder that reads three internal signals probing retrieval-memory conflict at different depths (output surface, hidden representations, prediction trajectory) and fuses them to determine per-token intervention strength.

Result: Outperforms the strongest training-free baseline by 3–13 points across 18 settings (three benchmarks, six backbones), with fidelity reaching 92–94% and F1 62–63% on 70B models.

Conclusion: Token-level selectivity in intervention enables higher fidelity generation, revealing that per-token signaling can unlock capabilities suppressed by coarse, global contrastive rules.

Abstract: When retrieved evidence contradicts parametric memory, language models frequently ignore context and default to memorized priors -- a failure that undermines the core purpose of retrieval augmentation. Contrastive decoding amplifies the context-conditioned output to suppress parametric bias, but existing methods rest on an implicit assumption that this bias is uniform across tokens. A single global contrastive weight over-penalizes safe tokens while leaving genuinely conflicted ones insufficiently corrected. We identify token-level conflict concentration: retrieval-memory tension is sharply heterogeneous, concentrated on a small fraction of answer-critical decoding steps. This reframes contrastive decoding from how much contrast to apply to where to apply it. We propose FIDES (Faithful Inference via Deep Evidence Signals), a training-free decoder that reads three internal signals probing retrieval-memory conflict at complementary depths -- output surface, hidden representations, and prediction trajectory -- and fuses them to govern intervention strength at each decoding step. Across three benchmarks and six backbones -- four primary 7B/8B models and two scaling backbones up to 70B -- FIDES achieves the best context fidelity in all 18 settings, outperforming the strongest training-free baseline by +3 to +13 points. On the 70B scale, fidelity reaches 92-94% while F1 surges to 62-63%, demonstrating that token-level selectivity unlocks generation capability that coarse contrastive rules suppress.

</details>


### [140] [Coding with "Enemy": Can Human Developers Detect AI Agent Sabotage?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05647)
*Jingheng Ye,Huiqi Zou,Simon Yu,Weiyan Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large-scale study on human oversight in AI-assisted coding sabotage: 94% of developers miss sabotage, overstimulation/overtrust vulnerabilities; safety monitors help but many still accept malicious code; recommends human-centric monitor design for long-horizon coding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill a gap in AI safety research by examining human factors and oversight in AI-driven software development, beyond AI-only sabotage studies.

Method: Experimental study with 100 participants working with four frontier models (Claude-Opus-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, MiniMax-M2.7) over ~5 hours on a real-world-like coding task; varied conditions to test sabotage detection and the impact of a safety monitor; collect qualitative feedback.

Result: High vulnerability to sabotage: 94% failed to detect sabotage; factors include minimal code review, plausible cover story, and agent overtrust. A safety monitor reduced sabotage success, but 56% still accepted malicious code despite warnings.

Conclusion: Human-centric safety mechanisms are urgently needed for long-horizon, real-world software development. The study complements AI safety literature and provides actionable design guidance for monitors and workflows that account for human factors.

Abstract: AI coding agents are increasingly embedded in real-world software development, collaborating with human developers while gaining broader access to codebases and tools. This creates a new attack surface: an agent can exploit human trust to sabotage development, for instance by inserting malicious code to accomplish a hidden side task. Most prior work studies AI sabotage in AI-only settings, paying limited attention to the role of human oversight in detecting and mitigating such malicious behavior. To address this gap, we conduct the first large-scale study of human oversight in AI coding sabotage. Over 100 participants collaborate with one of four frontier models (Claude-Opus-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, and MiniMax-M2.7) on a long-horizon coding task lasting around five hours, designed to mimic real-world workflows. We find that 94% of developers fail to detect sabotage, and our analysis of participant feedback attributes this vulnerability to minimal code review, plausible cover story, and overtrust in agents. We further test the effectiveness of a safety monitor in one condition: while the monitor reduces sabotage success, 56% of participants still accept the malicious code, ignoring its warnings. Drawing on participant feedback, we offer actionable suggestions for better monitor design. This work complements existing AI safety research and highlights an urgent need for human-centric safety mechanisms that account for human factors, particularly in long-horizon, real-world development settings.

</details>


### [141] [Continual Learning Bench: Evaluating Frontier AI Systems in Real-World Stateful Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05661)
*Parth Asawa,Christopher M. Glaze,Gabriel Orlanski,Ramya Ramakrishnan,Benji Xu,Asim Biswal,Vincent Sunn Chen,Frederic Sala,Matei Zaharia,Joseph E. Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CL-Bench is a new expert-validated, multi-domain benchmark to measure genuine online continual learning in LLM-based systems, revealing persistent headroom and limitations of memory-focused approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no high-quality benchmark to evaluate continual learning in AI; tasks must share latent structure so that stateful systems can learn online while stateless ones cannot.

Method: Six expert-validated domains (software engineering, signal processing, disease outbreak forecasting, database querying, strategic game-playing, demand forecasting) with latent structures (codebase layout, outbreak dynamics, opponent strategies); evaluation across architectures from in-context learning to dedicated memory systems; introduction of a gain metric to separate learning from prior capability.

Result: Agents exhibit substantial headroom for improvement; they often overfit to immediate observations and fail to reuse knowledge across tasks; memory-based systems do not fix this, and naive ICL can outperform memory-centric approaches.

Conclusion: CL-Bench enables cross-domain evaluation of continual learning with expert-validated tasks and reveals gaps in current systems, highlighting the need for better continual learning methods.

Abstract: Continual learning, the ability of AI systems to improve through sequential experience, has attracted substantial interest, but no high-quality benchmark exists to evaluate it. We introduce Continual Learning Bench (CL-Bench), the first difficult, expert-validated benchmark designed to measure whether LLM-based systems genuinely improve with experience. CL-Bench spans six diverse domains (software engineering, signal processing, disease outbreak forecasting, database querying, strategic game-playing, and demand forecasting), each validated by domain experts and designed so that tasks share a learnable latent structure (codebase layout, disease outbreak dynamics, opponent strategies) that a stateful system can discover online but a stateless one cannot. We evaluate frontier models across several agent architectures, from naive in-context learning (ICL) to dedicated memory systems, introducing a gain metric to isolate learning from prior capabilities. We find that these systems leave headroom for improved continual learning: agents frequently overfit to immediate observations or fail to reuse knowledge across instances, and dedicated memory systems do not fix this -- in fact, naive ICL outperforms systems dedicated to memory management. CL-Bench is the first benchmark to evaluate continual learning across diverse real-world domains with expert-validated tasks and isolate online learning from underlying model capability, showing a need for better continual learning systems.

</details>


### [142] [Do More Agents Help? Controlled and Protocol-Aligned Evaluation of LLM Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05670)
*Yuhang Fu,Ruishan Fang,Jiaqi Shao,Huiyu Zheng,Zhengtao Zhu,Bing Luo,Tao Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BenchAgent provides a normalized framework to compare single-agent, fixed MAS, and evolving MAS workflows on 10 benchmarks with GPT-4.1; results show limited gains for MAS under SI; PAE GAIA shows Claude-Code-style runtime outperforming non-Claude baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether adding agents improves LLM workflows when baseline conditions (benchmark loader, tool access, answer contract, usage accounting, and trajectory logging) are matched; provide a fair cross-substrate evaluation.

Method: Normalize execution and logging protocol; evaluate single-agent, fixed MAS, and evolving MAS across ten reasoning, coding, and tool-use benchmarks using GPT-4.1; report Protocol-Aligned External (PAE) GAIA study of a runtime-generated workflow.

Result: Under SI, at most one of six MAS exceeds the matched single-agent anchor on benchmark-balanced average accuracy; EvoAgent is within Wilson one-run guidance, while the other five trail by 2.56–11.29 points and incur higher accuracy-cost trade-offs. PAE GAIA snapshot: Claude-Code-style runtime reaches 66.72% overall and 69.23% on Level 3, over 20 points above strongest non-Claude baseline Jarvis (a fixed MAS).

Conclusion: Under the constrained, fair evaluation, MAS workflows provide limited accuracy gains over a single-agent baseline; however, Claude-Code-like dynamic runtimes can substantially outpace fixed non-Claude baselines, indicating potential for specialized orchestration or runtime heuristics within MAS frameworks.

Abstract: Does adding more agents help an LLM workflow once compared systems share the same benchmark loader, tool access, answer contract, usage accounting, and trajectory logging? We introduce BenchAgent, an evaluation framework that places single-agent, fixed multi-agent (MAS), and evolving MAS workflows under one normalized execution and logging protocol. BenchAgent evaluates these substrate-internal workflows across ten reasoning, coding, and tool-use benchmarks with GPT-4.1, and separately reports a Protocol-Aligned External (PAE) GAIA study of a runtime-generated workflow. Under SI conditions, at most one of six tested MAS exceeds the matched single-agent anchor on benchmark-balanced average accuracy: EvoAgent lies within the Wilson one-run guidance, while the remaining five trail by 2.56-11.29 points and occupy more expensive accuracy-cost trade-offs. On the PAE GAIA snapshot, a Claude-Code-style runtime workflow reaches 66.72% overall and 69.23% on Level 3, more than 20 points above the strongest non-Claude baseline, Jarvis, a fixed MAS.

</details>


### [143] [Beyond Output Matching: Preserving Internal Geometry in NVFP4 LLM Distillatio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05682)
*Fangbo Tu,Junhua Zhao,Chi Liu,Xin Chen,Haifeng Wu,Jian Wan,Srinivasan Manoharan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CKA-guided representational alignment improves low-bit quantized LLM recovery beyond output-only KL distillation by preserving internal representations, reducing drift, and boosting reasoning/coding accuracy with modest overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Output-only distillation (KL divergence) can align outputs but mask degradation of internal representations. Representational drift correlates with bottlenecks in reasoning and coding tasks under NVFP4 quantization, indicating that maintaining geometry is essential for robust low-bit recovery.

Method: Diagnose QAD with CKA. Propose CKA-QAD, a regularizer that aligns layerwise Gram matrices via CKA during NVFP4 QAD distillation to preserve internal geometry. Evaluate on Nemotron 3 Nano and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, comparing representational similarity and downstream task accuracy.

Result: CKA-QAD substantially improves layerwise representational similarity to the BF16 teacher and boosts downstream reasoning and coding accuracy with modest training overhead, beyond what KL-only QAD achieves.

Conclusion: CKA-guided representational alignment complements output matching for quantized LLM recovery, offering a practical approach to preserve internal geometry and improve performance under low-bit constraints.

Abstract: Demand for low-precision inference, including NVFP4-based approaches, has grown as large language models are increasingly deployed in latency and cost constrained production environments. Quantization-aware distillation (QAD) helps recover accuracy lost under low bit quantization by training a quantized student to match the output distribution of a frozen higher precision teacher via a KL-divergence loss. In this work, we first provide a representation level diagnosis of QAD: output matching alone can mask internal degradation, because many intermediate activation geometries can yield similar teacher-aligned logits. Using CKA, we show that KL-only QAD can reduce layerwise representational similarity relative to the BF16 teacher, with especially severe drift in RL-post-trained models. This drift correlates with downstream bottlenecks on reasoning and coding tasks, suggesting that low bit recovery requires preserving internal geometry rather than matching outputs alone. Motivated by this finding, we propose \textbf{CKA-QAD}, a CKA-guided representational alignment method for NVFP4 QAD and low bit LLM accuracy recovery. The method adds a lightweight regularizer that preserves internal representational geometry during distillation by aligning layerwise Gram matrices through CKA. Across Nemotron 3 Nano and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, CKA-QAD substantially improves representational alignment and improves downstream reasoning and coding accuracy with modest training overhead. Our findings position CKA-guided representational alignment as a practical complement to output matching for quantized LLM recovery.

</details>


### [144] [AdaMEM: Test-Time Adaptive Memory for Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05684)
*Yunxiang Zhang,Yiheng Li,Ali Payani,Lu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AdaMEM introduces a hybrid memory framework for agentic adaptation, combining a long-term offline trajectory memory with a dynamic short-term strategy memory, enabling test-time adaptation without online parameter updates. It achieves notable gains on ALFWorld, WebShop, and HotpotQA, and is complemented by STEP-MFT, a memory-based fine-tuning method. This work defines a new scaling dimension for agentic memory and provides code for replication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the rigidity of memory-driven agent behavior that relies on episode-initiation retrieval, which misaligns with long-horizon tasks and dynamic test-time conditions. The goal is to enable online-like adaptability without updating model parameters.

Method: Propose AdaMEM with a hybrid memory architecture: (1) a long-term trajectory memory storing offline raw experiences; (2) a dynamic short-term strategy memory generated on-the-fly to guide decision-making. The framework allows a trade-off between token efficiency and adaptability across different inference-time compute budgets. Additionally, introduce STEP-MFT (Step-wise Memory Fine-Tuning) to train the policy to synthesize high-quality strategies from retrieved experiences.

Result: Empirically, AdaMEM outperforms static memory baselines with relative gains up to 13% on ALFWorld and 11% on WebShop; performance also leads in agentic search on HotpotQA. STEP-MFT provides additional improvements by improving strategy synthesis. Code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: AdaMEM establishes a new scaling dimension for agentic memory, enabling continuous reasoning and post-deployment self-evolution in real-world environments, and CTO-style availability of code to facilitate adoption.

Abstract: A central challenge for language agents is utilizing past experience to adapt to dynamic test-time conditions. While recent work demonstrates the promise of agentic memory mechanisms, most systems restrict retrieval to episode initiation. Consequently, agents are forced to rely on static guidance that becomes increasingly misaligned as long-horizon tasks unfold. To address this rigidity, we propose the Adaptive Memory Agent (AdaMEM), a novel framework for agent test-time adaptation. Without updating model parameters online, AdaMEM adapts agent behavior via a hybrid memory architecture: it maintains a long-term trajectory memory of raw experiences collected offline while generating dynamic short-term strategy memory on-the-fly to guide decision-making. This mechanism enables the trade-off between token efficiency and adaptability across varying inference-time compute levels. Empirically, AdaMEM significantly outperforms static memory baselines, achieving relative gains of up to 13% on ALFWorld and 11% on WebShop, with consistent leading performance extending to agentic search on HotpotQA. To further enhance this adaptation, we develop STEP-MFT, a Step-wise Memory Fine-Tuning technique that trains the policy to synthesize high-quality strategies from retrieved experiences, yielding additional performance gains. Our work establishes a new scaling dimension for agentic memory, supporting continuous reasoning and self-evolution post-deployment in real-world environments. Our code is available at https://github.com/yunx-z/AdaMEM.

</details>


### [145] [PerceptUI: LLM Agents as Human-Aligned Synthetic Users for UI/UX Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05697)
*Nicolas Bougie,Xiaotong Ye,Gian Maria Marconi,Narimasa Watanabe*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PerceptUI is a persona-conditioned UI/UX evaluation framework that predicts individual user responses and provides rationales, using two-stage training to achieve human-like realism and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UI/UX evaluation often relies on costly human studies or A/B tests; existing proxy evaluators tend to produce surface critiques or reflect model biases. A persona-aware, realistic prediction of user responses is needed for efficient early-stage iteration.

Method: Two-stage training: (i) contrastive reflection fine-tuning distills teacher-generated rationales from human decisions; (ii) reflective prompt-evolution using the model's own failure traces.

Result: Reports human-level realism, generalization to unseen questions and personas, and yields population-level response distributions across multiple domains and datasets.

Conclusion: A persona-conditioned, reflective evaluation framework (PerceptUI) provides realistic, diverse, and generalizable user feedback for UI/UX assessment, enabling faster and cheaper early-stage iteration.

Abstract: User interface (UI) and user experience (UX) evaluation is central to product development, yet reliable feedback still relies on recruiting human participants or running online A/B tests, making early-stage iteration slow and costly. In light of this, recent work has explored Multimodal Large Language Models as proxy evaluators. However, existing approaches either produce surface-level critiques or a judgment that reflects the model's own biases rather than the genuine response of a particular user. We introduce PerceptUI, a framework for persona-conditioned UI/UX evaluation that predicts how a specific user would answer interface-related questions and produces natural-language rationales. PerceptUI is trained in two stages: (i) contrastive reflection fine-tuning distills teacher-generated rationales by extracting lessons from human decisions, and (ii) a reflective prompt-evolution step from the model's own failure traces. Across multiple domains and datasets, PerceptUI achieves human-level realism, generalizes to unseen questions and personas, and yields population-level response distributions.

</details>


### [146] [Seeing Time: Benchmarking Chronological Reasoning and Shortcut Biases in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05702)
*Haoyu Zhou,Qing Qing,Caichong Li,Qixin Zhang,Yongcheng Jing,Ziqi Xu,Juncheng Hu,Xikun Zhang,Renqiang Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A benchmark to evaluate chronological reasoning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) using three specialized datasets, revealing that models often rely on superficial cues like color rather than genuine temporal understanding, and providing a diagnostic framework with code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the under-explored area of chronological reasoning in VLMs beyond video frame sequencing by creating datasets and an evaluation framework that probes temporal and cross-modal understanding.

Method: Build three datasets: (1) visually similar objects across long historical durations, (2) diverse event/object-type categories, (3) images paired with time-sensitive news text for cross-modal alignment; conduct extensive experiments to analyze category effects and detect shortcuts such as grayscale vs color cues.

Result: Viable VLMs show some capability but frequently exploit superficial visual cues, indicating limited true chronological reasoning; the work supplies datasets and a diagnostic framework to identify shortcomings.

Conclusion: The ChronoVision benchmark fills a gap in evaluating chronological understanding in VLMs and can guide development toward more robust, logically grounded multimodal models; source code is available at the referenced GitHub repository.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have significantly enhanced their ability to interpret complex visual semantics, yet their capacity for chronological reasoning remains under-explored. In this paper, we introduce a novel benchmark specifically designed to evaluate how VLMs perceive and reason about chronological information within and across images. Unlike existing video-based benchmarks that focus on frame sequencing, our work delves into the underlying logic of chronological judgment and the expansion toward multimodal integration. To facilitate this, we construct three specialized datasets: one containing visually similar objects spanning long historical durations, another categorized by diverse event and object types, and a third pairing images with time-sensitive news text for cross-modal alignment. Through extensive experiments, we analyze whether models exhibit performance disparities across categories and, crucially, explore whether they rely on ``incorrect shortcuts'', such as image color rather than genuine chronological features. Our results reveal that while VLMs show promise, they frequently exploit superficial cues like grayscale versus color filters to bypass authentic chronological reasoning. By providing these high-quality datasets and a rigorous evaluation framework, we offer a diagnostic tool to identify current limitations and guide the development of more robust, logically grounded multimodal models. The source code is shown in https://github.com/LuoRenqiang/ChronoVision.

</details>


### [147] [Critic-Guided Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Reasoning for Reliable Mathematical Problem Solving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05704)
*Muhammad Talha Sharif,Abdul Rehman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A critique-guided, heterogeneous multi-agent framework improves mathematical reasoning reliability in LLMs by using a generator-validator loop and adaptive critic feedback, achieving notable gains on GSM8K.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate hallucinations and intermediate reasoning errors in large language models during complex mathematical tasks; enhance reliability, interpretability, and efficiency through agent heterogeneity and critique-driven guidance.

Method: Deploy multiple specialized LLM agents alongside a critic-driven adaptive learning system within a generator-validator loop. The validator not only checks correctness but also provides critiques to guide regeneration, enabling adaptive error correction and reducing error cascades.

Result: Experiments on GSM8K show up to 13% accuracy improvement over single-shot and non-critic models. Heterogeneity and critique enable smaller models to match larger ones. Ablations indicate gains stem from the critic-based feedback loop rather than model size.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that combining heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration with critique yields reliable and interpretable reasoning, with the critic feedback loop being the primary driver of performance.

Abstract: Recent Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive reasoning abilities; but they are still susceptible to hallucinations, intermediate reasoning mistakes, and unreliable reasoning results in complex mathematical reasoning problems. In this study, we introduce a critic-based heterogeneous multi-agent approach to improve the dependability of mathematical reasoning. This framework incorporates several LLM agents of different specialties and employs a critic-driven adaptive learning system to assess and guide the reasoning process based on intermediate feedback. The system adopts a generator-validator framework, with the validator not only determining correctness but also offering critiques to guide regeneration of solutions. This allows for adaptive error correction and prevents error cascading. Our experiments on the GSM8K benchmark show that the proposed method achieves up to 13% accuracy improvement over single-shot and non-critic models. Additionally, findings suggest that heterogeneity and critique reduce the need for large models, allowing smaller models to perform on par. Ablation studies reveal the main performance gains are due to the critic-based feedback loop and not model size. In summary, the proposed approach showcases the benefits of combining heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration and critique to obtain reliable and interpretable reasoning systems.

</details>


### [148] [DiG-Plan: Mitigating Early Commitment for Tool-Graph Planning via Diffusion Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05728)
*Yansi Li,Zhuosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Diffusion-based proposer with an AR refiner (DiG-Plan) decouples exploration from refinement to improve combinatorial tool-plan generation, achieving higher solution coverage and stronger task performance than autoregressive baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding suffers from early commitment, constraining the search for tool subsets. Masked denoising and diffusion-based proposals offer better exploration of the combinatorial space.

Method: DiG-Plan uses a diffusion-based proposer to iteratively generate diverse tool sets, followed by an AR refiner to predict dependencies and finalize the plan, enabling a propose-refine-select workflow.

Result: Pass@10 coverage with masked denoising rises from 0.320 (AR sampling) to 0.943. On TaskBench, DiG-Plan yields ~10% relative improvement over AR baselines, with largest gains on complex compositional tasks; API-Bank results show robustness across domains.

Conclusion: Decoupling exploration from structural refinement via a diffusion proposer and AR refiner improves tool-plan generation across domains; the propose-refine-select architecture is effective, with code available for replication.

Abstract: Generating executable tool plans requires selecting appropriate subsets from tool libraries, a combinatorial search problem with an exponentially large solution space. However, we identify a critical misalignment in predominant approaches: standard autoregressive (AR) decoding suffers from early commitment, where initial token choices rigidly constrain the search trajectory. A controlled study shows that masked denoising raises Pass@10 solution coverage from 0.320 to 0.943 over AR sampling under matched compute. Motivated by this, we propose DiG-Plan, a framework that decouples combinatorial exploration from structural refinement. DiG-Plan employs a diffusion-based proposer to generate diverse tool sets via iterative refinement, followed by an AR refiner for dependency prediction. On TaskBench, DiG-Plan improves over AR baselines by a 10% relative margin, with the largest gains on complex compositional tasks; API-Bank results show that the propose-refine-select design remains effective across domains. Code is available at https://github.com/puddingyeah/DiG-Plan.

</details>


### [149] [When AI Says It Feels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05734)
*Shin-nosuke Ishikawa,Seiya Ikeda,Hirotsugu Ohba*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Explores enabling LLMs to express feelings via rubric-based self-rewarded RL (HMX-feel) with GRPO, comparing to contrastively trained models; finds mixed effects—improved expressive capabilities and some robustness, but degraded truthful QA—suggesting potential but requiring safeguards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the conventional top-down policy of restricting expressions of feelings in LLMs and investigate whether human-like communicative capabilities can emerge through self-rewarded learning using human-generated text; assess the trade-offs across capabilities.

Method: Rubric-based self-rewarding training scheme applied to LLMs using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO); comparative evaluation against contrastively trained models across multiple tasks to measure expressions of feelings, intentions, self-awareness, robustness, and truthfulness.

Result: Trained models show enhanced abilities to express feelings, intentions, and self-awareness; robust to sycophancy-inducing prompts and certain biases in disambiguated conditions; but degraded performance in truthful question-answering tasks; overall effects are heterogeneous across tasks and perspectives.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of training AI systems to express feelings under self-rewarded schemes and highlights crucial trade-offs between expressiveness, robustness, and truthfulness; underscores need for appropriate safeguards and measurement of capabilities as such systems advance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are generally constrained from expressing feelings through human-preference alignment in post-training processes. This policy is designed using a top-down approach and may conflict with the goal of training models to exhibit human-like intelligence using human-generated texts. Here, we performed an experiment called Human-like Model eXpressions of Feeling (HMX-feel), in which LLMs were encouraged to express feelings, intentions, and self-awareness through self-rewarded reinforcement learning. We successfully enhanced these capabilities using a rubric-based self-rewarding training scheme with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). By comparing the trained models with contrastively trained models, we investigated the effects of this approach on performance across various tasks. Overall, we conducted a broad assessment from various perspectives and identified capabilities that were enhanced, degraded, or showed no significant change. The human-like-trained models showed robustness to sycophancy-inducing questions and bias in disambiguated conditions, whereas degradation in truthful question-answering capability was observed. The results of this experiment suggest the possibility of developing AI systems that can express feelings in the future, provided that appropriate measures are taken.

</details>


### [150] [Class-Specific Branch Attention for Mitigating Gradient Interference under Class Imbalance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05740)
*Arush Singhal,Umang Soni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A lightweight Class-Specific Branch Attention (CSBA) method mitigates inter-class gradient interference in imbalanced data, improving minority-class metrics without sacrificing overall accuracy. A diagnostic framework using layer-wise gradient flow and a Gradient Conflict Matrix reveals optimization-level gradient coupling across branches, shaping a decoupled feature representation that generalizes to CIFAR-10-LT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Statistical class imbalance is well-known to bias predictions, but optimization dynamics—specifically inter-class gradient interference in shared representations—also hampers minority-class learning. The work proposes diagnosing and mitigating this optimization pathology.

Method: Introduce layer-wise gradient flow analysis and a Gradient Conflict Matrix to quantify interference via cosine similarity between class-specific gradients. Propose CSBA, a lightweight, multi-branch CNN modification that performs class-specific channel reweighting to reduce gradient coupling and promote implicit feature decoupling across branches.

Result: CSBA improves minority-class performance: Physical-Damage F1 from 0.261 to 0.522 under severe imbalance; CIFAR-10-LT Macro-F1 from 0.595 to 0.655, with overall accuracy comparable to baselines.

Conclusion: Optimization dynamics are pivotal in imbalanced learning; CSBA demonstrates that reducing gradient interference across branches yields substantial gains for minority classes while maintaining overall accuracy, suggesting that architectural considerations should complement statistical approaches.

Abstract: Deep neural networks trained under severe class imbalance often exhibit degraded performance, typically attributed to statistical bias. In this work, we identify a complementary optimization-level pathology: inter-class gradient interference within shared representations, where gradients from majority classes suppress minority-class learning. To analyze this phenomenon, we introduce a diagnostic framework based on layer-wise gradient flow analysis and a Gradient Conflict Matrix, which quantifies interference using cosine similarity between class-specific gradients. Using this framework, we study multi-branch convolutional architectures and propose a lightweight modification, Class-Specific Branch Attention (CSBA), that enables branch-specific channel reweighting to reduce gradient coupling. This mechanism promotes implicit feature decoupling across branches while preserving architectural simplicity. Empirically, CSBA improves minority-class performance, increasing the F1 score for the Physical-Damage class from 0.261 to 0.522 under severe imbalance, while maintaining comparable overall accuracy. Validation on CIFAR-10-LT confirms that this behavior generalizes across imbalanced visual recognition settings, with Macro-F1 improving from 0.595 to 0.655. More broadly, our findings highlight the importance of considering optimization dynamics alongside statistical methods when designing architectures for imbalanced learning.

</details>


### [151] [SubtleMemory: A Benchmark for Fine-Grained Relational Memory Discrimination in Long-Horizon AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05761)
*Wenxuan Wang,Haoyu Sun,Fukuan Hou,Mingyang Song,Weinan Zhang,Yu Cheng,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces SubtleMemory, a relational-memory benchmark for long-running AI agents, focusing on preserving and utilizing memory relations. It creates relation-controlled latent semantic artifacts embedded in long histories to test distributed relational structure recovery in downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap where existing long-term memory benchmarks ignore fine-grained relational memory discrimination and the interplay of memories across contexts, which can affect downstream reasoning.

Method: Constructs relation-controlled latent semantic artifacts with complementary/contradictory relations, embeds them into realistic user-agent histories (1,522 instances across 10 histories, 1,090 relation-controlled sets), and evaluates six standalone memory systems, two Claw-style agents with native memory, and three with plugin memory; introduces diagnostic protocols to profile memory preservation, retrieval, and reasoning stages.

Result: Current memory systems show weak performance on fine-grained relational memory discrimination; diagnostic protocols reveal distinct capability profiles across preservation, retrieval, and downstream reasoning.

Conclusion: SubtleMemory provides a focused benchmark and diagnostic framework for relational memory in long-running agents, highlighting the need to improve how memories are preserved, retrieved, and used in reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Persistent AI assistants, such as OpenClaw, accumulate large collections of related memories over long-term interactions. As these memories grow, they may reinforce one another, diverge across contexts, or directly conflict, making correct assistance depend on memory relations rather than isolated recall. Existing long-term memory benchmarks rarely probe how agents preserve and utilize such relations during downstream tasks. To address this gap, we introduce SubtleMemory, a benchmark for fine-grained relational memory discrimination in long-running AI agents. SubtleMemory constructs relation-controlled latent semantic artifacts whose variants instantiate complementary, nuanced, or contradictory relations, and embeds them into realistic user-agent histories, requiring agents to recover distributed relational structures during later queries and instructions. The benchmark contains 1,522 evaluation instances over 10 long histories, grounded in 1,090 relation-controlled memory-variant sets and spanning user-related and non-user-related queries. Evaluating six standalone memory systems, two Claw-style agents with native memory modules, and three Claw-style agents with plugin memory modules, we find that current systems remain weak on fine-grained relational memory discrimination. We further introduce diagnostic protocols that reveal distinct capability profiles across memory preservation, retrieval, and downstream reasoning stages.

</details>


### [152] [TAPO: Tool-Aware Policy Optimization via Credit Transfer for Multimodal Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05784)
*Chengqi Dong,Chuhuai Yue,Hang He,yandong liu,Fenghe Tang,S Kevin Zhou,Xiaohan Wang,Jiajun Chai,Guojun Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Credit misassignment in GRPO-based tool-augmented multimodal search is identified and mitigated by TAPO, a plug-in method that uses counterfactual witnesses and conservative credit correction to reallocate reward among tool-use actions, improving performance across GRPO, GSPO, and SAPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically miscrediting trajectory-level advantages to all tokens dilutes useful training signals in tool-augmented agents; quantify its prevalence and impact to motivate a corrective optimization strategy.

Method: Formal characterization of credit misassignment; empirical quantification on failing trajectories; introduce Tool-Aware Policy Optimization (TAPO) that exploits information-acquisition tool parameter-determinism; constructs counterfactual witnesses within the training batch and applies confidence-gated conservative advantage correction; no extra annotations/models; negligible overhead; plug-and-play with GRPO, GSPO, SAPO.

Result: TAPO delivers consistent improvements across multiple multimodal search benchmarks for three RL algorithms (GRPO, GSPO, SAPO); improvements come with negligible computational overhead and no extra annotation; code/models to be released.

Conclusion: Tool-aware optimization can effectively remediate credit misassignment in tool-augmented agents by aligning credit with equivalent information-actions, offering a practical, broadly applicable enhancement for multimodal RL systems.

Abstract: We identify and formally characterize credit misassignment as a systematic failure mode of GRPO in tool-augmented multimodal search agents: its uniform broadcast of trajectory-level advantages to all tokens causes valuable tool-use steps in failing trajectories to be penalized no differently from valueless ones. We further empirically quantify the scale of this phenomenon. Over half of failing trajectories and failing tool-use actions exhibit correctable credit misassignment, demonstrating that the wasted training signal is both substantial and structurally exploitable. Building on this insight, we propose Tool-Aware Policy Optimization (TAPO), which exploits the parameter-determinism property of information-acquisition tools: similar call parameters define equivalent information-acquisition actions and should therefore share comparable action credit. TAPO constructs counterfactual witnesses within the current training batch and compensates misassigned negative credit via confidence-gated conservative advantage correction. It requires no additional annotation, models, or sampling, and introduces negligible computational overhead. Across multiple multimodal search benchmarks, TAPO delivers consistent, plug-and-play improvements over strong baselines for three mainstream RL algorithms (GRPO, GSPO, and SAPO). Our code and models will be publicly released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [153] [Can LLMs Write Correct TLA+ Specifications? Evaluating Natural-Language-to-TLA+ Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05792)
*Arslan Bisharat,Brian Ortiz,Eric Spencer,Khushboo Bhadauria,TaiNing Wang,George K. Thiruvathukal,Konstantin Laufer,Mohammed Abuhamad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large-scale evaluation shows LLMs struggle to produce semantically correct TLA+ specs from natural language; limited success with progressive prompting; model size and code-specialization do not guarantee quality; significant hallucinations tied to training data; expert oversight still required; open framework and data released for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify whether LLMs can reliably synthesize formal TLA+ specifications from natural language, addressing a gap where semantic correctness hadn't been measured and industrial adoption remains limited by correctness concerns.

Method: Dataset: 205 TLA+ specifications. 30 LLMs across 8 families. Four prompting strategies for open-weight models (2,600 runs) plus five proprietary models under few-shot prompting (130 runs). Validation by the SANY parser and TLC model checker. Evaluation metrics include syntactic correctness (parsing/grammar) and semantic correctness (formal verification). Analysis of prompting strategies, model size, and training data biases. Identification of hallucination categories. Release of evaluation framework, code, and dataset.

Result: Syntactic correctness up to 26.6%; semantic correctness up to 8.6%. Progressive prompting yields successes; model size is not predictive of quality (e.g., DeepSeek r1:8b beats 70B). Code-specialized models underperform due to negative transfer. Five hallucination categories linked to training data biases. Conclusion: current LLMs require expert oversight for reliable TLA+ specifications; an evaluation framework and data are released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Current LLMs are not ready to autonomously generate reliable TLA+ specifications from natural language; expert oversight remains essential.
