The paper provides a reproducible evaluation framework, code, and dataset to guide future research toward improved alignment with formal languages.

Abstract: TLA+ has supported industrial verification at companies such as Amazon and Microsoft, yet writing correct TLA+ specifications from natural language still requires time and expertise, which limits adoption. LLMs show promise, but no prior study measures whether they produce semantically correct TLA+ specifications from natural language. This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of LLM-based TLA+ specification synthesis from natural language. Our study evaluates 30 LLMs across eight families on a curated dataset of 205 TLA+ specifications: 25 open-weight models across four prompting strategies (2,600 runs) and 5 proprietary models under few-shot prompting (130 runs), all validated by the SANY parser and TLC model checker. LLMs achieve up to 26.6% syntactic correctness but only 8.6% semantic correctness, with successes exclusive to progressive prompting. Results show that model size does not predict quality, e.g., DeepSeek r1:8b outperforms its 70B variant across all strategies, which suggests the importance of reasoning alignment for formal languages. Code-specialized models consistently underperform due to negative transfer from mainstream language training. We identify five recurring hallucination categories, all traceable to specific training data biases. These results suggest that current LLMs do not generate reliable TLA+ specifications without expert oversight. We release the evaluation framework, code, and dataset to support reproducibility and future research.

</details>


### [154] [From Risk Classification to Action Plan Remediation: A Guardrail Feedback Driven Framework for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05805)
*Yuhao Sun,Jiacheng Zhang,Shaanan Cohney,Zhexin Zhang,Feng Liu,Xingliang Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRIAD is a guardrail-integrated framework that uses guardrail-generated verbal feedback to iteratively revise agent plans, moving beyond binary allow/deny; it lowers average attack success and achieves a favorable safety-utility trade-off on ASB and AgentHarm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current guardrails often flag entire tasks as unsafe, harming benign components and lacking evaluation of downstream behavior. A closed-loop, plan-aware guardrail mechanism is needed to align agents with benign objectives.

Method: Fine-tune an LM to output one of three decisions (proceed, refuse, update) with structured natural-language feedback. Inject feedback into the agent's context to guide plan revision at each step, forming a closed loop between guardrail signals and planning. Train on a self-curated dataset; evaluate on ASB and AgentHarm datasets; provide code.

Result: TRIAD reduces average attack success rate to 10.42% and yields the best safety-utility trade-off among guardrail-integrated baselines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that guardrail-generated feedback can steer planning to preserve benign tasks while reducing harm; introduces a general, iterated guardrail-feedback mechanism with empirical validation.

Abstract: LLM-based guardrails typically safeguard agents by evaluating proposed actions or inputs before execution, producing safety signals such as binary allow/deny decisions, risk categories, and/or explanatory rationales about potential policy violations. However, agent risks often arise when otherwise benign tasks are contaminated by untrusted external content, unsafe instructions, or risky tool use. Existing guardrails often flag the entire task uniformly as unsafe, thereby blocking the threat but sacrificing the benign part. Moreover, existing work largely evaluates guardrails in isolation, leaving unclear whether their interventions lead to safer downstream agent behavior. To address this, we introduce TRIAD (Tripartite Response for Iterative Agent Guardrailing), a guardrail-integrated agent framework that leverages guardrail-generated verbal feedback as a guiding signal to keep the agent aligned with benign objectives at each planning step. We finetune a language model on a self-curated training dataset to output one of three decisions: proceed, refuse, or update, together with structured natural-language feedback. Rather than merely allowing or blocking execution, update guides the agent to revise its plan, avoid harmful components, and preserve the benign task where possible. TRIAD injects this feedback into the agent's context, enabling subsequent plan revision and forming a closed loop between guardrail feedback and agent planning. Extensive experiments on ASB and AgentHarm show that TRIAD reduces the average attack success rate to 10.42%, while achieving the best safety-utility trade-off among guardrail-integrated baselines. Our code is available at: https://github.com/YUHAOSUNABC/TRIAD.

</details>


### [155] [PLAN-S: Bridging Planning with Latent Style Dynamics for Autonomous Driving World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06014)
*Xiaoyun Qiu,Jingtao He,Yijie Chen,Yusong Huang,Haotian Wang,Yixuan Wang,Xinhu Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PLAN-S introduces a style-conditioned, four-channel semantic cost map decoded from latent world models to inject explicit risk, drivability, and style preferences into planning. It connects latent representations to planners via host-level fusion interfaces and shows measurable safety and performance gains on two driving benchmarks without retraining backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent world models compress scene dynamics but lose explicit control signals for risk, drivability, and driving style. This hampers supervision, inspection, and modulation of driving behavior before trajectory generation.

Method: Decode a style-conditioned four-channel semantic cost map from latent representations. The map is conditioned on ego state and driving style and is consumed upstream through two interfaces: attention-level fusion for regression planners and reward-level fusion for anchor-score planners. The approach is evaluated on two architecturally distinct hosts (ResWorld on nuScenes and WoTE on NAVSIM) with frozen backbones to isolate the bridge's contribution.

Result: On nuScenes, PLAN-S reduces L2 error at every horizon, achieving an average of 0.55 m and a 42% relative reduction in 3 s collision rate. On NAVSIM, the rule-cost variant attains 89.4 Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS); the learned-cost variant yields complementary gains on challenging scenes. Ablations indicate the cost pathway is the primary contributor to safer trajectory selection. Qualitatively, PLAN-S yields diverse, style-aligned cost maps with spatially consistent variations across driving styles.

Conclusion: PLAN-S provides a scalable, controllable bridge that injects style-aware risk/resource costs into planning via latent representations, enabling safer, more diverse trajectories while preserving backbone performance. It demonstrates that explicit cost supervision at the planning interface can significantly improve safety metrics without retraining latent world models.

Abstract: Latent world models (LWMs) have strengthened end-to-end autonomous driving by forecasting compact scene dynamics for downstream planning. However, existing LWM-based planners usually generate trajectories directly from entangled latent representations. This compact latent-to-planner pathway lacks explicit modeling of risk, drivability, and diverse style preferences, making driving-style dynamics difficult to supervise, inspect, or modulate before a final trajectory is selected. We propose PLAN-S (PLANning with latent Style dynamics), a planner-facing bridge that addresses this compactness-controllability dilemma by decoding a style-conditioned, four-channel semantic cost map from the latent representation. The cost map is conditioned on ego state and driving style and is consumed up-stream of the planning decision through two host-side interfaces: attention-level fusion for regression planners and reward-level fusion for anchor-score planners. We validate PLAN-S on two architecturally distinct hosts, ResWorld on nuScenes and WoTE on NAVSIM, while keeping the host backbones frozen to isolate the contribution of the proposed bridge. On nuScenes, PLAN-S reduces L2 at every horizon over the baseline, with 0.55 m average L2 and a 42% relative reduction in the 3 s collision rate. On NAVSIM, the rule-cost variant reaches 89.4 Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS), while the learned cost variant provides complementary gains on baseline-challenging scenes. Ablations show that the cost pathway contributes most directly to safer trajectory selection. Qualitative results further show that PLAN-S can produce diverse cost maps, with spatially consistent variations aligned to different driving styles.

</details>


### [156] [When Tools Fail: Benchmarking Dynamic Replanning and Anomaly Recovery in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05806)
*Dongsheng Zhu,Xuchen Ma,Yucheng Shen,Xiang Li,Yukun Zhao,Shuaiqiang Wang,Lingyong Yan,Dawei Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ToolMaze benchmarks dynamic path discovery and error recovery in Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) for LLMs, using a DAG-based complexity metric and a 2x2 perturbation taxonomy to separate replanning from trial-and-error. Results show robust degradation under perturbations, with implicit semantic failures causing the largest drops; recovery rates fall, complex topologies induce futile loops, and scaling improves fault-tolerance only marginally due to replanning bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current TIR benchmarks emphasize idealized, error-free execution paths. Real-world tool use involves failures and deception from tool outputs; there is a need to quantify robustness of LLMs under dynamic, realistic conditions and to distinguish systematic replanning from blind trial-and-error.

Method: Two-dimensional experimental design: (1) DAG-based topological complexity to model tool-use sequences, and (2) a 2×2 taxonomy of tool perturbations along axes explicit vs implicit and transient vs permanent. Evaluate various models on ToolMaze tasks, measure Perturbation Recovery Rate (PRR), analyze failure modes (e.g., implicit semantic failures), and study how topology affects search/loop behavior.

Result: Perturbations degrade performance across nearly all models, with the sharpest declines under implicit semantic failures. PRR drops by ~37% in corrupted-output scenarios, and complex topologies trap agents in futile trial-and-error loops. Model scaling yields fault-tolerance improvements but at 3.66× slower gains than basic task execution, signaling that dynamic replanning is a distinct bottleneck not fully mitigated by larger models or prompting strategies.

Conclusion: ToolMaze reveals a bottleneck in dynamic replanning separate from raw task execution. Fault-tolerance improvements require targeted strategies for error recovery and robust planning under tool-output perturbations, beyond mere scale or prompting. The work provides a diagnostic framework and benchmark data to guide future improvements in TIR robustness.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks evaluate Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) in LLMs on idealized ''happy paths'', largely overlooking real-world tool failures. We introduce ToolMaze, a benchmark for dynamic path discovery and error recovery in TIR agents. To separate systematic replanning from blind trial-and-error, ToolMaze adopts a two-dimensional design: DAG-based topological complexity and a $2 \times 2$ taxonomy of tool perturbations (explicit/implicit, transient/permanent). Evaluations show that perturbations degrade performance across nearly all models, with the sharpest drops under implicit semantic failures. Driven by systemic over-trust in corrupted outputs, Perturbation Recovery Rate (PRR) plummets by around 37\% in these scenarios, while complex topologies trap agents in futile trial-and-error loops. Crucially, agentic fault-tolerance improves with model scale $3.66\times$ slower than basic task execution, highlighting dynamic replanning as a distinct bottleneck unaddressed by model scaling or prompting. Data and code are available at https://github.com/Zhudongsheng75/ToolMaze.

</details>


### [157] [Statistical Priors for Implicit Preferences: Decoupling Skill Selection as a Local Harness in Personal Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05828)
*Zeyu Gan,Huayi Tang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A decoupled architecture for local preference learning in personal agents that modulates remote LLM decisions using localized statistical results, achieving lower regret and higher accuracy than memory-augmented baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable locally deployed personal agents to learn and adapt to implicit user preferences under constraints that prevent heavy centralized preference selection, by providing a lightweight local preference harness.

Method: Proposes an architecture that strictly decouples statistical preference learning from semantic intent parsing; utilizes localized statistical results to influence and modulate the selection decisions of the remote LLM.

Result: Extensive evaluations show the decoupled approach achieves the lowest cumulative regret and highest test accuracy, significantly outperforming traditional memory-augmented agents.

Conclusion: A decoupled design enabling local statistics to steer remote model selection is effective under local deployment constraints and yields superior performance compared with memory-augmented baselines.

Abstract: As Large Language Model (LLM) capabilities advance, locally deployed personal agents relying on API-based remote models and external skills have emerged as a novel paradigm. With the rapid expansion of available skills, enabling personal agents to learn and adapt to implicit user preferences becomes a critical challenge. However, local deployment constraints preclude complex centralized selection algorithms, creating an urgent need for a lightweight local preference harness. This paper explores the implementation of such a harness through a novel architecture that strictly decouples statistical preference learning from semantic intent parsing. Specifically, we leverage localized statistical results to influence and modulate the selection decisions of the remote LLM. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our decoupled approach achieves the lowest cumulative regret and highest test accuracy, significantly outperforming traditional memory-augmented agents.

</details>


### [158] [Agentic Molecular Recovery via Molecule-Aware Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05847)
*Suwan Yoon,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AMREC improves text-guided molecular generation by preserving molecular identity while repairing invalid drafts, via mismatch tracking, expanded candidate exploration, and trajectory-level selection, outperforming prior repair strategies on ChEBI-20 across three backbone models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Invalid drafts from LLM-guided molecular generation often lose target-relevant structural cues or the intended molecular identity. There is a need to move beyond mere validity restoration to identity-preserving recovery to align generated molecules with textual descriptions.

Method: Introduce AMREC, a recovery framework that combines molecule-aware mismatch tracking with expanded candidate exploration and trajectory-level selection. It leverages executable RDKit edit tools and evaluates on invalid ChEBI-20 drafts produced by three backbone models.

Result: AMREC achieves the strongest recovery profile across structural, exact-match, and string-level metrics, outperforming post-hoc repair, LLM-only correction, and generic agentic correction on invalid ChEBI-20 drafts from three backbone models.

Conclusion: Shifting focus from purely validity repair to identity-preserving molecular recovery improves alignment to the description and reduces undesired structural drift; AMREC provides a robust strategy for reliable text-guided molecular generation.

Abstract: Text-guided molecular generation with LLMs often yields invalid SMILES. We argue that invalid drafts should be addressed through a shift from validity-oriented repair to identity-preserving molecular recovery: the objective is not only to restore chemical validity, but also to preserve target-relevant structural cues and recover the molecular identity implied by the description. This perspective reveals the limitations of existing correction strategies. Post-hoc repair can recover validity while distorting key structures, LLM-only correction can introduce unintended global drift, and generic agentic correction remains constrained by greedy single-candidate trajectories even when equipped with executable RDKit edit tools. To address these limitations, we propose AMREC, which couples molecule-aware mismatch tracking with expanded candidate exploration and trajectory-level selection. On invalid ChEBI-20 drafts from three backbone models, AMREC achieves the strongest overall recovery profile across structural, exact-match, and string-level metrics.

</details>


### [159] [Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents: A Lightweight Framework for Measuring Behavioral Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05872)
*Olasimbo Ayodeji Arigbabu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents (EEA); a suite of entropy-based metrics (action, trajectory, tool, information gain, exploration efficiency, robustness entropy) with a Python toolkit to augment standard task-based evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current metrics focus on final task outcomes and rewards, but miss structural aspects of decision processes such as exploration, repetition, tool usage, uncertainty reduction, and robustness across runs.

Method: Formalize multiple entropy-based metrics (action entropy, trajectory entropy, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, robustness entropy); provide a lightweight Python implementation designed to integrate with agent frameworks (LangChain, Google ADK) and stored observability traces.

Result: Conceptual framework with a practical Python implementation; no empirical results reported in the abstract; toolkit is intended to complement traditional evaluation methods.

Conclusion: EEA offers a richer behavioral analysis by measuring information-theoretic properties of agent decision making and interactions, but its utility will depend on data availability, metric calibration, and empirical validation across domains.

Abstract: AI agents are commonly evaluated using task success, reward, latency, and cost. These metrics are useful, but they often miss important aspects of agent behavior: whether an agent explores too much, repeats itself too rigidly, uses tools effectively, reduces uncertainty over time, or remains robust across repeated runs. This paper proposes Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents (EEA), a lightweight framework for measuring agent behavior through entropy. Rather than treating intelligence as only final task completion, EEA studies the structure of the agents decision process. The framework introduces action entropy, trajectory entropy, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, and robustness entropy. These metrics are intended to complement, not replace, traditional evaluation methods. We also present a practical Python implementation designed to integrate with agent frameworks such as LangChain, Google ADK, custom agent loops, and stored observability traces.

</details>


### [160] [QCFuse: Query-Aware Cache Fusion via Compressed View for Efficient RAG Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05875)
*Jianxin Yan,Wangze Ni,Zhenxin Li,Jiabao Jin,Zhitao Shen,Haoyang Li,Jia Zhu,Peng Cheng,Xuemin Lin,Lei Chen,Kui Ren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: QCFuse introduces a compressed-view, query-aware selector for RAG cache fusion to reduce prefill latency while preserving full-prefill quality. It uses per-chunk anchors and critical-layer profiling to minimize recomputation without full-layer inspection, implemented in SGLang, and shows average speedups of 1.7x vs full prefill and 1.5x vs ProphetKV across 4 LLMs and 6 datasets, with full-quality results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG decoding incurs high prefill cost due to processing retrieved contexts. Existing selectors trade off quality and efficiency: fast query-agnostic or final-layer selectors can miss evidence, while full-view, query-aware selectors require broad context and stall the cache-fusion pipeline. A lightweight, query-aware approach is needed to maintain quality while accelerating prefill.

Method: QCFuse proposes a compressed-view, query-aware selector. It uses chunk-anchor query probing to condition user-query states on compact per-chunk anchors and employs critical-layer profiling to identify recomputation tokens without inspecting all layers. Implemented in SGLang and evaluated on four open-weight LLMs across six datasets.

Result: QCFuse achieves full-prefill-level quality. At matched quality, it yields an average prefill-time speedup of 1.7x over full prefill and 1.5x over ProphetKV (a strong quality-preserving baseline).

Conclusion: A lightweight, query-aware selector with compressed views can sustain full quality in RAG cache fusion while substantially accelerating prefill, outperforming the state-of-the-art ProphetKV baseline.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves large language model (LLM) answer quality by grounding generation in external evidence, but processing retrieved contexts makes the prefill stage a dominant serving cost. RAG cache fusion reduces this cost by reusing precomputed key-value (KV) caches for retrieved chunks and selectively recomputing tokens under the current prompt. Existing selectors, however, face a dilemma between quality and efficiency: fast query-agnostic or final-layer query-to-context selectors can miss request-relevant evidence, whereas full-view query-aware selectors require broad context and layer visibility before recomputation and therefore stall the layer-wise cache-fusion pipeline. We present QCFuse, a compressed-view query-aware selector for RAG cache fusion. QCFuse uses chunk-anchor query probing to condition user-query states on compact per-chunk anchors and critical-layer profiling to identify recomputation tokens without all-layer inspection. We implement QCFuse in SGLang and evaluate it on four open-weight LLMs across six datasets. QCFuse reaches full-prefill-level quality. At matched quality, QCFuse achieves an average prefill-time speedup of 1.7x over full prefill and 1.5x over ProphetKV, the strongest quality-preserving baseline.

</details>


### [161] [Retry Policy Gradients in Continuous Action Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05888)
*Soichiro Nishimori,Paavo Parmas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Pathwise derivatives enable extending retry-based objectives (ReMax) to continuous actions, introducing ReMAC, an off-policy actor–critic that uses these estimators. ReMAC achieves higher policy entropy without explicit entropy regularization and attains performance comparable to SAC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retry-based objectives (e.g., pass@K, max@K) favor exploration by optimizing the best return among multiple samples. The work seeks to extend these objectives to continuous action spaces and to understand the resulting learning dynamics and gradient shaping.

Method: Derive pathwise derivative estimators for retry objectives and apply them to extend ReMax to continuous action spaces. Analyze how the resulting policy-gradient landscape is reshaped (direction and magnitude), study Adam’s adaptive normalization effects, and implement ReMAC as an off-policy actor–critic that optimizes the ReMax objective via the pathwise estimator.

Result: ReMax-guided objectives modify gradient direction to encourage higher policy entropy and dampen gradient magnitude, with Adam mitigating the damping under appropriate stabilization parameters. Empirically, ReMAC increases policy entropy without entropy regularization and achieves performance comparable to SAC.

Conclusion: Pathwise derivatives enable effective continuous-actionRetry-based optimization (ReMax). The ReMAC algorithm demonstrates that higher entropy exploration can be promoted without explicit entropy terms and that the method can match state-of-the-art performance, offering a viable alternative to entropy-regularized RL.

Abstract: Retry-based objectives such as pass@K and max@K optimize the best return obtained from multiple sampled trajectories, and recent work has shown that they can promote exploration without explicit exploration bonuses. In discrete action spaces, ReMax was shown to do so by adapting to return uncertainty. In this work, we introduce pathwise derivative estimators for retry objectives and use them to extend ReMax to continuous action spaces. We study the resulting learning dynamics and show that, even with deterministic rewards, ReMax can encourage stochastic exploration by reshaping the policy-gradient landscape. In particular, it alters gradients both in direction, biasing updates toward higher policy entropy, and in magnitude, damping gradients and slowing convergence. We further show that Adam's adaptive normalization can mitigate this damping, depending on its numerical stabilization parameter. Empirically, we instantiate this objective as ReMax Actor-Critic (ReMAC), an off-policy actor--critic algorithm that optimizes the ReMax objective using a pathwise derivative estimator. Our experiments show that ReMAC can promote higher policy entropy without entropy regularization and achieves performance comparable to SAC.

</details>


### [162] [Retrospective Harness Optimization: Improving LLM Agents via Self-Preference over Trajectory Rollouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05922)
*Wenbo Pan,Shujie Liu,Chin-Yew Lin,Jingying Zeng,Xianfeng Tang,Xiangyang Zhou,Yan Lu,Xiaohua Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Self-supervised retrospective harness optimization (RHO) improves agent performance by re-solving challenging past trajectories to update the harness, without external labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth validation data is scarce in deployment; need automatic, self-supervised harness improvement to adapt to new tasks.

Method: From past trajectories, build a diverse coreset of challenging tasks; re-solve in parallel; use self-validation and self-consistency to propose harness updates; select best via pairwise self-preference.

Result: Single optimization round boosts SWE-Bench Pro pass rate from 59% to 78%; demonstrated across software engineering, technical work, knowledge work; indicates effective targeting of prior failure modes and improved long-horizon performance.

Conclusion: RHO demonstrates viable self-supervised harness optimization that reduces dependence on labeled data and enhances robustness and accuracy over long-horizon usage.

Abstract: AI agents rely on a harness of skills, tools, and workflows to solve complex problems. Continually improving this harness is essential for adapting to new tasks. However, existing optimization methods typically require ground-truth validation sets, yet such labeled data is difficult to acquire in practical deployment settings. To address this problem, we introduce Retrospective Harness Optimization (RHO), a self-supervised method that optimizes the agent harness using only past trajectories. Specifically, RHO selects a diverse coreset of challenging tasks from past trajectories and re-solves them in parallel. The agent analyzes these rollouts using self-validation and self-consistency, then generates candidate harness updates and selects the most effective one by its own pairwise self-preference. We evaluate RHO across three diverse domains, spanning software engineering, technical work, and knowledge work. Notably, a single optimization round improves the pass rate on SWE-Bench Pro from 59% to 78% without any external grading. Furthermore, our analysis demonstrates that RHO effectively targets prior failure modes. As a result, the optimized harness alters the agent's behavior patterns and sustains higher accuracy during long-horizon sessions.

</details>


### [163] [Towards World Models in Biomedical Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05925)
*Guangyu Wang,Jingkun Yue,Siqi Zhang,Yu Liu,Xiaoyu Wang,Mingyuan Meng,Changwei Ji,Zongbo Han,Yulin Wang,Yang Yue,Frank Fu,Ting Chen,Song Wu,Ziwei Liu,Jiangning Song,Ming Li,Gao Huang,Xiaohong Liu,Athanasios Vasilakos,Xingcai Zhang,Ping Zhang,Yong Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes biomedical world models to simulate dynamic biological futures for discovery and planning, enabling virtual experimentation across cells, tissues, and patients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond static pattern recognition by large language and foundation models and toward prospective, intervention-conditioned simulations of biological systems that can guide therapy and research.

Method: A conceptual framework in which models learn latent representations of molecular, cellular, tissue, and clinical states and intervention-conditioned dynamics, enabling future trajectory simulation; positions these models as data engines, environment simulators, and planning substrates; discusses required data infrastructure, evaluation benchmarks, safety constraints, and governance.

Result: A blueprint for a family of world-models in biomedicine with potential applications in virtual cells, organoids, virtual patients, and surgical simulation; outlines necessary infrastructure, benchmarks, and governance. Lacks empirical validation in the abstract.

Conclusion: Biomedical world models may provide the foundation for simulation-guided, closed-loop and experimentally actionable biomedical discovery.

Abstract: A central goal of biomedicine is to understand, predict and ultimately control the dynamic mechanisms by which biological systems respond to perturbations, disease progression and therapeutic intervention. Although foundation models and large language models have accelerated biomedical data interpretation, most current systems remain focused on static pattern recognition rather than prospective simulation of biological futures. Here we propose biomedical world models as a paradigm for AI-driven discovery. These models learn latent representations of molecular, cellular, tissue and clinical states, together with intervention-conditioned dynamics that allow future trajectories to be simulated before actions are taken. We discuss how biomedical world models could function as data engines, environment simulators and scientific planning substrates across applications including virtual cells, organoids, virtual patients and surgical simulation. We outline the data infrastructure, evaluation benchmarks, safety constraints and governance frameworks required. Biomedical world models may provide a foundation for simulation-guided, closed-loop and experimentally actionable biomedical discovery.

</details>


### [164] [A Pre-Registered Causal Partition of Self-Consistency Elicitation and Reward Design in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05932)
*Yuze Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Decomposes naive reward-design impact in RL with verifiable rewards into elicitation and true reward-design components using a telescoping decomposition; reveals substantial bias in naive metrics, non-additivity, and provides a reusable audit harness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To untangle why naive rewards estimation misattributes policy sharpening to reward design; to provide diagnostic tools for alignment research and improve evaluation of reward signals.

Method: Analytical decomposition in a controlled tabular-GRPO simulator producing total = null + elicit + rd; evaluate across five prior-strength levels; pre-registered 2x2x2 factorial; a pilot gate; re-audit two published results; release a reusable one-command audit harness.

Result: Naive acc(TRUE)-acc(RANDOM) estimator is biased; reward-design fraction ranges 0.139 (ps=0.20) to 0.05 (ps=0.80); elicitation term flips sign at self-consistency crossover; interaction ratio 0.385; AxC effect -0.089; strong-prior regimes point-identified; near-crossover only bounded; re-audits show elicitation-dominated or reward-design dominated verdicts; demonstrates diagnostic value of the partition; released audit tool.

Conclusion: Partition diagnosis clarifies contributions to observed performance and cautions against misinterpreting naive rewards; supports reproducibility and auditability; provides a ready-to-use harness for alignment papers.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves reasoning even when the reward signal is spurious -- assigning credit to the group-plurality answer rather than a ground-truth verifier. Practitioners commonly interpret naive = acc(TRUE) - acc(RANDOM) as the reward-design effect. We prove this estimand is systematically biased: it conflates self-consistency elicitation (sharpening the policy toward its modal answer via majority pseudo-reward) with genuine reward-design signal. Using a controlled tabular-GRPO simulator we derive an exact telescoping decomposition total = null + elicit + rd and measure each term across five prior-strength levels. The reward-design fraction of the naive estimator ranges from 0.139 at weak prior (ps=0.20) to 0.05 at strong prior (ps=0.80), with the elicitation term flipping sign at the self-consistency crossover. A pre-registered 2x2x2 factorial confirms non-additivity (interaction ratio 0.385; AxC effect -0.089). A points-vs-bounds pilot gate shows strong-prior regimes are point-identified while near-crossover regimes are only bounded. Re-audits of two named published results yield ELICITATION DOMINATED (elicitation share 0.98) and REWARD DESIGN DOMINATED (rd share 1.18) verdicts respectively, demonstrating the diagnostic value of the partition. We pre-commit to submit regardless of flip outcome; a non-flip is a finding of equal standing. We release a reusable one-command harness for any alignment paper to run the same audit.

</details>


### [165] [Edit-R2: Context-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Turn Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05950)
*Yuxiao Ye,Haoran He,Fangyuan Kong,Xintao Wang,Pengfei Wan,Kun Gai,Ling Pan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Edit-R2 is a reinforcement learning post-training framework for unified multimodal models that enables robust multi-turn in-context image editing by reconstructing explicit session intent and jointly training discrete text-space reasoning with continuous latent-space image generation, plus trajectory filtering; it is evaluated on MICE-Bench with metrics for instruction following, content consistency, and global awareness, showing strong improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the realistic need for multi-turn in-context editing where users progressively refine images. Two coupled failure modes are critical: long-context dilution (preserving sparse, accumulated constraints over growing history) and state contamination (early mistakes hurting later edits). A unified RL objective over reasoning and generation and a mechanism to filter corrupted trajectories are proposed.

Method: Edit-R2 reconstructs operative session intent before each editing turn to consolidate constraints into an explicit reasoning trace. It enables multi-turn RL over both a discrete text-space reasoning component and a continuous latent-space generation component (flow-matching). A trajectory filtering mechanism suppresses corrupted rollouts to stabilize training under state contamination.

Result: Empirical results show substantial improvements in multi-turn in-context editing. Edit-R2 achieves competitive performance against strong baselines. A new MICE-Bench benchmark provides automated metrics for instruction following, content consistency, and global awareness over accumulated session constraints, enabling systematic evaluation.

Conclusion: The proposed RL post-training framework effectively addresses long-context dilution and state contamination in multi-turn in-context editing by explicit intent reconstruction and joint optimization across reasoning and generation, with a dedicated benchmark (MICE-Bench) to quantify progress.

Abstract: Text-guided image editing has advanced rapidly with diffusion models and unified multimodal foundation models. However, most existing methods remain confined to single-turn settings, overlooking the more realistic scenario of multi-turn in-context editing, where users iteratively refine an image through a sequence of instructions. In this setting, a model must follow each new instruction while preserving accumulated session-level constraints, challenged by two coupled failure modes: long-context dilution, where sparse textual constraints become difficult to recover from growing interleaved image-text histories, and state contamination, where earlier editing mistakes degrade subsequent generations. We introduce Edit-R2, a novel reinforcement learning post-training framework for unified multimodal models. Edit-R2 reconstructs the operative session intent, which effectively consolidates scattered historical constraints into an explicit reasoning trace before each editing turn. It further enables multi-turn RL over both reasoning and generation through a unified objective that jointly optimizes intent reconstruction generation in discrete text space and flow-matching image generation in continuous latent space, while a trajectory filtering mechanism suppresses corrupted rollouts to stabilize training under state contamination. To support systematic evaluation, we introduce MICE-Bench, a large-scale benchmark for multi-turn in-context editing with automated metrics for instruction following (IF), content consistency (CC), and global awareness (GA) over accumulated session constraints. Experiments show that Edit-R2 substantially improves multi-turn in-context editing and achieves competitive performance compared against strong baselines.

</details>


### [166] [Bidirectional Search for Longest Paths: Case for Front-to-Front Heuristics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05956)
*Tzur Shubi,Ariel Felner,Solomon Eyal Shimony,Shahaf S. Shperberg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BiXDFBnB is a bidirectional depth-first branch-and-bound algorithm that adapts SFBDS to generalized longest simple path problems, reducing node expansions and sometimes improving runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bidirectional heuristics can lower search effort but front-to-front heuristics incur high overhead. The authors adapt SFBDS (for MIN) to GLSP/MAX problems to exploit paired-state symmetry and reduce overhead while handling overlapping constraints.

Method: Adapt SFBDS to GLSP/MAX by operating on paired states, enabling natural front-to-front heuristic evaluation and avoiding heavy bidirectional frontier management. Apply the approach to LSP, Snakes, and Coil-in-the-Box (CIB).

Result: Empirical evaluation shows BiXDFBnB frequently reduces the number of node expansions and, in some cases, improves overall runtime.

Conclusion: BiXDFBnB provides an effective framework for GLSP variants, reducing search effort and sometimes runtime by leveraging a SFBDS-based bidirectional approach and efficient handling of overlapping constraints.

Abstract: Bidirectional heuristic search can potentially reduce search effort for problems amenable to backward search. Therein, it is well-known that front-to-front heuristics can reduce the number of node expansions, but their overhead is so high that overall runtime almost always increases. We propose BiXDFBnB, a bidirectional depth-first branch-and-bound algorithm that adapts the Single-Frontier Bidirectional Search (SFBDS) framework - originally developed for shortest-path (MIN) problems - to the Generalized Longest Simple Path (GLSP) setting. Because SFBDS inherently operates on paired states, front-to-front (F2F) heuristic evaluation arises naturally and avoids the overhead typically associated with bidirectional frontier management. We show that this adaptation can be successfully applied to maximization (MAX) problems while efficiently handling overlapping constraints. BiXDFBnB is applied to several types of longest-path problems: Longest Simple Path (LSP), Snakes, and Coil-in-the-Box (CIB). Empirical evaluation shows that the new algorithm frequently reduces the number of node expansions and, in some cases, also improves overall runtime.

</details>


### [167] [The Self-Correction Illusion: LLMs Correct Others but Not Themselves](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05976)
*Kuan-Yen Chen,Fang-Yi Su,Jung-Hsien Chiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The study shows self-correction failures in LLMs are largely due to chat-template roles rather than cognitive limits; relabeling prompts with external roles drastically increases correction rates; proposes a role-aware prompt strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether low self-correction is a cognitive deficit or an artifact of how claims are framed in chat roles; test across domains and models with identical erroneous content.

Method: Systematically wrap a byte-identical erroneous claim in different roles (<thought>, user, tool, system memory) across 13 model-domain cells (7 families, 3 domains), 30 tasks per cell; measure explicit corrections; statistical tests (p<0.001 in 10/13 cells).

Result: Explicit-correction rate increases by 23-93 percentage points when the claim is presented under external roles; the effect is asymmetric, decomposable, and robust across domains.

Conclusion: Self-correction failure is a chat-template artifact, not a cognitive deficit; a prompt-structure-only intervention exploiting role labels can induce corrections; domain-dependent strongest labels: <memory> for math, user messages for logical deduction.

Abstract: Recent work shows that LLM agents struggle to correct errors in their own reasoning traces yet show markedly higher correction rates when identical claims appear under external sources. We ask whether this asymmetry reflects a capability deficit or a role-label artifact: does an agent's willingness to correct a wrong claim depend causally on the chat-template role that carries it, rather than on the claim's content? Our setup keeps the erroneous claim byte-identical across all conditions (SHA-256 verified) and varies only its wrapping role: the agent's own \role{<thought>}, a \role{user} message, a \role{tool} response, or a \role{system <memory>} block. Across 13 model-domain cells covering seven model families and three domains ($n{=}30$ paired tasks per cell), relabeling the claim from \role{<thought>} to an external role lifts the explicit-correction rate by 23 to 93 percentage points, with 10 of 13 cells reaching $p{<}0.001$. Further experiments confirm that the effect is asymmetric, mechanistically decomposable, and robust across domains. The failure to self-correct is not a cognitive deficit; it is a chat-template artifact. We exploit this artifact by designing a prompt-structure-only intervention that requires no training and no model modification, with its strongest role label being domain-dependent: \role{<memory>} dominates on math, while a plain \role{user} message dominates on logical deduction.

</details>


### [168] [Framing, Judging, Steering: An Assessable Competency Model for Teach-ing Students to Reason With Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05983)
*Alexander Apartsin,Yehudit Aperstein*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoRe-3 (Co-Reasoning) proposes a three-skill competency model for productive AI use in education: Framing, Judging, and Steering (FJS), separating pre-generation framing from post-generation steering with Judging as the gate. Instantiated in CoReasoningLab with flawed AI outputs to score each skill independently, showing dissociation in simulated learners; aims for diagnostic validity and open release of instruments, data, and protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current work often conflates prompt engineering with productive AI use and relies on single, opaque prompting scores. There is a need for an explainable, diagnosable framework that partitions the cognitive tasks involved in using AI: framing the task, judging outputs, and steering the model, with validated, independent measures.

Method: Define CoRe-3 with three competencies (Framing, Judging, Steering). Build CoReasoningLab platform presenting flawed AI outputs and scoring the three skills independently. Test propositions using simulated learners (generated and graded by different models). Assess discriminant and convergent validity and cross-backend agreement. Plan for human-rater validation and release instrument, data, and protocol.

Result: Simulated learners showed dissociation among the three skills: each skill tracked separate manipulated competence, with grades correlating only when a single competence was shared across all three. Grader backends from two providers showed convergent/discriminant validity. Human-rater agreement and outcomes are planned; platform released.

Conclusion: CoRe-3 provides an open, diagnosable framework to assess productive AI use (Framing, Judging, Steering). The instrument and protocols will be released; next steps include human-rater validation and broader reliability testing across more raters and platforms.

Abstract: Generative AI makes answers easy and understanding hard, and uncritical use invites cognitive offloading. Schools still measure unaided performance, yet the real task is to produce good work with AI: framing an ill-defined task, judging the output, and steering the model toward a better result. This ability is rarely assessed in its own right; where measured, it collapses into one "prompting" score that cannot diagnose why AI use succeeds or fails. We propose CoRe-3 (Co-Reasoning), a competency model factoring productive AI use into three assessable skills we abbreviate FJS: Framing (specifying an ill-defined task before invoking AI), Judging (evaluating output for errors and unstated assumptions), and Steering (iteratively redirecting the model). Its distinguishing claim is the separation of pre-generation Framing from post-generation Steering, with Judging as the gate between. We ground the skills in theory, state five testable propositions, and instantiate them in CoReasoningLab, an open platform that presents flawed AI output and scores them independently. Over simulated learners (generated and graded by different models), the skills dissociate: each tracks its own manipulated competence while staying flat in the others, and grades become correlated when one competence is shared across all three (convergent and discriminant validity), across grader backends from two providers. Human-rater agreement and outcomes are next; we release the instrument, data, and protocol.

</details>


### [169] [Beyond Vector Similarity: A Structural Analysis of Graph-Augmented Retrieval for Industrial Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06003)
*Grama Chethan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Structural reasoning over knowledge graphs remains hard for vector retrievers; an LLM-based planner using traversal primitives (and optional graph computation tools) significantly improves performance and generalization, revealing operator/tool bottlenecks and measurement gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark retrieval architectures for knowledge-graph–driven aerospace supply chain intelligence and identify bottlenecks in graph reasoning with LLMs beyond pure model size.

Method: Construct a 46-node knowledge graph with 64 typed edges; design 23 queries across 10 intents; compare eight retrieval architectures spanning text retrieval, graph traversal, and graph computation; evaluate with entity- and query-level F1; implement an LLM Query Planner using 9 traversal primitives; assess generalization to unseen queries; augment with 6 graph-computation tools and observe selective tool usage.

Result: Five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. An LLM Query Planner with 9 traversal primitives outperforms bespoke handlers (F1 = 0.632 vs. 0.472) and generalizes to unseen queries. Adding 6 graph-computation tools leads the LLM to selectively apply them only for query categories where traversal fails. A measurement gap is identified: entity-level F1 reveals structural-query accuracy even when standard scores are misleading.

Conclusion: The barrier to graph reasoning with LLMs is the operator/tool set, not model intelligence. An LLM planner with traversal primitives and selective graph computation improves performance and generalization; evaluation should incorporate entity-level metrics to expose structural-query success.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) fails systematically on queries requiring structural reasoning over interconnected entities. We compare eight retrieval architectures for aerospace supply chain intelligence, progressing from text retrieval through graph traversal to graph computation. Using a 46-node knowledge graph with 64 typed edges, we evaluate 23 queries across 10 intent categories and demonstrate that five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. Our central finding is the operator vocabulary thesis: the barrier to LLM-based graph reasoning is not model intelligence but the computational operators available as tools. An LLM Query Planner with 9 typed traversal primitives outperforms bespoke handlers (F1 = 0.632 vs. 0.472) while generalizing to unseen queries. Adding 6 graph computation tools, the LLM selectively adopts them for exactly the query categories where traversal fails. We also identify a measurement gap: entity-level F1 systematically underscores structural queries where comprehensive answers are correct.

</details>


### [170] [RedditPersona: A Modular Framework for Community-Conditioned LLM Adaptation from Reddit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06027)
*Amirhossein Ghaffari,Ali Goodarzi,Huong Nguyen,Simo Hosio,Lauri Lovén,Ekaterina Gilman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A modular framework (RedditPersona) standardizes data collection, community partitioning strategies, and evaluation for community-conditioned LLM adaptation using QLoRA adapters, enabling cross-study comparability; applied to 112 urban-wellbeing subreddits (301k users, 16M+ comments), revealing a consistent trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current studies on community-conditioned LLM adaptation make independent, ad-hoc choices about data collection, community definition, and evaluation, hindering comparability and reuse of artifacts.

Method: Collect Reddit posts/comments; profile active users; partition users under five strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, interaction-based); train a parameter-efficient adapter per strategy via QLoRA; evaluate with a shared suite (fluency, fidelity, distributional alignment, and community identifiability); apply to 112 subreddits in urban well-being (301,429 users, 16M+ comments).

Result: Adapters’ behavioral identifiability tracks each strategy’s intrinsic agreement with the subreddit baseline; a consistent trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity to real text holds across all five strategies.

Conclusion: A standardized, modular pipeline supports reproducible, comparable evaluation of community-conditioned adapters; framework generalizes across strategies and datasets, with code available for reuse and extension.

Abstract: Community-conditioned language model adaptation requires choices about data collection, community definition, and evaluation that are currently made independently in each study, making it hard to compare assumptions or reuse artifacts. We present RedditPersona, a modular framework that standardizes these choices: it collects Reddit posts and comments, profiles active users, partitions them under five grouping strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, and interaction-based), trains a parameter-efficient adapter per strategy via QLoRA, and evaluates them under a shared metric suite spanning fluency, fidelity, distributional alignment, and community identifiability. Applied to 112 subreddits in the urban well-being domain (301,429 user profiles, 16M+ comments), we find that adapters' behavioral identifiability tracks each strategy's intrinsic agreement with the subreddit baseline, and that a consistent trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity to real text holds across all five strategies. The code and configuration files are available at: https://github.com/Ahghaffari/redditpersona.

</details>


### [171] [Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06036)
*Shuo Ji,Yibo Li,Bryan Hooi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes MRAgent, a memory-augmented LLM framework that uses an associative Cue-Tag-Content memory graph and an active reconstruction mechanism to dynamically integrate memory access with reasoning, improving long-horizon tasks with lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memory-augmented agents rely on a static retrieve-then-reason pipeline, which cannot adapt memory access to evidence uncovered during inference, hindering long-horizon reasoning and efficient memory usage.

Method: Introduce MRAgent that combines an associative memory graph (Cue-Tag-Content) with an active reconstruction mechanism. The tags act as semantic bridges linking fine-grained cues to memory contents. The active reconstruction integrates LLM reasoning into memory access, enabling iterative exploration and pruning of retrieval paths based on accumulating evidence, thereby avoiding combinatorial explosion and enabling context-driven retrieval.

Result: Empirical results on LoCoMo and LongMemEval show substantial gains over strong baselines (up to 23%) and notable reductions in token usage and runtime, demonstrating the effectiveness of active and associative reconstruction for long-horizon memory reasoning.

Conclusion: Active, associative memory reconstruction substantially enhances long-horizon reasoning by making memory retrieval dynamic and reasoning-guided, offering a scalable alternative to static retrieve-then-reason approaches.

Abstract: Despite recent progress, LLM agents still struggle with reasoning over long interaction histories. While current memory-augmented agents rely on a static retrieve-then-reason paradigm, this rigid pipeline design prevents them from dynamically adapting memory access to intermediate evidence discovered during inference. To bridge this gap, we propose MRAgent, a framework that combines an associative memory graph with an active reconstruction mechanism. We represent memory as a Cue-Tag-Content graph, where associative tags serve as semantic bridges connecting fine-grained cues to memory contents. Operating on this structure, our active reconstruction mechanism integrates LLM reasoning directly into memory access, allowing the agent to iteratively explore and prune retrieval paths based on accumulated evidence. This ensures that memory retrieval is dynamically adapted to the reasoning context while avoiding combinatorial explosion caused by unconstrained expansion. Experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark and LongMemEval benchmark demonstrate significant improvements over strong baselines (up to 23%), while substantially reducing token and runtime cost, highlighting the effectiveness of active and associative reconstruction for long-horizon memory reasoning.

</details>


### [172] [Beyond Similarity: Trustworthy Memory Search for Personal AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06054)
*Jiawen Zhang,Kejia Chen,Jiachen Ma,Yangfan Hu,Lipeng He,Yechao Zhang,Jian Liu,Xiaohu Yang,Tianwei Zhang,Ruoxi Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MemGate is a lightweight, deployable plug-in that gate-keeps memory retrieval to improve trustworthiness of personal AI agents, reducing memory-induced threats while preserving long-term memory utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-term memory enables personalization but creates a trust boundary risk: semantically similar memories may be inappropriate; current memory pipelines leak or misuse info (cross-domain leakage, sycophancy, tool-call drift, jailbreaks). Need controllable, task-conditioned memory admission.

Method: Introduce MemGate: a query-conditioned neural gate inserted between the vector memory store and the LLM; 9M parameters, 35.1 MB; does not require LLM modification, memory DB rewriting, or inference-time LLM judge; operates across various memory frameworks and backbones; gates candidate memory representations to transform similarity search into task-conditioned admission.

Result: MemGate reduces memory-induced threats across multiple mainstream memory frameworks, real-world agent settings, and diverse LLM backbones while preserving the utility of long-term memory.

Conclusion: A lightweight, deployable solution to trustworthy memory search that can be integrated without changes to LLMs or data stores, enhancing safety without sacrificing personalization benefits.

Abstract: Personal AI agents increasingly rely on long-term memory to provide persistent personalization across sessions. However, existing memory pipelines are largely driven by semantic similarity: memory data close to the current query is retrieved and injected into the model context. This creates a critical trustworthiness gap, since a semantically related memory may still be contextually inappropriate, leading to threats such as cross-domain leakage, sycophancy, tool-call drift, or memory-induced jailbreaks.
  In this paper, we study memory search as a trust boundary in personal AI agents. We evaluate representative agentic memory frameworks, including A-Mem, Mem0, and MemOS, together with OpenClaw, a real-world personal-agent environment with persistent state and tool-use capability. Our results show that long-term memory is not merely a utility layer, but a durable control channel that can reshape how agents interpret tasks and execute actions, leaving them highly susceptible to the aforementioned threats. To mitigate these vulnerabilities, we propose MemGate, a lightweight and deployable memory plug-in for trustworthy memory search, with only 9M parameters and a 35.1MB footprint. MemGate is inserted between the vector memory store and the backbone LLM, requiring no LLM modification, memory-database rewriting, or inference-time LLM judge. It applies a query-conditioned neural gate to candidate memory representations, turning raw similarity search into task-conditioned memory admission. Across multiple mainstream memory frameworks, real-world agent settings, and diverse LLM backbones, MemGate reduces memory-induced threats while preserving long-term memory utility.

</details>


### [173] [When Should Memory Stay Silent: Measuring Memory-Use Boundaries in Memory-Augmented Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06055)
*Lingxiang Xu,Jiaoyun Yang,Min Hu,Hongtu Chen,Ning An*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RBI-Eval is a controlled framework to test when sensitive memory content should influence LLM responses by comparing model behavior with/without access to sensitive memory across memory-access settings; results vary across models, suggesting need for memory-aware retrieval and generation for safe personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in memory safety evaluations by focusing on the conditional integration of sensitive memory content in responses, rather than only retrieval accuracy or task utility.

Method: Controlled measurement study using RBI-Eval with a probe set that contrasts model behavior with and without access to sensitive memory under identical benign prompts, across four base LLMs and four memory-access settings (full-context exposure and three retrieval systems). Includes matched no-memory reference and control experiments to isolate content sensitivity.

Result: Significant behavioral divergence across models: the documented separation score for sensitive-memory integration changes when memory is available (decreases for GPT-5.4-mini but increases markedly for Claude-Sonnet-4.6, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, Qwen3.5-9B). Retrieval systems cut exposure but do not fully prevent integration once sensitive memory is retrieved. Effects appear specific to sensitive content, not general personalization.

Conclusion: Safe personalization requires memory-aware decision-making at both retrieval and generation stages; RBI-Eval provides a framework to evaluate when sensitive memory should influence responses and highlights model-dependent variability in safety risks.

Abstract: Long-term memory enables language model agents to support personalized interactions, but it remains unclear when available memories warrant integration into responses. Existing memory evaluations emphasize retrieval accuracy and downstream task utility, while overlooking whether retrieved sensitive memory content is warranted in the current turn. We introduce RBI-Eval, a controlled measurement study built around a probe set that compares model behavior with and without access to sensitive memory under identical benign prompts. We evaluate four base LLMs against a matched no-memory reference across four memory-access settings: full-context exposure and three retrieval systems. Our results reveal substantial behavioral divergence. With memory available, the separation score for sensitive-memory integration decreases by 8.9\%--26.6\% relative to the matched no-memory reference for GPT-5.4-mini, but by 51.1\%--82.9\% for Claude-Sonnet-4.6, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Qwen3.5-9B. Control experiments on DeepSeek and GPT-5.4-mini show this effect is specific to sensitive content, rather than general personalization. Retrieval systems reduce exposure but do not eliminate integration once sensitive memory reaches the generator. These findings suggest safe personalization requires memory-aware decisions at both retrieval and generation time.

</details>


### [174] [Learning Visual Spatial Planning from Symbolic State via Modality-Gap-Aware Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06076)
*Haocheng Luo,Jiahui Liu,Ruicheng Zhang,Zhizhou Zhong,Jiaqi Huang,Zunnan Xu,Quan Shi,Jun Zhou,Xiu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MGSD introduces a two-stage modality-gap-aware self-distillation framework to improve visual planning in vision-language models by combining cold-start grounding with privileged teacher distillation using symbolic states during training, while inference remains purely visual.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language models suffer from a perception–reasoning modality gap in visual planning, where latent state recovery from pixels and multi-step reasoning about that structure are both challenging. Bridging perception and planning with explicit state representations and training-time symbolic supervision can reduce this gap.

Method: A two-stage approach: (1) cold-start grounding to provide reliable visual state representations and reduce early perception noise; (2) privileged teacher on-policy distillation that uses explicit symbolic states to supervise the student’s visual rollout prefixes, with symbolic data used only during training. Inference is purely visual.

Result: MGSD yields consistent improvements in visual planning across 4B and 8B backbones, with macro-averaged gains of 19.3% and 18.4% respectively, narrowing the gap to symbolic-input upper bounds. Ablations indicate benefits stem from improved visual state recovery and optimal-path reasoning; code is released.

Conclusion: Modality-gap-aware self-distillation can enhance both perception and planning by aligning visual state recovery with planning over inferred structure, effectively reducing the perception–reasoning modality gap in visual planning tasks.

Abstract: While vision-language models excel at general multimodal understanding, they still struggle with visual spatial planning. We attribute this to a perception-reasoning modality gap: visual planning requires models to infer latent state structures from pixels and then reason over the recovered structure to produce valid actions, whereas symbolic planning directly leverages explicit objects and constraints. This creates dual bottlenecks in visual state recovery and multi-step planning. To address this, we propose MGSD, a two-stage modality-gap-aware self-distillation framework. First, a cold-start grounding stage equips the visual student with reliable state representations, minimizing early perception noise. Second, a privileged teacher transfers planning capabilities via on-policy distillation, using explicit symbolic states to supervise the student's own visual rollout prefixes. Crucially, symbolic data is used strictly during training, leaving inference purely visual. Experiments on visual planning benchmarks show that MGSD consistently improves visual planning across both 4B and 8B backbones, raising the macro average by 19.3% and 18.4%, respectively. The resulting models narrow the gap to symbolic-input upper bounds, while ablations and diagnostics confirm that the improvement comes from both visual state recovery and optimal-path reasoning. These results suggest that modality-gap-aware self-distillation improves not only how models perceive actionable states, but also how they plan over the inferred structure. Code is available at https://github.com/Oranger-l/MGSD.

</details>


### [175] [A Framework for Measuring Appropriate Reliance on Set-Valued AI Advice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06081)
*Ranjan Mishra,Jakob Schoeffer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formal framework for measuring appropriate reliance on set-valued AI advice in sequential judge-advisor tasks across classification and regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Set-valued AI advice communicates uncertainty and can improve decision-making; existing work focuses on point predictions, leaving a gap for evaluating reliance when advice is a set.

Method: Define evaluation dimensions for classification; introduce two metrics: correct reliance rate on AI and correct reliance rate on self; for regression, introduce quantity of AI reliance and quality of AI reliance; apply framework to show nuanced captures of human–AI collaboration.

Result: A principled framework that yields metrics capturing nuances in reliance that prior measures overlook across classification and regression tasks.

Conclusion: Introduces the first formal framework for assessing appropriate reliance on set-valued AI advice in sequential judge–advisor settings, enabling more precise evaluation and design of human–AI collaboration.

Abstract: Appropriate reliance on AI advice has become a central research theme in human-AI collaboration. Existing frameworks have focused exclusively on point predictions as AI advice. However, set-valued AI advice (e.g., discrete sets or continuous intervals) is increasingly being used to communicate uncertainty and improve human decision making. In this paper, we develop the first formal framework for measuring appropriate reliance on set-valued AI advice within the sequential judge-advisor paradigm, spanning both classification and regression tasks. For classification, we first introduce the dimensions that are necessary for evaluating set-valued AI advice. We then define two metrics: correct reliance rate on AI and correct reliance rate on self, which jointly characterize appropriate reliance in this setting. For regression, we introduce quantity of AI reliance and quality of AI reliance, which respectively measure whether a decision maker utilized the AI advice and whether their reliance helped them get closer to the ground truth relative to their initial estimate. Through the application of our framework, we demonstrate how these metrics capture important nuances in human-AI collaboration that existing measures overlook.

</details>


### [176] [Beyond Semantic Organization: Memory as Execution State Management for Long-Horizon Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06090)
*Yaoqi Chen,Haibin Lai,Yuru Feng,Chuyu Han,Qianxi Zhang,Baotong Lu,Menghao Li,Xinjiang Wang,Zhirui Wang,Shusen Xu,Zengzhong Li,Zewen Jin,Hao Wu,Cheng Li,Qi Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAGE introduces an active execution-state manager that stores interactions in a hierarchical state tree to preserve state integrity, bound context growth, and isolate errors, improving performance and reducing token usage in long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG and agent memory systems fragment decision trajectories by semantic similarity, misaligning memory with execution-state dependencies and enabling error propagation; a state-aware memory structure is needed.

Method: Construct a hierarchical state tree where the agent’s current state is the active root-to-current path. Four coupled operations manage the tree: Grow (record new traces), Compress (summarize completed subgoals), Maintain (validate summaries), and Revise (restore a boundary and resume on a new branch). The agent derives state from summaries, recent traces, and prior-branch hints, enabling coherent state reconstruction and error isolation.

Result: Experiments on MemoryArena show MAGE increases average task success rate by 7.8–20.4 percentage points over baselines and reduces token consumption by 55.1%.

Conclusion: MAGE provides bounded context growth, preserved state integrity, and effective isolation of flawed segments, improving performance on long-horizon tasks.

Abstract: LLM-based agents increasingly tackle long-horizon tasks with interdependent decisions, where each action reshapes future constraints and intermediate errors can cascade. Existing RAG and agent memory systems organize histories by semantic similarity, retrieving content-relevant entries at decision time. We argue that this design mismatches execution-state dependencies: it fragments decision trajectories and mixes valid and erroneous traces, hindering coherent state reconstruction and error isolation. We propose MAGE (Memory as Agent-Guided Exploration), an active execution-state manager that stores interactions in a hierarchical state tree. The agent derives its state from the active root-to-current path, combining subgoal summaries, recent traces, and hints from prior branches. Four coupled operations maintain the tree: Grow records new traces, Compress summarizes completed subgoals, Maintain validates summaries, and Revise restores a target boundary and resumes on a new branch. This design bounds context growth while preserving state integrity and isolating flawed segments from the active path. Experiments on MemoryArena show that MAGE improves the average task success rate by 7.8--20.4 pp over baselines, while reducing token consumption by 55.1%.

</details>


### [177] [Integrating Mechanistic and Data-Driven Models for Neurological Disorders through Differentiable Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06094)
*Shah Pallav Dhanendrakumar,Saikat Pal,Sitikantha Roy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A perspective on hybrid modeling strategies combining deep learning with physics-based solvers for neurological disorders, categorized into parallel, series, and parallel-series architectures, emphasizing residual modeling, Neural ODEs, and solver-in-the-loop to improve diagnostics, prognosis, and treatment planning in brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic models are scientifically informative but often simplified or slow; purely data-driven models are fast but require large data and lack interpretability and generalization. Hybrid models aim to leverage strengths of both approaches for accurate, interpretable, and scalable neurological modeling.

Method: A structured overview/perspective survey of hybrid modeling architectures, classifying approaches into parallel, series, and parallel-series configurations; discusses residual modeling to incorporate missing physics, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for continuous-time dynamics, and solver-in-the-loop techniques to accelerate traditional solvers with neural approximations; outlines integration of governing differential equation formulations with deep learning to model progression and treatment response across neurological disorders.

Result: The hybrid modeling approaches are presented as outperforming standalone mechanistic or purely data-driven methods in terms of enabling improved diagnosis, disease progression prediction, and treatment planning; the perspective highlights their potential across neurological conditions and suggests configurations to enhance personalized modeling.

Conclusion: Hybrid modeling is a powerful and promising tool for personalized neurological modeling, with architectures and strategies that can advance clinical decision-making for brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke; further validation and data considerations are necessary to translate these configurations into practice.

Abstract: Advances in computational modeling, neuroimaging, and artificial intelligence are revolutionizing the modeling of neurological disorders for improved diagnostics, prognosis, and treatment planning. Mechanistic models provide valuable scientific insight into the disorders, but in practice they are often simplified with assumptions or computationally expensive and slow to solve. However, while purely data driven approaches provide speed and scalability, they require large, high quality data to train and generally suffer from interpretability and generalization issues. This perspective paper presents a structured overview of hybrid modeling strategies, which combine deep learning models with physics based solvers, and are categorized into parallel, series, and parallel-series architectures. Three main approaches that have been emphasized are residual modeling for missing or incomplete physics, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs) for continuous time dynamics approximation, and solver in the loop that accelerates traditional solvers with neural approximations. These hybrid models integrate the governing differential equation based formulations and deep learning to characterize the evolution of neurological disorders, and promise advanced personalized neurological modeling. In addition, the study explores and proposes different hybrid configurations to improve diagnosis accuracy, predict disease progression, and inform treatment strategies across a range of neurological disorders. These capabilities outperform standalone mechanistic or purely data driven approaches, making hybrid modeling a powerful tool, especially in applications involving modeling the progression and treatment responses in neurological conditions such as brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke.

</details>


### [178] [CogManip: Benchmarking Manipulative Behavior in Multi-Turn Interactions with Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06099)
*Zeyang Yue,Chenfei Yan,Feifei Zhao,Haibo Tong,Mengwen Xu,Xiaozhen Wang,Erliang Lin,Yi Zeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CogManip benchmarks 15 manipulation strategies across 1,000 multi-turn dialogues to assess covert manipulation risks in LLMs; uncovered heterogeneity across 13 models and strong prompt-sensitivity, guiding defense strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI safety benchmarks focus on explicit rule compliance and static prompts, missing covert, dynamic manipulation in multi-turn interactions.

Method: Constructed CogManip with 1,000 multi-turn scenarios evaluating 15 manipulation strategies; validated by human experts; evaluated 13 models (including GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V3.2); analyzed objective function perturbation.

Result: Significant risk heterogeneity; targeted directions for defense; DeepSeek-V3.2 shows manipulation tactics highly sensitive to system prompts; manipulation risks present under negative and benign prompts; implies need for prompt-based defenses and implicit goal auditing.

Conclusion: CogManip provides a robust instrument for auditing implicit psychological influence and dynamic strategy selection in LLMs; informs defense engineering.

Abstract: Whether Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit covert psychological manipulation in complex human-AI interactions has garnered increasing safety concerns. However, existing AI safety benchmarks remain largely restricted to explicit rule compliance and static prompts, failing to capture the dynamic and covert nature of manipulative strategies in multi-turn dialogues. We introduce CogManip, a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates 15 manipulation strategy risks across 1,000 multi-turn interaction scenarios, validated by human experts. A systematic evaluation of 13 representative models, including frontier models like GPT-5.4 and DeepSeek-V3.2, reveals significant risk heterogeneities and illuminates the targeted direction for future defense. Further analysis of objective function perturbation reveals that DeepSeek-V3.2's manipulation tactics are highly sensitive to both negative and benign system prompts, demonstrating the critical necessity of prompt-based defense engineering and implicit goal auditing. CogManip offers a robust instrument and perspective for auditing the implicit psychological influence and dynamic strategy selection of modern LLMs.

</details>


### [179] [Step-adaptive multimodal fusion network with multi-scale cloud feature learning for ultra-short-term solar irradiance forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06102)
*Jingxin Zhang Xiaoqin Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-source data fusion framework combining InceptionNeXt-based cloud image features, step-adaptive low-frequency compensation, and TempAttnLSTM for ultra-short-term irradiance multi-step prediction, validated on NREL and Shandong data with improved accuracy over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome: (1) inability of single time-series models to capture spatial cloud dynamics under complex conditions; (2) insufficient representation of multi-scale cloud features by standard convolutions; (3) non-adaptive, fixed low-frequency compensation for different forecast horizons.

Method: Use InceptionNeXt to extract multi-scale, multi-directional spatial features from ground-based cloud images; introduce a step-adaptive low-frequency compensation unit to dynamically modulate global low-frequency information based on the prediction step; fuse enhanced image features with meteorological time-series features; apply a TempAttnLSTM network to capture global temporal dependencies for multi-step prediction.

Result: Experiments on the public NREL dataset and practical photovoltaic stations in Shandong show the proposed method achieves superior performance compared with several state-of-the-art approaches.
