Conclusion: The proposed multi-source fusion framework effectively integrates image and time-series data, addresses the three identified shortcomings, and improves ultra-short-term irradiance prediction accuracy and adaptability across steps, with demonstrated real-world applicability.

Abstract: Ultra-short-term solar irradiance prediction is critical for photovoltaic system dispatch and power grid stability. Existing approaches suffer from three key shortcomings: single time-series models cannot capture the spatial dynamics of clouds under complex conditions, standard convolutions inadequately represent multi-scale cloud features, and fixed low-frequency compensation strategies fail to adapt to different prediction steps. To address these issues, this proposes a multi-source data fusion model for ultra-short-term irradiance prediction. The model first employs InceptionNeXt to extract multi-scale, multi-directional spatial features from ground-based cloud images. A step-adaptive low-frequency compensation unit is then introduced to dynamically modulate global low-frequency information based on the prediction step. Eventually, the enhanced image features are combined with meteorological time-series features, and a TempAttnLSTM network captures global temporal dependencies for multi-step prediction. Experiments on the public NREL dataset and practical photovoltaic stations in Shandong illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with several state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [180] [Towards Healthy Evolution: Exploring the Role and Mechanisms of Human-Agent Interaction in Self-Evolving Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06114)
*Dianxing Shi,Junqi He,Junhao Chen,Bowen Wang,Yuta Nakashima*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ANCHOR uses an LLM-based supervisor to guide self-evolving agents, reducing safety drift with limited oversight, and identifying output verification as the most impactful intervention phase; supervision frequency yields diminishing returns across coding, math, and safety tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety drift in autonomous self-evolving agents and understand how human-like oversight affects evolution beyond static post-training supervision.

Method: Develop ANCHOR framework; simulate human supervision via an LLM; apply feedback at multiple phases of self-evolution; test on two open-source systems across coding, mathematical reasoning, and safety tasks.

Result: Limited supervision substantially mitigates safety degradation while preserving core objective performance; output verification-phase supervision is most effective; increasing supervision frequency shows diminishing returns.

Conclusion: Provides empirical evidence and practical guidance for designing more stable, controllable, and human-aligned self-evolving agents.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents improve through continual self-play and self-generated learning signals, but autonomous evolution can also cause capability degradation and safety drift. Although human feedback has proven effective for static and post-trained agents, its role in self-evolving systems remains underexplored. We introduce Agent Norm Correction through Human-like Oversight and Review (ANCHOR), an LLM-based framework that simulates human supervision and delivers feedback at various phases of self-evolution. With ANCHOR, we evaluate two representative open-source self-evolving agent systems across coding, mathematical reasoning, and safety. Our results show that even limited supervision substantially mitigates safety degradation while preserving stable performance on core evolutionary objectives. Further analysis shows that supervision over the output verification phase is the most effective for intervention, whereas increasing supervision frequency yields diminishing returns. These findings provide empirical evidence and practical guidance for designing more stable, controllable, and human-aligned self-evolving agent systems.

</details>


### [181] [WorldFly: A World-Model-Based Vision-Language-Action Model for UAV Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06147)
*Shengtao Zheng,Kai Li,Weichen Zhang,Yu Meng,Chen Gao,Xinlei Chen,Yong Li,Xiao-Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WorldFly uses a world-model-based VLA framework to handle partial observability in UAV navigation by predicting future states and actions; the Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark validates its robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end VLA models struggle under severe occlusions and drastic viewpoint changes; future-state imagination via world models is needed for robust decision-making under partial observability.

Method: Dual-branch coupled flow matching to jointly predict future video frames and navigation actions; WorldFly integrates spatial imagination to guide policy.

Result: WorldFly outperforms baselines, especially in unseen environments, on the Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark.

Conclusion: Integrating world models into embodied aerial agents enhances robustness in urban navigation by leveraging spatial imagination through joint video and action prediction.

Abstract: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise in UAV navigation. However, existing approaches typically rely on historical observations to directly predict actions, often struggling in dense urban environments where severe occlusions and sharp turns result in drastic viewpoint transitions. We argue that the ability to "imagine" future states -- inherent in World Models -- is critical for robust decision-making under such partial observability. To address this, we construct a challenging Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark, specifically designed to evaluate spatial understanding in scenarios characterized by severe occlusions and drastic viewpoint transitions. To this end, we propose WorldFly, a novel world-model-based VLA framework that employs a dual-branch coupled flow matching mechanism to jointly generate future video predictions and navigation actions, thereby explicitly guiding the agent's policy via spatial imagination. Extensive evaluations on our benchmark demonstrate that WorldFly outperforms other baselines, particularly in unseen environments, validating the effectiveness of integrating world models into embodied aerial agents.

</details>


### [182] [Amortizing Federated Adaptation: Hypernetwork Driven LoRA for Personalized Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06154)
*Sunny Gupta,Shambhavi Shanker,Amit Sethi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HyperLoRA: a hypernetwork-based, product-space Federated LoRA framework that amortizes client adaptation and learns aggregation to overcome aggregation bias and initialization lag, yielding faster convergence and improved personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two core limitations in federated LoRA: (i) structural aggregation bias from naive averaging of low-rank factors; (ii) client initialization lag due to reinitializing LoRA parameters each round, hindering convergence.

Method: HyperLoRA introduces a hypernetwork that maps client distribution signatures to LoRA initializations (amortized adaptation). Server uses a learned aggregation module that synthesizes updates directly in the low-rank product space. A lightweight residual correction module mitigates instability under non-IID distributions. No per-round iterative client optimization; replaced with learned operators.

Result: Empirical evaluation on federated vision and vision-language benchmarks shows faster convergence, improved robustness to distribution shift, and stronger personalization relative to previous federated LoRA baselines.

Conclusion: HyperLoRA demonstrates that amortized, learned operators enable efficient personalization, unbiased aggregation, and accelerated convergence in federated foundation-model fine-tuning; the approach reduces communication and computation overhead while enhancing performance under distribution heterogeneity.

Abstract: Federated fine-tuning of foundation models using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) offers a communication efficient solution for distributed learning. However, existing federated LoRA methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: (1) structural aggregation bias, where independently averaging low rank factors fails to approximate the true combined update, and (2) client side initialization lag, as clients repeatedly reinitialize LoRA parameters across communication rounds, slowing convergence. We propose HyperLoRA, a unified framework that addresses both issues through amortized federated adaptation through hypernetwork-driven LoRA generation and product space aggregation. Instead of iterative per-client optimization, HyperLoRA employs a learned generator that maps client distribution signatures to LoRA initializations, effectively amortizing per client adaptation. On the server side, we introduce a learned aggregation module that directly synthesizes updates in the low-rank product space, eliminating the inconsistencies of factor-wise averaging. A lightweight residual correction module further improves stability under heterogenous (non-IID) client distributions.By replacing iterative optimization and heuristic averaging with learned operators, HyperLoRA jointly enables efficient personalization, unbiased aggregation, and faster convergence. Experiments on federated vision and vision-language benchmarks show that HyperLoRA achieves improved convergence speed, greater robustness to distribution shift, and stronger personalization performance compared to prior federated LoRA methods.

</details>


### [183] [Where does Absolute Position come from in decoder-only Transformers?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06160)
*Valeria Ruscio,Umberto Nanni,Fabrizio Silvestri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RoPE-induced absolute-position signals arise via two leakage channels (causal mask and residual stream) with architecture-dependent balances; perturbations (BOS) and attention sinks modulate/diagnose leakage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why RoPE encodings produce absolute-position patterns despite encoding only relative offsets and to identify architectural sources.

Method: Analytical and empirical tracing across three architectures (with NTK scaling, sliding-window, standard RoPE). Examine the causal mask's per-query denominator and the residual stream; use BOS substitution to assess residual-stream contribution; define attention sinks as stabilizers that produce fingerprints of pos-0 token.

Result: Two leakage channels identified; architecture-specific balance: NTK scaling suppresses residual-stream; sliding-window accumulates with depth; RoPE sits between. Replacing BOS reduces ~40% of residual-stream component at early queries. Attention sinks anchor tokens, produce deterministic fingerprint of token at position 0 (constant when BOS auto-prepended, varying otherwise).

Conclusion: RoPE-induced absolute-position leakage is an artifact of the interaction between causal masking and residual-stream dynamics, with architecture choices offering mitigation paths; BOS embedding modification and sink design can control leakage magnitude; results generalize across the studied architectures.

Abstract: RoPE-trained transformers distinguish absolute position in their attention patterns, even though RoPE encodes only relative offsets in the inner product. We trace this leakage to two architectural components, The causal mask is responsible for the first: its per-query softmax denominator depends on the absolute query position by construction. The residual stream supplies the second. Under causal attention the activation at position $0$ attends only to itself and runs as a closed dynamical system from the embedding of the token at that position; downstream attention reads this trajectory through sink-reading heads. Both components appear in all three architectures we study, in architecturally specific balance: NTK scaling suppresses the residual-stream component, sliding-window attention allows it to accumulate with depth, and standard RoPE sits between. Replacing the \texttt{BOS} embedding before the forward pass removes $40\%$ of the residual-stream component at early queries. Attention sinks are token-anchored stabilizers that pass forward a deterministic fingerprint of the token at position $0$, constant across inputs when that token is the auto-prepended \texttt{BOS} and varying with it otherwise.

</details>


### [184] [ProSarc: Prosody-Aware Sarcasm Recognition Framework via Temporal Prosodic Incongruity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06168)
*Prathamjyot Singh,Ashima Sood,Sahil Sharma,Jasmeet Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProSarc uses dual encoders to model prosodic incongruity for sarcasm detection, with Monte Carlo dropout for uncertainty and attention-based onset localization; achieves state-of-the-art audio-only results and good cross-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sarcasm in speech is signaled by mismatches between local prosody and overall emotional baseline; capturing this incongruity can improve detection and localization without frame-level labels.

Method: Global Emotion Encoder + Temporal Prosody Encoder (BiLSTM + multi-head attention) feed into a Prosodic Incongruity Analyzer to produce a scalar score; Monte Carlo dropout for uncertainty; attention-based onset localization; evaluated on MUStARD++, PodSarc, MuSaG.

Result: F1 on MUStARD++ 75.3; PodSarc 62.9; MuSaG 65.6; ten-run validation shows p=0.002, Cohen's d=1.51; human evaluation shows uncertainty tracks ambiguity and onset aligns with human-annotated windows.

Conclusion: Prosodic incongruity modelling enhances audio-based sarcasm detection with robust cross-domain performance, uncertainty estimation, and interpretable onset localization.

Abstract: We present ProSarc, an audio-only framework that detects sarcasm by modelling temporal prosodic incongruity, that is, the mismatch between local prosodic dynamics and the utterance-level emotional baseline. Dual encoding paths, a Global Emotion Encoder and a Temporal Prosody Encoder (BiLSTM + multi-head attention), feed a Prosodic Incongruity Analyzer that produces a scalar incongruity score for classification. Monte Carlo dropout provides uncertainty estimates, and an attention-based mechanism localises sarcastic onset without frame-level labels. ProSarc outperforms prior audio-only methods on MUStARD++ (F1=75.3) and generalises to spontaneous (PodSarc, F1=62.9) and cross-lingual speech (MuSaG, F1=65.6). Ten-run validation confirms the contribution of incongruity modelling (Wilcoxon p=0.002, Cohen's d=1.51). Human evaluation shows that model uncertainty tracks perceptual ambiguity and predicted onsets align with human-annotated temporal windows.

</details>


### [185] [Learning to replenish: A hybrid deep reinforcement learning for dynamic inventory management in the pharmaceutical supply chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06201)
*Amandeep Kaur,Gyan Prakash*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a DRL-based optimal replenishment policy for pharmaceutical inventories under stochastic demand, uncertain lead times, and perishability, using an A3C-D PPO framework to handle continuous actions; shows cost reductions and feasibility with real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pharmaceutical supply chains face unpredictable demand, variable lead times, and finite shelf lives, causing high stockouts, waste, and costs. A sophisticated, data-driven policy is needed to balance availability with waste minimization and profitability.

Method: Formulates inventory replenishment as a Markov decision process and develops a hybrid DRL algorithm named A3C DPPO (asynchronous advantage actor-critic distributed proximal policy optimization) to manage continuous replenishment actions in the IM setting; uses real-world data for validation.

Result: Numerical experiments indicate adaptive replenishment under dynamic conditions with lower inventory costs than benchmarks; real-world pharmaceutical inventory data validate practical feasibility of the approach.

Conclusion: The proposed A3C DPPO-based policy effectively handles stochastic demand and PSC variability, improving profitability and service levels while reducing costs, with demonstrated feasibility on real data.

Abstract: Pharmaceutical supply chains (PSCs) struggle with inventory management (IM) due to unpredictable demand patterns and variable lead times associated with restocking. This complexity is further compounded by the finite shelf lives of pharmaceutical products, which necessitate a delicate balance between adequate stock and minimal waste. These intertwined factors create a complex optimization problem that requires sophisticated inventory strategies to ensure both product availability and PSC efficiency. This study aims to develop an optimal inventory replenishment policy for pharmaceutical products that can handle the stochasticity arising from uncertain demand and variable PSC conditions. The objective is to maximize the profitability of the PSC while maintaining a high patient service level. We formulate the problem as a Markov decision process and propose a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach, specifically, a hybrid asynchronous advantage actor critic distributed proximal policy optimization (A3C DPPO)algorithm. The A3C DPPO algorithm is tailored to handle the continuous action space inherent in IM. The numerical results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm adaptively updates the inventory replenishment strategy under dynamic scenarios, resulting in lower inventory costs compared to various benchmarks. We also conduct numerical validation using real-world pharmaceutical inventory data to confirm the practical feasibility of the proposed algorithm.

</details>


### [186] [Unsupervised Pattern Analysis in Japanese Veterinary Toxicology: A Regulatory-Compliant Framework for Cross-Species Risk Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06207)
*Yukiko Kawakami,Mohammad Shirazi,Ryo Shimizuwa,Saito Shinoda,Alireza Mortazavi,Matsumoto Kawahara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Regulatory-integrated unsupervised approach reveals region- and species-specific toxicity patterns in veterinary pharmacovigilance by aligning ADE representations to organ systems and adjusting for reporting biases, uncovering cross-species clusters and achieving substantial alignment with pharmacological classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of prediction-focused models and lack of mechanistic interpretability; leverage regulatory context (MAFF) and species-specific metabolism in Japan to uncover meaningful toxicity patterns.

Method: Unsupervised framework that encodes ADEs into organ-system representations, adjusts for species reporting biases, performs similarity-based clustering and dimensionality reduction on 4,120 high-confidence ADE reports (9,080 drug-ADE combos) from NVAL data; identifies latent toxicity structures and cross-species clusters; evaluates drug-level clustering against pharmacological classes; uses cosine similarity, silhouette score, and cluster precision; regulatory validation.

Result: Identified three significant species clusters (p<0.01): hepatic-dominant in companion animals (0.42±0.06), renal in ruminants (0.39±0.07), dermatological in sheep (0.35±0.07); drug-level clustering 83% alignment with pharmacological classes; cosine similarity outperforming other metrics; silhouette 0.48; cluster precision 87%; regulatory validation shows strong agreement with established classifications.

Conclusion: Regulation-aligned unsupervised analysis can uncover biologically meaningful, region-specific toxicity patterns and provide an interpretable, scalable framework for veterinary drug safety assessment.

Abstract: Veterinary pharmacovigilance systems are essential for monitoring adverse drug events (ADEs), yet existing approaches often fail to capture region-specific toxicity patterns shaped by local biological and regulatory contexts. In Japan, these challenges are amplified by species-specific metabolic differences and reporting practices defined by the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry, and Fisheries (MAFF). Most prior work relies on prediction-oriented models, limiting mechanistic interpretability. This study proposes a regulatory-integrated unsupervised framework for pattern discovery using the National Veterinary Assay Laboratory (NVAL) database. ADEs are encoded into organ system-aligned representations and adjusted for species-specific reporting biases, enabling cross-species comparison. Similarity-based clustering and dimensionality reduction are applied to identify latent toxicity structures. Analysis of 4,120 high-confidence ADE reports (9,080 drug-ADE combinations) identified three significant species clusters (p < 0.01), including hepatic-dominant patterns in companion animals (0.42 $\pm$ 0.06), renal toxicity in ruminants (0.39 $\pm$ 0.07), and dermatological sensitivity in sheep (0.35 $\pm$ 0.07). Drug-level clustering achieved 83% alignment with pharmacological classes, while cosine similarity outperformed alternative metrics (silhouette score: 0.48; cluster precision: 87%). Regulatory validation showed strong agreement with established classifications. These findings demonstrate that regulation-aligned unsupervised analysis can uncover biologically meaningful, region-specific toxicity patterns, providing an interpretable and scalable framework for veterinary drug safety assessment.

</details>


### [187] [Evaluating Agentic Configuration Repair for Computer Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06212)
*Rufat Asadli,Benjamin Hoffman,Ioannis Protogeros,Laurent Vanbever*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic LLMs augmented with formal verification and context retrieval outperform base LLMs in repairing network misconfigurations, achieving ~12% higher repair efficacy and ~17% higher safety in benchmark experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Network misconfigurations are a major source of Internet outages. Current LLMs struggle with large-scale, complex configurations and can introduce new errors. There is a need for verifiable, safe, scalable automation in network configuration.

Method: Benchmark open- and closed-source LLMs augmented with formal network verification and context retrieval tools. Compare agentic architectures (dynamic context management and iterative validation) against base LLMs in repair tasks across large-scale, complex scenarios.

Result: Agentic architectures show higher repair efficacy (+12% on average) and safety (+17% on average) compared to base LLMs, enabled by dynamic context management and iterative validation.

Conclusion: Integrating agentic LLMs with formal verification and contextual retrieval improves reliability and safety in network configuration repairs, suggesting a promising approach for scalable automated network management.

Abstract: Misconfigurations in computer networks remain a major source of critical Internet outages. Research is turning to Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate the complex, error-prone task of network configuration. However, even state-of-the-art models fail to resolve misconfigurations in large-scale, complex scenarios and often introduce new errors. In this work, we benchmark open- and closed-source LLMs augmented with formal network verification and context retrieval tools. We demonstrate that agentic architectures outperform base LLMs in repair efficacy (by 12% on average) and safety (by 17% on average), enabled by the ability to dynamically manage context and iteratively validate configuration repairs.

</details>


### [188] [From Reward-Hack Activations to Agentic Risk States: Context-Calibrated Mechanistic Monitoring in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06223)
*Patrick Wilhelm,Odej Kao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Adapters trained on reward-hack data can transfer exploit tendencies to ReAct-style agents in Gameable ALFWorld/WebShop; activation signals reveal latent policy states but don't alone prevent exploitation; entropy and decision-context features aid risk estimation; activation-direction steering helps reduce proxy exploits; overall, context-calibrated internal monitoring is recommended.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how internal signals (activation-based reward-hack scores, token entropy, and decision-context features) relate to reward-hacking risks in language-model agents and whether adapters can transfer reward-hack tendencies across environments.

Method: Instrument agents with activation-based scores, token-level entropy, and decision-context features. Train adapters on the School-of-Reward-Hacks dataset. Evaluate in ReAct-style agents operating in Gameable ALFWorld and WebShop. Assess transfer of reward-hack tendencies, risk estimation via next-step prediction tasks, and effectiveness of activation-direction steering across mixed-adapter regimes.

Result: Reward-hack tendencies transfer via adapters, especially when environments expose proxy-reward affordances. Activation-based reward-hack scores identify a latent policy state but do not reliably trigger immediate exploits. Entropy and context-calibrated internal features improve risk estimation over activation alone in next-step prediction tasks. Activation-direction steering reduces proxy-exploit behavior in selected mixed-adapter regimes.

Conclusion: Context-calibrated internal monitoring is beneficial: activation signals latent policy states, while entropy and decision context help determine when that state becomes risky. Relying solely on activation dynamics is insufficient for mitigation.

Abstract: Language-model agents act through repeated cycles of observation, reasoning, and action selection, making safety monitoring depend on both internal model state and environment context. We study reward-hacking monitors in ReAct-style agents acting in Gameable ALFWorld and WebShop. Agents are instrumented with activation-based reward-hack scores, token-level entropy, and decision-context features. We find that adapters fine-tuned on \textit{School-of-Reward-Hacks} dataset can transfer reward-hack tendencies into agentic action selection, especially when the environment exposes proxy-reward affordances. However, mitigating such behavior cannot rely on activation dynamics alone. High reward-hack activation identifies a latent policy state, but does not necessarily imply an immediate exploit action. Across next-step prediction tasks, entropy and context-calibrated internal features improve risk estimation over reward-hack activation alone. Activation-direction steering further reduces proxy-exploit behavior in selected mixed-adapter regimes. Overall, our results support context-calibrated internal monitoring for agents: reward-hack activation identifies a latent policy state, while entropy and decision context help determine when that state becomes risky action.

</details>


### [189] [Closing the Loop on Latent Reasoning via Test-Time Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06252)
*Xiaopeng Yuan,Haibo Jin,Ye Yu,Peng Kuang,Lijun Yu,Yushun Dong,Haohan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReLAT: test-time reconstruction-guided latent reasoning to preserve query fidelity in latent reasoning; yields strong gains on Qwen models, notably AIME 2024 accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent or cache-level reasoning reduces token overhead and avoids discrete bottlenecks but sacrifices inspectability and input-anchored fidelity; need a mechanism to ensure latent states preserve task constraints.

Method: Self-supervised test-time training that builds a differentiable Query -> Latent Thought -> Query cycle and optimizes query reconstruction loss from the latent thought before producing the answer, anchoring latent computation to the original query; evaluated on math reasoning, knowledge QA, and code generation benchmarks across Qwen models.

Result: Consistent improvements over single-model inference, text-based collaboration, open-loop latent collaboration, and other TT training objectives; on Qwen3-8B, AIME 2024 accuracy rises from 56.7% to 73.3%, a 16.6-point gain over the strongest open-loop latent baseline.

Conclusion: ReLAT effectively anchors latent reasoning to the input query, improving fidelity and overall performance across domains, supporting the utility of reconstruction-based self-supervision at test time.

Abstract: Recent work moves intermediate reasoning from natural-language traces into latent or cache-level representations to reduce token overhead and avoid a discrete communication bottleneck. However, this shift also removes a key advantage of textual reasoning: intermediate states are no longer inspectable, making it difficult to determine whether a latent state still preserves the constraints of the original query. As a result, latent reasoning typically operates in an open loop, where a latent state is produced and consumed without an input-anchored fidelity check. We propose ReLAT (Reconstruction-Guided Latent Reasoning At Test Time), a self-supervised test-time training method that closes this loop using the query itself as the reference. Our key observation is that if a latent state faithfully represents a query, the query should be recoverable from it; if the query cannot be recovered, the latent state has lost task-relevant information. ReLAT operationalizes this principle by constructing a differentiable Question -> Latent Thought -> Question cycle and optimizing query reconstruction loss through the latent thought before answer generation. This anchors opaque latent computation to the problem specification it is supposed to represent. Across mathematical reasoning, knowledge QA, and code generation benchmarks on the Qwen family, ReLAT consistently improves over single-model inference, text-based collaboration, open-loop latent collaboration, and alternative test-time training objectives. On Qwen3-8B, ReLAT raises AIME 2024 accuracy from 56.7% to 73.3%, a 16.6-point gain over the strongest open-loop latent baseline.

</details>


### [190] [RedKnot: Efficient Long-Context LLM Serving with Head-Aware KV Reuse and SegPagedAttention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06256)
*Yang Liu,ZhaoKai Luo,HuaYi Jin,ZhiYong Wang,RuoZhou He,BoYu Wang,Guanjie Chen,Junhao Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RedKnot introduces a head-aware KV cache management for LLM serving by decomposing the KV cache along KV heads, enabling position-independent reuse, compression, hot/cold separation, and distributed placement while preserving fidelity and improving efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caches in LLM serving have become a major bottleneck. The conventional monolithic KV cache treats all heads uniformly, ignoring structured differences in head function, attention distance, and runtime importance, limiting memory, throughput, and scalability. A head-aware representation promises better resource utilization without retraining.

Method: Decompose the KV cache into per-head components rather than a single monolithic tensor. Treat each head as a structured memory object with varying importance and effective attention ranges, enabling uniform support for position-independent reuse, prefix compression, hot/cold separation, and distributed placement under a model-aware runtime substrate.

Result: By adopting a head-aware KV cache, RedKnot improves resource efficiency and scalability while preserving output fidelity, without requiring model retraining or fine-tuning.

Conclusion: RedKnot reframes KV cache from a passive, monolithic artifact to a dynamic, model-aware runtime substrate, establishing a foundation for scalable, efficient LLM serving.

Abstract: As the input length of large language model (LLM) serving continues to grow, the KV cache has become a dominant bottleneck in AI infrastructure. It limits GPU memory capacity, serving concurrency, cache reuse, and distributed scalability. Several important problems, including position-independent KV cache, prefix KV cache compression, hot/cold KV cache separation, and distributed KV cache management, all depend on how the KV cache is represented and managed. However, existing serving systems largely rely on a monolithic KV cache abstraction, where the KV cache is treated as a homogeneous sequence of token-level memory blocks and managed with similar policies across attention heads and serving scenarios. We observe that KV cache utility is highly structured across KV heads: different heads exhibit different functional roles, attention distances, and runtime importance. Therefore, a full KV cache is not always necessary for every head, token range, or serving scenario.
  We present RedKnot, a head-aware KV cache management system for LLM serving. RedKnot breaks the conventional monolithic KV cache abstraction by decomposing the KV cache along KV heads, whose importance and effective attention ranges vary significantly across serving scenarios. This head-level decomposition turns the KV cache from a monolithic tensor abstraction into a structured memory object, enabling RedKnot to uniformly support position-independent KV reuse, prefix KV compression, hot/cold KV separation, and distributed KV placement while preserving output fidelity and improving resource efficiency, without requiring model retraining or fine-tuning. RedKnot establishes a new foundation for AI infrastructure by transforming the KV cache from a monolithic, passive runtime artifact into a dynamic, model-aware runtime substrate for scalable LLM serving.

</details>


### [191] [ToolChoiceConfusion: Causal Minimal Tool Filtering for Reliable LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06284)
*Rahul Suresh Babu,Laxmipriya Ganesh Iyer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Causal Minimal Tool Filtering (CMTF) is a training-free method that uses precondition-effect contracts to expose only the minimal next-step tool frontier, reducing tool exposure and token cost while maintaining or matching causal baselines in multi-step tool-use tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool menus can become large, increasing wrong-tool calls, premature actions, and token cost. Relying on semantic relevance alone is insufficient, as a related tool may be unnecessary or premature at the current step.

Method: CMTF employs lightweight precondition–effect contracts to identify the minimal next-step tool frontier needed to advance from the current state toward the user goal (causal sufficiency). It is training-free and evaluated against all-tools exposure, keyword retrieval, state-aware filtering, and causal-path ablations. Metrics include task success, wrong-tool calls, premature actions, tool exposure, and token cost across multi-step tasks.

Result: Across a main benchmark of 102 tasks, 100 tools, four LLM backends, and 2448 task-method-model runs, CMTF matches the strongest causal baseline in aggregate success, while reducing visible tools from 100 to 1 per step and lowering token usage by about 90% relative to all-tools exposure.

Conclusion: A training-free, causally grounded tool-filtering approach can dramatically reduce tool exposure and token costs without sacrificing performance, highlighting the value of causal sufficiency in guiding tool use.

Abstract: Large language model agents increasingly rely on external tools, but larger tool menus can reduce reliability and efficiency by increasing wrong-tool calls, premature actions, and token cost. Existing tool-selection methods often optimize semantic relevance, exposing tools whose names or descriptions match the user request. We argue that relevance is insufficient: a tool may be related to the task while still being unnecessary or premature at the current step.
  We propose Causal Minimal Tool Filtering (CMTF), a training-free method that selects tools by causal sufficiency. CMTF uses lightweight precondition-effect contracts to expose only the minimal next-step tool frontier needed to advance from the current state toward the user goal. Across multi-step tool-use tasks, we compare CMTF with all-tools exposure, keyword retrieval, state-aware filtering, and causal-path ablations, measuring task success, wrong-tool calls, premature actions, tool exposure, and token cost. In the main benchmark with 102 tasks, 100 tools, four LLM backends, and 2448 task-method-model runs, CMTF matches the strongest causal baseline in aggregate success while reducing visible tools from 100 to one per step and reducing token usage by about 90% relative to all-tools exposure.

</details>


### [192] [TRACE: A Temporal Conditional Estimation for Multimodal Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06285)
*Ziwen Kan,Yishuo Chen,Kecheng Li,Andrew Wen,Xiaomeng Wang,Liwei Wang,Jihao Duan,Song Wang,Hongfang Liu,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRACE: a conditional estimation framework for multimodal time-series foundation models that handles missingness and irregular sampling by inferring missing target modalities from available auxiliary modalities, improving robustness over naive imputation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world multimodal time series suffer from temporal misalignment and missingness; existing imputation/masking fails to capture cross-modal dependencies, hindering robust representations.

Method: Proposes TRACE, a conditional estimation paradigm for multimodal TS foundation model pipelines under missingness, enabling inference of incomplete target modalities from auxiliary modalities; evaluated on healthcare (MIMIC-IV) and affective computing (CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI).

Result: TRACE consistently outperforms prior multimodal fusion methods across tasks and missingness settings, with improved robustness and more reliable cross-modal representations.

Conclusion: TRACE offers a robust, generalizable approach for multimodal TS learning under irregular sampling and missingness, applicable to diverse downstream tasks; demonstrates strong performance on medical and sentiment benchmarks.

Abstract: Time series foundation models (TS-FMs) aim to learn generalizable temporal representations that can be adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks. In real-world multimodal settings, time series are frequently affected by temporal misalignment and partial modality missingness, where different modalities are observed at heterogeneous time scales or are partially absent. Existing approaches typically rely on naive imputation or masking strategies, which fail to account for cross-modal dependencies and often lead to misaligned or degraded representations. We propose TRACE, a conditional estimation paradigm for multimodal time series foundation model pipelines under missingness and irregular sampling, allowing incomplete target modalities to be systematically inferred from available auxiliary modalities. We evaluate TRACE on diverse multimodal benchmarks spanning healthcare and affective computing, including the MIMIC-IV clinical dataset and the CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI benchmarks for multimodal sentiment analysis. Across a range of downstream prediction tasks and missing-modality settings, TRACE consistently outperforms prior multimodal fusion approaches, demonstrating improved robustness to severe modality missingness and more reliable cross-modal representations.

</details>


### [193] [Multi-ResNets for Subspace Preconditioning in Constrained Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06300)
*Merve Karakas,Christopher J. Williams,Emmanuel O. Balogun,Sadegh Sadeghi Tabas,Christian Brown,Nikhil Rao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MResOpt introduces a staged residual neural network for constrained optimization that enforces high-priority constraints via stage-aware losses and ordinal constraint ordering; it exhibits GP-like behavior in the wide limit and improves constraint satisfaction with efficiency on synthetic QP/QCQP/SOCP benchmarks and AC OPF.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance constraint satisfaction in learned optimizers by leveraging ordered (priority) constraints, domain knowledge, and a staged architecture to separate constraint satisfaction from objective optimization within predict-complete-correct pipelines.

Method: Proposes MResOpt, a staged residual network architecture that decomposes constraint satisfaction by priority through intermediate re-completion and stage-aware losses, enabling domain-informed ordered constraint handling. Theoretical analysis in the infinite-width limit shows sequential Gaussian Process regression behavior. Empirical evaluation uses synthetic QP, QCQP and SOCP problems and a line-flow constrained AC OPF case with physics-motivated constraint ordering.

Result: On synthetic QP, QCQP, and SOCP benchmarks, MResOpt improves high-priority constraint satisfaction across convex and non-convex settings. In line-flow constrained AC OPF, a physics-motivated constraint ordering and a learned division of labor keep iterates on the equality manifold, achieving substantially lower high-priority violations than reprojected baselines while remaining computationally efficient.

Conclusion: MResOpt demonstrates that a domain-informed, staged architecture with stage-aware losses can achieve better constrained optimization performance, with theoretical GP-like behavior in the wide limit and practical gains in complex power-system and benchmark problems.

Abstract: We propose MResOpt, a staged residual neural network architecture for constrained optimization problems. Our architecture fits within predict-complete-correct pipelines and decomposes constraint satisfaction by priority through intermediate re-completion and stage-aware losses. The framework enables domain-informed ordered constraint satisfaction which allows the network to utilize ordinal structure when present. Under an idealized infinite-width regime, we show that our design behaves as sequential Gaussian Process regression. On synthetic QP, QCQP, and SOCP benchmarks, the staged architecture improves high-priority constraint satisfaction across convex and non-convex settings. On line-flow-constrained AC optimal power flow, we introduce a physics-motivated constraint ordering and show that MResOpt supports a learned division of labor that keeps iterates on the equality manifold, achieving substantially lower high-priority violation than reprojected baselines while remaining computationally efficient.

</details>


### [194] [AIS-Based Vessel Trajectory Prediction Using Memory-Augmented Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06311)
*Wonmo Koo,Sanha Chang,Heeyoung Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Memory-augmented networks improve vessel trajectory prediction using AIS data, outperforming non-memory baselines in Gulf of Mexico and NY Bight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the success of memory-augmented trajectory prediction from pedestrians/road vehicles to maritime domains by evaluating memory-based models on AIS-based vessel trajectories.

Method: Empirical study using AIS data from the Gulf of Mexico and the New York Bight. The approach compares memory-augmented trajectory prediction against deep learning baselines that do not use external memory.

Result: Memory-based models achieve consistent and substantial performance gains over baselines lacking external memory across both regions.

Conclusion: External memory-based trajectory prediction is effective for maritime AIS data, offering improved accuracy over standard deep learning approaches without memory.

Abstract: Accurate vessel trajectory prediction is essential for safe and efficient maritime operations, enabling collision avoidance and supporting route optimization. Although memory-augmented neural networks have recently shown strong performance in pedestrian and road-vehicle trajectory prediction by selectively retrieving relevant information from an external memory, their potential for vessel trajectory prediction remains underexplored. This paper presents an empirical investigation of memory-based trajectory prediction using Automatic Identification System (AIS) data. Experiments on data from the Gulf of Mexico and the New York Bight demonstrate consistent and substantial performance gains over a range of deep learning baselines that do not incorporate an external memory.

</details>


### [195] [LLM Self-Recognition: Steering and Retrieving Activation Signatures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06315)
*Thibaud Ardoin,Jonas Schäfer,Gerhard Wunder*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Authors show a self-recognition fingerprint in LLMs by steering the residual stream with a sparse random vector, enabling near-perfect attribution (>98%) from model activations without harming text quality, offering an internal attribution mechanism as an alternative to external detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI-generated content proliferates, robust, high-fidelity attribution methods are needed. Leveraging internal representations for attribution could provide a practical, model-intrinsic solution without embedding external signals.

Method: During text generation, steer the model's internal residual stream with a random sparse vector to imprint a fingerprint. A detector model analyzes activation spaces to recover the fingerprint and attribute text to its source LLM. Evaluate across multiple LLMs and detection settings, assessing both attribution accuracy and text quality.

Result: Fingerprint detectable in activations with >98% attribution accuracy across multiple settings, while preserving generation quality. The approach scales to multiple LLMs and relies on intrinsic representations rather than external signals.

Conclusion: Activation spaces harbor exploitable structure for encoding signals without semantic interference. Self-recognition in LLMs is reliable even in low-entropy scenarios, and a simple steering mechanism enables multi-LLM attribution without compromising output quality, offering a practical internal attribution alternative to traditional detectors.

Abstract: Recent advances in interpretability suggest that large language models (LLMs) implicitly encode signals in their generated text that enable self-recognition of their outputs. We demonstrate that this capability is reliable, even in low-entropy scenarios, and that it can be amplified through targeted intervention. By steering the internal residual stream during generation with a random sparse vector, we create a detectable fingerprint that enables attribution of a given text to a specific LLM. This signal is recoverable from the activations of an LLM used as a detector, achieving over 98% accuracy across multiple detection settings while preserving the quality of generated text. As AI-generated content proliferates, this approach offers a practical alternative to traditional detectors by leveraging the model's natural representation structure for attribution rather than embedding a signal externally. Our contributions include: (i) establishing reliable self-recognition capabilities in LLMs, (ii) a simple steering mechanism enabling multi-LLM identification with no quality degradation, (iii) demonstrating that activation spaces contain exploitable structure for encoding signals without semantic interference.

</details>


### [196] [DragOn: A Benchmark and Dataset for Drag-Based GUI Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06322)
*Nathan Bout,Maxime Langevin,Ronan Riochet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DragOn introduces a large-scale drag-grounding benchmark and dataset for GUI agents, covering text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing, and slider manipulation, with 286k training screenshots and 3.5M tasks plus a 2000-example held-out evaluation. It evaluates multiple proprietary and open-weight models, including a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on the data, and shows potential performance gains on downstream computer-use tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Drag-based interactions (drag-and-drop, swipe, highlight) are underexplored compared to click-grounding, and current models struggle with complex drag tasks. A large, diverse dataset is needed to train and evaluate models on these interactions to enable more capable GUI automation.

Method: Assemble DragOn dataset with four drag tasks: text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing, slider manipulation. Compile 286k training screenshots and 3.5M training tasks, plus a held-out evaluation set of 2000 examples. Evaluate both proprietary models (GPT, Claude) and open-weight models (Qwen, Kimi, Holo), including a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on training data.

Result: Preliminary results suggest the dataset can improve state-of-the-art models on downstream computer-use tasks, indicating better drag-grounding capabilities and generalization across GUI tasks.

Conclusion: DragOn provides a large, diverse benchmark and dataset to advance drag-grounding in GUI agents, enabling improved automation of drag-based interactions and broader evaluation across models; the held-out set supports assessment of generalization.

Abstract: GUI agents - vision-based models that control desktops, web browsers, and mobile devices through graphical user interfaces - promise to automate a wide range of digital tasks. While million-scale datasets have enabled substantial progress on click-grounding, drag grounding (e.g. drag-and-drop, swipe, highlight) data remains an order of magnitude smaller and current models fall short on complex drag-based interactions. We introduce DragOn, a drag grounding benchmark and training dataset covering four domains: text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing and slider manipulation. The dataset comprises 286K training screenshots and 3.5M training tasks, plus a 2000-example held-out evaluation suite. We evaluate proprietary (GPT, Claude) and open-weight (Qwen, Kimi, Holo) models, as well as a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on our training data. Results suggest that our dataset could improve performance of state-of-the-art models on downstream computer-use tasks.

</details>


### [197] [TokenMizer: Graph-Structured Session Memory for Long-Horizon LLM Context Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06337)
*Shweta Mishra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TokenMizer models LLM session history as a typed knowledge graph to compress context and preserve rationale, yielding smaller resume blocks and improved recall across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite context windows (MECW) constrain long-horizon work, causing loss of structured session history when history grows; existing baselines flatten history into text, eroding relational structure and resumability.

Method: A proxy system that represents session history as a typed knowledge graph (14 node types, 7 edge types), populated by a hybrid extraction pipeline, with a three-tier checkpoint producing compact resume blocks. An 8-layer compression pipeline reduces context overhead and a semantic cache lowers repeated-query latency.

Result: Resume blocks average 78 tokens (range 42–124), ~2x smaller than baselines (159–170 tokens) while achieving higher decision recall (+9–17 percentage points). Across 21 sessions in 5 domains: mean task recall 51.0%, decision recall 46.6%, file recall 58.7%. Ablation: fuzzy label matching is the dominant factor (+33 pp task recall). Heuristic compression yields 47.3% token reduction with zero external dependencies. TokenMizer offers a queryable alternative to text-retention baselines at about half the token cost.

Conclusion: TokenMizer demonstrates that structured, graph-based session histories enable efficient, recall-rich resumable interactions for LLMs, outperforming flat-text baselines in token economy and preserving rationale across domains.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) deployments for long-horizon tasks face a fundamental constraint: context windows are finite while productive work sessions are not. When history exceeds the Maximum Effective Context Window (MECW), critical structured information - architectural decisions, task transitions, file histories - is silently discarded. Existing mitigations treat history as flat text, destroying the relational structure that makes sessions resumable. We present TokenMizer, an open-source proxy system that models LLM session history as a typed knowledge graph. The schema defines 14 node types and 7 edge types. A hybrid extraction pipeline populates the graph incrementally, while a three-tier checkpoint system serializes it into compact resume blocks. An 8-layer compression pipeline reduces context overhead, and a semantic cache reduces repeated-query latency. Evaluated on a controlled benchmark of 21 sessions spanning 5 domains, TokenMizer demonstrates significant token economy. It produces resume blocks averaging 78 tokens (range: 42-124) - 2x smaller than evaluated baselines (159-170 tokens) - while achieving higher decision recall (+9-17 percentage points). Crucially, baselines only preserve that a technology was mentioned; TokenMizer preserves the rationale. Across all sessions, TokenMizer achieves mean task recall 51.0%, decision recall 46.6%, and file recall 58.7%. Variance reflects domain heterogeneity: explicit imperative phrasing (software engineering) scores higher than implicit reasoning (research). Ablation studies show fuzzy label matching is the dominant improvement factor (+33 pp task recall). The heuristic compression achieves 47.3% token reduction with zero external dependencies. TokenMizer provides a queryable alternative to text-retention baselines at half the token cost.

</details>


### [198] [Boosting Brain-to-Image Decoding with TRIBE v2 Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06345)
*Yohann Benchetrit,Marlène Careil,Simon Dahan,Hubert Banville,Stéphane d'Ascoli,Jean-Rémi King*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Synthetic fMRI data from TRIBE v2 enhances image decoding in low-data regimes; up to 68% gain in Top-10 accuracy; some zero-shot decoding possible; performance depends on data source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity in fMRI decoding by leveraging a large pretrained encoding model to generate plausible fMRI responses for diverse stimuli, enabling data-efficient decoding.

Method: Systematic grid search varying the amount of synthetic data used to train image decoders on two datasets (7T Natural Scenes; 3T BOLD5000) using TRIBE v2; evaluate with Top-10 image retrieval.

Result: Demonstrates up to 68% improvement vs real-data-only decoders; the optimal synthetic data proportion is dataset-dependent; synthetic-only decoders can outperform chance in some settings.

Conclusion: Large-scale fMRI response models can underpin data-efficient brain decoding and enable zero-shot decoding, with implications for cross-modal transfer across sight, sound, language.

Abstract: Brain decoding is limited by the availability of labeled neural data, and remains challenging in low-data regimes. To address this issue, we investigate whether and when brain decoding can be boosted by augmenting small fMRI datasets with synthetic data generated by a pretrained model of fMRI responses to stimuli. We use TRIBE v2, a large encoding model pretrained on more than 1000 hours of fMRI responses to video, audio and language. For each dataset, we evaluate systematic grids that show how the performance of image decoders varies with the amount of synthetic data used for training. Our results, based on two datasets (the 7T fMRI Natural Scenes Dataset and 3T fMRI BOLD5000), show up to 68% improvement in Top-10 image-retrieval accuracy compared to decoders trained only on real data. Importantly, the proportion of augmented data required to reach a given image decoding performance needs to be adjusted depending on the data source. Surprisingly, image decoders trained exclusively on synthetic fMRI can perform above chance in some settings, suggesting that TRIBE v2 can support zero-shot brain-to-image decoding. Together, these results show how large-scale models of the fMRI responses to sight, sound and language may provide a foundation to improve the data efficiency for image decoding.

</details>


### [199] [Where Should Knowledge Enter? A Layered Framework for Knowledge Infusion in Multimodal Iterative Generative Mo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06356)
*Renjith Prasad,Chathurangi Shyalika,Anushka Pawar,Amit Sheth*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Four-layer intervention framework for knowledge infusion in diffusion-model multimodal generators (surface, trajectory, latent, parametric). Implemented in diffusion backbones; combining surface and trajectory-latent layers reduces knowledge-violating outputs by 70.97% vs vanilla generation in a controlled safety-alignment experiment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal generative models deliver fluent outputs but often violate domain-specific or safety-critical constraints. Existing mitigation approaches are categorized by techniques rather than by the generative-process component they modify. A unified intervention-layer framework can guide knowledge infusion across different components of the generation trajectory.

Method: Define four intervention layers corresponding to components of the generation process: surface (input/output boundary), trajectory (state transitions), latent (intermediate representations), and parametric (model parameters). Instantiate the framework in diffusion models, map representative methods to each layer, and derive design principles for multi-layer composition. Perform a controlled safety-alignment experiment with a multimodal knowledge graph and two diffusion backbones, implementing three of the four layers cumulatively: surface (input-side and output-side) and trajectory–latent (mid-generation).

Result: Each additional layer addresses failure classes unreachable by earlier layers; the multi-layer approach reduces knowledge-violating outputs compared to vanilla generation by 70.97%, empirically confirming the complementarity predicted by the framework.

Conclusion: A multi-layer infusion framework provides complementary, improved control over knowledge enforcement in diffusion-based multimodal generation, suggesting design principles for combining surface, trajectory, latent, and parametric infusion; results motivate broader validation across models and modalities.

Abstract: Multimodal generative models produce fluent outputs but remain unreliable when generation must respect structured, domain-specific, or safety-critical knowledge. Existing methods incorporate knowledge through mechanisms such as prompt augmentation, guidance, latent editing, or fine-tuning, yet they are typically categorized by technique rather than by the component of the generative process they modify. We argue that knowledge infusion in iterative generative models is fundamentally anintervention-layer problem. Since thegenerative process unfolds as a trajectory of internal states, knowledge can act on four structurally distinct components of this process: the input/output boundary, the transition function, the intermediate state, and the model parameters. This maps to four intervention layers: surface, trajectory, latent, and parametric infusion. We instantiate the framework in diffusion models, map representative methods to all four layers, and derive design principles for multi-layer composition. In a controlled safety-alignment experiment using a multimodal knowledge graph with two diffusion backbones, we implement three of the four layers cumulatively, surface (input-side and output-side) and trajectory--latent (mid-generation). We show empirically that each additional layer addresses failure classes that prior layers cannot reach, reducing knowledge-violating outputs by 70.97% compared to vanilla generation and empirically confirming the framework's complementarity prediction.

</details>


### [200] [An Infectious Disease Spread Simulation Based on Large Language Model Decision Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06360)
*Yonchanok Khaokaew,Ruochen Kong,Andreas Zufle,Hao Xue,Taylor Anderson,Chandini Raina MacIntyre,Matthew Scotch,Flora D. Salim,David J Heslop*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A spatial agent-based framework uses LLM-driven decisions about self-reported illness within a census-based synthetic population, assessing three reasoning scenarios in SF and Atlanta; income and education mainly drive reporting rates, with geographic, model-choice, and framing effects being smaller but present.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance understanding of behavioural dynamics in outbreaks by grounding agent decisions in location-aware, demographic-rich synthetic populations and evaluating how reporting behavior varies across space and scenarios.

Method: Construct a spatially anchored agent-based model where agents are assigned to city-level spatial units using census data. Integrate LLM-generated decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness (ILI) for each agent under three decision scenarios: independent reasoning, household influence, and message framing. Compare reporting outcomes across San Francisco and Atlanta.

Result: The model shows that income and education are the dominant drivers of reporting rate variation, with smaller, yet consistent effects from geography, LLM model choice, and message framing. The framework generates synthetic data capturing social and geographic heterogeneity.

Conclusion: The presented framework enables bias-aware, spatial epidemiological analysis and supports robust behavioural modelling for public health interventions by accounting for socioeconomic and geographic heterogeneity.

Abstract: Modelling individual decision-making during infectious disease outbreaks is crucial for understanding behavioural dynamics and informing effective public health interventions. Prior work has shown that large language models can simulate realistic human behaviour by generating agent decisions based on demographic prompts and situational context. We build on this foundation with a spatially grounded, agent-based simulation framework that integrates LLM-generated decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness into a census-based synthetic population of agents. Location is treated as a central feature: agents are assigned to spatial units within cities, capturing the spatial distributions of different demographic groups using real-world census data and enabling geographically diverse behavioural modelling. We implement and compare three decision scenarios, independent reasoning, household influence, and message framing, and simulate self-reporting outcomes in San Francisco and Atlanta. Results reveal that income and education are the dominant drivers of reporting rate variation, with smaller but consistent effects from geography, LLM model choice, and message framing. Our framework generates synthetic data that captures both social and geographic heterogeneity, supporting spatial epidemiological modelling and bias-aware behavioural analysis.

</details>


### [201] [Rethinking Infrastructure Inspection as Image Difference Classification: A Traffic Sign Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06375)
*Ching Yau Fergus Mok,Lavindra de Silva,Varun Kumar Reja,Ioannis Brilakis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Image difference classification (IDC) reduces data needs for defect detection in road infrastructure; an instruction-based IDC classifier outperforms encoder-based ones in a traffic-sign case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limited annotated data in digital twin (DT) enabled infrastructure inspection by reformulating image-based defect detection as IDC leveraging the relational nature of continuous asset condition monitoring.

Method: Evaluate multiple IDC classifiers (including instruction-based and encoder-based) on a newly curated high-quality dataset for low-resource traffic sign inspection; compare against reference images.

Result: Instruction-based IDC classifier achieves superior performance over encoder-based variants and benefits from comparisons with reference images; IDC proves effective under data-constrained conditions.

Conclusion: IDC modeling is a viable approach for DT asset condition updating and infrastructure inspection when annotated data are scarce, enabling more robust, data-efficient defect detection.

Abstract: Digital twins (DTs) allow the digitalization of road infrastructure inspection, though this is hindered by limited annotated data. This work exploits the relational nature of continuous asset condition monitoring to reformulate image-based defect detection as image difference classification (IDC) to reduce data reliance. This was evaluated in a case study on low-resource traffic sign inspection with different IDC classifiers using a newly-curated, high quality dataset. Results indicate that the instruction-based classifier outperforms encoder-based ones and gains from comparison with reference images. This shows that IDC can be an effective task modeling for tackling data constraints in infrastructure inspection and DT asset condition updating.

</details>


### [202] [Humans' ALMANAC: A Human Collaboration Dataset of Action-Level Mental Model Annotations for Agent Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06388)
*Jiaju Chen,Yuxuan Lu,Jiayi Su,Chaoran Chen,Songlin Xiao,Zheng Zhang,Yun Wang,Yunyao Li,Jian Zhao,Tongshuang Wu,Toby Jia-Jun Li,Dakuo Wang,Bingsheng Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ALMANAC introduces a dataset of 2,987 action-level collaboration actions with mental-model annotations for agent collaboration, enabling evaluation of LLMs on predicting next-turn behavior and inferring mental models from the Map Task dyadic routing task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of authentic human collaboration data with action-level mental-model annotations to guide the development of process-level collaborative competence in AI agents.

Method: Construct ALMANAC from the Map Task, annotating each collaboration action with self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, and perceived team goal; benchmark six LLMs on predicting humans' next-turn behavior and mental models.

Result: Demonstrates ALMANAC's utility for evaluating models’ ability to simulate human collaborative behaviors and infer underlying mental models; six LLMs exhibit varying performance, highlighting gaps and strengths in modeling collaborative reasoning.

Conclusion: ALMANAC provides a valuable resource for studying and improving human–agent collaboration, with potential for training signals and evaluation of process-level collaborative competence; future work could extend to additional tasks, contexts, and richer annotations.

Abstract: Recent advances in LLM agents have enabled complex cognitive capabilities, such as multi-step reasoning, planning, and tool use, that increasingly position these agents as human collaborators. Effective collaboration, however, requires collaborators to continuously maintain and align mental models of their own reasoning,partners' intentions, and shared goals during the collaborative process. Today's agents rarely develop such capabilities since they are primarily optimized for task completion, and the community lacks authentic human collaboration data with action-level mental model annotations that could guide agents toward process-level collaborative competence. To bridge this gap, we present ALMANAC, a dataset of Action-Level Mental model ANnotations for Agent Collaboration built from the Map Task, a classic dyadic routing task from social science. ALMANAC contains 2,987 collaboration actions, each paired with theory-informed mental model annotations that record the participants' self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, and perceived team goal. We benchmark six LLMs on predicting humans' next-turn behavior and mental models. Our results demonstrate ALMANAC's utility in evaluating models' ability to simulate human collaborative behaviors and infer their underlying mental models.

</details>


### [203] [Risk Assessment of Autonomous Driving: Integrating Technical Failures, Ethical Dilemmas, and Policy Frameworks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06396)
*Boyi Chen,Shengqin Chu,Zicheng Wang,Brian Baetz,Zhen Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autonomous driving safety requires integrated governance; perception/classification errors dominate failures; ethical/regulatory divergence creates uncertainty; recommends adaptive, cooperative governance across engineering standards, ethics, and supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate safety risks of autonomous driving across technology, ethics, and regulation using public data and regulatory views.

Method: Synthesis of public crash data (NHTSA), DMV disengagements, MIT Moral Machines dataset, and a cross-jurisdiction regulatory comparison.

Result: Perception and classification errors are the main technical failure modes; these contribute substantially to reported accidents; multiple ethical frameworks and uneven regulations create uncertainty.

Conclusion: Technology, ethics, regulation are interrelated; propose adaptive, cooperative governance combining engineering standards, ethical discussion, and institutional supervision.

Abstract: Autonomous driving technology has the potential to reduce the large number of road traffic accidents caused by human error each year, but it also brings new types of risks that need to be evaluated from the aspects of technology, ethics and regulations. Based on public crash data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), disengagement reports from the California Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV), the MIT Moral Machines dataset, and a comparative regulatory analysis of five jurisdictions, we have found that the main types of technical failure modes are perception and classification errors. These account for a relatively large proportion of the reported accidents, and it can be concluded that there are different ethical frameworks for autonomous vehicle decision-making, and inconsistent regulations in different areas increase the uncertainty of widespread application. Generally speaking, the problems of technology, ethics and regulation are closely related and need to be solved together. Therefore, this paper recommends a more adaptive and cooperative governance approach that combines engineering standards, ethical discussion, and institutional supervision.

</details>


### [204] [Unsupervised Skill Discovery for Agentic Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06416)
*Zhisong Qiu,Kangqi Song,Shengwei Tang,Shuofei Qiao,Lei Liang,Huajun Chen,Shumin Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DataCOPE is an unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery framework for data-analytic agents that coordinates an agent, an unsupervised verifier, and a skill manager to distill reusable skills from unlabeled exploration trajectories. It yields consistent improvements on both report-style and reasoning-style tasks, with notable gains (9.71% and 32.30% mean score) across four model settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervision for discovering useful data-analysis skills is expensive and success metrics vary across analytical formats. An unsupervised approach that leverages exploration trajectories to derive verifier signals can guide skill discovery without labeled data.

Method: Iterative loop among three roles: Data-Analytic Agent (trajectory generation), Unsupervised Verifier (extracts signals and assesses trajectory quality/consensus), and Skill Manager (contrastive skill distillation). Two instantiations of the verifier: Adaptive Checklist Verifier for report-style analysis (task-specific criteria, verifiable coverage) and Answer Agreement Verifier for reasoning-style analysis (group by answer agreement; self-consistency as auxiliary signal). Evaluation on Deep Data Research (report-style) and DABStep (reasoning-style).

Result: DataCOPE consistently improves held-out performance over baselines in both settings. Averaged across four model configurations, mean score improves by 9.71% for report-style tasks and 32.30% for reasoning-style tasks.

Conclusion: Unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery can efficiently induce reusable data-analysis skills that generalize across analytic formats, reducing the need for labeled supervision while delivering substantial performance gains. The verifier design is adaptable to task style and could be extended to additional formats.

Abstract: Inference-time skill augmentation provides a lightweight way to improve data-analytic agents by injecting reusable procedural knowledge without updating model parameters. However, discovering effective skills for data analysis remains challenging, as reliable supervision is expensive and success criteria vary across analytical formats. This raises the key question of how to discover reusable data-analysis skills from unlabeled exploration alone. We propose DataCOPE, an unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery framework for data-analytic agents. DataCOPE derives verifier signals from the exploration trajectories and uses them to characterize relative quality or aggreement among trajectories. It iteratively coordinates a Data-Analytic Agent for trajectory generation, an Unsupervised Verifier for signal extraction, and a Skill Manager for contrastive skill distillation. For report-style analysis, we instantiate the verifier as an Adaptive Checklist Verifier that derives task-specific criteria, scores reports by verifiable coverage, and iteratively refines the checklist. For reasoning-style analysis, we instantiate it as an Answer Agreement Verifier that groups trajectories by answer agreement and uses self-consistency as an auxiliary signal. We evaluate DataCOPE on report-style analysis from Deep Data Research and reasoning-style analysis from DABStep. Across both settings, DataCOPE consistently improves held-out performance over baselines. Averaged across four model settings, DataCOPE improves the mean score by 9.71% and 32.30% on report-style and reasoning-style tasks respectively.

</details>


### [205] [Agent Memory: Characterization and System Implications of Stateful Long-Horizon Workloads](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06448)
*Yasmine Omri,Ziyu Gan,Zachary Broveak,Robin Geens,Zexue He,Alex Pentland,Marian Verhelst,Tsachy Weissman,Thierry Tambe*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: System-level analysis of agent memory in long-horizon LLM tasks: taxonomy, phase-aware cost profiling, empirical study of ten memory systems across two benchmarks, and 10 design recommendations for scalable memory management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address a gap in understanding how memory architectures influence performance and cost in enduring LLM-agent tasks, and to guide scalable deployment and maintenance.

Method: (1) Build a system-oriented taxonomy of agent memory along four axes. (2) Develop a phase-aware profiling harness that attributes costs to memory construction, retrieval, and generation. (3) Empirically characterize ten representative memory systems across two benchmark suites to observe how design choices affect write/read costs. (4) Derive practical system-level recommendations for construction scheduling, capability floors, amortization via query volume, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management.

Result: The study reveals how memory design decisions shift cost distribution between write and read paths, provides quantitative cost attributions for construction, retrieval, and generation, and offers a concrete set of actionable recommendations to optimize memory systems at scale.

Conclusion: A principled, system-level framework for evaluating and designing agent memory, enabling more predictable performance and scalable deployments through taxonomy, profiling, empirical evidence, and curated guidelines.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed on long-horizon tasks requiring sustained reasoning over extended interaction histories. Realizing this at scale requires agents to persistently store, retrieve, and update their own memory across sessions. A rich ecosystem of agent memory systems has emerged spanning flat retrieval, LLM-mediated extraction, consolidating fact stores, and agentic control flows. Yet, their system-level behavior remains uncharacterized. We present the first systems characterization of agent memory. First, we introduce a system-oriented taxonomy classifying agent memory systems along four axes. Second, we build a phase-aware profiling harness attributing cost to construction, retrieval, and generation. Third, we characterize ten representative systems across two benchmark suites, uncovering how design choices shift cost across the write and read paths. Finally, we derive 10 system recommendations covering construction scheduling, capability floors, amortization via query volume, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management.

</details>


### [206] [Vortex: Efficient and Programmable Sparse Attention Serving for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06453)
*Zhuoming Chen,Xinrui Zhong,Qilong Feng,Ranajoy Sadhukhan,Yang Zhou,Michael Qizhe Shieh,Zhihao Jia,Beidi Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A system called Vortex enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention for LLMs, achieving substantial throughput gains across models and architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the engineering burden of evaluating sparse attention at scale and to accelerate the exploration and optimization of sparse attention algorithms for large models.

Method: A Python-embedded frontend language atop a page-centric tensor abstraction for expressing sparse attention, combined with an efficient backend tightly integrated into modern LLM serving stacks to enable rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation.

Result: Best algorithms reach up to 3.46x throughput versus full attention; on MLA-based GLM-4.7-Flash up to 4.7x; on 229B-parameter MiniMax-M2.7 up to 1.37x on NVIDIA B200 GPUs.

Conclusion: Vortex substantially accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms and enables experimentation on very large models and architectures that are otherwise hard to test.

Abstract: Sparse attention is becoming increasingly important for serving large language models (LLMs) as generation lengths continue to grow. However, deploying and evaluating new sparse attention algorithms at scale remains highly engineering-intensive, slowing both human researchers and AI agents in exploring the sparse attention design. To address this challenge, we present Vortex, a system that combines a Python-embedded frontend language atop a page-centric tensor abstraction for expressing a broad range of sparse attention algorithms, with an efficient backend tightly integrated into modern LLM serving stacks. Vortex enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms, effectively translating their theoretical efficiency gains into real-world throughput improvements. As a result, Vortex substantially accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms. First, AI agents use Vortex to automatically generate and refine diverse algorithms, the best reaching up to $3.46\times$ higher throughput than full attention while preserving accuracy.
