Second, Vortex extends sparse attention to emerging architectures and very large models that are otherwise hard to experiment with, reaching up to $4.7\times$ higher throughput on the MLA-based GLM-4.7-Flash and $1.37\times$ on the 229B-parameter MiniMax-M2.7 on NVIDIA B200 GPUs.

</details>


### [207] [Benchmark Everything Everywhere All at Once](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06462)
*Shiyun Xiong,Dongming Wu,Peiwen Sun,Yuang Ai,Bokang Yang,Wencheng Han,Xiao-Hui Li,Xiangyu Yue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Benchmark Agent is an autonomous agentic system that builds benchmarks for LLMs/MLLMs end-to-end, enabling scalable, reusable, and discriminative evaluation. It produces 15 benchmarks across text, multimodal, and domain-specific tasks, with human and LLM-based evaluation, and shows that benchmarks evolve and reveal model weaknesses. Code and demo to come.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarks guide progress but are costly to construct, hard to reuse, and tend to saturate quickly, limiting discriminability among models; there is a need for scalable, sustainable, and continually updated benchmarks.

Method: An autonomous agentic pipeline that analyzes user queries, designs subtasks, annotates data, and enforces quality control throughout the benchmark construction process. It includes 15 representative benchmarks spanning text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning, with human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge assessment, and consistency checks.

Result: Benchmarks are generated with high quality and minimal human input. The approach enables continual evaluation, identifying domain-specific reasoning weaknesses in current models.

Conclusion: Dynamic, automatically constructed benchmarks can accelerate sustained progress in AI evaluation; Benchmark Agent demonstrates a scalable framework and will release preview and code publicly.

Abstract: Benchmarks are fundamental for evaluating and advancing LLMs and MLLMs by providing standardized and explicit measures of performance. However, their construction is labor-intensive and hard to reuse, raising concerns about sustainability and scalability. Moreover, existing benchmarks often quickly reach performance saturation after their release, resulting in insufficient discrimination among state-of-the-art models. To address these challenges, we introduce Benchmark Agent, a fully autonomous agentic system designed for benchmark building. Our framework orchestrates the complete benchmark construction pipeline, from user query analysis and subtask design to data annotation and quality control. To assess Benchmark Agent, we implement it to produce 15 representative benchmarks, spanning diverse evaluation scenarios, including text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning. Extensive experiments, including human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge assessment, and consistency checks, demonstrate Benchmark Agent can generate high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement. More importantly, through continual evaluation, we observe several insightful findings, including that current models struggle with certain domain-specific reasoning tasks. We believe that rapidly evolving benchmarks can contribute significantly to the research community. The preview and code will be publicly available at the demo page and code repository.

</details>


### [208] [Goedel-Architect: Streamlining Formal Theorem Proving with Blueprint Generation and Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06468)
*Jui-Hui Chung,Ziyang Cai,Zihao Li,Qishuo Yin,Rohit Agarwal,Simon Park,Rodrigo Porto,Narutatsu Ri,Ziran Yang,Shange Tang,Xingyu Dang,Hongzhou Lin,Mengdi Wang,Danqi Chen,Chi Jin,Liam H Fowl,Sanjeev Arora*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Blueprint-driven agentic prover Goedel-Architect for Lean 4 achieves state-of-the-art open-source performance on formal benchmarks with parallel lemma closure and NL-seeded blueprint refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate inefficiencies of recursive lemma decomposition and dead-ends in formal proof search; leverage dependency-graph blueprinting and parallelization to accelerate proving high-level theorems with lower compute.

Method: Generate an initial blueprint (definitions/lemmas plus declared dependencies); optionally seed with natural-language proof; Lean prover closes open lemmas in parallel using dependencies; failed lemmas refine the blueprint; evaluate on MiniF2F, PutnamBench, IMO 2025/Putnam 2025/USAMO 2026; backbone DeepSeek-V4-Flash 284B-A13B; compare to mainstream approaches.

Result: 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test; 75.6% pass@1 on PutnamBench; with NL-seeding: 100% MiniF2F; 88.8% PutnamBench; 4/6 IMO2025; 11/12 Putnam2025; 3/6 USAMO2026; state-of-the-art for open-source pipelines; cost up to 500x less than comparable pipelines.

Conclusion: Blueprint-driven, agentic approach with optional NL seeding yields high efficiency and strong performance, establishing a new baseline for open-source automated theorem proving in Lean with scalable parallelism and iterative blueprint refinement.

Abstract: We introduce Goedel-Architect, an agentic framework for formal theorem proving in Lean 4 centered on blueprint generation and refinement. A blueprint is a dependency graph of definitions and lemmas that builds up to the main theorem. First, Goedel-Architect generates a blueprint of formally stated definitions and lemmas, along with declared dependencies. This blueprint is optionally guided by a natural language proof. Then, a tool-equipped Lean prover component closes each open lemma node in parallel using relevant dependencies. Failed lemmas in turn drive refinement of the global blueprint. This strategy contrasts with other mainstream approaches which use recursive lemma decomposition, and can inefficiently loop on dead-end strategies. Using the open-weight DeepSeek-V4-Flash (284B-A13B) as the backbone, Goedel-Architect attains 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test and 75.6% pass@1 on PutnamBench. With an optional natural-language proof seeding the initial blueprint on the harder problems, we additionally close the remaining two MiniF2F-test problems (reaching 100%), lift PutnamBench to 88.8% (597/672), and solve 4/6 on IMO 2025, 11/12 on Putnam 2025, and 3/6 on USAMO 2026. This represents state-of-the-art performance for an open-source pipeline at a price point up to 500x less than comparable open-source pipelines.

</details>


### [209] [MLEvolve: A Self-Evolving Framework for Automated Machine Learning Algorithm Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06473)
*Shangheng Du,Xiangchao Yan,Jinxin Shi,Zongsheng Cao,Shiyang Feng,Zichen Liang,Boyuan Sun,Tianshuo Peng,Yifan Zhou,Xin Li,Jie Zhou,Liang He,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MLEvolve is an LLM-based self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end ML algorithm discovery that tackles inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control by using Progressive MCGS with graph-based references, Retrospective Memory, and adaptive coding modes, achieving state-of-the-art results on MLE-Bench and AlphaEvolve tasks with cross-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon optimization in scientific discovery and machine learning engineering (MLE) requires sustained self-evolution. Existing MLE agents suffer from inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, hindering long-horizon optimization.

Method: Extend tree search to Progressive MCGS to enable cross-branch information flow via graph-based reference edges and an entropy-inspired progressive schedule; introduce Retrospective Memory combining a cold-start domain knowledge base with a dynamic global memory for task-specific retrieval and reuse; decouple strategic planning from code generation with adaptive coding modes for stable long-horizon iteration. Evaluation on MLE-Bench shows state-of-the-art performance across metrics like average medal rate and valid submission rate within a 12-hour budget; also outperforms AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks, indicating cross-domain generalization.

Result: MLEvolve achieves state-of-the-art performance on MLE-Bench across multiple dimensions (e.g., average medal rate, valid submission rate) within a 12-hour budget (half the standard runtime). It also outperforms specialized algorithm discovery methods such as AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization.

Conclusion: MLEvolve demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization and advances in long-horizon ML algorithm discovery through integrated cross-branch information flow, memory dynamics, and planning-coding decoupling. Code is available at the authors’ GitHub repository.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly applied to long-horizon tasks such as scientific discovery and machine learning engineering (MLE), where sustained self-evolution becomes a key capability. However, existing MLE agents suffer from inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, which together hinder long-horizon optimization. We present MLEvolve, an LLM-based self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end machine learning algorithm discovery. By extending tree search to Progressive MCGS, MLEvolve enables cross-branch information flow through graph-based reference edges and gradually shifts the search from broad exploration to focused exploitation with an entropy-inspired progressive schedule. To allow the agent to evolve with accumulated experience, we introduce Retrospective Memory, which combines a cold-start domain knowledge base with a dynamic global memory for task-specific experience retrieval and reuse. For stable long-horizon iteration, we further decouple strategic planning from code generation with adaptive coding modes. Evaluation on MLE-Bench shows that MLEvolve achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple dimensions including average medal rate and valid submission rate under a 12-hour budget (half the standard runtime). Moreover, MLEvolve also outperforms specialized algorithm discovery methods including AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at https://github.com/InternScience/MLEvolve.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [210] [The Evaluation Blind Spot: A Stereological Theory of Benchmark Coverage for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05169)
*Jason Z Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stereological theory of LLM benchmark coverage with provable bounds and a practical core-benchmark selection method; shows how many benchmarks suffice for stable coverage, and proves a minimax rate for C^2 support functions via optimal recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how well benchmark suites capture LLM capabilities, bound cross-suite similarity, and guide efficient benchmark design and dataset transfer across time and priors.

Method: Develop stereological framework linking effective dimensionality d_eff to bounds on the visible Hausdorff distance between convex capability profiles consistent with the same scores. Empirically evaluate on Open LLM v2, an extended 12-benchmark suite, and LiveBench; model with chi-squared projection and isotropic priors; use a submodular greedy algorithm with Nemhauser's 1-1/e guarantee to extract a stable core; perform eigenstructure-based counterfactual validation; resolve Gardner's Problem 1.5 (1995) for C^2 functions via optimal recovery to obtain minimax rate Theta(R/(kappa m^(2/(D-1)))).

Result: Bound on distance: epsilon + C R m^(-1/(d_eff-1)); d_eff in [2.86, 4.80]; structural blind spot dwarfs score gaps; top-two-model swap rate ~0.38–0.49 under chi-squared priors; 92% of 500 trials swap top-1 when 5 top-5 models considered; a core of 4 benchmarks via submodular selection; 7 of 12 benchmarks yield 90% coverage; transfers across quarters with 93–97% retention; eigenstructure predicts irreplacible evaluations (rho = -0.69, p = 0.013) and informative external evaluations (rho = +0.38). Minimax rate for C^2: Theta(R/(kappa m^(2/(D-1)))).

Conclusion: The framework provides both theoretical guarantees and practical guidance for benchmark design and evaluation, including principled selection of benchmark cores, transferability across time, and a resolution of Gardner’s problem for smooth support functions, tying abstract optimal recovery to concrete LLM benchmarking questions.

Abstract: We give a stereological theory of LLM benchmark coverage. For any suite with effective dimensionality d_eff, the visible Hausdorff distance between two convex capability profiles consistent with the same scores is bounded by epsilon + C R m^(-1/(d_eff-1)), with matching Lipschitz lower bound. Empirically, three independent leaderboards (Open LLM v2, an extended 12-benchmark suite, LiveBench) all have d_eff in [2.86, 4.80] on their competitive frontier; the structural blind spot exceeds the observed runner-up score gap by two orders of magnitude and dominates statistical noise by 52-127x. Under a chi-squared projection model, the isotropic prior is the optimistic case; across six hidden-capability priors and four ambient dimensions the simulated half-split swap rate of the top two models stays in [0.38, 0.49], and a 500-trial random visible/held-out split shows that 92% of trials swap the top-1 ranking with on average 2.83 of 5 top-5 models changing. A submodular greedy algorithm with the Nemhauser (1 - 1/e) guarantee finds a stable core of 4 benchmarks; 7 of 12 suffice for 90% coverage, and the trained subset transfers across temporal quarters with 93-97% retention. A counterfactual validation across 12 internal benchmarks and 27 Chatbot Arena categories confirms that the eigenstructure predicts which evaluations are irreplaceable (rho = -0.69, p = 0.013 for removal disruption) and which external evaluations bring new information (rho = +0.38). As a second, independent theoretical contribution, we resolve Gardner's Problem 1.5 (1995) for C^2 support functions, establishing the minimax rate Theta(R/(kappa m^(2/(D-1)))) in general dimension via optimal recovery theory on S^(D-1).

</details>


### [211] [ERRORQUAKE: Heavy-Tailed Error Severity Distributions in Open-Weight Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05170)
*Jason Z Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Errorquake-10k introduces a 10k-query benchmark to quantify LLM error severity on a continuous 0-4 scale across domains/difficulties, fitting per-model severity distributions (Gutenberg-Richter slope b) and showing non-redundancy with error rate. Uses 21 open-weight models; human validation confirms reliability and ranking; finds many model pairs with disjoint severity CI and a severity mechanism (low-severity=retrievals, high-severity=fabrications). Recommends reporting severity distributions alongside accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar error rate obscures how errors are distributed by type and severity. Different errors have orders of magnitude impact; a more nuanced, distributional view is needed for fair comparison, safety, and risk assessment of open-weight LLMs.

Method: Construct Errorquake-10k with 10,000 prompts; rate each response on a continuous 0-4 severity scale across 8 domains and 5 difficulty tiers. Fit per-model severity distributions for 21 open-weight models. Estimate severity distribution index b (Gutenberg-Richter upper-tail slope) with 95% bootstrap CIs. Assess CI disjointness across model pairs. Conduct a 519-item three-rater human validation study comparing LLM-judge rankings, compute ICC(2,k=3)=0.85, and correlations rho=0.89 and rho_s=-0.86. Prove a Non-Reducibility Theorem I(b; model | epsilon)=1.56 bits. Build a severity mechanism taxonomy (kappa=0.83) to relate severity to error types and model size.

Result: Across 210 model pairs, 85 pairs have disjoint 95% CIs for b at matched accuracy. Human validation supports reliability and alignment with model scaling. Severity distribution exhibits dependency beyond epsilon; 64.5% of cross-model b variance is unexplained by error rate. Low-severity errors are predominantly retrievals (71%), high-severity errors are fabrications (39%), with shifts by model size (p<0.0001). Severity distribution provides discriminative information beyond accuracy.

Conclusion: Severity distribution should be reported alongside accuracy; it contains information that error rate alone cannot capture, enabling better model comparison, risk assessment, and interpretability of LLM behavior.

Abstract: At matched accuracy, open-weight LLMs differ substantially in the shape of their error severity distribution -- a difference invisible to the scalar error rate. Hallucination benchmarks report a single error count and treat all errors as equivalent, yet a wrong date and a fabricated court ruling differ by orders of magnitude. We introduce Errorquake-10k, a 10,000-query benchmark scoring each response on a continuous 0-4 severity scale across 8 domains and 5 difficulty tiers, and we fit per-model severity distributions for 21 open-weight models. For each model we estimate a severity distribution index (b, the Gutenberg-Richter upper-tail slope) with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Headline: across the 210 model pairs, 85 have disjoint 95% b confidence intervals at matched accuracy (|Delta epsilon| < 0.05) on human-consensus scoring, e.g. deepseek-v3.2 vs. ministral-14b at epsilon = 0.586 and Delta b = 0.47. A 519-item three-rater human validation study confirms measurement reliability (ICC(2,k=3) = 0.85), validates the LLM-judge ranking (rho = 0.89), and confirms the dense-model scaling correlation on human data (rho_s = -0.86). We prove a Non-Reducibility Theorem showing that severity profile and error rate are informationally non-redundant (I(b; model | epsilon) = 1.56 bits; 64.5% of cross-model b variance is unexplained by epsilon). A severity mechanism taxonomy (kappa = 0.83) reveals that error type shifts categorically with severity: low-severity errors are retrievals (71%); high-severity errors are fabrications (39%) -- and this composition differs by model size (p < 0.0001). Severity distribution should be reported alongside accuracy; it carries discriminative information that the error rate cannot.

</details>


### [212] [Efficient Mean Curvature Computation on High-Dimensional Data Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06329)
*Alexandre L. M. Levada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable method to estimate local mean curvature in high-dimensional data for curvature-aware ML (e.g., MCBP) by removing the explicit shape operator H and using a truncated SVD with an analytic null-space approximation, achieving large speed-ups with negligible accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating local curvature is crucial for geometry-aware learning, but naive approaches scale poorly (O(m^4) per point) in high dimensions, hindering practical use beyond small feature counts.

Method: Two-part approach: (1) derive an exact algebraic identity from covariance eigenvector orthogonality and trace cyclicity to eliminate H, reducing per-point cost to O(m^2) after eigendecomposition; (2) replace the full covariance by a truncated SVD of the k x m centered data matrix (rank ≤ k-1), cost O(k^2 m), and derive an analytical approximation for null-space eigenvector contributions under the Haar measure, yielding a total cost O(k^2 m + k m p^2) with p = k-1.

Result: Empirical results on real datasets show 50–300× speedups compared to the original implementation, with negligible accuracy loss when using the fast estimator.

Conclusion: Provides a scalable, data-driven local curvature estimator, enabling curvature to be a practical geometric feature across classical and modern ML pipelines.

Abstract: Estimating local mean curvature at each point of a high-dimensional dataset is a key ingredient of geometry-aware machine learning algorithms, such as the Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP) method. The naive implementation of this computation, based on a local shape operator approximated from k-nearest neighbor patches, involves an explicit construction of a matrix $H$ whose trace form yields an $O(m^4)$ cost per point, rendering the approach intractable for datasets with more than a few dozen features. This paper introduces two complementary contributions that together reduce this cost by several orders of magnitude. The first contribution is an exact algebraic identity. This identity, derived from the orthogonality of the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix and the cyclicity of the trace operator, eliminates $H$ entirely and reduces the per-point cost to $O(m^2)$ after the eigendecomposition. The second contribution addresses the remaining $O(m^3)$ bottleneck of the full eigendecomposition. Since the local covariance matrix has rank at most $k-1 \ll m$, we replace it with a truncated SVD of the $k \times m$ centered data matrix, an $O(k^2 m)$ operation, and derive an analytical approximation for the contribution of the null-space eigenvectors based on the expected value of their outer product under the Haar measure. The resulting estimator has total cost $O(k^2 m + k m p^2)$, where $p = k-1$. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm speedups of 50 to 300 times relative to the original implementation, with negligible loss when the fast estimator is used to replace the original version. By providing a scalable and data-driven estimate of local curvature, the proposed method establishes curvature as a practical geometric feature for a broad range of machine learning tasks, from classical to modern deep learning pipelines.

</details>


### [213] [Staged Factorial Screening for Budget-Constrained Micro-Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05186)
*Felipe Chavarro Polania*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A staged fractional-factorial workflow effectively recovers early effect structure under budget constraints; a bridge-centered strategy with short screens and anchor refinement outperforms hardware-invariant hyperparameter optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether budget-aware, staged screening can identify influential factors and reliable anchors for micro-pretraining while avoiding spurious early signals and enabling efficient local refinement.

Method: 613 experiments across pilot/follow-up screens at 2/5/10 minutes; full 16-condition seeded reruns at 5/10 minutes; targeted seeded anchor checks; same-host greedy and matched-cost random baselines; 60-minute bridge package; bounded Windows A100 and Linux L40S anchor continuations through 24 hours; Benjamini-Hochberg correction within predeclared seeded full-screen families.

Result: Main penalties from total batch, depth, and width are largest at short budgets and decrease with more budget. In seeded full-screen families, factors D, A, B, C retain non-zero effects at 5 and 10 minutes after correction; E does not. Random search reaches strong incumbents but only in the same low-penalty region and lacks consistent factor attribution. The 60-minute bridge anchor has the lowest mean, though it conflates workflow refinement with the bridge model’s capacity. In 12- and 24-hour three-anchor continuations across two hosts, the bridge maintains the lowest mean while non-bridge ordering is host-sensitive.

Conclusion: Adopt a bounded methods result: screen early to identify high-penalty directions, confirm promising anchors with repeated runs, and refine locally within the reduced space. The evidence supports a bridge-centered recommendation through 24 hours on two hosts, rather than hardware-invariant ranking or general hyperparameter-optimization superiority.

Abstract: Budget-constrained micro-pretraining often requires triaging many candidate recipes on a shared accelerator before larger search budgets are spent. We study whether a staged fractional-factorial workflow can recover stable early effect structure in this setting. On a fixed autoresearch-derived single-GPU training loop, we run 613 experiments across pilot and follow-up screens at 2, 5, and 10 minutes; full 16-condition seeded reruns at 5 and 10 minutes; targeted seeded anchor checks; same-host greedy and matched-cost random baselines; a 60-minute bridge package; and bounded Windows A100 and Linux L40S anchor continuations through 24 hours. Main penalties from total batch, depth, and width are largest at short budgets and relax as budget increases. Within the predeclared seeded full-screen families, D, A, B, and C retain non-zero estimates at 5 and 10 minutes after within-budget Benjamini-Hochberg correction, while E does not. Random search can reach strong incumbents in this 32-condition space, but repeatedly in the same low-penalty region and without factor attribution. The 60-minute bridge anchor has the lowest mean, although that package does not separate workflow refinement from the larger bridge model's capacity advantage. In bounded 12-hour and 24-hour three-anchor continuations on both hosts, the bridge has the lowest sample mean while the non-bridge ordering stays host-sensitive. We therefore present a bounded methods result: use short designed screens to identify high-penalty directions, confirm promising anchors under repeated runs, and refine locally inside the reduced space. The evidence supports a bridge-centered recommendation through 24 hours on two hosts, not hardware-invariant ranking or general hyperparameter-optimization superiority.

</details>


### [214] [PyCC.id: A package for hypothesis-driven equation discovery with structural identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05191)
*Federico J. Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hypothesis-driven, skeleton-based ODE discovery via CCs; introduces PyCC library for modular integration with neural networks, symbolic regression, and sparse regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse, ill-conditioned problem in data-driven equation discovery; reducing search space via domain-informed skeletons and priors to yield more reliable models.

Method: Define skeletons as CC-inspired ODE families; incorporate domain hypotheses and priors; enable modular use of different discovery paradigms (neural nets, symbolic regression, sparse regression); provide identifiability checks for skeleton validity; implement in Python as PyCC.

Result: A flexible, modular framework and Python library (PyCC) enabling skeleton-based equation discovery with identifiability considerations across multiple discovery paradigms.

Conclusion: Skeletons with structural identifiability properties, when combined with domain priors, can constrain the search space and improve model discovery; the modular PyCC framework enables flexible experimentation across discovery methods.

Abstract: Data-driven equation discovery is fundamentally an inverse problem that seeks to infer the governing differential equations of a system directly from time-series measurements. A known issue is the ill-conditioned nature of the inverse problem, which frequently produces multiple mathematical models that fit the data similarly well. One path to address this issue is by incorporating known hypotheses and constraints into the training phase beforehand. While this approach effectively reduces the search space, it still results in multiple candidate models, forcing practitioners to rely on post-hoc manual filtering based on their own domain expertise. A recent approach incorporates structural `skeletons' inspired by characteristic curves (CCs), defining a hypothesis-driven methodology. In this methodology, practitioners define a skeleton, which is associated with a family of ordinary differential equations (ODEs), and then add their hypotheses and priors based on their domain knowledge to refine the obtained model iteratively. An important advantage of this approach is that some skeletons have demonstrable structural identifiability properties, which are useful for checking whether the skeleton is correct or should be discarded. Furthermore, this formalism enables the use of multiple equation discovery paradigms due to its modularity (such as neural networks, symbolic regression, and sparse regression). In this work, we present the Python library PyCC, which condenses these efforts into a flexible tool that allows researchers and engineers to seamlessly define their skeletons and hypotheses to discover ODEs from time-dependent data.

</details>


### [215] [Temporal Preference Concepts and their Functions in a Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05194)
*Ian Rios-Sialer,Shantanu Darveshi,Shuai Jiang,Avigya Paudel,Anastasiia Pronina,Ipshita Bandyopadhyay,Justin Shenk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Causal mechanistic interpretability identifies a mid-to-upper-layer subgraph encoding temporal horizon in a distilled LLM; the time horizon is represented in residual streams; LLMs discount the future more weakly than humans with unstable behavior across contexts; steering vectors can adjust temporal preference, underscoring the need for explicit control over temporal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reveal how LLMs internally represent near-term vs long-term tradeoffs and assess whether such temporal biases can be controlled rather than only learned.

Method: Causal localization using gradient-based attribution and activation patching in Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507; identify mid-to-upper-layer nodes; analyze residual-stream geometry; behavioral assessment of future discounting versus humans; test steering vectors.

Result: Identified a localized subgraph encoding temporal horizon within mid-to-upper layers; residual stream geometry encodes horizon; unintervened LLMs discount the future less steeply than humans with context-dependent instability; steering vectors can modulate temporal preference.

Conclusion: Mechanistic interpretability can enable reliable control over LLM planning and inference regarding future consequences; explicit control mechanisms may be necessary for robust temporal decision-making.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being deployed to make decisions that require trading off near-term gains against long-term consequences, yet little is known about how they internally represent or resolve these tradeoffs. In this work, we causally localize an underlying subgraph for temporal preference in a distilled LLM (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507), identifying mid-to-upper-layer nodes through converging evidence from gradient-based attribution and activation patching. We find that the geometry of time horizon is encoded in the residual stream at the expected localized layers. A behavioral analysis reveals that unintervened LLMs discount the future several times less steeply than humans, yet this preference is unstable across contexts, motivating explicit control rather than implicit reliance on training. Finally, we find suggestive evidence that steering vectors can shift temporal preference. Our work demonstrates how mechanistic interpretability can bring us closer to reliable control over how LLMs plan and reason

</details>


### [216] [State commitment learning: training language models to distinguish computation from memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05201)
*Fei Ding,Yongkang Zhang,Runhao Liu,Yuhao Liao,Zijian Zeng,Huiming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes state commitment learning to separate persistent state from temporary computation in reasoning LMs, via a counterfactual erasure framework (CERL) and the Erasure Dependence Protocol, showing reduced reliance on hidden thoughts with maintained accuracy across diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reasoning models leak hidden chain-of-thought into context, causing future predictions to depend on private scratch work. The work seeks to enforce a clean separation between computation and persistent state to improve reliability and safety.

Method: Introduce persistent-state sufficiency as a trainable criterion. Propose Counterfactual Erasure RL (CERL) that rewards paths where erasing hidden thoughts still yields correct answers under the same prefix, and an Erasure Dependence Protocol. Evaluate across mathematics, long-chain logic, scientific QA, and multi-turn tool use.

Result: CERL substantially reduces dependence on hidden thoughts while preserving or improving accuracy, outperforming correctness-only RL and long-answer SFT baselines across the tested domains.

Conclusion: A principled objective for training LMs to commit useful information to persistent state while discarding ephemeral computation, with broad applicability to reasoning tasks and potential robustness/safety benefits.

Abstract: Reasoning language models do not distinguish tokens used for computation from tokens that constitute persistent state: once generated, all hidden thoughts remain in context and influence future predictions. As a result, downstream reasoning may depend on failed attempts, dead ends, and private scratch work that should not be safely relied on later. We recast this phenomenon as a new training objective, state commitment learning: training models to explicitly distinguish information that should be committed as persistent state from temporary computation that can be discarded. We define a counterfactual criterion, persistent-state sufficiency, which makes it trainable and measurable whether an answer remains usable after hidden thoughts are erased. We then propose Counterfactual Erasure RL (CERL), which evaluates, under the same prefix, both a path that keeps hidden thoughts and a path that erases them, and gives reward only when the erasure path remains correct. We also introduce the Erasure Dependence Protocol and show across mathematics, long-chain logic, scientific QA, and multi-turn tool-use evaluation that CERL substantially reduces answer dependence on hidden thoughts without sacrificing accuracy, consistently outperforming correctness-only RL and long-answer SFT baselines.

</details>


### [217] [Gradient Descent with Large Step Size Restores Symmetry in Deep Linear Networks with Multi-Pathway](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05219)
*Hee-Sung Kim,Sungyoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Large-step discrete gradient descent on multi-pathway deep linear networks yields shared representations, not persistent single-path dominance; path distribution decreases loss landscape sharpness, and edge-of-stability oscillations induce a rebalancing across pathways.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconcile predictions from gradient flow (which suggests winner-takes-all specialization) with the behavior of discrete GD at large step sizes in multi-pathway deep linear architectures; understand how depth and pathway count influence competition and sharpness.

Method: Theoretical analysis of discrete GD dynamics on multi-pathway deep linear networks with large steps. The study compares single-path vs distributed-path solutions by examining solution sharpness (Hessian) and stability under Edge of Stability oscillations, deriving how sharpness scales with the number of pathways and depth.

Result: Single-path solutions are sharp minima; distributing signals across pathways lowers sharpness by a factor that decreases with both the number of pathways and depth. Early training mirrors depth-driven symmetry breaking predicted by gradient flow, but Edge of Stability oscillations later drive a re-balancing phase, redistributing signals across pathways. Overall, depth shapes pathway competition and large-step GD preferentially yields shared representations over persistent single-path dominance.

Conclusion: Depth-induced competition among pathways is modulated by step-size; large-step GD moves toward shared representations due to reduced sharpness from distribution across paths and EoS-driven rebalancing, resolving discrepancies between GF predictions and discrete optimization outcomes.

Abstract: Recent analyses of multi-pathway Deep Linear Networks use Gradient Flow to predict a "winner-takes-all" specialization in which path symmetry breaks and each feature concentrates in a single pathway. In this work, we show that discrete Gradient Descent (GD) with a large step size tells a different story. We prove that single-path solutions are sharp minima, whereas distributing signals across pathways reduces sharpness by a factor that decreases with both the number of pathways and depth. Consequently, while early training reproduces the depth-driven symmetry breaking predicted by GF, oscillations at the Edge of Stability subsequently override this tendency and drive the network into a re-balancing phase, where signals redistribute across pathways. Together, these results clarify how depth shapes pathway competition and explain why large-step GD favors shared representations rather than persistent single-pathway dominance.

</details>


### [218] [Differentiable Efficient Operator Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05232)
*Xiaohuan Pei,Jiyuan Zhang,Yuanfan Guo,Weiguo Feng,Tao Huang,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Chang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies token-reduction operators in multimodal models into a differentiable search space; Efficient Operator Search jointly optimizes where/how many tokens to reduce and how to process reduced tokens, achieving competitive accuracy-efficiency, especially under aggressive token reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual, ad hoc token-reduction operators are disparate; a unified differentiable framework can discover optimal and hybrid operators beyond hand-designed baselines and adapt to budget constraints.

Method: Define search space over layer activation, retention budget, and operator behavior; train a differentiable search policy to optimize task performance under one-sided budget and cost constraints; recover baselines as special cases and discover hybrid operators.

Result: On multimodal benchmarks, searched operators achieve competitive accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, with strong performance under aggressive visual-token reduction; demonstrates that operator design can be automated via differentiable search.

Conclusion: Efficient multimodal inference can be reframed from manual operator design to differentiable operator search, enabling discovery of effective hybrid operators beyond conventional designs.

Abstract: Efficient multimodal foundation models often rely on manually designed token-reduction operators, such as pruning, merging, pooling, and adaptive reweighting. Although these operators appear different, we show that they can be interpreted as distinct regimes of a shared operator space. Based on this view, we introduce Efficient Operator Search, a differentiable framework that jointly searches where to reduce tokens, how many tokens to retain, and how reduced token information should be processed. The proposed search space parameterizes layer activation, retention budget, and operator behavior, while the search policy optimizes task performance under one-sided budget and cost constraints. This formulation recovers representative hand-designed baselines as special cases and further discovers hybrid operators beyond isolated manual designs. Experiments on multimodal benchmarks show that the searched operators achieve competitive accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, especially under aggressive visual-token reduction. These results suggest that efficient multimodal inference can be reframed from manual operator design to differentiable operator search.

</details>


### [219] [DiffSlack: Learning under Nonlinear Inequality Constraints via Learnable Slack Variables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05247)
*Ziqian Wang,Chenxi Fang,Zhen Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiffSlack introduces a differentiable projection layer that enforces nonlinear inequality constraints by converting them to equalities with learnable slack variables, enabling end-to-end constrained predictions via a damped Gauss-Newton projection and a two-stage curriculum.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hard enforcement of nonlinear, coupled inequality constraints in neural networks is difficult, especially at scale, due to restrictive constraint set structures and computational overhead in existing methods.

Method: Inequalities are reformulated as equalities using learnable slack variables predicted by the network. The projection layer performs a damped Gauss-Newton projection onto the augmented feasible manifold, with a learnable warm-start from the slacks. A two-stage curriculum stabilizes training and improves constraint satisfaction.

Result: On vehicle path planning with ~200 nonlinear constraints (collision avoidance, curvature, waypoint spacing), DiffSlack yields higher planning success and stronger geometric constraint satisfaction under similar inference budgets compared to baselines. Ablation shows the hard projection reduces sensitivity to supervision quality. Closed-loop CARLA tracking and real-world vehicle experiments validate executability of generated trajectories.

Conclusion: DiffSlack provides a practical, scalable method to embed hard nonlinear inequality constraints into neural networks for engineering applications, combining differentiable projection with learnable slack variables and curriculum-based training.

Abstract: Enforcing nonlinear inequality constraints in neural networks remains challenging, especially when the output is subject to many coupled constraints. Existing hard constraint methods often impose structural restrictions on the constraint set or introduce substantial computational overhead for large-scale nonlinear problems. Here, we propose DiffSlack, a differentiable projection layer for nonlinear inequality-constrained neural prediction. DiffSlack reformulates inequalities as equalities with learnable slack variables, which are predicted as part of the augmented network output and provide a data-driven warm start for damped Gauss-Newton projection. The projection layer maps raw predictions onto the augmented feasible manifold while preserving end-to-end differentiability. A two-stage curriculum further stabilizes training and improves constraint satisfaction. We evaluate DiffSlack on vehicle path planning with 200 nonlinear inequality constraints from collision avoidance, curvature limits, and waypoint spacing. Compared with existing learning-based baselines, DiffSlack achieves a higher planning success rate and stronger geometric constraint satisfaction under a comparable inference budget. Ablation studies further show that the hard projection layer reduces sensitivity to supervision quality. Closed-loop tracking in CARLA and real-world vehicle experiments confirms the executability of the generated trajectories. These results demonstrate that DiffSlack provides a practical and scalable approach to embedding hard inequality constraints into neural networks for engineering applications.

</details>


### [220] [Alpha-RTL: Test-Time Training for RTL Hardware Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05253)
*Peilong Zhou,Zhirong Chen,Cangyuan Li,Haoyu Gao,Kaiyan Chang,Ziming Qu,Ying Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TTT-RTL is a per-design test-time training framework that enables an LLM to adapt to executable EDA feedback for RTL optimization, achieving substantial hardware-level improvements where previous approaches used frozen policies or pre-trained generators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between functional-correct RTL generation and physically optimized hardware by enabling per-design policy adaptation with real EDA feedback at test time.

Method: Per-design test-time training: sample candidate designs, verify via syntax checking and simulation, score with synthesis-derived PPA product, reuse high-reward variants through a PUCT-indexed design-state pool, update the policy with an entropic policy-gradient objective, and govern updates with an adaptive KL-budget controller based on reference KL, effective sample size, and reward saturation signals.

Result: On RTLLM v2.0 (Nangate 45nm), TTT-RTL reduces geometric-mean PPA product by 65.1% over a reference and beats the strongest frozen-policy baseline by 26.1%. On an industrial XuanTie C910 FPU (Sky130), it achieves a 59.4% ADP reduction. Ablations confirm that policy adaptation, state reuse, and KL-budget control each contribute to gains.

Conclusion: Test-time training with executable EDA feedback can push LLM-based RTL generation toward physically optimized hardware, beyond functional correctness.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown increasing promise in generating
  functionally correct register-transfer-level (RTL) hardware designs.
  Recent systems improve further through EDA-integrated reinforcement
  learning with syntax, simulation, and PPA rewards, but train a general
  RTL generator before deployment while test-time approaches search with
  a frozen policy. We instead perform reinforcement learning at test time,
  allowing the LLM policy to adapt to executable EDA feedback for the
  specific RTL problem at hand. We propose TTT-RTL, to our knowledge the
  first per-design test-time training framework that closes the loop
  between an LLM policy and an EDA pipeline for RTL optimization. TTT-RTL
  samples candidate implementations, verifies them through syntax checking
  and simulation, scores valid designs using synthesis-derived PPA product,
  reuses high-reward variants through a PUCT-indexed design-state pool,
  and updates the policy with an entropic policy-gradient objective. To
  stabilize policy updates under sparse or plateaued rewards, we introduce
  an adaptive KL-budget controller that adjusts the entropy constraint
  using reference KL, effective sample size, and reward saturation signals.
  On RTLLM v2.0 under Nangate 45nm, TTT-RTL reduces the geometric-mean
  PPA product by 65.1% over the reference, outperforming the strongest
  published frozen-policy agent baseline at 26.1%. On an industrial
  XuanTie C910 FPU leading-zero-anticipation unit under Sky130, TTT-RTL
  achieves a 59.4% ADP reduction, and ablations confirm that policy
  adaptation, state reuse, and KL-budget control each contribute. These
  results suggest that test-time training with executable EDA feedback can
  move LLM-based RTL generation beyond functional correctness toward
  physically optimized hardware.

</details>


### [221] [Flash-WAM: Modality-Aware Distillation for World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05254)
*Arman Akbari,Ci Zhang,Arash Akbari,Lin Zhao,Yixiao Chen,Weiwei Chen,Xuan Zhang,Geng Yuan,Yanzhi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Modality-aware step distillation for World-action Models (WAMs) enabling single-step inference by matching per-modality noise regimes, achieving >20x speedups with preserved task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce inference latency of joint video-action generation in WAMs, where video and action streams have mismatched noise schedules, preventing effective off-the-shelf distillation.

Method: Introduce Flash-WAM with a per-modality consistency function: linear-gradient-scaling for the action stream's low-noise regime and variance-preserving parametrization for the video stream's high-noise regime; grounded in a structural analysis of the consistency-function family under the consistency boundary condition; instantiate on LingBot-VA and RoboTwin 2.0 to achieve single-step inference per modality.

Result: Latency per chunk reduced from 8.1 seconds to 0.348 seconds on NVIDIA L40S (23×). Task success preserved on simulation benchmarks (85.5% RoboTwin 2.0; 95.7% LIBERO) and real-world average 60% on Unitree G1; naive consistency distillation drops to 24% at the same step budget.

Conclusion: Modality-aware consistency functions tailored to each modality’s noise regime enable real-time capable joint video-action generation in WAMs without substantial loss in performance; naive, single-modality distillation is insufficient in joint settings.

Abstract: World-action models (WAMs) jointly generate future video and robot actions through iterative diffusion, achieving strong performance on manipulation benchmarks but requiring tens of denoising steps, a cost that precludes real-time control. Step distillation has emerged as the natural remedy, but off-the-shelf methods break down in the joint video-action setting because video and action streams use different SNR-shifted noise schedules and reach training with substantially different marginal noise distributions, an asymmetry that single-modality distillation methods cannot accommodate. We introduce \textbf{Flash-WAM}, a modality-aware step-distillation framework inspired by consistency distillation that selects the consistency function for each modality to match its noise regime: a linear-gradient-scaling parametrization for the action stream's low-noise regime, paired with a variance-preserving parametrization for the video stream's high-noise regime, grounded in a structural analysis of the consistency-function family that characterizes the achievable gradient scaling under the consistency boundary condition. Instantiated on LingBot-VA, Flash-WAM compresses inference to a single step in each modality. On RoboTwin 2.0, this reduces per-chunk latency from $8.1$ seconds to $348$ ms on NVIDIA L40S, a $23{\times}$ speedup that enables real-time inference. Flash-WAM preserves task success on simulation benchmarks ($85.5\%$ RoboTwin 2.0, $95.7\%$ LIBERO) and substantially recovers real-world performance ($60\%$ average on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot), while naive consistency distillation drops to $24\%$ at the same step budget.

</details>


### [222] [Scaling Laws for Behavioral Foundation Models over User Event Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05257)
*Rickard Brüel Gabrielsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Empirical study of compute-aware scaling for two-part behavioral foundation models (embedder + decoder) on real interaction data; key findings on embedder size being compute-optimal, negative sampling, and metric-dependent scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap in scaling laws for behavioral foundation models and inform practical deployment by understanding compute/data tradeoffs and how evaluation metrics influence the optimal training recipe.

Method: Around 600 training runs spanning 10^15–10^19 FLOPs with a feature-based event embedder and a decoder-only transformer. Systematically vary four axes: (i) the two-part parameter split (embedder vs contextualizer/decoder), (ii) critical batch size, (iii) model/data allocation, and (iv) the number of sampled negatives after freezing the embedder. Experiments use real interaction data to assess compute efficiency and ranking quality.

Result: A small embedder (~2% of parameters) is compute-optimal across budgets because embedder parameters are costly per step and encounter many repeated items. Data efficiency is higher at low compute, but the data-to-parameter ratio (D/N) trends toward the Chinchilla heuristic with more compute. The training objective and deployed ranking metrics diverge and this divergence scales with compute: critical batch size, optimal negative count after freezing, and the agreement between loss and ranking quality depend on compute and metric. Larger compute budgets favor more negatives; by 10^19 FLOPs memory becomes the active constraint rather than FLOPs. Crucially, the evaluation metric is part of the scaling law, so changing it can alter the compute-optimal recipe.

Conclusion: In behavioral foundation models, the evaluation metric is entangled with compute scaling: the optimal training recipe depends on the deployed metric. This challenges straightforward transfer of language-model scaling laws and highlights memory and negative-sampling decisions as key levers that intertwine with the chosen evaluation criteria.

Abstract: Foundation models are increasingly trained on sequences of user actions in recommendation, payments, fraud, and commerce, but these models still lack the kind of compute calibration that scaling laws provide for language models. We study a common two-part behavioral-model architecture: a feature-based event embedder maps each multi-modal item to a vector, and a decoder-only transformer predicts the next event from the resulting sequence. Across roughly 600 runs on real interaction data, spanning $10^{15}$-$10^{19}$ training FLOPs, we jointly vary four deployment-relevant axes: the two-part parameter split, critical batch size, model/data allocation, and the number of sampled negatives used after freezing the embedder. A small embedder ($s^{\star}\!\approx\!2\%$ of parameters) is compute-optimal at every budget we test because embedder parameters are both more expensive per step and exposed to far more repeated items than contextualizer parameters. Compute-optimal training is data-heavy relative to text at low compute, but its $D/N$ ratio moves toward the Chinchilla heuristic as compute increases. The sampled training objective and deployed ranking metrics disagree in ways that themselves scale: critical batch size, optimal negative count after freezing, and the agreement between loss and ranking quality all shift with compute and with the chosen evaluation metric. For negative sampling, larger budgets increasingly prefer more negatives; by $10^{19}$ FLOPs the active constraint is candidate-axis memory rather than FLOPs. In behavioral foundation models, the evaluation metric is therefore part of the scaling law: changing it can change the compute-optimal recipe.

</details>


### [223] [Policy-Conditioned Counterfactual Credit for Verifiable Reinforcement Learning of Long-Horizon Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05263)
*Renwei Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CVT-RL introduces a constrained policy-gradient framework with dense verifiable rewards, intervention-based counterfactual credit, and validity gating to improve long-horizon RL for language agents. It shows improved task success, evidence fidelity, and reduced hacking on long-context QA tasks, with strong statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current process-based rewards are often correlational and do not quantify how intermediate steps contribute to final verified success under interventions; long-horizon language agents suffer belief drift, unsupported evidence chains, and shortcut actions.

Method: CVT-RL uses dense verifiable rewards, an intervention-validity gate, and a policy-conditioned counterfactual contribution (PCCC) estimator. Interventions include deletion, semantic substitution, evidence substitution, and tool-output perturbation. Continuations come from a frozen reference policy; a selection-adjusted doubly robust estimator augments the advantage. Belief control uses only prefix-observable labels; an augmented Lagrangian enforces constraints on unsupported claims, skipped verification, tool tampering, and unsafe calls.

Result: On ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, and web/tool tasks, CVT-RL raises average task success to 78.9% (from 71.8% and 75.4% baselines), boosts evidence F1 to 82.8 (from 78.9), and cuts hacking to 3.9% (from 7.2%). Independent human audits show 4.6% hacking (vs 8.1% baseline); detector-evasion attacks raise hacking to 7.1%. Statistical tests show p<0.01 after Holm correction for all primary metrics.

Conclusion: Carefully scoped counterfactual credit, combined with validity gating and verifiable constraints, supports a reproducible path toward more reliable long-horizon RL for language agents.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards improves reasoning and tool use, yet long-horizon language agents still learn unsupported evidence chains, belief drift, and shortcut actions that satisfy terminal checks. Existing process rewards are mostly correlational: they reward retrieval-, reflection-, or verification-like steps without estimating whether the step contributes to final verified success under a specified intervention. We propose CVT-RL, a constrained policy-gradient algorithm with dense verifiable rewards, intervention-validity gating, and a policy-conditioned counterfactual contribution (PCCC) estimator. Deletion, semantic substitution, evidence substitution, and tool-output perturbation define separate controlled interventions; continuations are sampled from a frozen reference policy, and a selection-adjusted doubly robust estimator augments the advantage. Belief control uses only prefix-observable labels, while an augmented Lagrangian constrains unsupported claims, skipped verification, tool tampering, and unsafe calls. On long-context QA, ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, and web/tool tasks, CVT-RL improves average task success from 71.8% for compute-matched non-causal RL and 75.4% for an information-matched counterfactual-process baseline to 78.9%, improves evidence F1 from 78.9 to 82.8 over the information-matched baseline, and reduces measured hacking from 7.2% to 3.9%. Independent human audit estimates 4.6% hacking for CVT-RL versus 8.1% for the information-matched baseline, and adaptive detector-evasion attacks raise hacking only to 7.1%. Stratified bootstrap and mixed-effects tests give p<0.01 after Holm correction for all primary metrics. Carefully scoped counterfactual credit, paired with validity gating, diagnostics, and verifiable constraints, provides a reproducible route toward more reliable long-horizon RL for language agents.

</details>


### [224] [REGEN: Reference-Guided Synthetic Multivariate Time Series Generation for Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05264)
*Moulik Gupta,Dhruv Kumar,Murari Mandal,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReGeN is a reference-guided generative pipeline that uses observed sequences as structural scaffolds to generate controllable, domain-grounded multivariate time series. It decomposes references into a phase-aligned periodic backbone, per-variable stochastic residuals via a deep-kernel Gaussian process, and lag-aware cross-variable dependencies through a structural causal model, with sampling at controllable temperature. The method achieves high data utility, substituting real data with minimal forecast degradation, and even outperforms real data in strongly periodic domains; foundation models pretrained on ReGeN data outperform those trained on prior-based or generic data, highlighting the importance of structural exploitation in low-data regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-data regimes for multivariate time series forecasting suffer from limited diverse sequences. Existing generators either produce domain-agnostic samples (prior-based) or rely on black-box supervision without explicit control of structure, limiting their usefulness for preserving periodicity, local variability, and cross-variable dynamics.

Method: Decompose each observed sequence into: (1) a phase-aligned periodic backbone capturing the main domain morphology; (2) per-variable stochastic residuals modeled with a deep-kernel Gaussian process; (3) lag-aware cross-variable dependencies injected via a structural causal model with fitted coupling coefficients. The components are sampled at controllable temperature to broaden distributional coverage while preserving structure.

Result: Synthetic data generated by ReGeN can substitute real sibling data with minimal forecasting degradation. In strongly periodic domains (e.g., traffic), ReGeN can even outperform the real source. A foundation model pretrained on ReGeN data outperforms models pretrained on prior-based or data-driven synthetic data.

Conclusion: How reference data are structurally exploited can be as important as the amount of data in low-data regimes. ReGeN demonstrates that controllable, structure-preserving synthesis guided by references yields higher data utility and better downstream performance than prior methods.

Abstract: Training robust multivariate time series forecasting models requires large, diverse corpora, yet many real-world domains provide only a handful of observed sequences. Existing generators fail to resolve this mismatch: prior-based approaches (e.g., CauKer, TimePFN) produce domain-agnostic samples, while data-driven methods (e.g., TimeGAN) treat references as black-box supervision, forfeiting explicit control over periodic structure, local variability, and cross-variable dynamics.
  We propose ReGeN, a reference-guided generative pipeline that treats observed sequences not as examples to imitate, but as structural scaffolds for controllable synthesis. ReGeN decomposes each reference into three interpretable components: a phase-aligned periodic backbone capturing dominant domain morphology; per-variable stochastic residuals modeled with a deep-kernel Gaussian process; and lag-aware cross-variable dependencies injected through a structural causal model with fitted coupling coefficients. Sampling these components at controllable temperature broadens distributional coverage while preserving domain-grounded structure.
  We show that ReGeN-generated data consistently substitutes for real sibling data with minimal forecasting degradation, and in strongly periodic domains such as traffic, can outperform the real source itself. We further show that a foundation model pretrained on ReGeN corpora outperforms those pretrained on prior-based and data-driven synthetic alternatives. This suggests that in low-data regimes, how reference data is structurally exploited can matter as much as how much data is available.

</details>


### [225] [Data-efficient flood depth prediction through domain-aware coreset selection and tabular foundation models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05265)
*Lipai Huang,Adithi Srinath,Manas Singh,Junwei Ma,Ali Mostafavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Domain-aware coreset construction enables data-efficient, watershed-transferable flood depth prediction using tabular foundation models without per-watershed training, achieving competitive accuracy with a tiny subset of data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Surrogate flood models require large labeled datasets per watershed and struggle to extrapolate to new watersheds; real-time prediction demands fast, transferable models.

Method: Stratify storms by return period and affected watershed; sample hexagonal grid (coreset) with a target-aware spatial selector; condition a tabular foundation model at inference; use only 0.7% of per-watershed training data for training.

Result: Mean R^2 of 0.663 across nine Houston watersheds, 98.5% of supervised reference (R^2=0.673); transfers to held-out watersheds without task-specific retraining; outperforms coreset-trained supervised baseline; on real storms, exceeds supervised reference on far out-of-distribution case and trails on mostly in-distribution case.

Conclusion: Domain-aware coreset construction makes data-efficient, transferable flood predictions feasible with tabular foundation models without per-watershed retraining.

Abstract: Near-real-time flood depth prediction demands surrogate models that are accurate, fast, and transferable across watersheds. Supervised surrogates can match physics-based simulators in accuracy but need millions of training rows per watershed and cannot extrapolate beyond their original mesh. We propose a domain-aware coreset construction pipeline that conditions a tabular foundation model at inference time. The pipeline stratifies storms by return period and most-affected watershed, then samples hexagons with a target-aware spatial selector. With 0.7% of the per-watershed training pool, the model attains a mean $R^2$ of 0.663 across nine Houston-area watersheds, within 98.5% of the supervised reference ($R^2$ = 0.673). It transfers to held-out watersheds without task-specific retraining, staying ahead of a coreset-trained supervised baseline. On real storms it exceeds the supervised reference on a far out-of-distribution case and trails it on a mostly in-distribution one. Domain-aware coreset construction lets tabular foundation models deliver data-efficient, watershed-transferable flood predictions without per-watershed training.

</details>


### [226] [Sharp Low-Degree Thresholds for Planted-vs-Planted Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05266)
*Anda Skeja,Daniel Gutiérrez Espinoza,Fiona Skerman,Alexander S. Wein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper establishes sharp low-degree testing thresholds for planted-vs-planted models (planted submatrix, planted dense subgraph), showing matching upper and lower bounds with the known low-degree recovery thresholds; it also reveals that weak testing lacks a sharp threshold and exhibits a smooth transition. A latent-variable expansion framework with pruning of non-signal contributions is developed to analyze these problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine precise statistical-computational boundaries for distinguishing between two structured planted mechanisms, and to unify low-degree analysis across planted-vs-planted models by connecting testing thresholds with recovery thresholds.

Method: Develop a framework based on low-degree polynomials and latent-variable expansions inspired by low-degree recovery; identify non-signal contributions and prune them; apply the framework to counting communities in planted submatrix and planted dense subgraph models to obtain sharp thresholds.

Result: Proved matching low-degree upper and lower bounds for counting communities in the planted submatrix and planted dense subgraph models; the sharp testing threshold matches the known low-degree recovery threshold up to a constant; weak testing exhibits a smooth transition rather than a sharp threshold.

Conclusion: The work closes the gap between recovery and testing in planted-vs-planted settings at the low-degree level and provides a practical framework with signal-pruning techniques to derive sharp thresholds across related models.

Abstract: We establish the first sharp thresholds for low-degree polynomial tests in planted-vs-planted settings, where the goal is to determine with vanishing error which of two structured planted mechanisms generated the observed data. We prove matching low-degree upper and lower bounds for counting communities in the planted submatrix and planted dense subgraph models. The resulting testing threshold coincides, down to the sharp constant, with the known low-degree recovery threshold. In contrast, the task of weak testing, where the goal is to outperform random guessing, does not have a sharp threshold but rather a smooth transition, which we identify. To prove our results, we develop a framework for planted-vs-planted testing that builds on a latent-variable expansion originating in low-degree recovery and employs new methods to identify and prune non-signal contributions.

</details>


### [227] [Learning Manifold and Itô Dynamics with Branched Neural Rough Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05272)
*Luke Thompson,Dai Shi,Lequan Lin,Junbin Gao,Andi Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Branched Neural Rough Differential Equations (B-NRDEs) extend NRDEs with a Hopf-algebraic framework that aligns driving algebras with the governing calculus, enabling Itô dynamics and manifold constraints via Grossman–Larson and Munthe-Kaas–Wright rooted trees, plus a branched signature-kernel objective for Itô-consistent training; demonstrated on volatility, rigid-body-like, and SPD covariance dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NRDEs are efficient for irregular sampling by summarizing the driver with the log-signature and advancing with the log-ODE method, but they rely on shuffle algebra and cannot access quadratic-variation terms or ordered covariant derivatives required by Itô dynamics and manifold-valued flows. A Hopf-algebraic reformulation (B-NRDEs) seeks to expose these terms and preserve manifold constraints.

Method: Introduce Branched NRDEs as a Hopf-algebraic framework. Recast the log-ODE step as geometric numerical integration on state-space manifolds. Match driving algebra to calculus: use Grossman–Larson rooted trees for Euclidean Itô dynamics, Munthe-Kaas–Wright planar rooted trees for ordered covariant derivatives on manifolds, and the shuffle algebra for Stratonovich. Develop intrinsic coarse-step dynamics that preserve manifold constraints. Propose a branched signature-kernel objective to reveal quadratic-variation terms during training for Itô-consistency. Apply to rough Bergomi volatility, sim-to-real SO(3) dynamics forecasting, and SPD covariance dynamics.

Result: The framework yields intrinsic coarse-step dynamics that exactly preserve manifold constraints and enables Itô-consistent law matching via the branched signature-kernel objective; demonstrated as a unified approach to stochastic and manifold-valued dynamics beyond Euclidean-Stratonovich settings (applications include rough Bergomi volatility, sim-to-real SO(3) dynamics, and SPD covariance dynamics).

Conclusion: B-NRDEs provide a unified algebraic–geometric extension of NRDEs that aligns the driving signals with the relevant calculus (Itô, covariant derivatives) and preserves manifold structure in coarse steps, expanding applicability to stochastic and manifold-valued dynamics.

Abstract: Neural rough differential equations (NRDEs) stay accurate under irregular sampling while taking far fewer integration steps than standard neural differential equations, summarising a finely sampled driver by its log-signature and advancing the hidden state over coarse intervals using the log-ODE method. This efficiency rests on the shuffle algebra, the algebraic counterpart of Stratonovich calculus. This reliance means NRDEs cannot expose the quadratic-variation terms Itô dynamics require, nor the ordered covariant derivatives that govern Itô flows on connection-equipped manifolds. Ameliorating this, we introduce Branched Neural Rough Differential Equations (B-NRDEs), a Hopf-algebraic framework that recasts the NRDE log-ODE step as geometric numerical integration on the state-space manifold, matching the driving algebra to the governing calculus: Grossman--Larson rooted trees for Euclidean Itô dynamics, Munthe-Kaas--Wright planar rooted trees for ordered covariant derivatives on manifolds, and the shuffle algebra in the classical Stratonovich case. This yields intrinsic coarse-step dynamics that exactly preserve manifold constraints. Finally, we introduce a branched signature-kernel objective to enable Itô-consistent law matching by making quadratic-variation terms visible during training. On rough Bergomi volatility, sim-to-real $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ dynamics forecasting, and SPD covariance dynamics, B-NRDEs offer a unified, effective approach to stochastic and manifold-valued dynamics beyond the Euclidean--Stratonovich setting.

</details>


### [228] [Anomaly Detection for Electro-Hydrostatic Actuators using LSTM Autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05274)
*Nehal Afifi,Abdelmonem Elhendawi,Felix Leitenberger,Nadine Piat,Sven Matthiesen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline univariate EHA sensor anomaly detection using reconstruction-based LSTM autoencoder shows high accuracy; applicable offline with potential online extension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EHA sensors generate large-volume, high-frequency data. Temporal dependencies are not well captured by traditional statistics or flat ML methods, leading to detection gaps and false alarms. There is a scarcity of comprehensive data-driven evaluations under varying operational conditions.

Method: Reconstruction-based LSTM autoencoder trained on univariate sensor signals (temperature and pressure) from a controlled test bench. Validation-set reconstruction-error distributions calibrate thresholds. Evaluation across fault-injection scenarios using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, plus sensitivity analyses across operating conditions.

Result: Average accuracy 99.0%, precision up to 100%, recall 90.2–99.6%, F1-score 93.1–99.8% across evaluated sensors, indicating high detection sensitivity and very low false alarms.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of data-driven offline anomaly detection for EHAs. Future work: adapt framework for online (real-time) operation.

Abstract: Electro-Hydrostatic Actuators (EHAs) are widely used in aerospace and industrial systems, where timely detection of sensor anomalies is essential to ensure safe and reliable operation. However, the large volume and high sampling frequency of EHA sensor data pose challenges for accurate and efficient anomaly detection. Conventional statistical and classical machine-learning methods such as Z-score, Interquartile Range (IQR), Median Absolute Deviation (MAD), Isolation Forest, Gaussian Mixture, and k-means often fail to capture the temporal dependencies inherent in EHA signals, resulting in limited detection accuracy and elevated false-alarm rates. Furthermore, systematic evaluations of data-driven anomaly detection approaches for EHA systems remain scarce, particularly under varying operational conditions. This study presents an offline anomaly-detection framework for univariate EHA sensor signals, focusing on temperature and pressure data collected from a controlled test bench. The method employs a reconstruction-based Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) autoencoder, calibrated and evaluated using validation-set reconstruction-error distributions. Performance is assessed across multiple fault-injection scenarios using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, complemented by sensitivity analyses under varying operating conditions. The LSTM autoencoder achieved an average accuracy of 99.0\%, precision up to 100\%, recall between 90.2\% and 99.6\%, and F1-scores from 93.1\% to 99.8\%, demonstrating high detection sensitivity and a very low false-alarm rate across all evaluated sensors. These results highlight the feasibility of data-driven offline anomaly detection for EHAs. Future work will focus on adapting the developed framework for an online (real-time) environment.

</details>


### [229] [Agentic Monte Carlo: Simulating Reinforcement Learning for Black-Box Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05296)
*Dae Yon Hwang,Raunaq Suri,Valentin Villecroze,Anthony L. Caterini,Jesse C. Cresswell,Noël Vouitsis,Brendan Leigh Ross*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Agentic Monte Carlo (AMC) enables principled RL-style optimization of black-box LLM agents by sampling from the trajectory-posterior rather than updating the model; it uses Sequential Monte Carlo guided by a learned value function, keeping the LLM fixed and improving over prompting baselines and GRPO with more compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable RL-like optimization for API-only, black-box LLM agents where parameter-level training is not possible; leverage the RL-Bayesian inference equivalence to optimize behavior at test time.

Method: Model the optimal policy as a posterior over trajectories with the fixed LLM as the prior. Apply Sequential Monte Carlo to sample trajectories from this posterior while learning a value function to steer proposals. Do not modify the underlying LLM; perform computation-heavy sampling at test time.

Result: AMC yields significant improvements over prompting baselines on three AgentGym environments and even surpasses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) when the test-time compute is scaled.

Conclusion: AMC demonstrates the feasibility of principled RL-style optimization for black-box LLM agents at test time by sampling-based inference, offering a practical alternative to retraining or parameter-tuning of proprietary models.

Abstract: LLM agents operate in two distinct regimes: open-weight agents amenable to reinforcement learning (RL) and black-box agents whose behaviour must be controlled purely at test time. Although black-box agents are often backed by state-of-the-art proprietary LLMs, API-only access precludes parameter-level optimization, rendering most RL methods inapplicable. To address this limitation, we turn to a known equivalence between RL and Bayesian inference. We propose Agentic Monte Carlo (AMC) to directly sample from the optimal policy of a black-box agent rather than training it through RL. The optimal policy is a posterior over trajectories whose prior we define as the fixed black-box LLM agent. We employ Sequential Monte Carlo to sample from this posterior by learning a value function to steer the agent while leaving the underlying black-box model unchanged. We validate AMC on three diverse environments from the AgentGym benchmark, demonstrating significant improvements over prompting baselines and even outperforming Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) as we scale the test-time compute of our method. AMC demonstrates the feasibility of performing principled RL-style optimization of black-box LLM agents. Code is available at https://github.com/layer6ai-labs/Agentic-Monte-Carlo

</details>


### [230] [Statistically Reliable LLM-Based Ranking Evaluation via Prediction-Powered Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05308)
*Abhishek Divekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRECISE/PPI yields unbiased, bias-corrected ranking-metric estimates by fusing a small human-labeled set with a large LLM-judged set, enabling efficient handling of hierarchical metrics and showing variance reduction and business impact in ESCI and production.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable, bias-free estimation of ranking metrics when using LLM judgments, by leveraging a small human-labeled subset to correct bias and reduce variance.

Method: Integrate a small human-labeled subset with a large LLM-judged set under Prediction-Powered Inference to obtain unbiased estimates (PPI). Extend to hierarchical metrics (e.g., Precision@K) by reducing output-space from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K). Validate on ESCI with hybrid judgments (30 human + Claude 3 Sonnet) and deploy in production to rank three variants using 100 human labels and domain-expert annotation.

Result: PPI yields provable unbiasedness regardless of the LLM's error profile. Empirically, on ESCI, precision@4 standard error drops from 4.45 to 3.50 (21% relative). In production, the framework correctly identified the best variant and AB-testing showed +407 bps daily sales.

Conclusion: The PRECISE/PPI framework delivers unbiased, variance-reduced estimates for ranking metrics, scalable to hierarchical metrics, with demonstrated practical impact in both benchmark and production settings.

Abstract: With PRECISE, we extended Prediction-Powered Inference to produce bias-corrected estimates of ranking evaluation metrics by combining a small human-labeled set with a large LLM-judged set. PPI is provably unbiased regardless of the LLM judge's error profile. We make it applicable to hierarchical metrics like Precision@K, where annotations are per-document but the metric is per-query, by reducing the output-space computation from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K). On the ESCI benchmark, augmenting 30 human annotations with Claude 3 Sonnet judgments reduces the standard error of Precision@4 estimates from 4.45 to 3.50 (a 21% relative reduction).
