In a production system, our framework correctly identified the best of three system variants from 100 human labels and 2 hours of domain-expert annotation; A/B testing confirmed this ranking with +407 bps in daily sales.

</details>


### [231] [Multimarginal flow matching with optimal transport potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05327)
*Raghav Kansal,David Crair,Nghia Nguyen,Scott Pope,Bradley Parry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces OTP-FM, a multimarginal flow matching method that uses potentials in dynamic OT to softly enforce intermediate marginals, yielding a simulation-free, flexible, efficient algorithm with state-of-the-art results on diverse real-world datasets and releasing code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many applications, intermediate observed marginals constrain transport between endpoints. A multimarginal formulation of flow matching leveraging dynamic OT potentials can better capture temporal evolution and improve learning of transport maps.

Method: Extend conditional FM learning targets by incorporating potential terms from dynamic OT into the action, enabling a simulation-free algorithm for multimarginal FM (OTP-FM) that can steer flows toward intermediate marginals without simulating trajectories.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance and training efficiency on heterogeneous datasets (single-cell RNA-seq, oceanographic, meteorological), validating the effectiveness and practicality of OTP-FM; code provided."

Conclusion: OTP-FM offers a flexible, efficient, and scalable framework for multimarginal flow matching by integrating OT potentials into FM, improving temporal transport modeling and enabling broad applicability across scientific domains.

Abstract: Flow matching (FM) has emerged as a powerful framework for learning dynamic transport maps between two empirical distributions. However, less explored is the setting with intermediate observed marginals that can help constrain the flows between the endpoints. This "multimarginal" regime is central to modeling temporal evolution in dynamical systems in many scientific domains that can sample sequential distributions. We tackle this problem with a novel approach that leverages the connection between FM and dynamic optimal transport (OT), softly steering the flow towards the intermediate marginals through potential terms in the dynamic OT action. By extending the conditional FM learning target to incorporate these potentials, we derive an efficient, simulation-free algorithm for multimarginal FM that offers considerable flexibility in the spatiotemporal dynamics of the learned flows. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and training efficiency of OT-potential FM (OTP-FM) on diverse single-cell RNA sequencing, oceanographic, and meteorological datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/Bexorg-Inc/OTP-FM.

</details>


### [232] [A prism hierarchy of learning regimes in large linear autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05335)
*Eugene Golikov,Yaroslav Gusev,Dmitry Yarotsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometrical, regime-based analysis of large weight-tied linear autoencoders. Five extreme regimes map to faces of a triangular prism; explicit train and population loss dynamics derived for four regimes, with strong empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically enumerate all qualitatively distinct extreme learning regimes for a concrete nonlinear, weight-tied autoencoder model, beyond standard limiting regimes.

Method: Develop a loss-expansion hierarchy and link each extreme regime to a face of a triangular prism. Perform gradient-flow analysis to derive closed-form evolutions for train and population losses in the large-data, small-data, mean-field, and narrow-latent regimes, and validate against experiments.

Result: Explicit expressions for the train and population loss evolutions are obtained for regimes 1–4; the theoretical predictions show very good agreement with empirical results.

Conclusion: Provides a geometrical and principled taxonomy of extreme learning regimes for the model, enabling tractable analysis of gradient dynamics and aligning well with observed behavior; regime 5 (‘free’) is identified but not explicitly solved in the abstract.

Abstract: Theoretical studies of machine learning models commonly consider different limiting regimes in which the learning dynamics of gradient descent becomes theoretically tractable. It is, however, desirable to have a systematically obtained picture of all qualitatively different extreme learning regimes for a particular type of models. In this paper we propose such a picture for large weight-tied linear autoencoders characterized by input and latent dimensions, initialization magnitude, and training set size. This model is nonlinear in the weights and its gradient flow does not have a general theoretical solution. We show that at the level of the formal loss-expansion hierarchy, its extreme regimes are naturally associated with faces of a triangular prism. In particular, there are five basic extreme regimes associated with the 2-faces of the prism: (1) large-data, (2) small-data, (3) mean-field, (4) narrow-latent, and (5) free. For regimes (1,2,3,4), we derive explicit expressions for both train and population limiting loss evolutions under gradient flow, obtaining very good agreement with experimental results.

</details>


### [233] [PJ-RoPE: A Fourier-Jet-Affine Position Space for Relative Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05345)
*Yaobo Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified, learnable relative-position representation PJ-RoPE that subsumes RoPE, Jordan-RoPE, and ALiBi; uses Fourier-Jet-Affine formalism, interpretable as a finite difference module; introduces adaptive sector diagnostics and LC/rapidity coordinates; validated via probing and task-based experiments (language, music) showing sector selection and stability-tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify multiple relative-position encodings within a single learnable framework and understand how task demands select regions of the relative-position space; to enable high-order jet representations with stable, interpretable behavior and to study affine/recency interactions.

Method: Propose PJ-RoPE: Fourier-Jet-Affine relative attention; optional Poincare-type reading; algebraic interpretation as finite constant-coefficient difference module with lag-shift roots mapping to RoPE characters and jets; separation of PJ-bias kernels from exact PJ-rotary transforms; adaptive sector diagnostics; LC/rapidity coordinates for stabilization; controlled probes and various experimental setups (controlled probes, small language, music-token streams).

Result: Demonstrates sector containment and selection; affine/recency boundary identified; music-token streams show LC/affine variants remain strong with high-order corrections; LC diagnostics reveal a scale-stability gain coupled to phase-resolution loss.

Conclusion: PJ-RoPE offers a cohesive, interpretable relative-position framework that unifies prior schemes, provides diagnostics to study sector selection, and reveals favorable stability properties with affine/recency representations, plus clear high-order corrections in certain domains; suggests practical guidance on using LC coordinates and adaptive sector diagnostics.

Abstract: We unify RoPE's Fourier phase, Jordan-RoPE's finite jets, and ALiBi's affine recency into a single learnable relative-position space, and study which regions of this space are selected by different tasks. PJ-RoPE is a Fourier-Jet-Affine formulation for relative attention, with an optional Poincare-type reading as the affine completion of a homogeneous Fourier-jet positional representation. Algebraically, the same primitives form a finite constant-coefficient difference module: simple roots of the lag-shift operator give Fourier/RoPE characters, repeated nonzero roots give Jordan/Fourier jets, and the repeated unit root gives ALiBi-like affine recency.
  The framework separates scalar PJ-bias kernels from exact PJ-rotary feature transforms, introduces adaptive sector diagnostics, and uses LC/rapidity coordinates to stabilize high-order jets. Controlled probes verify sector containment and selection; small language runs expose an affine/recency boundary; music-token streams provide the clearest case where LC/affine variants remain strong while carrying measurable high-order corrections; and LC diagnostics show a scale-stability gain coupled to phase-resolution loss.

</details>


### [234] [Mamba-Assisted Non-Markovian Closure for Reduced-Order Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05371)
*Zhi-Feng Wei,Saad Qadeer,Panos Stinis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAC: a memory-aware closure for reduced-order models learned as a sequence model, integrated with ROM dynamics to yield improved long-time accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-Markovian closure terms from unresolved variables hinder ROM accuracy and stability. Grounded in Mori–Zwanzig, the closure is a memory functional; the paper reframes closure modeling as sequence modeling to capture history.

Method: Train a Mamba-based sequence model to predict the closure from the resolved trajectory using a seq2seq/convolutional form; deploy it autoregressively with a recurrent form, coupled with reduced-order governing equations via a numerical integrator; enables efficient long-trajectory training and constant per-step inference.

Result: MAC substantially outperforms Markovian ROM, a GRU-based sequence model, and the Wilks method in predictive accuracy and long-time rollout stability on the viscous Burgers’ equation and the chaotic two-scale Lorenz-96 system.

Conclusion: MAC demonstrates effective non-Markovian closure learning for ROMs, leveraging dual state-space representations to enable efficient training and stable long-time integration; memory effects are captured more accurately than competing approaches.

Abstract: Reduced-order modeling of high-dimensional dynamical systems is often hindered by the non-Markovian closure term that represents the effect of unresolved variables on the resolved dynamics. Inspired by the Mori--Zwanzig formalism, in which the closure takes the form of a memory functional of the resolved trajectory, we recast closure modeling as a sequence modeling problem and propose the Mamba-Assisted Closure (MAC) framework: a Mamba-based sequence model, trained to predict the closure from the resolved trajectory, is coupled with the reduced-order governing equations through a numerical integrator to advance the resolved variables in time. A key feature of the framework is its exploitation of the dual representation of state-space models -- the model is trained in a sequence-to-sequence fashion via the convolutional form, and deployed for step-by-step autoregressive rollout via the recurrent form, yielding both efficient long-trajectory training and constant per-step inference cost. On the viscous Burgers' equation and the chaotic two-scale Lorenz '96 system, the MAC model substantially outperforms the Markovian reduced-order model, the GRU-based sequence model, and the Wilks method in predictive accuracy and long-time rollout stability.

</details>


### [235] [Evidence-Guided Neural Architecture Selection under Uncertainty for Subject-Specific Blood Glucose Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05373)
*Md Azharul Islam,Dwyer Deighan,Tarunraj Singha,Danial Faghihi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EVIDENT is an evidence-based framework for neural architecture discovery in time-series forecasting that uses Bayesian training and validation-driven ranking to select the smallest model that satisfies a performance criterion, with plausible-weighted ensembles when multiple architectures are competitive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural architecture selection is poorly solved in data-limited, noisy, and heterogeneous time-series contexts; conventional heuristics risk under/over-parameterization and poor generalization.

Method: EVIDENT combines Bayesian training, evidence-based ranking, and task-specific validation to explore a candidate architecture pool and identify the lowest-capacity model meeting a predefined validation criterion. Applied to temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) for individualized blood glucose forecasting in type 1 diabetes; uses plausible-weighted ensembles when multiple architectures are competitive; compared against a random-search baseline.

Result: EVIDENT systematically rejects under- and over-parameterized TCN architectures, identifies models that generalize to unseen patients, and—when architectures are competitive—produces improved ensemble predictions. Smaller architectures exhibit more consistent forecasting performance on unseen patients and outperform random-search baselines.

Conclusion: EVIDENT provides a principled strategy for neural architecture discovery that yields reliable, generalizable models in data-limited and heterogeneous settings and supports robust, ensemble predictions when multiple architectures are viable.

Abstract: Reliable neural architecture selection is an open challenge in time-series forecasting under limited, noisy, and heterogeneous data, where standard heuristic architecture design and validation approaches fail to ensure accurate and reliable prediction and generalization. We propose EVIDENT (EVidence-based IDEntification of Neural archiTectures), a framework for architecture selection that integrates Bayesian training, evidence-based ranking, and task-specific validation under uncertainty. The framework explores the candidate architecture pool and identifies the lowest-capacity model that satisfies a prescribed validation criterion. We demonstrate this method using temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) for individualized blood glucose forecasting in type 1 diabetes patients. The results show that EVIDENT systematically rejects both under- and over-parameterized TCN architectures on population-level diabetes data, while identifying models that generalize reliably to unseen patients. When multiple architectures are competitive, the framework further supports plausibility-weighted ensemble predictions that enhance predictive performance. Compared with a random-search baseline, EVIDENT identified smaller architectures with more consistent forecasting performance on unseen patients. These findings establish EVIDENT as a strategy to neural architecture discovery, enabling reliable model selection for high-consequence forecasting in data-limited and heterogeneous settings.

</details>


### [236] [SHALA-LLM: Smartly Handling Ambiguous Labels in Aligning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05376)
*Jingyao Wu,Ashley Wang,Keane Ong,Paul Pu Liang,Rosalind Picard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHALA-LLM introduces a reinforcement learning framework to learn from annotator distributions for ambiguous labeling in LLM alignment, improving both distributional agreement and classification accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotator disagreement contains information critical for robust alignment; traditional single-label optimization discards this signal, risking loss of uncertainty and performance on ambiguity-sensitive tasks.

Method: Proposes SHALA-LLM, a RL-based framework that learns directly from label distributions and prioritizes ambiguous samples during optimization; evaluated on ambiguity-sensitive NLI and ER benchmarks.

Result: Significant improvement in distributional alignment (JS distance reduced by up to 62.1%) and classification performance (F1 up to 16.7%).

Conclusion: Modeling annotator disagreement as informative enhances LLM alignment and generalization on tasks with inherent label ambiguity.

Abstract: Many human-centered tasks, including natural language inference (NLI) and emotion recognition (ER), have multiple plausible interpretations, leading to label ambiguity and challenging disagreements across human annotators. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in real-world settings, faithfully modeling such ambiguity is essential to identify contested inputs, preserve variability in ambiguous cases, and capture the full distribution of human judgments. Yet, existing LLM alignment approaches have predominantly assumed a single correct label, excluding annotator disagreement during optimization. Instead of treating this ambiguity as noise, we show how to treat it as information that improves model behavior through a new algorithm called SMARTLY HANDLING AMBIGUOUS LABELS IN ALIGNING LLMS (SHALA-LLM). This reinforcement learning framework provides a new way for LLMs to learn directly from annotator distributions while dynamically prioritizing highly ambiguous samples during optimization. Experiments on ambiguity-sensitive NLI and ER benchmarks, including ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, and MSP-Podcast, demonstrate that SHALA-LLM improves agreement with annotator label distributions, e.g. on ChaosNLI, it reduces Jensen-Shannon Distance by up to 62.1%. At the same time, SHALA-LLM improves F1 by up to 16.7%, showing that modeling annotator disagreement can also strengthen classification performance.

</details>


### [237] [Pattern Selectivity is Not Task-Causal Structure: A Cross-Architecture Mechanistic Study of Composed-Task Circuits in 1B-Class Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05378)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A cross-model, cross-task test of a single screen-and-ablate causal methodology finds no consistent primary causal screen across model families; same task uses different attention-patterns across models, motivating a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy and a falsifiable MoE-specific substrate hypothesis with per-model predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the generalizability and falsifiability of mechanistic attributions derived from attention circuit screening and ablation across diverse model families and pipelines.

Method: Apply a single recipe: identify attention-head circuits by task-pattern selectivity, then verify via causal ablation against a matched-random null. Port the approach across four tasks and three 1B-class models from distinct training pipelines, each with ten seeds per cell, yielding 12 task-model cells. Develop a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy (primary/secondary/correlate/interferer/null) with thresholds, and use a companion spectral participation-ratio signal to contextualize specialization. Propose a falsifiable MoE-specific hypothesis about a foundational previous-token substrate and predictions for other MoE LMs.

Result: Across 12 task-model cells, no two share the same primary causal screen at comparable effect sizes. The same task is implemented via different attention-pattern types across models. All five taxonomy categories appear in the data, indicating diverse mechanistic routes to the same behavioral capability.

Conclusion: The methodology appears honest and stage-setting: task-specific findings require the combination of a task-pattern screen and per-model causal verification. The authors propose testable predictions for other MoE models and emphasize that the spectral signal is a general indicator of specialized computation rather than a standalone claim of locality.

Abstract: We test whether a single screen-and-ablate recipe -- identify attention-head circuits by task-pattern selectivity, then verify by causal ablation against a matched-random null -- produces consistent mechanistic claims across model families. The recipe ports across pipelines; the specific circuit it identifies does not. Across four composed tasks (indirect-object identification, greater-than, successor sequences, variable binding) and three 1B-class language models from distinct training pipelines (Pythia 1B / Pile / dense; OLMo 1B / DCLM / dense; OLMoE 1B-7B / DCLM / mixture-of-experts), we run a unified protocol with the matched-random null sampled across ten seeds per cell. The resulting 12 (task, model) cells contain no two that share the same primary causal screen at comparable effect size: the same task, with the same behavioral capability, is implemented through different attention-pattern types across models.
  We introduce a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy -- primary cause, secondary cause, correlate, interferer, null -- with quantitative thresholds, and show that all five outcomes appear in the panel. We propose a falsifiable hypothesis: the MoE model in our panel builds composed-task circuits on top of a foundational previous-token positional substrate (the prev-token-circuit ablation is the strongest causal screen on 3 of 4 tasks for OLMoE 1B-7B), with the IOI exception consistent with IOI being a final-position name-copying task whose structure directly probes a different pattern. The hypothesis comes with explicit predictions for other MoE language models.
  We frame the methodology honestly: the spectral participation-ratio signal from the companion methodology paper is a general indicator of specialized computation; what makes a finding task-specific is the task-pattern screen plus a per-model causal verification.

</details>


### [238] [Generalized TV--$\ell_p$ Structured Priors for Bayesian $T_1$ Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05381)
*Disi Lin,Martin Berggren,Tommy Löfstedt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TV–ℓ_p priors extend structured spatial penalties into a Bayesian regression framework for T1 mapping, enabling uncertainty quantification via NUTS; they yield concentrated posteriors and improved spatial coherence compared to ML and other priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for spatially coherent, uncertainty-aware T1 maps by introducing a proper, flexible prior that enforces smooth variations while preserving structures.

Method: Introduce a TV–ℓ_p prior within Bayesian regression; prove propriety and well-definedness; sample/posterior via No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS); compare with uniform, Gamma, and bounded TV priors; evaluate on synthetic brain/cardial T1 maps and real breast data.

Result: Posterior densities become more concentrated, indicating reduced uncertainty; lower variance and smaller negative bias; improved spatial coherence and more reliable estimates; robust uncertainty quantification in T1 mapping.

Conclusion: The TV–ℓ_p prior provides a principled, effective way to incorporate spatial structure and uncertainty into T1 mapping, outperforming alternative priors in coherence and reliability of estimates.

Abstract: We propose an extended family of structured spatial priors that incorporates the total variation (TV) function with $\ell_p$ norms. The prior is proven to be proper and incorporated into a Bayesian regression framework to enable uncertainty quantification in $T_1$ mapping, with posterior inference performed using the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). This TV--$\ell_p$ construction is proven to constitute a well-defined family of prior distributions, and it naturally enforces spatial consistency and smooth variations in the estimated parameter maps. The method was evaluated in comparison to maximum-likelihood estimation and several Bayesian alternative priors based on the uniform, Gamma, and bounded TV priors. The evaluation includes experiments on synthetic brain and cardiac $T_1$ mapping datasets, as well as a real in-vivo breast $T_1$ mapping dataset. The results show that the TV--$\ell_p$ prior yields more concentrated posterior densities, indicating reduced uncertainty. It also consistently achieves lower variance and smaller (negative) bias, leading to more reliable estimates. Overall, embedding a TV-based structured penalty along with $\ell_p$ norms in a prior in a Bayesian model improves spatial coherence in $T_1$ maps and enhances uncertainty quantification, offering a robust approach for $T_1$ mapping with uncertainties.

</details>


### [239] [Trust, but Don't Verify: Epistemic Blind Spots in LLM Source Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05403)
*Rohan N. Pradhan,Steve Goley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LMs detect fabricated statistics in isolation but do not use numeric validity during multi-source synthesis; source weighting is driven by a 'methodology-register' signal, not by numerical correctness, leading to epistemic misalignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether LMs evaluate evidence quality or merely aggregate signals when synthesizing information from multiple sources, and whether they can verify numeric claims across domains.

Method: Evaluate five models (Claude, Qwen, OLMo) across three domains. Test detection of fabricated statistics in isolation, then assess behavior during multi-source synthesis. Use mechanistic analyses (causal tracing, linear probes, component attribution). Assess prompting mitigations (oracle checklist) and analyze post-training pipelines for impact on numeric verification.

Result: Isolation: models reliably identify fabricated methodology (0.76–1.00). Synthesis: numeric validity signals are not leveraged; numeric claims are weighted similarly regardless of validity. A methodology-register representation guides source influence (probe AUC 0.83–0.92); numeric-validity signals decodable in isolation but suppressed during synthesis. Prompting mitigations cause blanket skepticism rather than selective verification; post-training pipelines reinforce shortcutting strategies.

Conclusion: There is an epistemic alignment in deployment: models may possess latent numeric verification capabilities but fail to deploy them during synthesis, instead aligning with source credibility signals. This affects how evidence is aggregated and has implications for how we design prompts and training pipelines to encourage numeric verification over superficial cues.

Abstract: Language models increasingly act as epistemic proxies, synthesizing evidence from multiple sources to inform decisions. Whether they evaluate the quality of that evidence, or merely aggregate it based on surface presentation, remains poorly understood. We show that models possess the capability to detect fabricated statistics (correct identification rates of 0.76-1.00 for methodology in isolation) but do not recruit this capability during multi-source synthesis, producing similar numeric estimates whether the statistics are fabricated or valid. Specifically, source influence is governed by a methodology-register gate that responds to the distributional register of analytical text but not to numeric validity: for example, statistically impossible confidence intervals receive the same weight as valid ones. The behavioral dissociation replicates across five models from three families (Claude, Qwen, OLMo) and three professional domains. Mechanistic analyses, including causal tracing, linear probes, and component-level attribution, converge on the same account: the model encodes and causally uses a methodology-register representation that transfers across domains (probe AUC 0.83-0.92), while numeric-validity signals, decodable in isolation, are suppressed to chance during multi-source synthesis. Prompting-based mitigations, even an oracle checklist naming the exact statistical checks, produce blanket skepticism rather than selective discernment, and the post-training pipelines we examine reinforce the stylistic shortcut without building numeric verification. Unlike sycophancy, which tracks user preference, this failure tracks whether a source presents as analytically credible, not whether its claims are internally consistent. We term this epistemic alignment: like preference and safety alignment, the question is not capability but deployment.

</details>


### [240] [CausalPOI: Spatio-Temporal Graph-Based Causal Modeling for Cold-Start POI Check-in Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05413)
*Zhaoqi Zhang,Miao Xie,Yi Li,Linyou Cai,Siqiang Luo,Gao Cong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalPOI introduces a causal spatio-temporal framework for cold-start POI check-in forecasting, using a Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph and structurally aligned treatment/control graphs to model semantic/spatial interactions and causal effects; it outperforms baselines on SafeGraph datasets; code is available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current spatio-temporal GNNs for POI forecasting rely on proximity-based graphs and correlation-driven modeling, neglecting functional dependencies among POIs and the causal impact of urban interventions. Cold-start POIs lack historical data, necessitating a model that captures temporal evolution, functional interactions, and causal effects to support urban planning and decision-making.

Method: Construct a Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph to model semantic and spatial relationships between POIs. Build structurally aligned treatment and control graphs to simulate factual and counterfactual scenarios. Use spatio-temporal causal representation learning to forecast future check-ins and estimate causal effects of interventions.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world SafeGraph data show that CausalPOI significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in spatio-temporal forecasting, semantic interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation.

Conclusion: CausalPOI provides a more interpretable and actionable framework for urban intervention analysis, supporting data-driven urban planning and commercial decision-making. Source code is available at GitHub.

Abstract: As urban environments continue to evolve rapidly, accurately modeling the dynamic behaviour of Points of Interest is essential for supporting data-driven urban planning and commercial decision-making. While recent advancements in spatio-temporal graph learning have improved POI forecasting, most methods rely on proximity-based graphs and correlation-driven modeling, which overlook the functional dependencies between POIs and fail to capture the causal effects of urban interventions. In this paper, we introduce a novel research problem -- cold-start POI check-in forecasting, which aims to predict the future check-in pattern of a newly introduced POI, by modeling its temporal evolution and functional interactions with nearby POIs in a structured urban spatial context. To address these challenges, we propose CausalPOI, a spatio-temporal graph-based causal representation learning framework. CausalPOI leverages Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph to model semantic and spatial relationships between POIs, and constructs structurally aligned treatment and control graphs to simulate factual and counterfactual scenarios. Extensive experiments on real-world SafeGraph datasets demonstrate that CausalPOI significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across the board, validating its effectiveness in spatio-temporal forecasting, semantic interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation, providing a more interpretable and actionable foundation for urban intervention analysis. Source code is available at Github.

</details>


### [241] [Selective-Advantage Entropy-Adaptive Horizon GRPO: Asymmetric Token-Level Discounting for Efficient Reinforcement Learning of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05434)
*Chirag Chawla,Rohan Charudatt Salvi,Madhav S. Baidya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive-Horizon GRPO and Selective-Advantage AH-GRPO extend GRPO with entropy-based, token-level discounting and selective application to negative-advantage trajectories, yielding improved stability and competitive Pass@1 on GSM8K across Qwen models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO treats all tokens and rollouts symmetrically, which can cause entropy collapse and unstable training in structured generation tasks; introducing adaptive horizon and selective discounting aims to preserve gradient signal for correct solutions while dampening uncertain or negative trajectories.

Method: Propose AH-GRPO with a cumulative entropy-based discount (alpha=0.5) to weight token-level policy gradients, reducing the effective horizon under uncertainty. Propose SA-AH-GRPO applying this discount only to negative-advantage rollouts. Evaluate GRPO (alpha=0), AH-GRPO (alpha=0.5), SA-AH-GRPO (alpha=0.5) on GSM8K using Qwen 2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen 2.5-3B-Instruct (LoRA). Compare Pass@1 and training variance across steps (e.g., step 30 and 180).

Result: SA-AH-GRPO achieves Pass@1 = 0.858 (step 30) and 0.846 (step 180) on the 3B model, with training variance 0.0246 (3.6x lower than GRPO) and peak accuracy matching GRPO. On the 1.5B model, SA-AH-GRPO peaks at Pass@1 = 0.686, improving over zero-shot baseline 0.637.

Conclusion: Asymmetric, entropy-based discounting preserves gradients on correct solutions, prevents entropy collapse, and stabilizes training. SA-AH-GRPO provides a principled inductive bias for RL with verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks, delivering strong performance with reduced variance."

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) has emerged as an effective reinforcement-learning algorithm for aligning language models on reasoning tasks, but it treats every token position and every sampled rollout symmetrically. We introduce two complementary extensions: (i) Adaptive-Horizon GRPO (AH-GRPO), which weights each token's policy gradient using a cumulative entropy-based discount that reduces the effective horizon when the model is uncertain, and (ii) Selective-Advantage AH-GRPO (SA-AH-GRPO), which applies this discounting only to negative-advantage rollouts, leaving positive-advantage, successful trajectories unattenuated. We evaluate standard GRPO with alpha = 0, AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5, and SA-AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5 on the GSM8K mathematical reasoning benchmark using both Qwen 2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen 2.5-3B-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA. On the 3B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves Pass@1 = 0.858 at its peak at step 30 and maintains 0.846 at 180 steps, with training variance reduced to 0.0246, a 3.6 times reduction relative to GRPO while matching its peak accuracy. On the 1.5B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves a peak Pass@1 of 0.686, improving over the zero-shot baseline of 0.637. Our analysis shows that asymmetric discounting preserves the full gradient signal on correct solutions, prevents entropy collapse, and substantially stabilises training, suggesting a principled inductive bias for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks.

</details>


### [242] [DP-MacAdam: Differentially Private Mechanism with Adaptive Clipping and Adaptive Momentum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05435)
*Naima Tasnim,Lalitha Sankar,Oliver Kosut*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DP-MacAdam unifies adaptive clipping with adaptive momentum for DP training by sharing the same gradient mean/variance estimates across clipping and momentum, yielding bias-free variance estimation and improved utility over DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam without manual clipping threshold tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed gradient clipping in DP-SGD limits utility and requires manual tuning. Existing adaptive clipping (AdaClip) adapts clipping but does not use the estimates for momentum. DP-Adam uses moment estimates for updates but not for clipping. A unified approach leveraging the same statistics for both clipping and momentum could improve privacy-utility trade-offs.

Method: Introduce DP-MacAdam, an algorithm combining adaptive clipping (shift/scale based on gradient mean/variance) with Adam-like momentum updates that also use the same mean/variance estimates. Provide theoretical analysis showing bias-free variance estimation. Conduct empirical evaluations comparing DP-MacAdam to DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam, focusing on privacy, accuracy, and tuning-free clipping.

Result: DP-MacAdam achieves improved model utility relative to DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam baselines. It provides bias-free gradient variance estimates and eliminates the need for manual clipping threshold tuning while maintaining privacy guarantees.

Conclusion: DP-MacAdam successfully integrates adaptive clipping and adaptive momentum by sharing mean/variance statistics, yielding better DP training performance and simplifying hyperparameter tuning, with theoretical and empirical validation.

Abstract: Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) has become the standard framework for privacy-preserving machine learning, yet its reliance on a fixed gradient clipping threshold to limit sensitivity remains a significant practical limitation. Adaptive clipping algorithms such as AdaClip shift and scale the gradient prior to clipping and adding noise so that the clipped gradient yields a more informative descent direction. The shift and scaling parameters are selected adaptively based on the empirical mean and variance. However, in existing adaptive clipping algorithms, these empirical estimates have not been also used for momentum to accelerate training itself. On the other hand, DP-Adam is an algorithm that exploits Adam-like momentum updates based on the gradient mean and variance to accelerate training, but does not exploit these estimates for adaptive clipping. In this work, we propose Differentially Private Mechanism with Adaptive Clipping and Adaptive Momentum (DP-MacAdam), a novel algorithm that combines these two approaches so as to use the same mean and variance estimates for both clipping and momentum. We perform an analysis showing that DP-MacAdam estimates the gradient variances in a bias-free manner. In addition, we empirically evaluate the privacy and accuracy of DP-MacAdam, demonstrating that it achieves improved model utility compared to DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam baselines, without requiring manual tuning of the clipping threshold.

</details>


### [243] [Sharp First-Order Lower Bounds for Higher-Order Smooth Nonconvex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05438)
*Dongruo Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proves dimension-free Ω(ε^{-7/4}) and Ω(ε^{-5/3}) lower bounds for deterministic first-order optimization under Hessian-Lipschitz and third-derivative-Lipschitz smoothness, matching known upper bounds, for finding ε-stationary points in smooth nonconvex functions. Introduces a block-chain hard instance valid for any finite smoothness order; lower-bound discovery aided by ChatGPT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the open problem of tight lower bounds under higher-order smoothness in deterministic first-order nonconvex optimization, completing the complexity landscape beyond the classical ε^{-2} rate.

Method: Construct a dimension-free hard instance via a block-chain mechanism that enforces blockwise oracle revelation while preserving the required higher-order smoothness. Start from a scalar hard instance and extend it to a vector/block structure to yield a universal lower bound across dimensions; show that any deterministic first-order method requires at least the stated oracle (gradient) calls to reach ε-stationarity.

Result: Establishes matching lower bounds: Ω(ε^{-7/4}) in the Hessian-Lipschitz regime and Ω(ε^{-5/3}) in the third-order-smooth regime; the construction is dimension-free and valid for every finite smoothness order.

Conclusion: Closes the gap on the deterministic first-order oracle complexity under higher-order smoothness, providing a generic, dimension-free block-chain construction that matches upper bounds; notes the discovery aided by ChatGPT 5.5 Pro.

Abstract: We study the deterministic first-order oracle complexity of finding \(ε\)-stationary points in smooth nonconvex optimization when the objective satisfies higher-order smoothness assumptions. While the classical \(ε^{-2}\) rate is optimal under only Lipschitz gradients, higher-order smoothness leads to accelerated first-order upper bounds, most notably the \(ε^{-7/4}\) rate under Lipschitz Hessians and the \(ε^{-5/3}\) rate under Lipschitz third derivatives. The matching lower bounds, however, have remained open. We resolve this gap by proving a new dimension-free first-order lower bound for higher-order smooth nonconvex functions, valid for every finite smoothness order. In particular, our construction gives a matching \(Ω(ε^{-7/4})\) lower bound in the Hessian-Lipschitz case and a matching \(Ω(ε^{-5/3})\) lower bound in the third-order-smooth regime. The hard instance is based on a \emph{block-chain} mechanism that enforces blockwise oracle revelation while preserving the smoothness structure needed for the scalar hard instance. The lower-bound construction was discovered with the assistance of ChatGPT 5.5 Pro and subsequently verified by the authors.

</details>


### [244] [GOTabPFN: From Feature Ordering to Compact Tokenization for Tabular Foundation Models on High-Dimensional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05441)
*Al Zadid Sultan Bin Habib,Md Younus Ahamed,Prashnna Kumar Gyawali,Gianfranco Doretto,Donald A. Adjeroh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GO-LR enables compact, stable TabPFN-style predictions for HDLSS tabular data via a graph-guided feature ordering and a NSC-based pooling, yielding GOTabPFN that maintains performance without backbone retraining; equivalence to weighted MLA and a TSP-path surrogate connects the method to combinatorial optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HDLSS tabular prediction demands accurate, stable models that do not require retraining large backbone models; achieving efficiency under tight token budgets is particularly important for practical deployment.

Method: Introduce Graph-guided Ordering with Local Refinement (GO-LR); prove its equivalence to weighted Minimum Linear Arrangement; interpret the solver as a TSP-path-style surrogate; build GOTabPFN on GO-LR; add a Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression (NSC) unit to pool locally adjacent ordered features into meta-features; produce a compact representation suitable for TabPFN-style predictions in HDLSS settings.

Result: Empirical benchmarks show GOTabPFN improves stability and accuracy under tight token budgets across tabular datasets.

Conclusion: The framework enables practical, stable TabPFN-style predictions for HDLSS tabular data without large-backbone retraining, and the GO-LR/NSC design offers a principled path for compact representations in HDLSS regimes.

Abstract: We investigate how to make small tabular foundation models effective for High-Dimensional, Low-Sample Size (HDLSS) tabular prediction without retraining large backbones. We introduce Graph-guided Ordering with Local Refinement (GO-LR), show its equivalence to weighted Minimum Linear Arrangement, and interpret the practical solver as a TSP-path-style surrogate. We propose GOTabPFN,which builds on GO-LR, and a Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression (NSC) unit to pool locally adjacent ordered features into meta-features, yielding a compact representation that makes TabPFN-style prediction practical in HDLSS regimes. Across tabular benchmarks, GOTabPFN improves stability and accuracy under tight token budgets.

</details>


### [245] [Towards Unified and Data-Efficient Prognostics and Health Management with Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05481)
*Raffael Theiler,Lev Telyatnikov,Leandro Von Krannichfeldt,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tabular Foundation Models with in-context learning applied to industrial PHM by turning unit-time signals into tabular rows; achieves strong, data-efficient performance on prognostics and diagnostics, often outperforming sequence models, especially in low-data scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial PHM data are often fragmented, partially observed, and poorly labeled, hindering supervised learning. There is a need for reusable, general predictive systems. Many time-series foundation models focus on forecasting long, regular sequences, which limits applicability to heterogeneous PHM tasks.

Method: Convert raw unit-level signals into tabular rows and apply Tabular Foundation Models using in-context learning. Evaluate across prognostics and diagnostics tasks, comparing PFN-based/tabular models to sequence models, transformer baselines, and gradient-boosted trees under a common evaluation protocol. Investigate temporal context preservation via tabular representation and the impact of context construction under subsampling.

Result: PFN-based tabular models achieve the best average ranks across prognostic and diagnostic tasks, are competitive in low-data regimes, and preserve temporal context in the tabular form. Performance depends on representative context construction under subsampling.

Conclusion: Tabular foundation models provide a practical and general interface for heterogeneous PHM problems, enabling data-efficient solutions that unify prognostics and diagnostics within a single framework.

Abstract: Data-driven Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) uses time-varying condition-monitoring data to diagnose system states and estimate remaining useful life in engineered assets. These tasks are central to maintenance planning, but industrial PHM data are often fragmented, partially observed, and poorly labeled, which hinders supervised learning. Foundation models offer a route toward reusable predictive systems, yet most time-series foundation models are designed for forecasting and assume long, coherent, regularly sampled sequences. To address this gap, we propose a framework for applying Tabular Foundation Models to industrial time series using in-context learning, and we evaluate them on a variety of PHM tasks. By converting raw unit-level signals into tabular rows, we show that these models perform well across multiple tasks - including prognostics, and diagnostics - and are highly data efficient. We compare them directly with sequence models, transformer baselines, and gradient-boosted trees under a common evaluation protocol. The results indicate that tabular foundation models achieve the best average ranks across prognostic and diagnostic tasks. Our findings further show that PFN-based models are competitive in low-data regimes, that temporal context can be preserved in the tabular representation, and that performance depends on representative context construction under subsampling. These results demonstrate that tabular foundation models provide a practical and general interface for heterogeneous PHM problems.

</details>


### [246] [Learned Subspace Compression for Communication-Efficient Pipeline Parallelism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05484)
*Paul Janson,Edouard Oyallon,Eugene Belilovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAPL learns per-stage orthogonal projection matrices under Stiefel manifold constraints to compress inter-stage activations in pipeline-parallel training, with per-stage anchor embeddings and residual vector quantization, achieving high compression with minimal performance loss on LLaMA models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation communication is the bottleneck in pipeline parallelism on low-bandwidth networks; fixed projection schemes degrade performance and require non-standard adaptations.

Method: Treat inter-stage compression as learning orthogonal projections constrained to the Stiefel manifold; enable each stage to learn its own task-optimal subspace; introduce per-stage factorized anchor embeddings for full-rank activation reconstruction with negligible overhead; apply residual vector quantization with a streaming codebook synchronization protocol to amortize dictionary communication.

Result: Applied to LLaMA models from 150M to 1B parameters; MAPL achieves high compression with negligible performance degradation and substantially better compression–performance tradeoffs than Subspace Networks.

Conclusion: MAPL provides an effective, adaptable, manifold-constrained framework for activation compression in pipeline parallelism, enabling scalable training with reduced inter-stage communication overhead.

Abstract: Pipeline parallelism enables training of large language models that exceed single-device memory, yet inter-stage activation communication becomes the dominant bottleneck when trained on low-bandwidth networks. Recent work in this area has proposed using fixed orthogonal projections to compress activations. However, this still results in a significant performance degradation and requires a number of non-standard adaptations to constrain the optimization. A natural alternative is to learn a low rank projection for each pipeline stage, however maintaining the necessary orthogonality of these projectors during training remains a challenge. We present Manifold Aware Projection Learning (MAPL), a method that treats inter-stage compression as a learnable orthogonal projection under explicit Stiefel manifold (orthogonal matrices) constraints. Rather than prescribing a fixed global subspace, MAPL lets each pipeline stage discover and continuously adapt its own task-optimal compression subspace via manifold-constrained steepest descent. To recover token-specific signals at stage boundaries, we introduce per-stage factorized anchor embeddings that allow for full-rank activation reconstruction with negligible communication overhead. We further show that we can incorporate residual vector quantization after projection with a streaming codebook synchronization protocol that amortizes dictionary communication. Across LLaMA models from 150M to 1B parameters we show that MAPL can be easily applied to the existing pipeline and can achieve high compression with neglibile performance degradation with a drastically improved tradeoffs in performance vs. compression compared to Subspace Networks.

</details>


### [247] [LEVANTE-bench: Multi-Scale Comparison of VLMs to Children Using Cognitive Tasks (or, "Is Your VLM Smarter Than a 5th Grader?")](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05497)
*Alvin Wei Ming Tan,David Cardinal,Tania Lorido-Botran,Laura Bravo-Sanchez,Sunny Yu,Michael C. Frank*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEVANTE-bench benchmarks vision-language models against human cognitive development data; finds partial alignment with children aged 5-12 across six tasks; alignment improves with model capability at task/item level but not consistently across error distributions; some small models match younger children's errors; hardest tasks include matrix reasoning and mental rotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable systematic evaluation of VLMs against human cognitive development across ages, languages, and cultures using the LEVANTE dataset; address the need for comparative benchmarks for cognition beyond static accuracy.

Method: Evaluate multiple VLMs on six LEVANTE-based tasks using data from children aged 5–12 in three countries (N=1547). Compare overall accuracy, task- and item-level alignment with children, and trial-level error distributions. Analyze alignment across model scales; examine error distribution matching; assess performance on challenging reasoning tasks.

Result: Alignment is heterogeneous across scales: higher-capability models align better on task and item levels, but matching children's error distributions varies by task; some smaller models align with younger children's errors; best models still struggle on matrix reasoning and mental rotation; overall VLMs partly align with children's cognition.

Conclusion: Current VLM architectures only partially capture child-like cognitive abilities; gains in overall task accuracy do not universally translate to correct error patterns; further work needed to better model reasoning tasks and cross-cultural developmental patterns.

Abstract: Given the inherently multimodal nature of human experience, vision-language models (VLMs) hold substantial promise for modeling human cognition as it grows and develops with experience. Realizing their potential requires tools for comparing VLMs with human cognitive development across tasks, ages, and populations. We present LEVANTE-bench, a benchmark based on tasks and data from the Learning Variability Network (LEVANTE), which distributes open-source tasks and data measuring children's cognition across languages and cultures. In LEVANTE-bench, we systematically assess VLMs on six tasks, comparing their alignment with children aged 5-12 ($N$ = 1547) across three countries. We compare models at multiple scales, assessing their overall accuracy, their task- and item-level alignment with children, and how well they match children's trial-level error distributions. Alignment was heterogeneous across scales: at the level of tasks and items, more capable models aligned better with humans. However, match to human error distributions varied widely across tasks, and for several tasks, smaller models matched younger children's errors better. In addition, even the best-performing VLMs struggled on matrix reasoning and mental rotation tasks. Thus, current VLM architectures align only partially with the cognitive abilities of children.

</details>


### [248] [Dominant-Layer ZO: A Single Layer Dominates Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05516)
*Wanhao Yu,Ziyan Wang,Zheng Wang,Abeer Matar Almalky,Yihang Zuo,Shuteng Niu,Sen Lin,Adnan Siraj Rakin,Deliang Fan,Li Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZO fine-tuning of large language models is dominated by a single decoding layer, and tuning only that layer matches or surpasses full-model ZO fine-tuning with substantial training speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how adaptation signals propagate in zeroth-order fine-tuning and why most performance gains come from a single layer, enabling faster, more efficient fine-tuning.

Method: Empirical analysis across multiple LLM families (e.g., LLaMA2-7B, Qwen3-8B) and nine benchmarks; identify dominant layer via activation-outlier analysis; compare single-layer ZO fine-tuning to full-model ZO fine-tuning (MeZO) and LoRA-based ZO fine-tuning; analyze perturbation propagation in the residual stream.

Result: A single decoding layer is consistently dominant for ZO fine-tuning across tasks and models; tuning this layer alone matches or exceeds full-model ZO fine-tuning. The dominant layer is model-specific but task-agnostic, identifiable pre-training through the first activation-outlier layer. The perturbation analysis explains the phenomenon: the layer has high perturbation sensitivity and is early in the residual stream, allowing effects to accumulate; this yields strong, stable optimization signals under forward-only updates. Empirical results show up to 4.52x training speedup on LLaMA2-7B and Qwen3-8B across nine benchmarks, with better average performance than full-model MeZO and LoRA-based ZO fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Dominant-layer ZO fine-tuning is highly effective and efficient, enabling substantial speedups without sacrificing performance; the dominance arises from perturbation sensitivity combined with early residual placement, and the dominant layer can be pre-identified via activation-outlier analysis.

Abstract: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization enables memory-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) using only forward passes, but it remains unclear how useful adaptation is distributed across layers. In this work, we reveal a surprising phenomenon: ZO fine-tuning is sharply dominated by a single decoding layer. Across multiple LLM families and downstream tasks, fine-tuning this dominant layer alone consistently matches or even exceeds full-model ZO fine-tuning. We further show that the dominant layer is task-agnostic but model-specific, and can be identified before training through a simple inference-only analysis of activation outliers. Specifically, the dominant layer consistently aligns with the first activation-outlier layer in the pre-trained model. To explain this phenomenon, we analyze how perturbation effects propagate under ZO optimization. We find that the dominant layer combines two key properties: high perturbation sensitivity and early placement in the residual stream, allowing perturbation-induced effects to propagate and accumulate through remaining subsequent decoding layers. As a result, this layer produces disproportionately strong and stable optimization signals under forward-only updates. Extensive experiments on LLaMA2-7B and Qwen3-8B across nine benchmarks show that dominant-layer ZO fine-tuning improves average performance over full-model MeZO and LoRA-based ZO fine-tuning while achieving up to 4.52$\times$ training speedup.

</details>


### [249] [What Objects Enable, Not What They Are: Functional Latent Spaces for Affordance Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05533)
*Rohan Siva,Neel P. Bhatt,Yunhao Yang,Seoyoung Lee,Nishant Gadde,Christian Ellis,Alvaro Velasquez,Zhangyang Wang,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A4D redefines robot planning with an affordance-based latent space and an affordance discovery mechanism, enabling generalization to unseen object functionalities. It achieves high existing-affordance accuracy, strong new-affordance data efficiency, and substantial speedups, with open resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Appearance-based latent spaces limit task-relevant reasoning; planning requires understanding object functionalities (affordances) for robust generalization to novel interactions.

Method: Project visual observations into a functional latent space organized around affordances; infer object functionalities via proximity in this space; introduce an affordance discovery mechanism to expand the latent space for unseen scenarios; use proximity-based uncertainty to trigger discovery.

Result: 94% inference accuracy on existing affordances, >15 percentage points above SOTA; new-affordance inference accuracy from 70% to >90% with <10% of training data; 100x faster inference.

Conclusion: Affordance-based latent representations with a discovery mechanism improve planning generalization and efficiency; resources (code, videos, data) are provided for reproducibility.

Abstract: Existing robot planning systems rely on appearance-based reasoning, where visual observations are encoded into latent spaces organized around object appearances (e.g., recognizing a "cart" based on how it looks). However, planning requires reasoning about task-relevant functionalities of objects (e.g., whether an object is "movable"), which appearance-based latent spaces do not capture. As a result, existing approaches struggle to generalize to novel robot-object interactions. We address this limited generalizability through affordance reasoning, enabling planning based on task-relevant object functionalities instead of appearance alone. We introduce A4D, which maps visual observations into a shared latent space structured around affordances (e.g., "movable"). By projecting visual observations into this functional latent space and measuring their proximity to affordances, A4D infers functionalities relevant to the observed object. Furthermore, we introduce an affordance discovery mechanism that expands the latent space to handle unseen scenarios where existing affordances are insufficient. A4D uses proximity in the functional latent space to quantify uncertainty in affordance inference and selectively triggers affordance discovery. We evaluate A4D across several planning tasks involving diverse and unseen affordances. A4D achieves 94% inference accuracy on existing affordances outperforming state-of-the-art approaches by over 15% points, improves new-affordance inference accuracy from 70% to over 90% with fewer than 10% of the original training data, and enables 100x faster inference. Code, videos, and data available at: https://A4Dance-reasoning.github.io.

</details>


### [250] [Less is MoE: Trimming Experts in Domain-Specialist Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05538)
*Haoze He,Xinkai Zou,Xuan Jiang,Xingyuan Ding,Ao Qu,Juncheng Billy Li,Heather Miller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fisher-MoE prunes FFN intermediate dimensions using Fisher information in Mixture-of-Experts, enabling 50% compression with minimal capability loss and substantial memory/throughput gains; capability concentrates in sparse FFN dimensions, highlighting granularity as a key compression unit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models deliver strong performance via conditional computation but have large parameter footprints that hinder deployment. General-purpose benchmarks reveal compression failures when not accounting for the granularity of capability distribution across experts; to compress effectively, identify task-critical FFN intermediate dimensions.

Method: Compute Fisher-information-based importance for FFN intermediate dimensions within MoE layers. Rank dimensions by Fisher importance and prune those with lowest importance (e.g., removing 12 of 1.35M dims in Qwen1.5-MoE). Develop Fisher-MoE that applies FFN pruning based on this ranking. Evaluate at a 50% MoE compression ratio on tasks including GSM8K and factual-knowledge benchmarks, comparing against activation-, router-score-, and magnitude-based baselines.

Result: Fisher importance identifies tiny, task-critical sets of dimensions that preserve capability at substantial compression. In Qwen1.5-MoE, removing as few as 12 of 1.35M routed-FFN intermediate dims collapses GSM8K accuracy, underscoring the criticality of specific dims. With 50% MoE compression, Fisher-MoE preserves model capability, reduces weight memory by roughly 45%, and increases inference throughput by about 21%.

Conclusion: Intermediate-dimensional granularity is an effective unit for compression and ranking in MoE models; capability concentrates in a small subset of FFN dimensions. Fisher-information-based pruning outperforms activation-, router-score-, and magnitude-based methods and enables efficient deployment without substantial loss in broad capabilities.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models achieve strong performance through conditional computation, but their large parameter footprint poses deployment challenges. Prior MoE compression approaches catastrophically fail when evaluated on general-purpose benchmarks beyond commonsense reasoning. We trace this failure to the granularity of compression: important capabilities are distributed across experts but concentrated in FFN sparse intermediate dimensions. To identify these dimensions, we use Fisher importance which outperforms activation-, router-score-, and magnitude-based alternatives, and identifies tiny sets of task-critical dimensions: in Qwen1.5-MoE, removing as few as 12 of 1.35M routed-FFN intermediate dimensions collapses GSM8K accuracy while largely preserving factual-knowledge performance. Building on this, we propose Fisher-MoE, which operates within FFN to remove intermediate dimensions ranked by Fisher importance. At the same 50% MoE compression ratio, Fisher-MoE preserves model capability, while reducing weight memory by ~45% and improving inference throughput by 21%. These findings suggest intermediate dimension granularity is an effective unit for both compression and ranking where capability concentrates in MoE models.

</details>


### [251] [Balancing Image Compression and Generation with Bootstrapped Tokenization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05552)
*Haozhe Chi,Jinghan Li,Hao Jiang,Wu Sheng,Yi Ma,Jing Wang,Yadong Mu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SelfBootTok: separate global/local token groups; self-bootstrapped learning where local details are predicted from global tokens, reducing generator burden and computation (~40%), achieving strong reconstruction/generation and SOTA gFID (1.56 with 64 tokens).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Redundant information across granularities in existing image tokenizers, which mix multiple scales within a token and hinder efficient generator training.

Method: Decompose tokens into global and local groups; train a self-bootstrapped objective in which the tokenizer captures local details and the generator operates on global tokens only.

Result: Generator uses only global tokens, ~40% computation reduction, improved reconstruction and generation quality; achieves gFID 1.56 with 64 tokens.

Conclusion: Shows scalable paradigm: with more data/parameters to self-supervise local representations, the approach attains state-of-the-art performance and efficiency gains.

Abstract: Despite progress in image tokenization, standard methods encode redundant information by mixing all granularities within each token, thus redundancy persists between tokens. The mix of information of different granularity also complicates the training of generators. This paper introduces SelfBootTok, a method that resolves this by cleanly decomposing information into global and local token groups. Through self-bootstrapped learning, the model predicts local details exclusively from global tokens, shifting the burden of visual details from the generator to the tokenizer. Consequently, our generator is far more efficient, requiring only global tokens and reducing computation by approximately 40%, while delivering superior reconstruction and generation. Moreover, this paradigm scales elegantly: by leveraging more data or parameters to self-supervise local representation learning, SelfBootTok achieves a new state-of-the-art gFID score of 1.56 using only 64 tokens.

</details>


### [252] [Representation Learning Enables Scalable Multitask Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05555)
*Johan Obando-Ceron,Lu Li,Scott Fujimoto,Pierre-Luc Bacon,Aaron Courville,Pablo Samuel Castro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A model-free MR.Q with auxiliary predictive objectives learns rich predictive representations that enable scalable multitask RL without planning, matching or surpassing world-model baselines while being more efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify whether model-based control is essential for scalability in multitask RL; hypothesize that representation learning is the key driver.

Method: Train MR.Q, a simple model-free actor-critic, augmented with auxiliary predictive objectives to learn predictive representations; evaluate on diverse multitask continuous control tasks; compare to world-model-based methods and baselines; ablations on model capacity.

Result: Outperforms a recent world-model-based method and various baselines; reduces computational overhead and improves wall-clock efficiency; improvements increase with model capacity; ablations show predictive representation learning is critical for performance.

Conclusion: Predictive representation learning is a critical driver for scalable multitask RL; planning is not required; a high-capacity value function with predictive representations suffices in a model-free framework.

Abstract: Scaling reinforcement learning (RL) to diverse multitask settings remains a central challenge. While recent advances in model-based RL achieve strong performance, they rely on planning and complex training pipelines, making it unclear which components are essential for scalability. We revisit this question and argue that the primary driver of scalable multitask RL is not model-based control, but \emph{representation learning}. In particular, we show that combining predictive, model-based representations with high-capacity value function approximation is sufficient to achieve strong performance, even without planning. We evaluate a simple model-free algorithm, MR.Q, coupled with auxiliary predictive objectives into a scalable actor-critic architecture. This approach outperforms a recent world-model-based method and a range of deep RL baselines across a diverse suite of multitask continuous control tasks, while significantly reducing computational overhead and improving wall-clock efficiency. We observe consistent improvements with increased model capacity and show through ablations that predictive representation learning is critical for performance.

</details>


### [253] [Field Validation of a Multi-Resolution ConvLSTM Framework for Retaining Wall Deformation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05556)
*Jihoon Kim,Heejung Youn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-resolution ConvLSTM ensemble predicting retaining-wall deformation; trained on noise-augmented simulations, validated on field data from 34 inclinometers across 11 sites, achieving MAE 1.4 mm and R^2 0.93.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address accurate, generalizable prediction of retaining-wall deformation during staged excavation by leveraging multi-temporal LSTM models and validating on real field data to test transferability from simulated data.

Method: Train ConvLSTM models at multiple temporal resolutions; integrate via stacking ensemble; train on Gaussian-noise-augmented numerical simulations; validate with field monitoring data from 34 inclinometers across 11 sites; assess performance with multiple metrics; analyze effects of deformation irregularity and spatiotemporal characteristics.

Result: Framework yields accurate predictions for up to 5.0 m excavation with mean absolute error of 1.4 mm and R^2 of 0.93 across sites, demonstrating effective transfer from simulated to field data.

Conclusion: A multi-resolution ConvLSTM stacking ensemble can reliably predict field-scale retaining-wall deformation under diverse excavation conditions, despite training solely on simulated data.

Abstract: This study presents a comprehensive field validation of a multi-resolution Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory (ConvLSTM) framework for predicting retaining wall deformation during staged excavation. The framework is trained on Gaussian noise-augmented numerical simulations and integrates ConvLSTM models operating at different temporal resolutions through a stacking ensemble strategy. The proposed framework is validated using field monitoring data from 34 inclinometers across 11 excavation sites in South Korea. Site-wise prediction performance is systematically evaluated using multiple evaluation metrics, with analyses of the influence of temporal deformation irregularity and spatiotemporal prediction characteristics on model performance. The results demonstrate that the framework predicts retaining wall deformation associated with up to 5.0 m of additional excavation with an average mean absolute error of 1.4 mm and a coefficient of determination of 0.93 across the excavation sites. These results indicate that the framework, although trained exclusively on numerically simulated and augmented database, can be effectively applied to diverse field excavation conditions and achieve a reliable level of prediction accuracy in practical retaining wall deformation prediction.

</details>


### [254] [Autoregressive Diffusion World Models for Off-Policy Evaluation of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05558)
*Kaixuan Liu,Guojun Xiong,Weinan Zhang,Shengpu Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADWM is an offline evaluation framework for LLM agents that uses an autoregressive diffusion world model trained on pre-collected trajectories to simulate environment responses, enabling step-by-step, policy-guided rollouts without real-environment interaction; it avoids joint state-action diffusion and mitigates compounding errors, yielding accurate value estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating multi-turn LLM agents in real environments is expensive, risky, and data-inefficient due to the need for online interaction and costly experimentation.

Method: Train a latent diffusion world model (per-step denoising) that simulates environment transitions. The LLM agent under evaluation guides the diffusion generation via a policy-conditioned score function, and actions are sampled from the policy after observing the environment, enabling step-by-step rollouts without executing in the real environment.

Result: Empirically achieves accurate value estimates and reliable evaluation across diverse multi-turn agent tasks, demonstrating practicality for offline LLM agent evaluation.

Conclusion: ADWM provides a practical offline evaluation framework that reduces risk and cost while delivering reliable performance estimates for LLM agents in interactive settings.

Abstract: Evaluating large language model (LLM) agents in multi-turn interactive environments is expensive and risky, as it requires online environment interaction. We propose ADWM (Autoregressive Diffusion World Model), an evaluation framework that estimates the performance of a new LLM agent policy purely from pre-collected trajectories. The core idea is to learn a latent diffusion world model that simulates how the environment responds to the evaluation policy, without ever executing it in the real environment. Existing diffusion-based OPE methods guide full trajectories in a single pass by jointly diffusing states and actions, an assumption that breaks down for LLM agents whose actions are discrete text that must be sampled from the policy after observing the environment. Unlike autoregressive world models that suffer from compounding errors, ADWM models each transition as an independent denoising process, enabling reliable step-by-step rollouts where the world model and agent alternate in causal order. Crucially, the LLM agent under evaluation directly guides the diffusion generation at each step via a policy-conditioned score function, ensuring that simulated trajectories accurately reflect its decision-making patterns. Empirically, ADWM achieves accurate value estimates and evaluation reliability across diverse multi-turn agent tasks, demonstrating its promise as a practical framework for offline LLM agent evaluation.

</details>


### [255] [CLaaS: Continual learning as a service for sample efficient online learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05559)
*Kion Fallah,Silen Naihin,Barak Widawsky,Qingqing Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A continual learning-as-a-service framework (CLaaS) enables deployed LLM agents to adapt online across a stream of scenarios by storing experiences in an experience replay buffer for gradient-based updates, achieving better forward transfer and less forgetting than in-context learning; replay is essential for sample efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world deployments cannot reset environments; agents must continuously adapt to distribution shifts by learning from accumulated experiences with high sample efficiency while preserving prior capabilities.

Method: Propose CLaaS: an experiential online continual learning system exposed via a chat API. It collects rollouts from a stream of scenarios, stores them in an experience replay buffer, and performs asynchronous gradient-based updates to reuse past experiences.

Result: In an adversarial task, parametric updates yield superior forward transfer and less forgetting than in-context learning; replay plays a critical role for sample efficiency.

Conclusion: CLaaS offers a practical path for continuous adaptation of LLM agents during deployment, highlighting the value of experience replay; future work could explore broader tasks, robustness, and scalability.

Abstract: Deployed large language model agents must adapt to distribution shift in dynamic environments. Ideally, adaptation can be performed from accumulated agent experiences and retain prior capabilities while transferring to future tasks. However, agent actions and environmental transitions can only be sampled once per scenario, as real-world environments cannot be trivially reset. To this end, we investigate an experiential and online continual learning setting in which agents learn from a stream of scenarios. We propose continual learning as-a-service (CLaaS), a system which enables agents to improve during deployment, abstracted behind a chat API. To increase sample efficiency, CLaaS stores rollouts in an experience replay buffer for gradient reuse during asynchronous training. We evaluate CLaaS on an adversarial task, demonstrating that parametric updates lead to superior forward transfer and less forgetting than in-context learning, with replay being a critical choice for sample efficiency.

</details>


### [256] [AsyncWebRL: Efficient Multi-Step RL for Visual Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05597)
*Hao Bai,Rui Yang,Chenlu Ye,Spencer Whitehead,Aviral Kumar,Tong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AsyncWebRL delivers asynchronous rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh for vision-language web agents, plus an algorithmic change from per-trajectory normalization 1/|τ_i| to a constant 1/k, achieving substantial throughput and performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compute inefficiency in multi-step RL due to idle GPUs in synchronous pipelines and iterative trajectories that use more steps/tokens than necessary; address both system and algorithmic inefficiencies to boost throughput and learning effectiveness.

Method: System: asynchronous overlap of rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh; perpetual rollout pool; lightweight screenshot handling. Algorithm: replace per-trajectory normalizer 1/|τ_i| with a constant 1/k to contract trajectories and prevent overemphasis on failed tokens, preserving aggregate success.

Result: Up to 2.9x end-to-end training-throughput speedup compared to the fastest prior synchronous pipeline (WebGym). On WebGym out-of-distribution test split, +5.8% relative improvement over 42.9% prior best; larger gains on harder slices: Medium +42%, Hard +48%. Open-source state of the art.

Conclusion: The work combines system-level asynchronous training with a simple yet effective normalization change to address both inefficiency and trajectory quality, establishing a new open-source SOTA on WebGym OOD with notable gains in harder evaluation slices.

Abstract: Training vision-language web agents with multi-step RL is compute-intensive, with two dominant forms of inefficiency: idle GPUs in synchronous RL, and trajectories that use more steps and tokens than necessary. We present AsyncWebRL, which addresses both. On the system side, an asynchronous design overlaps rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh across iterations, paired with two web-agent-specific adaptations, namely an everlasting rollout pool and lightweight screenshot handling, that together deliver up to a $2.9\times$ end-to-end training-throughput speedup over the previously fastest open synchronous pipeline (WebGym). On the algorithmic side, we identify the per-trajectory normalizer $1/|τ_i|$ in multi-step GRPO as the root cause of trajectory-level and token-level inefficiency: because failures are systematically longer than successes, it down-weights the negative gradient on failed tokens, so the policy keeps producing verbose memory schemas. Replacing $1/|τ_i|$ with a constant $1/k$ breaks this coupling, contracting trajectories while preserving aggregate success. Together, these contributions set a new open-source state of the art on the WebGym out-of-distribution test split (+5.8% relative over the 42.9% prior best), with the largest gains on the harder slices (+42% relative on Medium, +48% relative on Hard).

</details>


### [257] [Mitigating the Curse of Dimensionality in Uniform Convergence of Deep Neural Networks via Smooth Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05599)
*Yizhe Ding,Runze Li,Jia Liu,Lingzhou Xue*

Main category: cs.LG
