TL;DR: Smoothly activated DNNs (including feedforward and residuals) provide non-asymptotic, uniform convergence guarantees that mitigate the curse of dimensionality observed with ReLU LS estimators; the paper develops pseudo-dimension, Hölder-norm, and approximation bounds and demonstrates rates across Huber, LS, quantile, and logistic regression, with empirical support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain reliable uniform convergence guarantees for deep learning estimators, addressing the limitations of ReLU-based least-squares in worst-case, high-dimensional settings where uniform convergence can suffer from the curse of dimensionality.

Method: Develop a theoretical framework for smooth DNNs, derive pseudo-dimension bounds, non-asymptotic approximation guarantees, and Hölder-norm bounds for smooth network estimators (including feedforward and residual architectures); prove non-asymptotic uniform convergence rates across multiple statistical models; show alignment with low-dimensional hierarchical composition structure of target functions to reduce dimensionality effects.

Result: Established non-asymptotic uniform convergence rates for smooth DNN estimators in multiple statistical contexts (Huber, least-squares, quantile, logistic regression); demonstrated that smooth DNNs can adaptively exploit hierarchical composition to mitigate the curse of dimensionality in uniform convergence; provided pseudo-dimension and Hölder-norm bounds; supported by simulations and a real-world application.

Conclusion: Smoothly activated DNNs are a theoretically grounded and practically viable alternative to ReLU networks for tasks demanding uniform guarantees, offering improved uniform convergence behavior by leveraging low-dimensional hierarchical structure.

Abstract: This paper establishes a theoretical framework for the uniform convergence of smoothly activated deep neural network (DNN) estimators. While standard ReLU networks achieve minimax-optimal rates in the $L^2(P)$ norm for various nonparametric regression tasks, we establish a theoretical lower bound demonstrating that least-squares ReLU estimators can suffer from the curse of dimensionality in their uniform convergence behavior. Motivated by the need for reliable uniform guarantees in downstream tasks requiring worst-case reliability, we address this limitation by analyzing smoothly activated DNNs (smooth DNNs), encompassing both feedforward and residual structures. We establish novel pseudo-dimension bounds, non-asymptotic approximation guarantees, and Hölder-norm bounds for the approximators of these models. Leveraging these results, we derive non-asymptotic uniform convergence rates for smooth DNN estimators across multiple statistical contexts, including Huber, least-squares, quantile, and logistic regression. We prove that smooth DNNs can mitigate the {curse of dimensionality} in uniform convergence by adaptively exploiting the low-dimensional hierarchical composition structure of the target function. Supported by both simulation studies and a real-world application, our results position smooth DNNs as a theoretically grounded and practically viable alternative to ReLU networks for statistical learning tasks requiring uniform guarantees.

</details>


### [258] [From Prediction to Self: Developmental Conditions for Agency in Minimal Neural Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05605)
*Evan Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Minimal GRU-based study shows four developmental conditions for distinguishing self-caused from world-caused changes; introduces agency gain as a metric; self-aware predictor outperforms self-blind across sinusoidal and chaotic environments; forward-sampled action selection is essential; predictive coding alone fails; 12 hypotheses falsified.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how an embodied predictive system develops self-agency and distinguishes self-caused vs world-caused changes, and formalize prerequisites for self-representation and action learning.

Method: 40 controlled experiments arranged developmentally by progressively adding components to a 192-dimensional GRU (randomly; minimal architecture details provided); testing in sinusoidal and Lorenz environments; compare self-aware vs self-blind predictors; measure prediction error Err_world and Err_self; compute agency gain; ablate auxiliary components; test forward-sampling vs gradient-based action selection.

Result: Four necessary sequential conditions identified: stable attractors, causal action loop, proprioceptive feedback, asynchronous learning; self-aware predictor consistently outperforms across environments; agency gain robust to ablations; only forward-sampled action selection yields meaningful agency gain; two gradient-based methods degenerate; 12 hypotheses about stall points falsified; abstract ends with truncated phrase.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a staged developmental route to self-agency in predictive systems, supporting explicit proprioception and forward-action sampling; suggests predictive coding alone is insufficient for self-representation and highlights design implications for self-aware AI.

Abstract: How does a system that merely predicts the world come to distinguish its own causal influence from everything else? We trace this transition in a minimal 192-dimensional GRU through 40 controlled experiments arranged as a developmental sequence, adding components one at a time and tracking whether the system can distinguish self-caused from world-caused changes.
  The developmental path reveals four conditions that must be satisfied in strict order: (1) persistent state forming stable attractors, (2) a causal action loop linking output to input, (3) proprioceptive feedback that makes implicit causal knowledge explicit, and (4) asynchronous awakening - perceptual learning must consolidate before action learning begins. We propose agency gain (A = Err_world - Err_self), the predictive advantage of knowing one's own action, as a metric to track this process. The self-aware predictor consistently outperforms the self-blind predictor across periodic (sinusoidal) and chaotic (Lorenz) environments, and the metric survives ablation of all auxiliary components. Only forward-sampled action selection produces meaningful agency gain; two gradient-based alternatives degenerate.
  Equally significant are 12 falsified hypotheses mapping where development stalls: predictive coding alone does not produce self-represent

</details>


### [259] [Cross-Epoch Adaptive Rollout Optimization for RL Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05606)
*Yiming Zong,Yige Wang,Jiashuo Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive rollout budgeting for LLM reinforcement learning with per-prompt Bayesian value estimation, achieving efficient allocation under a fixed global budget via Fenchel-dual online optimization; outperforms fixed-budget baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate inefficiency of fixed rollout budgets in prompt-level RL for LLMs by allocating samples adaptively to prompts with higher marginal value, given non-uniform training signals.

Method: Maintain a Beta posterior over each prompt's success probability; use posterior expected Bernoulli variance as a Bayesian estimate of the value of additional rollouts. Define a concave, saturating utility over cumulative allocations; couple decisions across prompts and epochs through a global budget. Apply a Fenchel-dual reformulation to derive a temporally nonseparable objective; update prompt-level and budget-level dual variables via projected online gradient descent. Prove O(√K) regret against offline allocation under fixed prompt utilities.

Result: CERO consistently outperforms GRPO across multiple open-weight LLMs and mathematical-reasoning benchmarks, improving sample efficiency under a fixed budget.

Conclusion: Adaptive, Bayesian-value-based rollout budgeting with dual optimization yields improved sample efficiency in LLM post-training reinforcement learning under a global budget, with theoretical regret guarantees and empirical gains.

Abstract: LLM post-training often relies on reinforcement learning methods that sample multiple rollouts per prompt, yet most existing approaches use a fixed rollout budget for every prompt, despite large differences in the training signal different prompts provide. In this paper, we study adaptive rollout allocation under a fixed global budget and formulate the problem as online resource allocation with prompt-level diminishing returns. Our method, CERO, maintains a Beta posterior over each prompt's success probability and uses the posterior expected Bernoulli variance as a Bayesian estimate of the value of additional rollouts. We use this estimate to construct a concave, saturating utility over cumulative allocations, yielding an objective in which decisions across prompts and epochs are coupled by the global budget. Since the resulting objective is temporally nonseparable, we derive a Fenchel-dual reformulation and update both prompt-level and budget-level dual variables via projected online gradient descent. Under fixed prompt utilities, we prove an $O(\sqrt{K})$ regret bound against the offline allocation benchmark. Experiments on mathematical-reasoning problems show that CERO consistently outperforms GRPO across multiple open-weight LLMs and benchmarks, demonstrating that adaptive rollout budgeting can improve sample efficiency.

</details>


### [260] [StableRCA: Robust Graph-Agnostic Mechanism-Level Root Cause Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05636)
*Xiaoyu Lin,Nicholas Tagliapietra,Kehan Li,Lavdim Halilaj,Juergen Luettin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StableRCA: a graph-free, local mechanism-level RCA method that estimates local Markov boundaries and detects conditional distribution shifts, with exponential convergence guarantees under faithful boundary recovery; robust to graph misspecification, scalable, validated on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of graph-based RCA requiring known/accurate causal graphs and the limitations of graph-free methods that miss structural causes or rely on strong assumptions.

Method: Local RCA by estimating Markov boundaries around variables and testing for conditional distribution shifts; relies on Independent Causal Mechanism principle; provides theoretical guarantee of exponential convergence in sample size; does not perform global graph discovery.

Result: Empirical results on synthetic benchmarks and five real-world datasets show robustness to graph misspecification, effectiveness with multiple interventions, scalability to large systems, reliable across domains.

Conclusion: StableRCA offers a practical, theory-backed framework for RCA in complex domains without full causal graphs; code release.

Abstract: Root-Cause Analysis (RCA) seeks to identify the variables responsible for abnormal system behavior in complex domains such as manufacturing, cloud computing, and healthcare. Existing approaches face a critical bottleneck: graph-based causal methods can identify intervention targets but typically require a known or accurately estimated causal graph, while graph-free statistical methods either localize marginal anomalies rather than structural causes, or rely on restrictive assumptions about graph structure or functional form. We propose StableRCA, a local mechanism-level RCA framework that avoids global graph discovery by estimating local Markov boundaries and detecting conditional distribution shifts within them. Leveraging the Independent Causal Mechanism principle, we show that intervention targets can be identified with probability converging exponentially in sample size under faithful Markov boundary recovery and non-degenerate mechanism shifts. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and five real-world datasets demonstrate that StableRCA is robust to graph misspecification, effective under multiple intervention targets, scalable to large systems, and reliable across diverse application domains. Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/StableRCA-E362

</details>


### [261] [Q-GNN: Query-Conditioned Graph Neural Networks with Type Awareness for Knowledge Graph Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05639)
*Dongxiao He,Ruqiong Zhang,Zhizhi Yu,Ling Ding,Di Jin,Guangquan Xu,Zhiyong Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Q-GNN for Knowledge Graph Completion that uses query entity information (structural context and LLM-derived semantic type) alongside the query relation to guide reasoning, improving performance on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNN-based KGC methods rely solely on the query relation and ignore the informative query entity; leveraging entity neighborhood and type can provide stronger priors and context for inference.

Method: Introduce a context encoder to encode the neighboring structure and relation patterns around the query entity; modulate messages with this structural context. Use a large language model to infer the semantic type of the query entity and incorporate this type into attention computation and final scoring as type-level priors. Combine with query relation to perform reasoning on query-centered subgraphs.

Result: Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of Q-GNN.

Conclusion: Integrating both structural context and semantic type of the query entity enables more informed reasoning for KGC and yields improved predictive performance.

Abstract: Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) aims at predicting missing triplets from incomplete knowledge graphs, which is crucial for downstream applications. Recently, Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based methods have achieved remarkable success by performing message passing over query-centered local subgraphs. However, in practice, a query is jointly defined by both the entity and the relation, with both carrying information indispensable for reasoning, yet these methods rely solely on the query relation as the guiding signal, while the information inherent in the query entity is not leveraged to guide inference - the entity serves merely as a structural anchor for subgraph extraction. To this end, we incorporate query entity information into the reasoning process from two perspectives: the first is structural context, i.e., the neighboring structure and relation patterns around the entity, which is encoded by a dedicated context encoder and used to modulate messages; the second is semantic type of the entity, inferred by a large language model, which is incorporated into attention computation and final scoring to provide type-level prior constraints. Together, these two sources of information enable the reasoning process to be guided by both the query relation and the query entity. Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed Q-GNN.

</details>


### [262] [Two-Way Is Better Than One: Bidirectional Alignment with Cycle Consistency for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05675)
*Hongye Xu,Bartosz Krawczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bidirectional projection alignment with a cycle-consistency loss (BiCyc) for exemplar-free class-incremental learning; mitigates prototype drift and forgetting by jointly learning old→new and new→old mappings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL), past data cannot be stored, causing prototype drift in a prototype-based setting. One-directional projection methods bias the geometry and fail to enforce global consistency across task boundaries, leading to accumulated cycle inconsistencies and forgetting.

Method: BiCyc optimizes two projection maps simultaneously: old→new and new→old, with stop-gradient gating. A cycle-consistency objective enforces mutual alignment while the whitened space encourages contraction of singular values toward unity. The transport of class means and covariances is encouraged to minimize perturbations to log-odds, preserving old-class decisions. The method jointly evolves representation and transport.

Result: Empirically, BiCyc reduces forgetting and improves accuracy on standard EFCIL benchmarks, performing well from scratch and remaining competitive in pretrained, fine-grained regimes.

Conclusion: Cycle-consistent, bidirectional projection alignment improves robustness to drift in exemplar-free CL, preserving old-class decisions and yielding strong empirical gains with a theoretical basis for the contraction and improved transport of class statistics.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) seeks models that acquire new skills without erasing prior knowledge. In exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL), this challenge is amplified because past data cannot be stored, making representation drift for old classes particularly harmful. Prototype-based EFCIL is attractive for its efficiency, yet prototypes drift as the embedding space evolves; therefore, projection-based drift compensation has become a popular remedy. We show, however, that existing one-directional projections introduce systematic bias: they either retroactively distort the current feature geometry or align past classes only locally, leaving cycle inconsistencies that accumulate across tasks. We introduce BiCyc, a bidirectional projector alignment approach with a cycle-consistency objective. BiCyc jointly optimizes two maps, old-to-new and new-to-old, with stop-gradient gating so that transport and representation co-evolve. Analytically, we show that the cycle loss contracts the singular spectrum toward unity in whitened space, and that improved transport of class means and covariances yields smaller perturbations of classification log-odds, preserving old-class decisions and mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Empirically, across standard EFCIL benchmarks, BiCyc substantially reduces forgetting and improves accuracy in from-scratch settings, while remaining competitive in the pretrained fine-grained regime.

</details>


### [263] [Causal Modeling of Selection in Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05689)
*Haoyue Dai,Zeyu Tang,Peter Spirtes,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distinguishes static vs evolutionary selection; static models fit one-shot data filters but fail for evolution; introduces an evolutionary-selection graphical model and a sound, complete identifiability procedure across environments/generations; experiments validate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selection biases impede causal discovery. Static selection captures one-shot filtering; evolutionary selection captures iterative differential fitness shaping generations. Existing methods conflating these two mischaracterize data under evolution.

Method: Propose a new graphical model specifically for evolutionary selection and develop a sound and complete procedure to identify such models from data collected across one or more environments or generations.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate the method's ability to recover the underlying evolutionary mechanisms from data across environments/generations.

Conclusion: Distinguishing static and evolutionary selection clarifies causal inference under evolution; the proposed model and identifiability procedure enable correct discovery of evolutionary mechanisms from data.

Abstract: Understanding potential selection in data is crucial for causal discovery; we argue that "selection" in common narratives takes two forms, which we term static and evolutionary selection, respectively. Static selection refers to a one-shot filtering process where observed data consist of a subset of the population of interest, as in survey volunteer bias. Evolutionary selection, in contrast, operates through repeated rounds of differential fitness in reproduction, where observed data constitute the latest generation shaped by a historical trajectory, as in immune adaptation, antibiotic resistance, and social norm emergence. Existing methods largely conflate these two forms and rely on an identical graphical model of selection. We show that this model is valid for static settings but fails to characterize data under evolution, yielding false discovery results. To address this, we introduce a new model that specifically characterizes evolutionary selection, and develop a sound and complete procedure for identifying such models from data across one or multiple environments or generations. Experimental results validate the method's ability to uncover the relevant mechanisms underlying evolution from data.

</details>


### [264] [Benchmarking Counterfactual Prediction in Epidemic Time Series with Time-Varying Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05692)
*Wenhao Mu,Facundo Yan,Anik Mumssen,Marisa Eisenberg,Alexander Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new large-scale benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series under dynamic interventions, built from a calibrated agent-based model across 150+ U.S. counties, enabling evaluation of static/time-varying and single/multi-policy interventions; reveals significant method performance gaps and the challenges of realistic causal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of realistic benchmarks with ground-truth counterfactuals for time-series causal inference in epidemics, where existing datasets rely on real observations without counterfactuals or on simplistic simulations.

Method: Develop a calibrated agent-based model anchored in real-world demographics, mobility, epidemiology, and policy data; generate counterfactual trajectories under static and time-varying treatments, including single-policy and multi-policy settings, across more than 150 counties; evaluate a range of causal inference methods on the generated benchmark.

Result: The benchmark dataset is created and used to benchmark methods, revealing substantial performance differences among widely used and state-of-the-art approaches and highlighting the challenges in realistic time-series causal reasoning.

Conclusion: The benchmark enables comprehensive evaluation of causal inference methods in epidemic time-series, guiding method development and providing insights for policy analysis under dynamic interventions.

Abstract: Deep learning has enabled significant advances in time-series causal inference, yet progress remains constrained by the lack of realistic benchmarks with observable counterfactual outcomes. Existing datasets either rely on real-world observations without ground-truth counterfactuals or on simplified simulations that fail to capture complex causal dynamics. To address this gap, we develop a large-scale benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series under dynamic interventions. Unlike existing benchmarks, it supports static and time-varying treatments, as well as both single-policy and multi-policy intervention settings, enabling evaluation of causal inference methods across a broad range of causal inference scenarios. Leveraging a calibrated agent-based model grounded in real-world demographic, mobility, epidemiological, and policy data, we generate realistic counterfactual trajectories across more than 150 U.S. counties. Using this benchmark, we evaluate widely used and state-of-the-art causal inference methods, revealing substantial performance differences and highlighting the challenges of realistic time-series causal reasoning.

</details>


### [265] [MolE-RAG: Molecular Structure-Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05693)
*Joey Chan,Wonbin Kweon,Ashley Shin,Niharika Bhattacharjee,Pengcheng Jiang,Yue Guo,Jiawei Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces MolE-RAG, a training-free, molecule-centric retrieval-augmented generation framework that augments LLM-based molecular property prediction with retrieved literature, molecule-specific metadata, and structurally similar molecules; yields substantial gains across nine tasks and multiple LLMs without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the semantic/knowledge gap between chemical structures (e.g., SMILES) and natural-language LLMs, enabling structural reasoning and integration of chemical knowledge at inference time without task-specific fine-tuning.

Method: Inference-time retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) where each prediction is augmented with three sources: (1) retrieved chemistry literature, (2) molecule-specific information (synonyms, identifiers, functional groups, physicochemical descriptors), and (3) structurally similar molecules from the training set; applied to nine molecular property prediction tasks across general-purpose LLMs (proprietary and open-source).

Result: Across tasks and models, MolE-RAG improves ROC-AUC by up to 28 percentage points on classification and reduces RMSE by up to 67% relative to a SMILES-only baseline; utility of each context source varies by model and task, indicating different sources contribute differently depending on the scenario.

Conclusion: Molecule-centric retrieval can enhance LLM-based molecular property prediction without fine-tuning, offering a flexible framework to integrate heterogeneous chemical knowledge at inference time and potentially generalizable to diverse chemical knowledge sources.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise for molecular property prediction, but their ability to reason over chemical structures remains limited, as molecular representations such as SMILES differ substantially from the natural language on which LLMs are primarily trained. To bridge this semantic and chemical knowledge gap, we propose MolE-RAG, a training-free, molecule-centric retrieval-augmented generation framework for LLM-based molecular property prediction. MolE-RAG augments each prediction with three complementary sources of inference-time context: retrieved chemistry literature, molecule-specific information including compound synonyms, identifiers, functional group annotations, and physicochemical descriptors, and structurally similar molecules retrieved from the training set. We evaluate MolE-RAG across nine molecular property prediction tasks using proprietary, chemistry-specialized, and open-source LLMs. Across general-purpose LLMs, MolE-RAG improves ROC-AUC by up to 28 percentage points on classification tasks and reduces regression RMSE by up to 67% relative to a SMILES-only baseline. We further find that the utility of each context source varies across models and tasks, with different models benefiting most from textual retrieval, molecular context, or structural retrieval. These results suggest that molecule-centric retrieval can improve LLM-based molecular property prediction without model fine-tuning while providing a flexible framework for integrating heterogeneous chemical knowledge at inference time.

</details>


### [266] [Revisiting Prototype Rehearsal for Exemplar-Free Continual Learning: Manifold-Aware Boundary Sampling with Adaptive Class-Balanced Loss](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05695)
*Hongye Xu,Bartosz Krawczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A manifold-aware enhancement of exemplar-free class-incremental learning that revitalizes prototype rehearsal via Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling and Adaptive Class-Balanced loss, achieving state-of-the-art performance and closing the gap with drift-compensation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EFCIL must acquire new classes without storing raw data, but prototype rehearsal struggles due to ignoring information from nearby classes and severe old-vs-new sample imbalance. Drift-compensation methods realign prototypes in feature space but rely on explicit adjustments. The work investigates whether the limitation is in how prototype rehearsal is instantiated and proposes principled improvements to restore competitiveness.

Method: Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling interpolates each old-class prototype toward its nearest new-class (enemy) features to generate boundary-aware, manifold-consistent rehearsal samples while preserving inter-class separation. Adaptive Class-Balanced loss uses time-based class weighting to amplify gradients from older prototypes when informative and gradually reduces their influence as richer supervision from later tasks accumulates.

Result: The proposed components render prototype rehearsal drift-resilient and imbalance-aware, reducing or reversing the performance gap with recent drift-compensation methods and achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple EFCIL benchmarks.

Conclusion: A careful re-examination of prototype rehearsal reveals that its effectiveness hinges on how samples are generated and weighted. By aligning rehearsal with the data manifold and adopting adaptive class-balancing, exemplar-free class-incremental learning can match or surpass drift-compensation approaches, suggesting broader applicability in EFCIL and related lifelong-learning settings.

Abstract: Exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL) aims to acquire new classes over time without storing raw data. Historically, prototype rehearsal, which samples around stored class prototypes and mixes them with current-task data, has been a popular strategy to reduce catastrophic forgetting. However, recent drift-compensation methods that explicitly realign prototypes in the evolving feature space consistently outperform prototype-based rehearsal, raising the question of whether rehearsal itself is fundamentally limited. We argue that the performance gap stems not from the idea of prototype rehearsal per se, but from how it is typically instantiated: existing approaches treat prototypes as isolated class summaries that ignore information from nearby enemy classes, and fail to correct the emerging class imbalance between a handful of synthetic old-class samples and hundreds of real instances from newly introduced classes. Building on this hypothesis, we revisit prototype rehearsal and propose a manifold-aware variant that restores its competitiveness in EFCIL. First, we introduce Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling, which interpolates each old-class prototype toward its nearest enemy features from new classes, generating boundary-aware rehearsal samples that better follow the underlying data manifold while preserving inter-class separation. Second, we design an Adaptive Class-Balanced loss that performs time-based class weighting, amplifying gradients from older prototypes when they are most informative and gradually annealing their influence as richer supervision from later tasks accumulates. Together, these components turn prototype rehearsal into a drift-resilient, imbalance-aware mechanism that closes, and often reverses, the gap to recent drift-compensation methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple EFCIL benchmarks.

</details>


### [267] [Intercomparison of Machine Learning Algorithms for Remote Sensing-based In-season Crop Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05731)
*August Posch,Jitendra Kumar,Forrest M. Hoffman,Auroop R. Ganguly*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extensive evaluation of ML algorithms for pre-harvest crop typing using harmonized Landsat-Sentinel time series and crop-rotation data; SVM most effective for CA almonds and IA corn by early June; interannual variability is the main uncertainty; future work includes multiclass, CONUS-wide in-season maps, and yield forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for in-season, pre-harvest crop-type maps to support emergency management and food security. Current post-harvest products (e.g., USDA Cropland Data Layer) limit timely decisions; prior methods lacked comprehensive cross-year algorithm comparisons and uncertainty quantification.

Method: Fusion of Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel surface reflectance time series with crop-rotation history. Systematic evaluation of thousands of model configurations across ten machine learning algorithms, using year-wise cross-validation and multiple metrics. Hyperparameter search identified SVM as the top performer. Maps produced at 30 m resolution for almonds in California and corn in Iowa by early June in unseen years, with explicit quantification of uncertainty due to phenology and crop distribution.

Result: SVM yielded the highest mean F1: 0.74 (0.59) across five unseen validation years for almonds in CA and corn in IA by early June. Interannual variation represented a major source of uncertainty, but results indicate potential improvements via ensembles or additional data. The approach achieves accurate pre-harvest mapping at 30 m with robust uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility of near real-time, pre-harvest crop-type maps at 30 m and highlights the importance of cross-year algorithm comparisons and uncertainty assessment. It points to extensions to multiclass, CONUS-wide mapping and in-season yield forecasting, with ensemble methods and ancillary data likely to enhance performance.

Abstract: In-season crop type mapping is critical for food security in the face of increasingly extreme climate-related threats to crops. Currently, the USDA Cropland Data Layer provides crop type labels at 30m resolution and is available the February after harvest, but no product exists that maps crop types before harvest with satisfactory accuracy that would allow emergency managers to respond to crop threats in near real time. Furthermore, the relative advantages of a wide range of algorithms have not been evaluated in a way that accounts for interannual variability, until this study. Here, Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel surface reflectance imagery time series and crop rotation history information are combined to map corn in Iowa and almonds in California at 30m resolution accurately by early June in unseen years, with robust quantification of uncertainty due to phenology and crop distribution. Thousands of model configurations across ten machine learning algorithms were compared using a year-wise cross-validation and a suite of metrics. Hyperparameter search revealed Support Vector Machines to be the most successful algorithm overall, with a mean F1 score of 0.74 (0.59) across five unseen validation years for almonds by early June in California (corn by early June in Iowa). Interannual variation was a large source of uncertainty, but patterns showed the potential to further improve performance with ensemble approaches or ancillary data. Future work may extend these methods to include multiclass maps of all crop types, CONUS-wide maps, and in-season crop yield forecasting.

</details>


### [268] [Zero-Copy Semantic Contagion: An In-Memory Streaming Architecture for Evolving Attention Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05733)
*Kabir Murjani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-company propagation is modeled via a Heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming graph using a Neural Hawkes Process with per-node CT-LSTM; achieves ~13 ms latency per news; yields 1.70x precision lift at the 90th percentile next-day return threshold (638 articles, 47 tickers); removing graph topology eliminates signal, establishing graph as essential driver.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Per-ticker models overlook cross-company information flow; macro events (e.g., foundry disruptions) can propagate across equities. A continuous-time, cross-asset signal extraction framework is needed to capture timely cross-company effects.

Method: A heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture maps cross-company attention as a continuous-time graph. Ingestion uses zero-copy Rust to parse news (~100 ns) and scan 47-ticker universe (~1.2 μs). Inference uses a multivariate Neural Hawkes Process with per-node CT-LSTM states and a bilinear latent projection to propagate directed excitation, with adaptive pruning to cap dynamic neighborhood updates.

Result: End-to-end processing latency ~13 ms per incoming news on a single commodity CPU. On a one-month holdout of FNSPID (638 articles, 47 tickers), achieved 1.70× precision lift over random at the 90th-percentile next-day return threshold and 3.36× over a same-sector baseline.

Conclusion: Dynamic attention network is the sole driver of cross-company signal; the architecture enables fast, cross-asset inference and nontrivial precision gains, contingent on maintaining the graph topology.

Abstract: Per-ticker forecasting models dominate financial time-series work yet remain blind to cross-company propagation: a foundry disruption in Taiwan does not register in a single-asset model until Apple's own price has already moved. To address this limitation, we introduce a heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture that maps cross-company attention as a continuous-time graph driven directly from text. We show that on the ingestion side, a zero-copy Rust edge parses news records in $\sim$100 ns and scans the target equity universe in $\sim$1.2 $μ$s. On the inference end, a multivariate Neural Hawkes Process featuring per-node continuous-time LSTM states and a bilinear latent projection propagates directed excitation, while an adaptive pruning rule bounds the computational cost of dynamic neighborhood updates. Combining these stages, we demonstrate an end-to-end processing latency of $\sim$13 ms per incoming news record on a single commodity CPU. Evaluated on a one-month temporal holdout of the FNSPID corpus (638 articles across 47 tickers), the system delivers a $1.70\times$ precision lift over random at the 90th-percentile next-day return threshold, and $3.36\times$ over a same-sector baseline. Crucially, removing the graph topology collapses precision to zero, confirming that the dynamic attention network is the sole driver of cross-company signal in this architecture.

</details>


### [269] [Beyond Soft Masks: Hard-Perturbation Mixup Explainer for Robust GNN Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05756)
*Jialiang Yin,Zheng Zhao,Linsey Pang,Bo Dong,Bin Shi,Jiaxing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HPME is a hard-perturbation mixup explanation framework for GNNs that uses graph pooling and a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck to extract discrete explanatory subgraphs and a structure-level mixup strategy to keep explanations in-distribution and robust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing post-hoc explanations rely on soft masks that leak label-relevant information and suffer from OOD issues during mixup, reducing explanation fidelity. A discrete, information-bottleneck-driven approach with in-distribution mixup is desirable to improve robustness and interpretability of GNN explanations.

Method: HPME combines hard perturbation-based extraction of explanatory subgraphs via graph pooling with a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck to compress away label-irrelevant information, yielding an information-capacity bound. It introduces a structure-level replacement mixup strategy that crafts in-distribution explanations by replacing subgraph structures rather than relying on soft masks.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that HPME achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating robust and interpretable explanations, indicating improved fidelity and resilience to distribution shift.

Conclusion: HPME provides a principled, discrete, and robust explanation framework for GNNs by enforcing compression of non-informative components and aligning explanations with in-distribution substructures, achieving superior explanatory quality across tasks.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across a range of applications involving graph-structured data, particularly in high-stakes domains. However, the opaque nature of their decision-making processes limits their trustworthiness and broader adoption. Existing post-hoc explanation methods aim to improve explainability by identifying subgraphs that influence GNN predictions and adopt mixup strategies to alleviate the out-of-distribution (OOD) issue caused by using subgraphs for prediction. Yet, these approaches typically rely on soft masks, which are inherently unable to fully eliminate label-irrelevant information, allowing redundant structures to leak into the mixup process and hindering the resolution of the OOD problem, thereby degrading explanation fidelity. In this work, we propose HPME, a Hard-Perturbation Mixup Explanation framework grounded in a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck, which leverages graph pooling to extract discrete explanatory subgraphs and to yield an information-capacity bound to thoroughly compress label-irrelevant components. Furthermore, we introduce a novel mixup strategy built upon structure-level replacement, generating in-distribution explanations to effectively mitigate the distribution shift. Extensive experiments on diverse tasks demonstrate that HPME achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating robust and interpretable explanations across both synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [270] [Domain-Adapted Small Language Models with Hybrid Post-Processing: Achieving Cost-Efficient, Low-Latency Multi-Label Structured Prediction via LoRA Fine-Tuning on Scarce Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05781)
*Srinivasan Manoharan,Dilipkumar Nallusamy,Sachin Kumar,Haifeng Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid small-LM plus rule-based post-processing achieves high-accuracy, structured compliance evaluation with low data and cost, and fast inference compared to frontier models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce latency, cost, and privacy risks of frontier LLMs for domain-specific structured evaluation by leveraging a domain-adapted small LLM with deterministic post-processing and targeted augmentation.

Method: Fine-tune LLaMA 3.1 8B using LoRA (~2.05% trainable params) on 219 curated examples; apply to multi-label compliance evaluation across 18 output fields; add deterministic post-processing rule layer; incorporate targeted hard-negative augmentation; evaluate on 53 unseen transcripts (production data); deploy on NVIDIA A100.

Result: 100% JSON structural validity on 53 unseen transcripts; 83.0% human-validated overall accuracy; 100% accuracy on the most critical field; inference ≈ 2 seconds; ~2–5x faster than frontier-model APIs; cost ≈ $0.013 per evaluation (vs $0.025–$0.055 for proprietary APIs); 46–76% cost savings.

Conclusion: Domain-adapted small LMs with deterministic post-processing can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while reducing latency, cost, and privacy risk.

Abstract: Deploying frontier large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific structured evaluation tasks often incurs substantial latency, cost, and data privacy overhead. We present a hybrid framework that combines a fine-tuned small language model (LLaMA 3.1 8B, with only 2.05% trainable parameters via LoRA) and a deterministic rule-based post-processing layer. Trained on just 219 curated examples, the system is applied to multi-label compliance evaluation of conversational transcripts spanning 18 heterogeneous output fields. In blind evaluation on 53 previously unseen production transcripts, it achieves 100% JSON structural validity, 83.0% human-validated overall accuracy, and 100% accuracy on the most critical classification field. The proposed approach formalizes a hybrid neural-symbolic decomposition and introduces targeted hard-negative augmentation to improve performance on critical decision boundaries. Running on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, inference completes in approximately 2 seconds, which is 2-5x faster than frontier-model APIs. The system costs only $0.013 per evaluation compared with $0.025-$0.055 for proprietary alternatives, resulting in 46-76% cost savings. These results demonstrate that domain-adapted small language models, when combined with deterministic post-processing, can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while substantially reducing operational cost, latency, and privacy risk.
  Keywords: small language models, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, LoRA, domain adaptation, hybrid inference, compliance evaluation, structured output.

</details>


### [271] [Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks for Counterfactual Outcome Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05797)
*Amirhossein Zare,Amirhessam Zare,Herlock Rahimi,Reza Salarikia,Mohammad Kashkooli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces CausalLongPFN, a pretraining-on-synthetic-episodes prior-fitted in-context predictor for longitudinal causal prediction, achieving competitive counterfactual performance and strong factual predictions without domain-specific retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle robust longitudinal causal inference under time-varying confounding, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear dynamics, and limited domain data by avoiding per-cohort retraining; leverage broad synthetic priors to enable a frozen predictive model.

Method: Pretrain a model on synthetic episodes generated from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, exposing it to treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear state evolution, delayed effects, and cumulative responses. At test time, freeze the model and condition on support trajectories, a query history, and a proposed future treatment sequence to output a predictive distribution for future outcomes. Multi-step predictions are obtained by recursive one-step prediction under the specified treatment sequence; no gradient updates or propensity-model fitting.

Result: On counterfactual benchmarks (branchable cancer, HIV, warfarin) the approach is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines; on factual MIMIC-III trajectory prediction, it performs strongly, suggesting synthetic causal pretraining provides a practical frozen alternative when domain-specific training is costly.

Conclusion: Broad synthetic causal pretraining can yield a useful frozen predictor for longitudinal causal inference, offering competitive performance without repeated domain-specific training.

Abstract: Longitudinal treatment decisions require predicting potential outcomes under future treatment sequences in the presence of time-varying confounding, heterogeneous patient dynamics, and limited domain-specific data. Existing longitudinal causal estimators typically train a new model for each cohort or simulator. We introduce Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks (CausalLongPFN), a prior-fitted in-context predictor for longitudinal causal prediction. The model is pretrained entirely on synthetic episodes sampled from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, exposing it to treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear state evolution, delayed effects, and cumulative treatment responses. At test time, CausalLongPFN is frozen: it conditions on support trajectories, a query history, and a proposed future treatment sequence, and returns a predictive distribution over future outcomes without gradient updates or propensity-model fitting. Multi-step predictions are obtained by recursively applying the one-step predictor under the specified treatment sequence. We evaluate on branchable cancer, HIV, and warfarin benchmarks with ground-truth counterfactual labels, and on factual-only rolling-origin prediction in MIMIC-III ICU trajectories. CausalLongPFN is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines on counterfactual benchmarks and performs strongly on factual MIMIC-III prediction, suggesting that broad synthetic causal pretraining can provide a useful frozen alternative when repeated domain-specific training is costly or impractical.

</details>


### [272] [CaliDist: Calibrating Large Language Models via Behavioral Robustness to Distraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05799)
*Mohammad Anas Jawad,Cornelia Caragea*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CaliDist is a post-hoc calibration method that uses prompt perturbations with semantic distractors to measure and penalize a model's susceptibility to distraction, improving calibration metrics (ECE, Brier Score) on multiple NLU benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To incorporate behavioral robustness into LLM calibration by treating a model's stability under cognitive pressure as a proxy for trustworthy confidence.

Method: Perturb input prompts with semantic distractors, quantify changes in predictions and uncertainty to derive a stability signal, and adaptively scale the model's initial confidence score; evaluated as a post-hoc calibration on seven NLU classification benchmarks across six LLMs.

Result: CaliDist consistently reduces calibration errors (ECE and Brier Score) across benchmarks; ECE drops from 23% to 7% on average (≈70% relative improvement); performance gains hold against strong baselines; code/datasets released.

Conclusion: Behavioral stability under distraction is a powerful, actionable signal for post-hoc calibration of LLMs, enabling significantly improved trustworthiness without retraining.

Abstract: Existing calibration methods for Large Language Models (LLMs) often overlook a critical dimension of trustworthiness: a model's {\em behavioral robustness} to irrelevant or misleading information. In this paper, we argue that a model's true confidence should reflect its stability under cognitive pressure. We introduce \textsc{CaliDist}, a novel post-hoc calibration approach that directly measures and penalizes a model's susceptibility to distraction. \textsc{CaliDist} quantifies how an LLM's predictions and uncertainty change when its input prompt is perturbed with semantic \textit{distractors}. This stability (or lack thereof) signal is then used to adaptively scale the model's initial confidence score. Our extensive experiments on seven Natural Language Understanding classification benchmarks using six distinct LLMs show that \textsc{CaliDist} consistently achieves lower Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier Score compared with strong baselines. Remarkably, our method reduces the ECE from 23\% to 7\% on average--a relative improvement of 70\%--demonstrating that behavioral stability is a powerful signal for calibration. We make our code and datasets available at github.com/m-anas-j/CaliDist.

</details>


### [273] [SALT: When More Rollouts Don't Help in Group-Based Policy Optimization and How to Make Them Matter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05800)
*Powei Chang,Jinpeng Zhang,Chaoqun Sun,MiniWell Tsao,Lianrui Li,Jianxiang Xiang,Chenyu Wang,Yukang Gao,Dongying Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SALT mitigates cancellation in group-relative policy-gradient updates (GRPO) for RL with verifiable rewards by adaptively reweighting gradient geometry via a dominant subspace, improving update effectiveness and performance without changing rewards or rollout sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under GRPO-style normalization, simply increasing rollout count can yield low-rank, signed cancellation in per-rollout gradients, weakening updates. A geometry-aware reweighting mechanism is needed to preserve informative gradient signals.

Method: SALT is a plug-in component that uses sample-wise gradient geometry to reweight group-relative updates. It estimates a dominant shared subspace from the mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies the residual channel when signed cancellation is severe, without modifying the reward model or rollout procedure.

Result: Across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales, SALT improves the effective update geometry and overall performance.

Conclusion: SALT provides a scalable, subspace-adaptive reweighting approach that strengthens gradient signals in GRPO-style updates for RLVR, validating the utility of geometry-aware updates without altering reward or sampling strategies.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) often adopts GRPO-style group-relative updates, sampling multiple rollouts per prompt to construct normalized learning signals. However, merely increasing the number of rollouts does not reliably strengthen learning: under GRPO-style group normalization, per-rollout policy-gradient features can concentrate into a low-rank, signed geometry, causing substantial cancellation during aggregation and weakening the effective update. We address this failure mode with SALT, a Subspace-Adaptive geometry pLug-in componenT that uses sample-wise gradient geometry to reweight the coefficients of group-relative updates. SALT estimates a dominant shared subspace from the mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies the residual channel when signed cancellation is severe. Across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales, SALT improves effective update geometry and performance without modifying the reward model or the rollout sampling procedure

</details>


### [274] [Robust and sparse support vector machine via hybrid truncated loss for supervised classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05814)
*Yuliang Yang,Chen Chen,Yuxiang Liu,Huiru Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid truncated loss for SVM (L_ht) that is sparse and bounded; extended to multi-view (Mv_L_ht-SVM) with consensus/complementarity, solved via ADMM with a working-set; yields better accuracy and fewer support vectors than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve robust, efficient SVM by combining bounded non-convex losses' robustness with sparsity and computational tractability; address outliers and heavy computation in existing convex or fully non-convex losses.

Method: Introduce L_ht loss; derive P-stationary point and first-order optimality; propose ADMM with working-set; extend to multi-view with structural information and view weights under consensus and complementarity.

Result: Single-view L_ht-SVM delivers higher accuracy, fewer support vectors, and better noise robustness than five baselines; multi-view Mv_L_ht-SVM surpasses six baselines in accuracy, precision, recall, F1.

Conclusion: The L_ht loss and optimization framework provide robust, sparse, scalable SVM variants for single- and multi-view learning, with convergence guarantees under P-stationarity and ADMM; future work could explore kernelization and larger datasets.

Abstract: The support vector machine (SVM) is a widely used classifier, but choosing an appropriate loss function remains difficult. Convex losses such as the hinge loss and least-squares loss are sensitive to outliers, while bounded non-convex losses often lead to high computational cost. To address this, we propose a hybrid truncated loss function ($L_{\mathrm{ht}}$) that is both sparse and bounded, and build the $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM model for single-view classification. We introduce the P-stationary point and use it to establish the first-order necessary and sufficient optimality conditions. Based on these conditions, we design an alternating direction method of multipliers with a working-set strategy that reduces computational cost and achieves global convergence. We further extend $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM to multi-view learning by adding structural information and view weights, resulting in Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM, which follows both the consensus and complementarity principles. Experiments on synthetic, real-world, and image datasets show that $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM achieves higher accuracy with fewer support vectors and better noise robustness than five single-view methods, while Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM outperforms six multi-view methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

</details>


### [275] [Consistency Training Along the Transformer Stack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05817)
*Sukrati Gautam,Neil Shah,Arav Dhoot,Bryan Maruyama,Caroline Wei,Rohan Kapoor,Robert Sidey,Prakhar Gupta,Zi Cheng Huang,David Demitri Africa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Consistency training with two new internal targets (MLPCT and AttCT) improves alignment across multiple safety threats, showing cross-threat generalization and suggesting a shared residual-stream mechanism; BCT is mechanistically distinct, supporting a unified, extensible defense framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To broaden the scope of consistency training beyond previously studied failure modes (sycophancy and jailbreak) and extend defenses to additional model pathologies (persona in-context attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment).

Method: Introduce two internal consistency targets (MLPCT: matching post-activation MLP states; AttCT: matching per-head attention distributions) and apply them to four safety threats across several models. Evaluate misalignment reduction, cross-threat generalization, and underlying mechanisms.

Result: Consistency training reduces misalignment beyond prior settings; evidence of cross-threat generalization where training on one failure mode aids robustness to others; identification of a shared residual-stream mechanism for ACT/MLPCT/AttCT, while BCT remains mechanistically distinct.

Conclusion: Positioning consistency training as a flexible and extensible alignment framework capable of defending a broader class of model pathologies and unifying defenses across multiple threat scenarios.

Abstract: Consistency training encourages models to behave similarly across different contexts, and has shown promise for reducing misalignment. We broaden the scope of consistency training in two ways. First, we introduce two new internal consistency targets: MLP Consistency Training (MLPCT), which matches post-activation MLP states, and Attention Consistency Training (AttCT), which matches per-head attention distributions. Second, we apply consistency training to four additional safety threats: persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment. Across several models and threat settings, we find that consistency training reduces misalignment well beyond the sycophancy and jailbreak settings studied in prior work. We also find cases of cross-threat generalization, where training against one failure mode improves robustness to another, and identify a shared residual-stream mechanism underlying ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT, while distinguishing BCT as mechanistically distinct. Our results suggest that consistency training is a flexible and extensible framework for alignment, capable of unifying defenses against a broader class of model pathologies.

</details>


### [276] [GenAutoML: An Agentic Framework for Dynamic Architecture Generation and Optimization in Time-Series Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05860)
*Oleeviya Babu Poikarayil,Cédric Schockaert,Abdulrahman Nahhas,Christian Daase,Mursal Dawodi,Jawid Ahmad Baktash*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GenAutoML uses LLMs as neural architects with a sandboxed reflection loop and runtime safeguards to automatically generate lightweight, task-specific time-series architectures. It introduces Dyn-RevIN for non-stationary data, and reports a WaveInterferenceNet with sub-0.01 ms per-sample latency on ETTh1, ETTm1, Weather benchmarks, aiming at edge-friendly models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce manual neural architecture search for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection; overcome static AutoML search spaces; enable adaptable, efficient models for resource-constrained and latency-sensitive settings.

Method: Leverages an LLM to design PyTorch architectures; uses a Sandboxed Reflection Loop to autonomously refine code; a Signature-Aware Runtime enforces consistency and safety during execution; Dyn-RevIN wrapper improves robustness to non-stationarity; evaluates on ETTh1, ETTm1, Weather; identifies WaveInterferenceNet with very low latency.

Result: GenAutoML demonstrates dynamic task-specific architecture generation; achieves ultra-lightweight models with competitive accuracy; WaveInterferenceNet achieves <0.01 ms per-sample inference latency; demonstrates robustness across non-stationary conditions; suitable for edge AI.

Conclusion: Highlights computational efficiency, adaptability, and stable optimization; presents a viable path to ultra-lightweight, efficient neural networks for edge deployments; further work likely required to assess generalization and reproducibility.

Abstract: Designing neural architectures for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection remains a resource-intensive task that often requires substantial domain expertise. Traditional Automated Machine Learning (AutoML) systems typically rely on static, predefined search spaces, limiting their ability to adapt to diverse data characteristics. We present GenAutoML, an agentic framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as neural architects to bridge natural-language requirements and executable PyTorch implementations. The framework incorporates a Sandboxed Reflection Loop for autonomous code refinement and a Signature-Aware Runtime that enforces architectural consistency and execution safety. To improve robustness under non-stationary conditions, we further introduce a Dynamic Reversible Instance Normalization (Dyn-RevIN) wrapper. Experiments on the ETTh1, ETTm1, and Weather benchmarks demonstrate that GenAutoML can dynamically generate task-specific neural architectures tailored to dataset characteristics. Among the generated models, WaveInterferenceNet achieves inference latency below 0.01 ms per sample while maintaining competitive predictive performance. By emphasizing computational efficiency, architectural adaptability, and stable optimization behavior, GenAutoML enables the creation of ultra-lightweight neural networks suitable for resource-constrained and latency-sensitive Edge AI deployments.

</details>


### [277] [Deciphering Two Training Clocks in Grokking via Deep Linear Network Theory with Conditional ReLU Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05863)
*Hu Tan,Kuo Gai,Shihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a two-timescale (two clocks) view of grokking: rapid loss minimization and slower representation simplification; proves logarithmic-time convergence for deep linear nets under certain conditions, and polynomial-time convergence with weight-decay regularization; extends to ReLU nets via conditional reductions, suggesting a two-stage learning where the classifier fits before the representation improves, supported by modular-addition experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain grokking as a separation of learning dynamics into fast fitting and slow representation shaping, and provide a theoretical framework that reconciles rapid loss decrease with gradual emergence of simple underlying representations.

Method: Theoretical analysis of deep linear networks with post-margin gap-growth or tail-contraction conditions; incorporate layerwise weight decay yielding a Schatten-type penalty and analyze convergence under a Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail; extend to ReLU MLPs by considering fixed activation patterns yielding linearity in active coordinates; use chain-rule estimates in a two-layer ReLU embedding to compare gradient magnitudes between classifier head and embedding; experimental focus on modular addition as the testbed.

Result: In deep linear networks, cross-entropy loss reaches ε on a logarithmic time scale under the stated conditions; with weight decay, the end-to-end map converges on a polynomial time scale due to a Schatten-type regularization and KL-tail; for ReLU models, a two-stage mechanism is supported in regions with fixed activation patterns, where the classifier can receive larger effective gradients and fitting occurs before representation simplification; results are presented as conditional reductions rather than a single global nonlinear training-proof, and experiments with modular addition illustrate the theory.

Conclusion: Two training clocks separate fitting from representation simplification, with the deep-linear theory providing a rigorous core and the ReLU results offering conditional reductions consistent with observed grokking; the work highlights a staged learning picture (classifier-first, representation-late) and acknowledges limits in extending the full nonlinear dynamics to a global proof.

Abstract: Grokking suggests that fitting the training data and learning a simple underlying rule may occur on different time scales. We formalize this phenomenon by separating the fast decay of the classification loss from the slower simplification of the learned representation, and we call the resulting pair of stopping times two training clocks. For deep linear networks, we show that a post-margin gap-growth or one-step tail-contraction condition reduces the cross-entropy loss to level epsilon on a logarithmic time scale. In contrast, when layerwise weight decay is present, the induced regularization on the end-to-end map can be expressed as a Schatten-type penalty; under a sharp late-time Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail, this structural energy closes on a polynomial time scale. The two clocks, therefore, separate fitting from representation simplification. We then explain how the same mechanism can appear in ReLU MLPs. In regions where the activation patterns on the training set remain fixed, the network reduces to a linear model in the active coordinates. In a two-layer ReLU embedding model, chain-rule estimates further show that the classifier head can receive larger effective gradients than the embedding block under controlled downstream norms. This supports a two-stage mechanism in which the classifier fits first, while the representation continues to simplify later. We use modular addition as the main experimental setting. The deep linear theory provides the rigorous core of the analysis. But the ReLU results are formulated as conditional reductions that account for empirical behavior without claiming a global proof for nonlinear training dynamics.

</details>


### [278] [TS-ICL: A Flexible Time-Indexed Foundation Model for Time Series via In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05878)
*Etienne Le Naour,Tahar Nabil,Adrien Petralia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TS-ICL is a probabilistic in-context learning encoder–regressor Transformer for time series that unifies forecasting and imputation, leveraging a causal data prior to incorporate covariates. It achieves state-of-the-art imputation and competitive forecasting, especially with partially observed look-back windows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time series are often irregular and partially observed, requiring models that can jointly forecast, impute missing values, and handle degraded sampling. Foundation models enable zero-shot generalization but current work mainly focuses on forecasting without integrating imputation or variable covariates.

Method: TS-ICL uses a probabilistic in-context learning encoder–regressor Transformer. It treats tasks as timestamp-aligned regression and trains on synthetic dependency structures generated from a novel causal data prior to incorporate covariates.

Result: Achieves new state-of-the-art in imputation and remains competitive with leading forecasting foundation models across univariate and covariate-aware benchmarks; exhibits strong performance for forecasting with partially observed look-back windows.

Conclusion: TS-ICL demonstrates a unified framework for joint forecasting and imputation in time series, leveraging causal priors to handle covariates and degraded sampling, and highlighting the potential of foundation-model-like approaches in time-series tasks.

Abstract: Foundation models mark a profound paradigm shift in time series modeling, with task-specific models being superseded by general-purpose zero-shot models. Yet, current approaches primarily focus on forecasting, while real-world time series are often irregularly and partially observed, requiring models that can jointly forecast, impute missing values, and handle degraded sampling conditions. To address these challenges, we introduce TS-ICL, a novel probabilistic In-Context Learning encoder--regressor Transformer that unifies forecasting and imputation. TS-ICL formulates time series tasks as timestamp-aligned regression and naturally incorporates covariates by training on synthetic dependency structures generated from a novel causal data prior. Empirically, TS-ICL achieves a new state-of-the-art in imputation, while remaining competitive with leading forecasting foundation models across both univariate and covariate-aware benchmarks. It shows particularly strong performance in forecasting with partially observed look-back windows.

</details>


### [279] [When Denser Credit Is Not Enough: Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon LLM Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05885)
*Yuanfan Li,Qi Zhou,Wenjing Duan,Lu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ECPO is a critic-free RL method for long-horizon LLM agents that calibrates step-level credit to avoid unreliable dense credit; it uses evidence-calibrated action advantage and variance-gated credit weighting, and shows gains over GiGPO on ALFWorld and WebShop with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve instability and divergence from dense, anonymous step-level credit in sparse, delayed reward settings; enable reliable credit allocation for long-horizon decision processes.

Method: Proposes ECPO: Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage groups rollouts by canonical actions and shrinks low-count estimates; Variance-Gated Credit Weighting suppresses anchor states dominated by within-action noise; critic-free; calibrates credit before policy updates.

Result: On ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen models (2.5-1.5B and 7B), ECPO outperforms GiGPO by +5.2 and +7.3 success points; adds only 0.1% extra overhead for advantage computation.

Conclusion: ECPO provides more stable and robust long-horizon policy optimization by calibrating credit, reducing anchor bias and oscillations, with strong empirical gains and minimal overhead.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents require reinforcement learning methods that can assign credit to intermediate decisions under sparse and delayed rewards. Recent group-based methods such as GiGPO improve over GRPO by constructing step-level advantages at repeated anchor states. However, we show that such dense credit can be statistically unreliable: under limited rollouts, rare but lucky actions may receive overly large advantages, producing divergent anchor bias and late-stage training oscillation. We propose Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization (ECPO), a critic-free policy optimization algorithm that calibrates step-level credit before policy updates. ECPO combines Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage, which groups rollouts by canonical actions and shrinks low-count estimates, with Variance-Gated Credit Weighting, which suppresses anchor states dominated by within-action noise. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B show that ECPO consistently outperforms strong baselines, improving GiGPO by +5.2/+7.3 success points on ALFWorld/WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B while adding only 0.1% additional advantage-computation overhead.

</details>


### [280] [High-Dimensional Theory of LoRA Fine-Tuning in a Solvable Attention Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05899)
*O. Duranthon,F. Boncoraglio,L. Zdeborová*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A solvable high-dimensional model for LoRA in a pre-trained single-head attention layer shows that pre-training acts as an effective noise term in the LoRA fine-tuning stage, enabling explicit order-parameter predictions of test error and alignment; reveals regimes where test error and representation quality diverge and provides implications for active fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how pre-training and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) interact in high-dimensional attention networks, and quantify their impact on test performance and learned representations to guide practical design.

Method: Construct a tractable, high-dimensional limit analysis of a two-stage setup: (i) pre-train a single-head attention layer on data-rich task; (ii) adapt via a rank-one LoRA update using limited data. Derive a finite set of order parameters that characterize both stages, enabling asymptotic predictions of test error and representation alignment; model pre-training impact as an effective noise term; analyze a regime with test-error vs representation-quality mismatch; discuss extension to active fine-tuning.

Result: The theory yields explicit asymptotic expressions for test error and representation alignment as functions of data regime and LoRA rank. It condenses pre-training effects into an effective noise parameter, from which optimal pre-training prescriptions are derived. Identifies a mismatch regime where good representations do not translate to the best test error and demonstrates applicability to active fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled, predictive lens for designing pre-training and LoRA-based fine-tuning in high-dimensional attention models, highlighting how pre-training noise shapes downstream adaptation and offering actionable guidance for active fine-tuning strategies.

Abstract: We develop a high-dimensional statistical theory of low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in attention models, capturing the interplay between pre-training and fine-tuning. We introduce a solvable framework in which a single-head attention layer is first pre-trained on a data-abundant task and subsequently adapted via a rank-one LoRA update on limited data. In the high-dimensional limit, both stages admit a sharp asymptotic characterization in terms of a finite set of order parameters, yielding explicit predictions for test errors and representation alignment. Our analysis shows that the impact of pre-training on LoRA is summarized by an effective noise term, from which we derive prescriptions for the optimal pre-training procedure. We also demonstrate a regime with a mismatch between the value of the test error and representation quality, and propose an application of our theory to active fine-tuning.

</details>


### [281] [Addressing Imbalance in Multi-Label Data via Label-Specific Distance-based Oversampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05927)
*Bin Liu,Jun Wu,Haoyu Peng,Ao Zhou,Jin Wang,QiaoSong Chen,Grigorios Tsoumakas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LSDMLO is a label-aware oversampling method for multi-label classification that uses label-specific distances in a weighted feature space to generate higher-quality synthetic samples, reducing label inconsistency and improving performance over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-label datasets often have highly imbalanced label distributions. Traditional oversampling relies on global Euclidean distances and can produce synthetic instances that mix unrelated labels, causing label confusion and overfitting.

Method: Introduce Label-Specific Distance-based Multi-Label Oversampling (LSDMLO). It computes label-specific distances by weighting features per label to identify label-consistent neighbors. Seed instances in boundary regions with strong label correlations are selected, and synthetic samples are generated to align with the original label distribution.

Result: Experiments show LSDMLO outperforms state-of-the-art multi-label sampling approaches across various base classifiers.

Conclusion: Incorporating label-aware distances and feature weighting yields more label-consistent synthetic data, improving oversampling effectiveness for multi-label classification.

Abstract: The complex imbalanced label distribution poses a crucial challenge to multi-label classification, as most classifiers are biased towards the majority class and high-frequent labels. Oversampling is an efficient and flexible solution that augments instances to provide a more balanced training dataset for multi-label classifiers. Most existing oversampling methods create synthetic instances in a heuristic way that essentially relies on neighborhood information retrieved using Euclidean distance within the entire feature space. However, they fail to consider the varying semantic relevance of features to different labels, leading to label inconsistency among proximate neighbors and further introducing label confusion and overfitting to synthetic instances. To overcome the above issue, we propose a novel sampling approach called Label-Specific Distance-based Multi-Label Oversampling (LSDMLO) that creates more useful and well-labeled synthetic instances to address the imbalance in multi-label datasets. LSDMLO derives the label-specific distance to identify label-consistent neighbors based on the weighted pertinent feature space, which facilitates selecting seed instances that express more label correlations in boundary areas and generating synthetic instances aligned with the label distribution of original data. The comprehensive experiments verify that the proposed LSDMLO outperforms the state-of-the-art multi-label sampling approaches under various base classifiers.

</details>


### [282] [Short paper: Models in the dark -- Rectification and erasure under GDPR in ML supply chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05946)
*Henrik Graßhoff,Malte Hansen,Meiko Jensen,Sara Ramezanian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GDPR rights to rectification and erasure are hard to enforce in ML due to supply-chain complexity; current work lacks integrated legal-technical treatment and introduces the 'models in the dark' risk; advocates interdisciplinary approaches to align law and practice for trustworthy AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protect individuals' data rights in ML by addressing gaps between GDPR requirements and technical capabilities, especially across development, distribution, and deployment stages of ML supply chains.

Method: Holistic literature review of academic sources and data protection guidance; conceptual analysis introducing 'models in the dark' and interdisciplinary assessment of enforcement challenges.

Result: Shows that many GDPR requirements are not technically feasible yet in practice; highlights under-addressed supply-chain issues; identifies 'models in the dark' as a pressing challenge needing transparency and traceability to satisfy data subject rights.

Conclusion: Bridging legal and technical perspectives is essential for trustworthy AI; the work motivates further research and policy guidance to mitigate enforcement gaps across ML supply chains.

Abstract: The rights to rectification and erasure, as established under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), are central to protecting individuals' privacy. However, their effective enforcement in machine learning (ML) systems remains challenging. Existing work has largely addressed these rights from either a legal or a technical perspective in isolation and disregards the fact that models are produced in complex supply chains involving multiple actors across development, distribution, and deployment. This paper presents a holistic survey of challenges in implementing the rights to rectification and erasure in ML models.
