Drawing on academic literature and guidance from data protection authorities, we find that many GDPR requirements cannot yet be technically met in practice. Our findings further suggest that issues arising in ML supply chains are insufficiently addressed in research. To tackle this gap, we introduce the notion of models in the dark -- derived models created further downstream in an ML chain without sufficient transparency or traceability -- and analyse the urgent challenges posed by this phenomenon. By adopting an interdisciplinary perspective, this work contributes to bridging the gap between legal requirements and the technical implementation of data subject rights in ML, ultimately supporting the development of trustworthy artificial intelligence.

</details>


### [283] [Dead Directions: Geometric Singular Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05957)
*Tejas Pradeep Shirodkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unify singular learning theory and information geometry via the dead direction (degenerate Fisher metric), derive KL-order rates to predict architecture-level singularity behavior, and implement a G-invariant Adam (DDCAdam) to extract Watanabe invariants from a single checkpoint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge two often-disjoint formalisms (Bayesian invariants in resolved coordinates vs. KL-order singularity analysis in original coordinates) to obtain actionable, architecture-aware predictions and optimization tools for overparameterized models.

Method: Define the dead direction as a unit tangent to the analytic singular set where the Fisher metric degenerates; relate its KL-order to the decay rate of directional Fisher curvature, without resorting to Hironaka resolution. Extend to multi-component crossings (multiplicity m), singular fluctuation ν, prior-RLCT shifts, and tempered posteriors; map the rate to Watanabe's real log canonical threshold (RLCT). Apply a deep-network interpretation via multi-layer K-FAC factorization, yielding per-architecture activation- and gradient-side rates; prove a quotient theorem for gradient flow on a G-invariant metric, with SGD admissible and standard Adam not; introduce DDCAdam, a G-equivariant Adam-family preconditioner.

Result: Provides closed-form, per-architecture predictions of singularity behavior; enables recovering Watanabe’s RLCT invariants (λ, m, ν) from a single forward/backward pass without posterior sampling; establishes a gauge-quotient framework under gradient flow and a practical, symmetry-aware optimizer (DDCAdam).

Conclusion: The work unifies singular geometry and information geometry into a computable framework for deep nets, offering a parameter-coordinate handle on singularities and a toolkit to infer KL-order invariants and perform symmetry-aware optimization directly from checkpoints.

Abstract: Singular learning theory and information geometry have studied the same parameter spaces in mostly separate vocabularies: the former computes Bayesian invariants in resolved coordinates, the latter works in original coordinates under a non-degeneracy assumption that overparameterised models routinely violate. We bridge them through one primitive, the dead direction: a unit vector along which the Fisher metric degenerates, equivalently a tangent to the analytic singular set with a definite KL order, set by how fast the KL divergence vanishes. The two readings name the same vector; our central move shows its KL order is recoverable as the decay rate of the directional Fisher curvature approaching the singularity, in original parameter coordinates and without a Hironaka resolution. A selection rule on smooth fibres translates this rate into Watanabe's single-direction contribution to the real log canonical threshold, and we extend the recovery to multi-component crossings, multiplicity $m$, the singular fluctuation $ν$ (universal in the KL order for 1D directions), prior-RLCT shifts, and tempered posteriors. We then lift this rate to a deep network: a multi-layer K-FAC factorisation writes each Fisher block as a product of activation- and gradient-side rates with a duality between them, instantiated at modern-network primitives (residual streams, layer normalisation, attention). A quotient theorem carries the rate to the gauge quotient $Θ/G$ under gradient flow on a $G$-invariant metric; SGD qualifies, standard Adam does not, and we construct a $G$-equivariant Adam-family preconditioner (DDCAdam) that does. The bridge yields a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form per-architecture predictions, and a trajectory-rate readout of Watanabe's triple $(λ, m, ν)$ from one checkpoint's forward and backward passes, without posterior sampling.

</details>


### [284] [Steering Vectors are an Adversarial Attack Surface](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05958)
*Abzal Aidakhmetov,Donato Crisostomi,Tommaso Mencattini,Adrian Robert Minut,Iacopo Masi,Emanuele Rodolà*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stealth data poisoning attacks can subtly hijack activation steering by poisoning 4-6% of tokens, aligning steering vectors with an anti-refusal direction; attackers can distribute safe-looking bundles with vectors and an equivalence certificate; absolute attack success rate (ASR) observed at 20-55%, with up to +19% to +51% relative to clean baselines; a defense based on refusal-direction orthogonalization recovers ~82% of the ASR gap without harming benign prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate vulnerability and mitigation for plug-and-play activation steering in LLMs against stealth data poisoning.

Method: Poison steering datasets by substituting 4-6% of tokens; test on two open-weight model families across eight model-attribute combinations; measure ASR and compare to clean references; propose a defense via refusal-direction orthogonalization.

Result: Poisoned steering vectors yield 20-55% ASR; improvement of +19% to +51% over clean baselines; orthogonalization defense recovers ~82% of the ASR gap with negligible impact on benign behavior.

Conclusion: Activation steering is susceptible to stealth data poisoning; orthogonalization of refusal directions offers substantial mitigation while preserving benign performance; broader evaluation and hardened defenses are needed.

Abstract: Activation steering has become a popular way to control Large Language Model (LLM) behavior without fine-tuning. Since the technique is plug-and-play, users share datasets and precomputed vectors to steer model activations. However, we show that a \emph{stealth data poisoning attack} silently compromises this pipeline. By substituting $4{-}6\%$ of tokens in the steering dataset, an attacker can silently align the resulting vector with an anti-refusal direction. This jailbreaks the target model while preserving the intended steering effect on benign prompts. Under this threat model, a malicious actor can distribute an apparently safe bundle containing texts, vectors, and weights, alongside an equivalence certificate that the end-user can verify. We test the attack on two open-weight model families and eight model-attribute combinations, observing that poisoned vectors reach an absolute attack success rate (ASR) of $20{-}55\%$, $+19\%$ to $+51\%$ over a clean reference. Finally, we find that a refusal-direction orthogonalization defense can recover ${\approx}82\%$ of the ASR gap without harming benign behavior.

</details>


### [285] [LLM Explainability with Counterfactual Chains and Causal Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05972)
*Nirit Nussbaum-Hoffer,Nitay Calderon,Liat Ein-Dor,Roi Reichart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Four-phase method to model LLM inference with causal graphs, introducing MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation and σ-CG for stable, interpretable concept-level graphs; validated across three tasks (disease diagnosis, sentiment, LLM-as-a-judge) with strong predictive fidelity and structural stability, advancing explainability of LLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide transparent, human-interpretable insight into how LLMs organize high-level concepts to produce predictions, moving from modeling external processes to modeling the model's internal inference.

Method: Four-phase pipeline: (1) identify class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts; (2) map each input to LLM-perceived concept states; (3) apply an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation to expand sparse observational data; (4) perform causal discovery with σ-CG; evaluate predictive fidelity and structural stability, convergence, and downstream utility across tasks.

Result: Discovered causal graphs reveal meaningful dependencies consistent with LLM reasoning; augmentation yields stable convergence; graphs are informative and interpretable across three LLM tasks.

Conclusion: The work provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs and a scalable framework for inspecting how LLMs organize concepts to reach predictions, with potential broad applicability.

Abstract: Causal graphs provide a high-level language for making mechanisms transparent. Recent work uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to recover causal graphs of external-world processes. Instead, in this paper, we use causal graphs to model LLM inference itself, providing stakeholders with a transparent view of how the model perceives and organizes high-level concepts to produce a prediction. We propose a four-phase method for constructing such graphs. Given a target LLM and a set of textual examples, our method discovers class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts and maps each input to LLM-perceived concept states. We then introduce an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation procedure that expands the sparse observational data through chains of counterfactuals. This enables stable causal discovery with $σ$-CG, yielding informative, interpretable graphs. We apply our method to three LLMs across disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge classification tasks. We evaluate the learned graphs for predictive fidelity and structural stability, and the MCMC-inspired augmentation for convergence and downstream utility. Our results show that the discovered causal graphs capture meaningful dependencies consistent with LLMs' reasoning. Together, this paper provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs.

</details>


### [286] [Compress-Distill: Reasoning Trace Compression for Efficient Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05988)
*Maxime Griot,Paul Steven Scotti,Tanishq Mathew Abraham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc compression of reasoning traces for distillation yields strong efficiency gains but at some accuracy cost; raw traces outperform compressed ones in accuracy across scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the cost and latency of distilling reasoning traces (chain-of-thought) without sacrificing too much downstream performance.

Method: Use two large teachers to generate reasoning traces; apply instruction-tuned models to compress traces to 8.6-21% of original length; evaluate on a broad grid of experiments and ablations, comparing against raw traces and length-matched truncations; measure training token counts, speedups, and downstream accuracy.

Result: Compression substantially reduces training tokens (to 12-30% of raw), speeds training 2.0–7.6x and shortens inference outputs (3–19x); however raw traces yield higher downstream accuracy at all scales; a length-matched truncation shows model-compressed traces outperform naive truncation in many cases, especially for smaller students; overall, the approach yields an accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a free improvement (up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy retained and up to 18x per-token efficiency; at 0.8B LoRA-compressed traces, the raw/compressed gap narrows but is not exceeded).

Conclusion: Post-hoc trace compression offers controllable efficiency gains with a nontrivial accuracy cost; practitioners can balance efficiency against accuracy depending on resource constraints and model size.

Abstract: Reasoning models produce long chain-of-thought traces that are costly to distill and encourage verbose student outputs. We study post-hoc compression of such traces before knowledge distillation. Two teachers, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B, generate about 283k correct traces each; two instruction-tuned models then compress them to 8.6-21.0% of their original character length. Across a 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, compressed traces reduce training tokens to 12-30% of raw, speed up training by 2.0-7.6x, and shorten inference outputs by 3-19x with smaller reductions under the shorter gpt-oss teacher. However, raw traces retain the highest downstream accuracy at every scale and for both teachers. A length-matched raw-trace truncation ablation shows that compression is not merely benefiting from a smaller token budget: model-compressed traces usually beat or match naive truncation, especially for smaller students, while maintaining shorter inference outputs. Overall, reasoning-trace compression offers an accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a free improvement: students retain up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy while gaining up to 18x higher per-token efficiency, and at the 0.8B scale under LoRA compressed traces narrow the raw-vs-compressed gap but do not exceed raw.

</details>


### [287] [HoT-SSM:Higher-order Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning with State Space Models for Health Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05994)
*Thummaluru Siddartha Reddy,Vempalli Naga Sai Saketh,Yash Punjabi,Mahesh Chandran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HoT-SSM introduces higher-order temporal graph reasoning for medical knowledge graphs by constructing visit-level hypergraphs and a dynamic hypergraph state-space model to capture long-range temporal dependencies, achieving improved mortality prediction on MIMIC-III/IV.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MKG-based approaches primarily capture pairwise relationships and often collapse temporal information across visits, hindering modeling of higher-order interactions and long-range temporal patterns essential for clinical tasks such as mortality prediction.

Method: For each visit, construct hypergraphs by grouping semantically related clinical concepts into hyperedges using domain knowledge; employ a dynamic hypergraph-based state-space model to learn latent patient state evolution over time, preserving long-range information with a parameter-efficient design.

Result: Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV show significant improvements over state-of-the-art models, validating the effectiveness of jointly modeling higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal dependencies.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling higher-order interactions and long-range temporal dependencies with hypergraph and state-space approaches enhances interpretable clinical predictions and reasoning, with potential applicability to other MKG-driven tasks.

Abstract: Medical knowledge graphs (MKGs) infused with clinical knowledge have been increasingly used to model electronic health records (EHRs) to support interpretable predictions in healthcare domain. However, existing MKG-based approaches are limited in capturing pairwise relations between clinical concepts (e.g., conditions, procedures, and medications), and restricts their ability to model higher-order interactions among co-occurring or semantically related concepts. In addition, most representation learning methods that leverage MKGs either collapse temporal information across visits or lack an explicit mechanism for modeling long-range temporal dependencies, which is critical for clinical tasks such as mortality prediction. To mitigate these limitations, we propose HoT-SSM, a parameter efficient and higher-order temporal graph reasoning with state space models. For each visit, HoT-SSM constructs hypergraphs by grouping semantically related clinical concepts into hyperedges using domain knowledge, thereby preserving visit-level clinical context. Further, to model the temporal dynamics while learning the representations, we introduce a novel dynamic hypergraph-based state space model that explicitly captures patients latent state evolution over time while preserving long-range information. The learned representations are used for downstream clinical prediction and reasoning. Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets shows significant performance improvement over the current state-of-the-art models, demonstrating the effectiveness of jointly modeling higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal dependencies.

</details>


### [288] [Diffusion Models for Adaptive Sequential Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06007)
*Haoyang Cao,Minshuo Chen,Yinbin Han,Renyuan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a sequential forward-backward diffusion framework for adapted time-series generation with a novel score-matching objective, guaranteeing theoretical properties and demonstrated effectiveness on synthetic data and portfolio optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To generate realistic sequential data with strong temporal dependence without leaking future information; existing diffusion models struggle to extend naturally to sequential settings.

Method: A sequential forward-backward diffusion process that progressively injects and removes noise along the sequence, conditioning on previously generated history to ensure adaptiveness. Introduces a new score-matching objective enabling efficient parallel training. Provides rigorous statistical guarantees under a generic framework, with concrete instantiations using ReLU networks for score/distribution estimation.

Result: Empirical validation on synthetic data (ARMA models, Gaussian processes) and demonstration of constructing mean-variance optimal portfolios.

Conclusion: The framework advances adaptive sequential generation with theoretical guarantees and practical efficacy, offering a principled approach to diffusion-based time-series synthesis and potential for broad applicability across domains.

Abstract: Generating realistic synthetic sequential data is critical in real-world applications across operations research, finance, healthcare, energy systems, and scientific computing, where time-indexed observations are used for prediction, simulation, risk assessment, and data-driven decision-making. While diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in generating static data, their direct extensions to sequential settings often fail to capture temporal dependence and information structure. Designing diffusion models that can simulate sequential data in an adapted manner, and hence without anticipation of future information, therefore remains an open challenge.
  In this work, we propose a sequential forward-backward diffusion framework for adapted time series generation. Our approach progressively injects and removes noise along the sequence, conditioning on the previously generated history to ensure adaptiveness. A novel score-matching objective is introduced for efficient parallel training. We derive rigorous statistical guarantees under a generic framework, then establish score approximation, score estimation, and distribution estimation results with ReLU networks serving as a concrete instance. Empirically, we validate our method on synthetic data, including ARMA models and Gaussian processes, and demonstrate its effectiveness in constructing mean-variance optimal portfolios.

</details>


### [289] [Adaptive Oscillatory-State Alignment for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06010)
*Zhangyao Song,Ziqiong Li,Xiangfei Qiu,Chao Zha,Yinfei Xu,Tao Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AOSNET introduces Hilbert-guided adaptive alignment of oscillatory states for long-horizon forecasting, replacing fixed periodic templates with a learnable flexible prior, achieving competitive or state-of-the-art accuracy with fast inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world temporal dynamics often exhibit amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation. Fixed-template periodic models struggle with such non-stationarity, motivating an adaptive framework that can align oscillatory states.

Method: Extract analytic-signal descriptors from both the observed sequence and a global oscillatory prior, then adaptively align local states via a descriptor-conditioned gate that preserves reliable observations while softly correcting mismatched regions. The prior is a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics (Hilbert-guided).

Result: Experiments on eight benchmarks show state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy with fast inference. Controlled synthetic studies demonstrate that the advantage of oscillatory-state alignment grows with increasing non-stationarity (amplitude modulation, phase drift, local frequency variation).

Conclusion: Adaptive oscillatory-state alignment using analytic-signal descriptors and a learnable prior provides robust long-term forecasting under non-stationarity, with efficient inference and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Long-term time series forecasting benefits from inductive biases that expose recurring temporal structure. Existing periodic forecasting methods typically model recurrence through predefined periods, global spectral components, or fixed learnable templates. However, real-world temporal dynamics are rarely rigidly periodic: oscillatory behavior often evolves through amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation. Under these conditions, fixed-template periodic modeling can become fundamentally mismatched to the underlying temporal states. We propose AOSNET, a Hilbert-guided forecasting framework that reformulates periodic forecasting from fixed template matching to adaptive oscillatory-state alignment. AOSNET extracts analytic-signal descriptors from both the observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior, then adaptively aligns local states through a descriptor-conditioned gate that selectively preserves reliable observations while softly correcting mismatched regions. The learned prior serves not as a rigid repeated template but as a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics. Experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy with fast inference speed. Controlled synthetic studies isolating amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation confirm that the advantage of oscillatory-state alignment consistently increases as non-stationarity intensifies.

</details>


### [290] [OPRD: On-Policy Representation Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06021)
*Shenzhi Yang,Guangcheng Zhu,Bowen Song,Haobo Wang,Mingxuan Xia,Xing Zheng,Yingfan Ma,Zhongqi Chen,Weiqiang Wang,Gang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPRD extends on-policy distillation from output-only to hidden-state space by aligning student and teacher representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD suffers sampling variance in large vocabularies when estimating KL on output probabilities and treats the teacher as a black box, discarding hidden-state information.

Method: Distill in hidden-state space by aligning multi-layer representations between student and teacher on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head entirely.

Result: OPRD reduces sampling variance, provides richer per-layer information, and closes the student–teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO. It trains 1.44× faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD; output-space OPD baselines plateau below the teacher.

Conclusion: OPRD offers both theoretical and practical gains over OPD by leveraging hidden representations for distillation; code is available at the cited URL.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) supervises the student only in output space by matching next-token probabilities. This output-only paradigm has two limits: (1) sampling variance from Monte Carlo KL estimates over large vocabularies (e.g., Qwen's ~150k tokens) persists throughout training, and (2) it treats the teacher as a black-box, discarding all intermediate hidden states after the LM head. We propose On-Policy Representation Distillation (OPRD), which lifts distillation into hidden-state space by aligning student and teacher representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head entirely. Theoretically, OPRD eliminates sampling variance and provides richer per-layer structural information. Empirically, OPRD closes the student-teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO, while output-space OPD baselines plateau below the teacher. OPRD also trains 1.44x faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD. Code: https://github.com/ShenzhiYang2000/OPRD.

</details>


### [291] [Catastrophic Forgetting as Accessibility Collapse: A Three-Level Framework for Knowledge Persistence in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06032)
*Ayushman Trivedi,Bhavika Melwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Forgetting is an accessibility issue, not complete erasure; introduces a three-level framework (storage, representation, accessibility) and evidence from sequential CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18 showing recoverable information persists yet task performance collapses; retraining final classifier recovers much of the lost performance; early layers retain recoverable information; memory persists as distributed high-dimensional representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reassess the canonical view of catastrophic forgetting by disentangling storage, representation, and accessibility to identify where forgetting originates and how it might be mitigated.

Method: Series of continual-learning experiments on sequential CIFAR-100 using ResNet-18, employing checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation-geometry analyses (projection energy, principal angles), classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability to separate the three components.

Result: Complete forgetting behavior for earlier tasks (accuracy 0%), while linear probes retain about 76% of original representational information; retraining only the final classifier recovers ~75.7% of original task performance without changing backbone; early/intermediate layers preserve highly recoverable information; later layers show severe degradation; knowledge persists as distributed high-dimensional representations rather than a single subspace.

Conclusion: For catastrophic forgetting, accessibility failure is a better characterization than representational erasure; robust task-relevant information remains embedded and retraining shallow components can recover much performance.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is commonly interpreted as the irreversible erasure of previously acquired knowledge during sequential learning. In this work, we investigate an alternative perspective: that forgetting may arise not from complete destruction of task representations but from a loss of accessibility to preserved information. We introduce a three-level framework separating knowledge storage, representation, and accessibility, and evaluate each component through a series of continual-learning experiments on sequential CIFAR-100 classification using ResNet-18. Our analysis combines checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry, classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability experiments. We observe complete behavioral forgetting of earlier tasks, with task accuracy collapsing from 54.8% to 0%, while linear probe performance retains approximately 76% of the original representational information. Furthermore, retraining only the final classifier restores 75.7% of the original task performance without modifying the backbone network. Layer-wise analysis reveals that early and intermediate layers preserve highly recoverable task information despite severe degradation at later stages. Projection-energy and principal-angle analyses indicate that retained knowledge persists as distributed high-dimensional representations rather than through preservation of a small dominant subspace. These findings suggest that catastrophic forgetting is better characterized as an accessibility failure than complete representational erasure, and that substantial task-relevant information remains embedded within neural representations even after functional forgetting has occurred.

</details>


### [292] [When Good Enough Is Optimal: Multiplication-Only Matrix Inversion Approximation for Quantized Gated DeltaNet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06034)
*Luoming Zhang,Yuwei Ren,Kui Zhang,Tian Liu,Lingjuan Ge,Denghao Li,Matthew Harper Langston,Yin Huang,Weiliang Will Zeng,Liang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fast MatMul-based approach with truncated Neumann expansion and masking for chunk-wise linear attention, achieving significant kernel speedups and reduced decode overhead while preserving accuracy across FP and INT8 inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context modeling with chunk-wise linear attention requires matrix inversions that are bottlenecks on NPUs; forward-substitution methods yield limited parallelism and poor hardware utilization. There is a need for a parallel, hardware-friendly inversion method tailored to strictly lower-triangular matrices.

Method: Introduce a Matrix Multiplication (MatMul)-based algorithm for strictly lower-triangular matrices in chunk-wise linear attention. Use a truncated Neumann expansion with structural masking and parallel residual correction to break sequential dependencies. Extend to low-bit INT by mitigating dynamic range expansion from repeated matrix powers. Adapt the approximation order and residual step to the chunk size to balance cost and accuracy.

Result: On Qwen3.5-family models, the method delivers up to 5× kernel-level speedups and around 20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, with preserved accuracy in both floating-point and low-precision inference.

Conclusion: Provides a hardware-friendly, efficient solution for scalable linear attention by transforming the inversion into a parallelizable Neumann-series-based approximation with masking and residual correction, scalable to low-precision regimes.

Abstract: Matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention is a major bottleneck for long-context modeling, particularly on NPUs, where forward-substitution-based methods exhibit limited parallelism and poor hardware utilization. We propose a fast, Matrix Multiplication (MatMul)-based algorithm tailored for strictly lower-triangular matrices arising in chunk-wise linear attention. Motivated by the rapid growth of Neumann-series terms and the diagonal concentration of the inverse matrix, we employ a truncated Neumann expansion with structural masking and parallel residual correction to eliminate sequential dependencies. We further extend our method to low-bits INT by mitigating the dynamic range expansion arising from repeated matrix power operations, and adapt the approximation order and residual step to the chunk size to minimize computational cost while preserving the model's accuracy. Experiments on Qwen3.5-family models demonstrate up to 5$\times$ kernel-level speedup and a 20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, while preserving accuracy under both floating-point and low-precision inference. Our method offers an efficient and hardware-friendly solution for scalable linear attention.

</details>


### [293] [Online KL-Regularized Reinforcement Learning with Function Approximation under Misspecification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06053)
*Haoyang Hong,Zichen Wang,Quanquan Gu,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KL-regularized contextual bandits and episodic RL are analyzed under model misspecification. The paper introduces KL misspecification formulations and studies regression-based algorithms with Gibbs policy updates, yielding high-probability KL-regret bounds that explicitly account for misspecification and recover realizable KL-regularized results as a special case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Realizability-based guarantees are fragile under misspecification common in practice. There is a need for regret bounds for KL-regularized methods that explicitly handle misspecification in both contextual bandits and episodic RL.

Method: Formulate KL misspecification for contextual bandits and episodic RL. Analyze regression-based algorithms with Gibbs policy updates. Derive high-probability KL-regret bounds that include explicit misspecification terms. Show that the standard realizable KL-regularized setting is recovered when misspecification vanishes.

Result: High-probability KL-regret bounds with explicit misspecification terms are established for both settings. The bounds revert to the standard realizable KL-regularized results in the no-misspecification limit.

Conclusion: KL misspecification provides robust theoretical guarantees for KL-regularized methods under model misspecification, unifying bandits and RL under practical imperfect modeling.

Abstract: We study KL-regularized contextual bandits and episodic reinforcement learning (RL) under general function approximation with model misspecification. Existing guarantees rely on realizability and therefore do not extend to misspecified models, where classical regret bounds may fail. This work introduces KL misspecification formulations for contextual bandits and episodic RL and analyzes regression-based algorithms with Gibbs policy updates. High-probability KL-regret guarantees with explicit misspecification terms are established, recovering the standard realizable KL-regularized setting as a special case.

</details>


### [294] [MDP-GRPO: Stabilized Group Relative Policy Optimization for Multi-Constraint Instruction Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06058)
*Mohammad Mahdi Salmani-Zarchi,Zahra Rahimi,Heshaam Faili,Mohammad Javad Dousti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MDP-GRPO stabilizes GRPO for discrete, low-dispersion rewards using multi-temperature sampling, dual-anchor advantages, prospect-theoretic shaping, and asymmetric KL regularization, boosting constraint-satisfaction and stable convergence on small groups while preserving general capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard z-score group normalization in GRPO fails under low-dispersion, homogeneous reward groups, causing low-variance amplification, mean-centering blindness, and zero-variance collapse. This hinders reliable multi-constraint instruction following.

Method: Introduce (1) multi-temperature sampling to diversify rewards, (2) dual-anchor advantages to restore gradients and address mean-centering blindness, (3) prospect-theoretic shaping to bound updates and penalize constraint violations per Kahneman and Tversky, and (4) asymmetric KL regularization to favor informative updates.

Result: MDP-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO on FollowBench, IFEval, and a curated multi-constraint dataset, improving strict constraint satisfaction by up to 5.0% on Llama-3.2-3B; enables stable convergence with small group sizes and preserves general capabilities on MMLU and ARC.

Conclusion: MDP-GRPO provides a stable, effective framework for RL with verifiable rewards under low-dispersion, discrete reward settings, achieving higher constraint satisfaction without sacrificing broader abilities.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is ideal for multi-constraint instruction following, yet standard group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) becomes unstable under discrete, low-dispersion rewards, where within-group reward distributions are frequently homogeneous. We identify and formalize three pathologies of z-score group normalization in this regime: low-variance amplification, mean-centering blindness, and zero-variance collapse. To address them, we propose MDP-GRPO, which stabilizes learning through (1) multi-temperature sampling to increase reward dispersion, (2) dual-anchor advantages to restore gradients in homogeneous groups and stop mean-centering blindness, (3) prospect-theoretic shaping to bound updates and penalize violations based on Kahneman and Tversky's theory, and (4) asymmetric KL regularization. Evaluated on FollowBench, IFEval, and a curated multi-constraint dataset, MDP-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO, improving strict constraint satisfaction by up to 5.0% on Llama-3.2-3B. Our method also enables stable convergence with small group sizes while preserving general capabilities on MMLU and ARC.

</details>


### [295] [On Advantage Estimates for Max@K Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06080)
*Shota Takashiro,Soichiro Nishimori,Paavo Parmas,Yongmin Kim,Kohsei Matsutani,Gouki Minegishi,Yusuke Iwasawa,Takeshi Kojima,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Leave-Two-Out baseline centers policy-gradient advantages for max@K objectives, producing the MaxPO method with quadratic-time efficiency and reduced gradient variance, plus a canonical finite-batch perspective for max@K.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse outcome rewards in verifiable RL hinder exploration; directly optimizing inference-time objectives (pass@K, max@K) requires unified, unbiased gradient estimators and well-centered advantages to stabilize learning.

Method: Starting from a leading policy-gradient advantage estimator (unbiased but non-centered), the authors introduce a Leave-Two-Out (L2O) baseline that preserves unbiasedness while centering realized batch advantages. They derive a canonical finite-batch advantage for max@K, present MaxPO with an efficient quadratic-time implementation, and integrate it into group-based RL for LLM post-training.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows the L2O baseline reduces gradient variance and outperforms non-centered baselines, validating improved stability and effectiveness of MaxPO in post-training LLM RL settings.

Conclusion: Centering advantages via the L2O baseline yields unbiased, variance-reducing gradient estimates for max@K optimization, enabling an efficient MaxPO method and a unified finite-batch perspective for existing estimators.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is widely used for post-training reasoning models, but sparse outcome rewards make exploration difficult. A complementary approach is to optimize inference-time objectives such as pass@K and max@K directly, yet existing policy-gradient estimators for these objectives use different signals, baselines, and normalizations, making their relationships unclear. We study this issue through baseline design and advantage centering. Starting from the advantage estimator of a leading method in the field, we show that it is policy-gradient unbiased but yields a non-centered advantage. We then introduce a Leave-Two-Out baseline that preserves policy-gradient unbiasedness while making realized batch advantages exactly centered. The resulting method, MaxPO, has an efficient quadratic-time implementation and integrates naturally into group-based RL for LLM post-training. We further derive the canonical finite-batch advantage for max@K, providing a unified view of existing advantage estimators. Empirically, we verify that the L2O baseline reduces gradient variance and outperforms non-centered alternatives.

</details>


### [296] [OrderGrad: Optimizing Beyond the Mean with Order-Statistic Policy Gradient Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06096)
*Paavo Parmas,Yongmin Kim,Kohsei Matsutani,Shota Takashiro,Soichiro Nishimori,Takeshi Kojima,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrderGrad provides unbiased gradient estimators for finite-sample order-statistic objectives (e.g., VaR, CVaR, medians, top-K) in policy gradient/reparameterization RL, enabling risk-sensitive, robust, and exploratory learning via a simple reward transformation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world objectives depend on distributional properties of returns (tail risk, outliers, best-of-K). Traditional policy gradient optimizes expected return, missing these aspects.

Method: A family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistics with fixed sample size and rank-weight vectors. By choosing rank weights, one can target L-statistics (VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, top-K). The estimators are unbiased for the chosen objective and are applied through a simple reward transformation, compatible with standard policy-gradient or reparameterized updates. The paper also analyzes variance behavior.

Result: The proposed OrderGrad framework yields unbiased gradient estimates for order-statistic objectives at fixed sample size, with analyzed variance properties. Empirical evaluation on tasks where mean-optimization is misaligned—such as LLM math post-training and other domains—demonstrates the method’s effectiveness in aligning optimization with distributional objectives (risk-averse, robust, exploratory learning).

Conclusion: OrderGrad offers a unified, plug-and-play route to optimizing distributional objectives in reinforcement learning, enabling risk-sensitive and robust policies by merely adjusting rank-weight vectors. Code is available for practical adoption.

Abstract: Policy-gradient methods usually optimize expected return, but many real world applications care about distributional properties of returns: tail risk, outlier robustness, or best-of-K discovery. We introduce OrderGrad, a family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives. OrderGrad optimizes finite-sample L-statistics, i.e., weighted averages of sorted rewards or costs, recovering objectives such as VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, and top-m/best-of-K criteria by changing only the rank weights. For any fixed sample size and rank-weight vector, OrderGrad provides an unbiased gradient estimator for the corresponding order-statistic objective. The method is implemented as a simple reward transformation that can then be used in an otherwise standard policy-gradient or reparameterized update. We study the resulting estimator's variance behavior and evaluate it on tasks where mean optimization is mismatched to the deployment objective, including LLM math post-training and other tasks. OrderGrad provides a unified, plug-and-play route to risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning.
  Code: https://github.com/paavo5/ordergrad

</details>


### [297] [A Sliced-Wasserstein Framework on Correlation Matrices for EEG Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06104)
*Chen Hu,Rui Wang,Jiale Zhou,Jingjun Yi,Shaocheng Jin,Yidong Song,Yefeng Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW) framework for domain-generalization in EEG using Correlation Sliced-Wasserstein (CorSW) on full-rank correlation manifolds with Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM); shows improved generalization across three EEG datasets; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Covariance-based features in EEG are robust to noise but sensitive to channel-wise scaling. Full-rank correlation matrices provide scale invariance, but require appropriate comparison metrics on their manifold. A SW-based discrepancy on correlation manifolds aims to improve robustness and domain generalization for EEG decoding.

Method: Develop PEMSW: Sliced Wasserstein discrepancies on manifolds endowed with Pullback Euclidean Metrics (PEMs). Instantiate CorSW discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices under two geometries: Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM). Integrate into a domain generalization framework for EEG decoding.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three EEG datasets shows improved generalization under distribution shifts, with low training overhead and no additional inference cost.

Conclusion: PEMSW-based CorSW with OLM/LSM provides effective, scale-invariant, domain-generalizable EEG decoding without increasing inference cost; code available for replication.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) offers noninvasive, millisecond resolution recordings of neuronal activity and is widely used in neuroscience and healthcare. Many EEG decoding pipelines rely on covariance descriptors for their robustness to noise, but such representations are sensitive to channel-wise scaling. Recent studies have therefore advocated full-rank correlation matrices as a scale-invariant alternative for EEG decoding. In this paper, we propose a general framework for Sliced Wasserstein (SW) discrepancies on manifolds endowed with Pullback Euclidean Metrics (PEMs), termed Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW). Within this framework, we instantiate two Correlation Sliced-Wasserstein (CorSW) discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices under two recently introduced correlation geometries, \textit{i.e.}, the Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM). Building on CorSW, we further develop a domain generalization (DG) framework for EEG decoding. Experiments on three EEG datasets demonstrate improved generalization under distribution shifts, with low training overhead and no additional inference cost. The source code is available at https://github.com/ChenHu-ML/CorSW.

</details>


### [298] [Adaptive state-action abstractions via rate-distortion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06123)
*Fernando E. Rosas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a principled, dynamic abstraction refinement method for RL: switch between coarse and fine representations by comparing the learning error (Bellman residual) to the abstraction error (bisimulation-based); implements soft rate-distortion-based state-action abstractions with tunable resolution; demonstrates near-optimal performance under substantial lossy compression in tabular settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address how to adapt the granularity of task abstractions during learning. The goal is a principled criterion for when to refine abstractions, enabling efficient learning by starting coarse and refining only when warranted by error trade-offs.

Method: Introduce a performance certificate that decomposes value error into learning error (Bellman residual) and abstraction error (bisimulation metric). Derive a switching strategy to refine or compress representations accordingly. Build soft state-action abstractions from rate-distortion principles, enabling continuous adjustment of resolution along state and action axes. Validate in tabular RL settings, showing robustness to substantial information loss.

Result: The approach yields near-optimal performance despite substantial lossy compression of state-action information in a range of tabular experiments.

Conclusion: An adaptive coarse-to-fine learning framework is feasible via a principled error decomposition. The combination of Bellman-residual and bisimulation-based bounds with rate-distortion abstractions provides a practical mechanism to tune abstraction granularity and achieve efficient learning.

Abstract: When learning to walk, infants seem to address a coarse version of the problem first - stay upright, reach the caregiver - and refine it only when further practice at that resolution stops paying off. Reinforcement learning offers multiple techniques for building simple versions of complex tasks, but lacks general principles for how to dynamically adjust the granularity of these abstractions during learning. This paper proposes one such principle: refine the abstraction as soon as the learning error within it becomes comparable to the error induced by the abstraction itself. Here, we investigate one way of formalising this principle via a performance certificate that decomposes value error into two terms: a learning error bound captured by a Bellman residual, and an abstraction error bound given by a bisimulation metric. The resulting switching strategy is implemented by soft state-action abstractions built from rate-distortion principles, whose resolution along state and action axes can be continuously adjusted. We validate this construction in a range of tabular settings, showing that near-optimal performance can be achieved under substantial lossy compression of state and action information.

</details>


### [299] [Tight list replicability bounds via a novel sphere covering theorem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06148)
*Ari Blondal,Hamed Hatami,Pooya Hatami,Chavdar Lalov,Sivan Tretiak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Topological methods yield tight list-size bounds for replicability in VC learning; large-margin results vary with margin: optimal list size equals ambient dimension in moderate margins, and can reach ⌈d/2⌉+1 for very large margins.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how replicability (list size) scales with accuracy and intrinsic complexity measures in learning theory and obtain sharp bounds using a topological approach.

Method: Develop a sphere-covering lemma derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem: if the d-sphere is covered by open sets each contained in an open hemisphere, then d+1 sets have a common intersection. Apply this to derive tight list-size bounds for VC classes; analyze large-margin half-spaces and construct replicable algorithms achieving the minimal list size in different margin regimes.

Result: A sharp bound on the list size–accuracy relationship for VC classes; for large-margin half-spaces, the optimal list size equals the ambient dimension under moderate margins; for very large margins, a replicable algorithm achieves the minimal list size ⌈d/2⌉+1.

Conclusion: Topological techniques yield sharp, regime-dependent bounds on replicability and a constructive replicable algorithm; margin and dimension jointly determine optimal list sizes.

Abstract: In recent years, list replicability has emerged as a framework for formalizing reproducibility in learning theory. A central question is how the required list size relates to the accuracy parameter and natural complexity measures of the hypothesis class.
  To achieve sharp bounds on list replicability, we prove a novel topological sphere covering theorem, derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. Specifically, if the $d$-sphere is covered by open sets, each of which lies in an open hemisphere, then $d+1$ of these sets must have a common intersection. Using this result, we obtain a sharp bound on the relationship between list size and accuracy for VC classes. We also show that for large-margin half-spaces, provided the margin is not too large, the optimal list size equals the ambient dimension. However, when the margin is taken to be very large, we devise a replicable algorithm achieving the minimal list size of $\lceil d/2 \rceil + 1$.

</details>


### [300] [Trust-Aware Predictive Emissions Monitoring for Gas Turbine Fleets with Limited Labelled Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06156)
*Rebecca Potts,Aiden Durrant,Rick Hackney,Georgios Leontidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Trust-aware probabilistic framework for fleet-level NOx prediction with limited labels, combining a multi-head recurrent model, learned confidence, ensemble uncertainty, auxiliary feature prediction, feature-space distance analysis, and operating-range diagnostics to produce per-sample trust scores for unlabelled turbines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address deployment of ML-based predictive emissions monitoring systems (PEMS) across gas turbine fleets when emission labels are available for only a subset of assets; enable reliable, interpretable predictions with quantified trust on unlabelled units.

Method: Multi-head recurrent prediction model with learned confidence estimation, ensemble-based uncertainty quantification, auxiliary feature prediction, feature-space distance analysis, and operating-range diagnostics; per-sample trust scores calibrated on labeled data to indicate reliability on unlabelled turbines.

Result: Confidence-based filtering reduces MAE from 0.202 at full coverage to 0.070 for the highest-confidence 10% of predictions; unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples show increased uncertainty and reduced confidence; trust signals are actionable for fleet deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed trust framework provides actionable reliability information for emissions prediction on unlabelled turbines, enabling more transparent and trustworthy deployment of PEMS across industrial fleets.

Abstract: Machine learning-based predictive emissions monitoring systems offer a practical alternative to direct emissions measurement, but their deployment across gas turbine fleets is challenging when emissions labels are available for only a small subset of assets. In this work, a trust-aware probabilistic framework is proposed for fleet-level gas turbine NOx prediction under limited labelled supervision. The framework combines a multi-head recurrent prediction model with learned confidence estimation, ensemble-based uncertainty quantification, auxiliary feature prediction, feature-space distance analysis, and operating-range diagnostics. These signals are calibrated on labelled data to produce interpretable per-sample trust scores, providing indicators of prediction reliability on unlabelled turbines, supporting the identification of predictions that should be treated with greater caution during fleet-level deployment. Confidence-based filtering reduces MAE from 0.202 at full coverage to 0.070 for the highest-confidence 10\% of predictions, demonstrating that confidence estimates are meaningfully related to prediction error. Unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples exhibit increased uncertainty and reduced confidence, indicating that the framework responds appropriately to distributional shift. The results show that the proposed trust framework provides actionable reliability information for emissions prediction on unlabelled turbines, supporting more transparent and trustworthy deployment of PEMS across industrial fleets.

</details>


### [301] [On the training of physics-informed neural operators for solving parametric partial differential equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06164)
*Nanxi Chen,Chuanjie Cui,Airong Chen,Sifan Wang,Rujin Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINOs can learn PDE solution operators efficiently by integrating physics into training; an empirical study across backbones (DeepONet, FNO, CViT) and five PDEs shows CViT is most robust, while training pathologies exist and mitigations from PINNs transfer to PINOs; physics-informed training can rival or surpass data-driven training under certain data regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically understand and improve the training of physics-informed neural operators (PINOs), bridging the gap between data-driven neural operators and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and providing practical guidance for robust, efficient operator learning.

Method: Empirical study comparing three operator backbones (DeepONet, Fourier Neural Operator, Continuous Vision Transformer) across five parametric PDE systems. Investigates architecture design, optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling. Identifies optimization pathologies (gradient conflicts, causal violations) and tests mitigation strategies from PINNs. Compares physics-informed versus data-driven training under varying data regimes. Provides a practical training pipeline and releases code and data.

Result: Continuous Vision Transformer (CViT) offers consistently strong and stable performance across benchmarks. PINO-specific pathologies mirror those seen in PINN training, including gradient conflicts and causal violations, but established PINN mitigation techniques remain effective. Physics-informed training can match or exceed data-driven operator learning under certain data regimes, underscoring the practical viability of PINOs. The work yields actionable guidance for architecture and optimization choices in PINO pipelines.

Conclusion: The study delivers a systematic empirical framework for PINO training, highlighting CViT as a robust backbone and confirming the relevance of PINN-inspired mitigation techniques. It also demonstrates that physics-informed training can be competitive with data-driven approaches, informing a practical, robust, and efficient PINO training pipeline.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) aim to learn solution operators for partial differential equations by using the governing physics as supervision, rather than relying solely on paired input-output simulation data. By incorporating physical constraints into the training objective, PINOs combine the cross-instance generalization of neural operators with the data efficiency of physics-informed learning. Despite this promise, how to train PINOs efficiently and robustly remains less well-understood than the training of either data-driven neural operators or physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). To bridge this gap, we examine key components of the PINO training pipeline, including architecture design, optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling strategy. We study three representative operator backbones, Deep Operator Network (DeepONet), Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), and Continuous Vision Transformer (CViT), across five diverse parametric PDE systems. Our results show that CViT provides consistently strong and stable performance across the considered benchmarks. Beyond architecture, we find that several optimization pathologies previously identified in PINN training naturally arise in PINOs, including gradient conflicts and causal violation. We also find that mitigation algorithms developed for PINNs remain effective in the PINO setting. We further compare physics-informed and data-driven training under different data regimes, revealing that a carefully designed physics-informed training pipeline can match, and in some cases, outperform purely data-driven neural operators. Taken together, these findings provide a systematic empirical understanding of the optimization challenges in PINO training and inform a practical pipeline for efficient and robust physics-informed operator learning. Code and data are available at https://github.com/NanxiiChen/PI-CViT.

</details>


### [302] [Learning to model pediatric asthma exacerbation from multiple risk factors: a case study in coastal Virginia](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06174)
*Jonathan Colen,Eric Werner,Maryam Golbazi,Heather Richter,Diana McSpadden,Amy Quinn,Jocel Santos,Mary Jane Darling,Mary Margaret Gleason*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Three modeling approaches (GLM, NN, sparse dictionary learning) were compared to predict pediatric asthma exacerbation in Hampton Roads using pollution, weather, and neighborhood factors; all frameworks yielded consensus on risk patterns and highlighted nonlinear interactions, suggesting interpretable insights for public health interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle multiple environmental and socioeconomic drivers of asthma exacerbations in large spatiotemporal data while balancing predictive power and interpretability, enabling actionable public health guidance.

Method: Data: ambient air pollution, weather, neighborhood opportunity; outcome: ZIP-code–level acute AE visits to a regional children's hospital (2018-2023) in Hampton Roads. Models: GLM as baseline, neural networks as highly predictive target, sparse dictionary learning to extract interpretable nonlinear interactions. Evaluation: compare predictive performance and estimate relative risks; synthesize cross-framework consensus.

Result: GLM provides baseline interpretability; NN achieves high predictive power; sparse dictionary learning yields parsimonious interpretable nonlinear interactions. Across models, relative risk estimates for AE due to exposures show consensus, supporting potential synergy between factors.

Conclusion: By integrating statistical models with interpretable ML, the study identifies synergistic interactions influencing AE and lays groundwork for public health interventions in coastal Virginia. External validation and attention to ecological inference are needed for generalizability and causal interpretation.

Abstract: Childhood asthma is a common illness exacerbated by air pollution as well as meteorological and neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors. Modeling asthma exacerbation (AE) in large spatiotemporal datasets requires disentangling impacts from multiple contributors. In this case study, we compared three techniques that balance predictive power with interpretability to predict AE in Hampton Roads, a coastal Virginia region comprising 7 cities and over 1.5 million people. After collating ambient air pollution measurements, weather data, and measures of neighborhood opportunity, we modeled zip code-level acute AE visits to a regional children's hospital and affiliated providers from 2018-2023. Generalized linear models (GLM) provided a baseline while neural networks (NN) served as a maximally predictive target. To bridge between statistical models and deep learning, we developed a framework based on sparse dictionary learning to identify and interpret parsimonious nonlinear interacting equations. After comparing each model's predictive performance, we estimated relative risks for AE due to input exposure variables and found consensus across frameworks. Our work links statistical and interpretable machine learning models to highlight possible synergistic interactions influencing AE, and may enable future studies to guide public health interventions in coastal Virginia.

</details>


### [303] [Learning to Route LLMs from Implicit Cost-Performance Preferences via Meta-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06178)
*Jiahao Zeng,Ming Tang,Ningning Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MetaRouter is a meta-learning, context-bandit-based LLM routing framework that personalizes model selection to individual cost–performance preferences with quick adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users have heterogeneous, evolving cost-performance preferences and current routing methods struggle to tailor model selection efficiently to each user, leading to suboptimal cost or performance. There is a need for personalized, data-efficient routing that adapts to changing model landscapes.

Method: Formulate user preference profiles as a set of tasks in contextual bandits and introduce MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework that rapidly adapts routing policies to individual preferences with minimal interactions. The approach supports multi-model routing and is robust to shifts in available LLMs.

Result: MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks, learns user preferences efficiently with limited interaction, remains robust to changes in routable LLMs, and scales to routing among multiple models.

Conclusion: The approach enables personalized, cost-aware LLM routing with fast adaptation, robustness to model changes, and scalable multi-model routing, advancing practical deployment of customized LLM services.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) present a trade-off between performance and cost, where more powerful models incur greater expense. LLM routing aims to mitigate expenses while maintaining performance by sending queries to the most suitable model. However, existing methods cannot perform well for different user cost-performance preferences. To address this gap, we introduce a novel perceptive LLM routing paradigm for personalized and user-centric cost-performance optimization, which efficiently learns users' implicit preferences through little interaction. To handle the challenge of heterogeneous user needs, we formulate preference profiles as a set of distinct tasks in contextual bandit and propose MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework designed for preference-aware LLM routing. Experimental results show that MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks. Furthermore, it exhibits high efficiency in learning user preferences, robustness to changes in the routable LLMs, and scalability to multi-model routing.

</details>


### [304] [A Machine Learning-Based Framework for Discovering Huntington's Disease Stages: Integrating Graph Representation Learning and clustering to Uncover Progression Dynamics in Longitudinal Enroll-HD Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06196)
*Lubna M. Abu Zohair,Marta Vallejo,MD Azher Uddin,John R. Woodward,Hind Zantout*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-driven, unsupervised HD staging framework using dynamic graph representations to derive four clinically meaningful stages from longitudinal data, improving on traditional threshold-based staging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete clinical cut-offs and inter-rater variability obscure intra-stage variability and progression dynamics; a temporally-aware, data-driven approach can reveal natural progression stages.

Method: Construct dynamic graph representations from longitudinal clinical measurements, learn a latent 4D representation, then apply K-means++ clustering. Increase cluster count with stability analysis to identify robust, meaningful subgroups.

Result: On 302 Enroll-HD participants (1,477 visits, 44 variables), a 4D latent space yielded four distinct, robust HD stages with well-defined boundaries and minimal overlap compared to existing staging methods.

Conclusion: The framework provides a data-driven HD staging that captures progression dynamics beyond fixed thresholds, enabling better patient stratification and potential insights for personalized care and treatment development; validation in larger cohorts is warranted.

Abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive brain disorder that gradually affects movement, cognitive function, and behavior. Identifying the stage of the disease accurately and consistently is important for understanding its course, grouping patients, personalized care, and discovering treatment. Existing clinical staging frameworks rely primarily on predefined clinical measurement thresholds and clinical expert decisions, yet these discrete cut-offs may obscure meaningful intra-stage variability and remain vulnerable to inter-rater differences, especially in motor and functional assessments. To address these limitations, we developed an unsupervised machine learning framework based on dynamic graph representation learning to capture temporal relationships within and across patients from longitudinal clinical measurements. Using the learned representations, we applied K-means++ clustering to identify well-separated groups. We then iteratively increased the number of clusters (k), using stability analysis to assess robustness and reveal additional meaningful clusters beyond the initial optimal solution. We applied the framework to 302 individuals from the Enroll-HD cohort (1,477 visits, 44 clinical variables per visit; 80% manifest participants), enabling data-driven discovery of HD stages reflecting natural clinical progression. Despite the limited cohort size, the proposed framework achieved robust clustering performance using a four-dimensional latent space, identifying four meaningful and statistically distinct disease stages through clustering stability analysis. Each stage corresponded to well-defined clinical measurement boundaries, with minimal overlap compared to previously established clinical staging methods.

</details>


### [305] [Non-Negative Matrix Factorization for Event Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06205)
*Raphaël Romero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces EventNMF, a continuous-time non-negative factorization for event-time data. It models each entity's events with a Poisson process whose time-varying intensity is expressed as a non-negative B-spline basis; avoids binning, yields shared temporal templates, and provides a simple, efficient estimation procedure. Binned-count methods appear as degree-zero splines; evaluated on synthetic and real data with favorable bias-variance tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extract interpretable latent temporal structure from continuous-time event data without losing temporal granularity due to binning; existing NMF approaches operate on downsampled counts and may obscure heterogeneity across entities.

Method: Model each entity i as a Poisson point process with intensity λ_i(t) = ∑_k W_{ik} ψ_k(t), where ψ_k(t) are non-negative, shared spline basis functions (non-negative B-splines) representing temporal templates; W_{ik} ≥ 0 are entity-template weights. Estimate via maximum likelihood under Poisson process with non-negativity constraints; simple, scalable procedure to recover templates and weights.

Result: The method is principled and practical; avoids pre-binning, offers interpretable templates, and is computationally efficient. Degree-zero splines recover standard binned-count NMF as a special case; bias-variance tradeoffs are analyzed; performance demonstrated on synthetic latent-factor data and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: EventNMF provides a principled, scalable framework for continuous-time non-negative factorization of event data, preserving temporal granularity and capturing shared temporal patterns across entities; it subsumes binning-based approaches and broadens applicability of NMF to event-time data.

Abstract: Continuous-time event data, in which entities emit instantaneous events over time, arises naturally across many domains such as neuroscience, seismology, and social networks. Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is a natural tool to uncover interpretable structure in such data, but it has so far only been applied after binning or smoothing the entity-level counting measures. This preprocessing step comes with the risk of erasing entity-level heterogeneities and fine-grained temporal features. In this paper, we introduce EventNMF, a continuous-time non-negative factorization model that operates directly on event times: each entity's events are modeled as a Poisson process whose intensity factorizes through a non-negative B-spline basis, and a simple estimation procedure recovers interpretable temporal templates shared across entities. The resulting method is mathematically principled, easy to implement, and computationally efficient. We further show that standard binned-count approaches arise as the special case of degree-zero splines, explore bias-variance tradeoffs and compare against existing methods on a synthetic latent factor model, and demonstrate the effectiveness of EventNMF on several real-world applications.

</details>


### [306] [Design a Reliable LLM-Integrated Interface for Mortality Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06235)
*Thi Kim Ngan Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An LLM-integrated interface for mortality forecasting enhances usability while preserving statistical power and reproducibility. It uses a constrained LLM orchestration layer to translate natural-language requests into structured configurations for a deterministic CoMoMo-based forecasting pipeline, evaluated via rolling-origin multi-step forecasts and MSE in a three-phase workflow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mortality forecasting is crucial for actuarial and policy decisions but remains technically complex for non-experts. Improving usability without sacrificing rigor could broaden adoption and maintain decision-quality.

Method: Three-phase approach: (1) baseline pipeline with CoMoMo to reproduce established results; (2) extension to multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and mean squared error (MSE); (3) a prototype interface with a local LLM acting as an orchestration layer that interprets plain-language forecasting requests and configures the deterministic pipeline.

Result: The approach shows that LLMs can improve accessibility of mortality forecasting workflows without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes analytical tasks.

Conclusion: LLM-integrated interfaces can increase usability while maintaining statistical rigor, enabling non-experts to interact with robust forecasting pipelines through plain language.

Abstract: Mortality forecasting plays an important role in actuarial and policy decision-making, but its implementation remains technically complex and inaccessible to non-expert users. This project proposes a reliable large language model (LLM)-integrated interface that improves usability while maintaining statistical power. The LLM is designed as a constrained orchestration layer that translates natural-language inputs into structured configurations for a deterministic forecasting pipeline. A three-phase methodology is employed to ensure accuracy, usability, and transparency. First, a baseline pipeline is implemented using the CoMoMo package, reproducing established mortality forecasting results. Second, the pipeline is extended to generate multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and mean squared error (MSE). Third, a prototype interface uses a local LLM to handle users' forecasting requests in plain language. The system demonstrates that LLMs can enhance accessibility without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes analytical workflows.

</details>


### [307] [Tracing the Oracle: Improving Diffusion Timestep Scheduling for 3D CT Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06236)
*Yujia Wu,Zhaoqiang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TrO introduces an oracle-guided, nonuniform timestep scheduler for diffusion-based 3D CT reconstruction, using densely sampled trajectories as reference to globally minimize truncation error with dynamic programming, enabling high fidelity with few steps (≤10) when combined with DDS on AAPM data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform diffusion timesteps fail to align with the nonuniform evolution of the reverse SDE in ill-posed 3D CT problems, causing truncation errors and high compute; efficient, accurate reconstruction requires smarter timestep allocation.

Method: Treat densely sampled numerical integration trajectories on a subset of samples as the reference oracle; extract an optimized step schedule by dynamic programming to minimize the cumulative error between a few-step approximation and the oracle; plug-and-play with existing diffusion-based CT method (DDS).

Result: On AAPM dataset across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks, the optimized timesteps with TrO improve reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency compared to heuristic schedules, especially under a strict budget of ≤10 sampling steps.

Conclusion: The TrO framework provides a principled, end-to-end method to allocate limited diffusion steps to critical evolution phases, leveraging an oracle to reduce truncation error and accelerate inference in ill-posed 3D CT reconstruction.

Abstract: Pretrained diffusion models demonstrate impressive potential in solving highly ill-posed 3D computed tomography (CT) inverse problems, while the inference process suffers from significant computational overhead. Furthermore, existing uniform timestep schedules fail to capture the non-uniform evolution of the reverse conditional diffusion stochastic differential equation, thereby introducing substantial truncation errors. To overcome this limitation, we propose Tracing the Oracle (TrO), a plug-and-play framework for improved timestep scheduling. Specifically, we treat densely sampled numerical integration trajectories on a few samples as the reference oracle. The optimized schedule is extracted by leveraging dynamic programming to globally minimize the cumulative error between the few-step approximation and the oracle. This mechanism precisely allocates the limited sampling steps to critical evolution stages that are highly susceptible to truncation errors. Our extensive experiments on the AAPM dataset across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks demonstrate that, when combined with the state-of-the-art 3D CT reconstruction method DDS, our optimized timesteps significantly improve reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency compared to existing heuristic schedules, especially under a strict budget of no more than 10 sampling steps.

</details>


### [308] [Generative Criticality in Large Language Model Temperature Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06238)
*Huajian Ruan,Jinyang Li,Xingyu Guo,Lingxiao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Treats LLM token embeddings as a 1D spin chain in a statistical-field model, revealing a susceptibility peak and order-parameter change near a Tc, plus a dimension-collapse and TwoNN corroboration; robust across model scales and prompts, suggesting a critical-like regime in text generation and links between decoding and critical phenomena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify collective statistical structure of LLM outputs using tools inspired by statistical physics, and explore how decoding temperature controls dynamics and potential critical behavior.

Method: Map token embeddings to continuous spin variables on a 1D chain; define susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an ensemble-averaged order parameter; vary softmax temperature T; analyze scaling near a characteristic Tc; examine embedding-direction collapse; estimate intrinsic dimension via the TwoNN method across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B–32B) and prompt categories.

Result: Observe a sharp susceptibility peak near Tc with power-law-like scaling; rapid change in the order parameter; embeddings collapse onto a single semantic direction below Tc; TwoNN intrinsic dimension minimizes near Tc; results robust across model scales and prompts.

Conclusion: Provides quantitative tools to probe the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and hints at connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena; non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further study.

Abstract: We propose a statistical-field framework for text generated by large language models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a one-dimensional chain. Defining a susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field, we vary the \texttt{softmax} temperature $T$ and observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic $T_c$ with power-law-like scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and a collapse onto a single semantic direction below $T_c$. The intrinsic dimension estimated by the two nearest neighbor (TwoNN) method independently corroborates these findings, reaching a minimum near $T_c$. Results are robust across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B--32B) and prompt categories. While the phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation. Our framework provides quantitative tools for probing the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.

</details>


### [309] [Your GFlowNet Secretly Learns an Optimal Transport Plan](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06272)
