*Ian Maksimov,Nikita Morozov,Denis Belomestny,Sergey Samsonov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-acyclic GFlowNets with fixed initial flow reduce OT on graphs to Kantorovich OT; the learned policy encodes an optimal transport plan; sampling recovers the optimal coupling; enables scalable OT on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization; experiments align with exact OT solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) with optimal transport (OT) to leverage stochastic graph-based sampling for solving OT problems, enabling scalable OT on large graphs.

Method: Fix the initial flow in a minimum-flow GFlowNet; show equivalence to Kantorovich OT with graph-based shortest-path costs; prove that the optimum policy encodes the OT plan; demonstrate that sampling trajectories yields the optimal coupling; implement edge-flow neural parameterization to scale to large graphs.

Result: Established a theoretical equivalence between non-acyclic GFlowNets (with fixed initial flow) and Kantorovich OT on graphs; empirical results show agreement with exact OT solvers and ability to learn high-quality transport plans.

Conclusion: GFlowNets provide a scalable, neural-parameterizable framework for OT on graphs, with a solid theoretical link to Kantorovich OT and practical recovery of optimal couplings via trajectory sampling.

Abstract: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are a framework for sampling structured objects via stochastic trajectories in a directed graph. In this work, we establish a theoretical connection between non-acyclic GFlowNets and optimal transport (OT). We show that fixing the initial flow distribution in a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces its objective to a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest path costs. At the optimum, the learned GFlowNet policy therefore encodes an optimal transport plan from the source distribution to the target distribution: we show that sampling trajectories from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding optimal coupling. Our formulation enables applying the GFlowNet learning framework to OT problems on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization. Experiments confirm agreement with exact OT solvers and demonstrate that GFlowNets can learn high-quality transport plans.

</details>


### [310] [PAC-Bayesian Adversarially Robust Generalization for Message Passing Graph Neural Networks: A Sensitivity Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06293)
*Ziling Liang,Xinping Yi,Qingsong Wen,Shi Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayes framework is extended to message passing GNNs to obtain tighter robust generalization bounds under adversarial perturbations by aligning anisotropic posteriors with output Jacobians. The key insight leverages the rank-K structure of the Jacobian (K-class setting) to reduce complexity from hidden widths to the number of classes, resulting in tighter bounds for MPGNN robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in robust generalization analysis for GNNs under adversarial attacks. Existing PAC-Bayesian bounds rely on isotropic posteriors and full-parameter perturbations, yielding loose bounds and width-dependent complexity. A data-dependent, sensitivity-aware framework aims to produce tighter, more informative guarantees specific to MPGNNs.

Method: Derive output Jacobians of MPGNNs with respect to weight parameters; exploit rank at most K to construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices; adopt anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to bound the KL divergence tightly; refine spectral-norm dependence on weights and reduce leading dimension from width to K; extend a sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayes analysis from deep nets to MPGNNs.

Result: A theoretically tighter robust generalization bound for MPGNNs in adversarial settings, with reduced dependence on hidden width and dependence scaled by the number of classes K, enabling more informative guidance for designing robust MPGNNs.

Conclusion: The proposed Jacobian-aligned, anisotropic posterior PAC-Bayes framework yields substantially tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs and offers principled design implications to enhance adversarial robustness, particularly by leveraging the rank-K Jacobian structure in K-class graph classification.

Abstract: Whilst the vulnerability of graph neural networks (GNNs) to adversarial attacks poses a critical threat to graph representation learning, the understanding of the robust generalization behavior remains a fundamental challenge in the adversarial setting. Recently, PAC-Bayesian margin-based generalization analysis substantially advances this line of research by providing a flexible and data-dependent analytical framework. However, existing robust analyses often rely on isotropic Gaussian posteriors and control weight perturbations in the full parameter space, which limits the ability to capture heterogeneous parameter sensitivity yet hinges on hidden-width-dependent complexity terms, resulting in not-tight-enough generalization bounds. In this paper, we extend a recently proposed sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework from deep neural networks to message passing GNNs (MPGNNs) and derive a tighter robust generalization bound in the adversarial setting. Specifically, we first quantify how sensitive the perturbations across different parameter blocks are to the network outputs by deriving the output Jacobians with respect to the weight parameters. Exploiting the fact that these Jacobian matrices have rank at most $K$ in $K$-class graph classification, we then construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices and use anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to upper bound the KL divergence in a tight way. Notably, by refining the spectral-norm dependence on the learned weights and reducing the leading dimension factor from hidden-width-dependent terms to the number of classes $K$, our analysis yields much tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs, thereby guiding their designs to enhance adversarial robustness.

</details>


### [311] [Reactive Flux Matching: Mechanism Discovery and Adaptive Sampling of Rare Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06295)
*Rishal Aggarwal,David Ryan Koes,Nicholas M. Boffi,Eric Vanden-Eijnden*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flux Matching learns a current velocity u(z) and a potential h(z) from reactive trajectory data to reveal dominant reaction pathways and a data-driven reaction coordinate, without assuming Markov dynamics or stationary distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide mechanistic insight from reactive path ensembles and enable adaptive sampling in non-Markovian CVs; address limitations of committor-based approaches.

Method: From reactive currents, compute a weighted Helmholtz–Hodge decomposition to obtain u(z) and h(z); minimize quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble (flow-matching loss); requires no explicit dynamics or stationary distribution.

Result: Validated on molecular systems: generated current velocity trajectories and computed rate constants; demonstrated robustness under projection onto non-Markovian CVs; enables adaptive interfaces for enhanced sampling.

Conclusion: Flux Matching offers a data-driven, dynamics-agnostic framework for pathway analysis and sampling optimization, complementary to existing methods and applicable to non-Markovian variables.

Abstract: Path sampling methods generate ensembles of reactive trajectories connecting metastable states, but extracting mechanistic insight from these data remains nontrivial. We introduce Flux Matching, a framework that learns two complementary objects directly from reactive trajectory data: a current velocity $u(z)$, whose streamlines trace the dominant reaction pathways, and a scalar potential $h(z)$, obtained from a weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition of the reactive current, that serves as a data-driven reaction coordinate. Both minimize quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble, analogous to the flow matching loss in generative modeling, and require no knowledge of the underlying dynamics or stationary distribution. Unlike committor-based methods, $u$ and $h$ remain well-defined under projection onto non-Markovian collective variables, and their level sets in turn provide adaptive interfaces for improved sampling with enhanced sampling methods. Flux Matching is validated through the generation of current velocity trajectories and rate constant calculations on molecular systems.

</details>


### [312] [Tangram: Unlocking Non-Uniform KV Cache for Efficient Multi-turn LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06302)
*Hyungmin Kim,Minsoo Kim,Hongseok Kim,Jungwook Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tangram proposes a practical system for non-uniform KV caches in multi-turn LLM serving, achieving substantial throughput gains while preserving accuracy by using deterministic budgets, head-group page clustering, and AOT load balancing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-uniform KV caches can preserve more information but cause fragmentation, scheduling complexity, and low kernel utilization, hurting throughput and resource efficiency in LLM serving.

Method: Three techniques: deterministic budget allocation per attention head to fix memory footprints; head group page clustering with independent vectorized page tables to improve memory reclamation; ahead-of-time load balancing using static budgets to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead.

Result: Throughput improved up to 2.6x over baselines with no accuracy loss; public implementation provided.

Conclusion: Tangram demonstrates a practical pathway to deploy non-uniform KV caches in production-like LLM serving by addressing systemic inefficiencies; the approach is broadly applicable to KV-cache-based acceleration, contingent on stable budget profiling.

Abstract: Multi-turn Large Language Model (LLM) serving is critical for consistent user experiences, yet the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache imposes significant pressure on GPU memory and bandwidth. Non-uniform KV compression effectively preserves more information by considering the individual importance of each KV cache. However, such KV cache heterogeneity introduces various systemic challenges - including memory fragmentation, scheduling complexities, and diminished kernel utilization - which collectively lead to significant inefficiencies in existing LLM serving systems.
  To overcome these challenges, we present Tangram, a novel serving system designed to make Non-uniform KV caches practical. Tangram addresses systemic inefficiencies through three core techniques: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation assigns a static memory footprint to each head based on its intrinsic pattern, entirely eliminating dynamic scheduling overhead and prefill stalls; (2) Head Group Page clusters attention heads with similar retention demands and manages them with independent, vectorized page tables, thereby maximizing physical memory reclamation; and (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing leverages static budget profiles to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead. Experimental results show that Tangram improves throughput by up to 2.6x compared to existing baselines, while fully preserving model accuracy. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/TANGRAM.

</details>


### [313] [Plug-and-Play Guidance for Discrete Diffusion Models via Gradient-Informed Logit Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06303)
*Hongkun Dou,Zike Chen,Fengji Li,Hongjue Li,Yue Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient-Informed Logit Correction (GILC) offers a plug-and-play, training-free method for guiding discrete diffusion models by repurposing a pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy, with a Jacobian-free mechanism to safely correct logits in high-dimensional discrete spaces. It supports both differentiable and non-differentiable rewards and achieves state-of-the-art performance across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks without retraining, often outperforming fine-tuning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete diffusion with guidance suffers from high computational cost and gradient instability in high-dimensional discrete spaces; retraining or fine-tuning is expensive or impractical. A lightweight, stable, and training-free guidance mechanism is needed to leverage pretrained denoisers for controllable generation.

Method: Repurpose a pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy to estimate guidance signals. Introduce a Jacobian-free correction mechanism that directly adjusts the clean logits, avoiding gradient instability. The framework is plug-and-play and supports both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions, enabling flexible constrained generation across domains.

Result: Empirical evaluation on DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks shows GILC achieves state-of-the-art performance without additional training and often outperforms fine-tuning baselines.

Conclusion: GILC provides an efficient, stable, and versatile guidance framework for discrete diffusion models, enabling high-quality controllable generation without retraining and with broad applicability across biological and chemical sequence domains.

Abstract: Controllable generation with discrete diffusion models is often hindered by high computational overhead or the need for retraining. In this paper, we present \underline{\textbf{G}}radient-\underline{\textbf{I}}nformed \underline{\textbf{L}}ogit \underline{\textbf{C}}orrection (\textbf{GILC}), a plug-and-play framework that efficiently estimates guidance signals by repurposing the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy. To circumvent the gradient instability inherent in high-dimensional discrete spaces, we introduce a Jacobian-free mechanism that directly corrects the clean prediction logits, facilitating stable and effective guidance. Our method accommodates both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions. Extensive experiments across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks demonstrate that GILC achieves state-of-the-art performance without additional training, frequently outperforming fine-tuning approaches.

</details>


### [314] [Learning What to Forget: Improving LLM Unlearning via Learned Token-Level Importance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06320)
*Gizem Yüce,Giorgos Nikolaou,Nicolas Flammarion*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ATWU jointly learns token forget-specificity and model parameters via alternating optimization with a linear hidden-state scorer, achieving strong forget-retain trade-offs without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address heterogeneity in token forget relevance and reduce reliance on heuristics or auxiliary models; provide a principled objective based on retain vs forget loss conflict.

Method: Formulates a joint optimization over model parameters and token weights; uses an alternating optimization with a simple linear scorer over hidden states; no external supervision; applied to TOFU and RWKU benchmarks.

Result: State-of-the-art forget-retain trade-offs; outperforms sample-level methods, probability-based token weighting heuristics, and auxiliary-model approaches; learned scores align with ground-truth forget-specific spans; interpretable token-level signals; low computational overhead.

Conclusion: Retain-conflict is an effective criterion for identifying what to forget; token-level forget-specificity can be learned unsupervised from model representations; ATWU is practical for unsupervised token-level unlearning.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model while preserving its general capabilities. For autoregressive language models, not all tokens in a forget sample are equally relevant to forgetting. Existing approaches either ignore this heterogeneity or rely on auxiliary models, heuristics, or external annotations to estimate each token's relevance for forgetting. We instead characterize it through the interaction with the retain objective: a token is forget-specific to the extent that minimizing the forget loss on that token does not conflict with retain optimality. We formalize this perspective as a joint optimization problem over the model parameters and the token weights and show that, under a natural separation condition, the resulting objective recovers the oracle forget-specific token support. Motivated by this formulation, we introduce Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU), a lightweight framework that jointly learns token forget-specificity and model parameters during unlearning using a simple linear scorer over the hidden states, without external token level supervision. Across TOFU and RWKU, ATWU achieves state of the art forget-retain trade-offs, outperforming sample-level methods, probability-based token weighting heuristics, and auxiliary-model-based approaches. Moreover, the learned scores align substantially better with ground truth forget-specific spans, indicating that ATWU identifies semantically meaningful token level forgetting signals. Overall, our results suggest that retain conflict provides an effective criterion for identifying what language models should forget, enabling unsupervised learning of token level forget-specificity directly from model representations with minimal computational overhead.

</details>


### [315] [PAMF: Prior-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Incomplete Time Series Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06328)
*Ziwen Kan,Wugeng Zheng,Tianlong Chen,Song Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PAMF explicitly handles two missingness patterns in multimodal healthcare time-series by prior-aware flow matching and weight-sharing encoders that couple imputation with task prediction, achieving strong downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Healthcare multimodal time-series often have within-modality and modality-level missingness; existing methods either ignore missingness specifics or separate imputation from prediction, limiting downstream utility.

Method: PAMF initializes flow-matching with type-specific priors to distinguish missingness types; uses architecturally matched encoders with weight sharing to connect imputation and prediction; integrates imputation and classification through prior-aware flow matching and shared representations.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple multimodal healthcare time-series benchmarks shows PAMF achieves strongest overall downstream performance across diverse datasets and missing settings, outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling different missingness patterns and coupling imputation with prediction via shared representations improves robustness and predictive performance in multimodal healthcare time-series.

Abstract: In healthcare, multimodal time series tasks often operate on incomplete observations in practice, for example when ECG segments are lost because electrodes detach or an entire respiratory channel is unavailable during overnight monitoring. Such missingness typically appears in two structurally distinct patterns: within-modality missing, where values are absent within an otherwise observed modality, and modality-level missing, where an entire modality is unavailable. Existing methods typically represent unobserved data implicitly through masks or missing embeddings, without learning instance-specific missing information, and most are designed for only one missingness pattern. A natural approach is to explicitly estimate the missing data; however, existing imputation methods treat missingness uniformly despite their different structural priors, and the imputation process is often isolated from downstream tasks, preventing downstream tasks from guiding imputation toward more informative representations. To address these limitations, we present PAMF, a multimodal time-series framework that explicitly handles different missingness patterns while coupling imputation with downstream prediction through prior-aware flow matching and weight sharing. Specifically, the method initializes the flow-matching source state with type-specific priors to distinguish two missing types. It further connects imputation and classification through architecturally matched encoders with weight sharing, transferring task-relevant representations into the imputation process. Experiments on multiple multimodal healthcare time-series benchmarks show that the proposed method achieves the strongest overall downstream performance across diverse datasets and missing settings compared with existing baselines.

</details>


### [316] [Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders for Effective Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06333)
*Seyed Arshan Dalili,Mehrdad Mahdavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SASA fixes a core limitation of Sparse Autoencoders for LLMs by moving from single-vector decoders to subspace-based decoders with group sparsity and nuclear-norm regularization, addressing feature splitting due to multi-dimensional latent structure. This changes the sample complexity from exponential to polynomial in the intrinsic feature dimension and improves interpretability on large models, matching or surpassing standard SAEs with half the token budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SAEs force each latent feature to have a one-dimensional decoder direction, which is incompatible with the multi-dimensional intrinsic structure of model features. This mismatch causes feature splitting, spurious multiplicity, and degraded interpretability in mechanistic analyses of LLMs.

Method: Replace single-vector decoders with learned decoder subspaces, enforce block sparsity via Top-s group gating, and adapt each group's rank using a nuclear-norm regularizer. Theoretical result: when block size r >= feature dimension d_i, a single group can represent the feature slice and is the global minimizer of the SASA objective, yielding polynomial rather than exponential sample complexity. Empirical evaluation on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B.

Result: Theoretically, there exists a continuous path from the true d_i-dimensional basis to a lower-risk, l1-regularized SAE that drives dictionaries into an exponential regime-less preferred multi-group structure. Practically, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches or exceeds standard SAEs while using about half the token budget for training.

Conclusion: Allowing subspace decoders with block sparsity and rank-adaptive regularization yields more faithful, compact representations of multi-dimensional features, mitigating fragmentation, improving interpretability in mechanistic analyses, and reducing data/compute requirements for training SAEs on large language models.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for mechanistic interpretability in large language models, yet their formulation assigns each latent feature a single decoder direction, implicitly assuming features to be one-dimensional. We show that this assumption mismatches with the multi-dimensional structure of model features, provably inducing feature splitting through two distinct mechanisms. Geometrically, reconstructing a feature of intrinsic dimension $d_i \ge 2$ to error $\varepsilon$ with single-direction decoders forces a number of atoms that is exponential in $d_i$. From an end-to-end optimization perspective, this splitting is not merely possible but actively preferred. We prove that there exists a continuous path from the true $d_i$-dimensional basis to a strictly lower risk of the $\ell_1$-regularized SAE objective, whose descent directions drive any trained dictionary into that exponential regime. A single coherent feature is therefore fragmented across many near-collinear latents, producing spurious multiplicity and obscuring the intrinsic geometry. Motivated by this, we introduce Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA), which replace single-vector decoders with learned decoder subspaces, enforce block sparsity via Top-$s$ group gating, and adapt each group's effective rank with a nuclear-norm regularizer. We then show that once the block size satisfies $r \ge d_i$, a single group not only can represent the entire feature slice but is the global minimizer of the SASA objective. This consolidation yields a sample complexity polynomial in $d_i$ rather than exponential -- a decisive advantage given that every training activation costs an LLM forward pass. Empirically, on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches or exceeds standard SAEs while training on roughly half the token budget.

</details>


### [317] [Quantifying the Privacy of Counterfactuals by Leveraging Membership Inference Attacks Against Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06334)
*Maryam Babaei,Yingke Wang,Hadrien Lautraite,Heber H. Arcolezi,Ulrich Aivodji,Sebastien Gambs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Counterfactual explanations can leak private information; membership inference attacks can succeed on counterfactuals, even without model access, implying privacy risks in releasing counterfactuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactuals are used to explain model decisions and resemble synthetic data; releasing them may reveal information about training data and model semantics, enabling privacy breaches.

Method: Repurpose membership inference attacks designed for synthetic data to counterfactuals; evaluate across types of counterfactuals; additionally show black-box style inference using only a set of counterfactuals without querying the model.

Result: Attacks are effective at inferring membership of training data from counterfactuals; can be performed with no model access using only counterfactuals; demonstrates privacy vulnerabilities in releasing counterfactuals.

Conclusion: Researchers and practitioners should exercise caution when disseminating counterfactual explanations; implement privacy-preserving measures (e.g., DP, sanitization) or constrain counterfactual exposure; consider defense strategies.

Abstract: Counterfactuals are typically used in high-stakes decision areas to explain a machine learning model by showing how changes to the user profiles result in the desired outcome. However, explaining the model's decisions through counterfactuals can also be exploited by an adversary to conduct privacy attacks against the model or its training data. Drawing on the analogy that counterfactuals provide realistic substitutes for real training data, similar to synthetic data, we demonstrate in this paper how it is possible to successfully perform privacy attacks on counterfactuals by drawing on the attacks developed against synthetic data. More precisely, we investigate the effectiveness of the membership inference attacks designed for synthetic data on various types of counterfactuals. Additionally, while existing membership inference attacks against counterfactuals usually require to be able to query the model, we show how it is possible to perform successful membership inference attacks using only a set of counterfactuals, with no access to the model from which they are generated. Our results demonstrate that model developers should be more cautious when releasing counterfactuals to various users, as it can lead to a privacy breach.

</details>


### [318] [Bridging Domain Expertise and Generalization for Performance Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06335)
*Shuxuan Li,Zhilin Zhao,Quyu Kong,Wei-Shi Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRAP: A performance-estimation method under distribution shift that fuses a foundation model and a base model. It aligns the base and foundation predictions via temperature-scaled calibration, then combines them with confidence-based weighting to form a reference distribution. The base model's predictions are then compared to this reference to estimate true performance, showing improvements over output-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Performance estimation under distribution shift requires reliable indicators without ground-truth labels. Existing approaches rely solely on a model's own outputs, which become biased and poorly correlated with true performance when distributions shift. A robust surrogate that integrates external knowledge and domain-specific expertise is needed.

Method: 1) Use an external foundation model and the base model. 2) Calibrate the foundation model's prediction distribution to align with the base model by temperature-scaled calibration to minimize divergence. 3) Fuse the aligned predictions via confidence-based weighting to create a refined reference distribution that combines robustness from the foundation model with domain-specific insight from the base model. 4) Estimate performance by measuring how closely the base model's predictions agree with this reference.

Result: Extensive experiments across varied datasets and architectures show that FRAP yields consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods under distribution shift.

Conclusion: FRAP demonstrates that leveraging the complementary strengths of a foundation model and the base model, through calibrated alignment and weighted fusion, provides a more reliable surrogate for ground-truth labels and improves performance estimation under distribution shift.

Abstract: Performance estimation under distribution shift aims to predict how a model behaves on an unlabeled test set whose distribution differs from the training data, a scenario that requires reliable indicators that can faithfully reflect model behavior without ground-truth labels. Existing approaches rely solely on the outputs of the given model whose biases are amplified once the distribution shifts, weakening the correlation with the true performance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose Fused Reference Alignment Prediction (FRAP), which leverages the complementary strengths of an external foundation model and the base model to construct a more reliable surrogate of the ground-truth labels. FRAP aligns the prediction distribution of the foundation model with that of the base model by applying temperature-scaled calibration that minimizes their divergence. The aligned predictions are fused through confidence-based weighting into a refined reference distribution that integrates robustness from the foundation model and domain-specific expertise from the base model, and performance estimation is obtained by measuring how closely the base model predictions agree with this reference. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and architectures show that FRAP provides consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods under distribution shift.

</details>


### [319] [Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06344)
*Zehua Cheng,Wei Dai,Jiahao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP) introduces SE(3)-equivariant Gaussian mixture messages in a factor-graph for probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables. Rank-2 precision is constructed via equivariant outer products and differentiable spectral decomposition, with a greedily reduced mixture that commutes with SE(3). It achieves accurate, multimodal, anisotropic uncertainty representations with fast inference, outperforming diffusion baselines and robustly converging in multi-agent robotic settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Probabilistic inference on SE(3)-embedded variables requires beliefs that transform correctly under SE(3) and can express anisotropic, multimodal uncertainty. Existing equivariant networks yield only scalars/vectors, forcing simplistic representations and collapsing multimodal landscapes to means.

Method: A factor-graph framework where messages are equivariant Gaussian mixture models. Rank-2 precision matrices arise from equivariant outer products; a differentiable spectral decomposition extracts eigenstructure. A greedy KL-based mixture reduction preserves SE(3) equivariance by commuting with the group action. Evaluations on molecular conformers (GEOM-QM9, GEOM-Drugs) and multi-body robotics.

Result: On GEOM-QM9/GEOM-Drugs: 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 Å with sub-second latency, >100x faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. In multi-body robotics: ENBP converges whereas vanilla loopy BP diverges with 15+ agents; achieves near-zero collision rates and machine-precision SE(3) equivariance error (~1e-7 vs ~1e-1 for augmented baselines).

Conclusion: ENBP provides principled, SE(3)-equivariant probabilistic inference with multimodal, anisotropic uncertainty and scalable, fast inference. It addresses key limitations of scalar/vector-only equivariant nets and improves convergence robustness in complex, spatially-embedded domains.

Abstract: Probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables requires beliefs that respect $SE(3)$ symmetry, yet existing equivariant networks produce only scalars and vectors -- not the rank-2 precision tensors needed for anisotropic uncertainty, and single-component messages collapse multi-modal energy landscapes to physically meaningless averages. We introduce Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP), a factor-graph framework whose messages are equivariant Gaussian mixture models with sufficient statistics that transform exactly under $SE(3)$. Rank-2 precision matrices are synthesised via equivariant outer products, ingested through differentiable spectral decomposition, and kept tractable by a greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with $SE(3)$. On GEOM-QM9 and GEOM-Drugs, ENBP achieves 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 $\mathring{A}$ error with sub-second latency -- over $100\times$ faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. On multi-body robotic inference, vanilla loopy BP diverges at 15+ agents while ENBP converges with near-zero collision rates and machine-precision equivariance error (${\sim}10^{-7}$ vs.\ $10^{-1}$ for augmented baselines).

</details>


### [320] [Performance Evaluation of GraphCast for Medium-Range Weather Forecasting over Brazil](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06348)
*Wolfgang R. Rowell,Lucas S. Kupssinskü*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphCast shows regime-dependent performance vs ECMWF IFS HRES across four Brazilian sub-regions; underperforms at mid-range for Z500 in austral winter but surpasses at longer lead times, and dampens convective noise while capturing large-scale moisture in austral summer, establishing Brazil-specific baselines for tropicalized AI models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess regional performance of ML Weather Prediction (MLWP) in the Global South and establish a baseline in Brazil to guide tropicalization and regional resilience of AI models.

Method: Benchmark GraphCast against deterministic IFS HRES using a cloud-native pipeline and WeatherBench-X across four Brazilian climatic sub-regions. Evaluate tropospheric variables T850, Q850, Z500 over four seasonal windows with IFS analysis as ground truth; compute statistical metrics.

Result: Regime-dependent skill emerges: winter (austral) mid-range 2–7 days shows GraphCast underperformance for Z500 over southern Brazil, while extended range favors GraphCast due to smoothing; summer wet season sees GraphCast capture large-scale moisture transport and dampen high-frequency convective variability, reducing deterministic forecast degradation.

Conclusion: Provides a Brazil baseline and delineates physical boundaries for tropicalization efforts to optimize AI models for regional resilience.

Abstract: The paradigm of global weather forecasting is rapidly shifting with the emergence of Machine Learning Weather Prediction models (MLWP). While these data-driven architectures demonstrate remarkable global skill, regional benchmarks in the Global South remain scarce, leaving their efficacy in complex, highly convective environments largely unverified. This study evaluates the performance of GraphCast operational against the deterministic ECMWF IFS HRES as baseline across four distinct Brazilian climatic sub-regions. Utilizing a scalable, cloud-native pipeline and the WeatherBench-X framework for benchmarking weather models, we assess selected tropospheric variables ($T_{850}$, $Q_{850}$, $Z_{500}$) over four selected seasonal windows, employing the operational IFS analysis as the ground truth to calculate the statistical metrics for both models. Results reveal a regime-dependent skill profile. During the austral winter, GraphCast underperforms in the medium range (lead days 2-7) for $Z_{500}$ when resolving fast-propagating baroclinic systems over southern Brazil, but regains an advantage in the extended range, where its inherent smoothing of chaotic small-scale variability becomes beneficial under deterministic skill metrics. Conversely, during the austral summer wet season, GraphCast accurately captures large-scale moisture transport while intrinsically dampening the high-frequency convective variability that degrades deterministic NWP temperature forecasts. These findings establish a baseline for Brazil and define the specific physical boundaries that will guide future ``tropicalization'' efforts, aiming to optimize these foundational AI models for regional resilience.

</details>


### [321] [Maximising the Set-Piece Return: Optimising Football Corner Tactics with Graph Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06353)
*Sean Groom,Michael Groom,Francisco Belo,Axl Rice,Liam Anderson,Victor-Alexandru Darvariu,Shuo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A graph-based reinforcement learning approach optimizes corner-kick routines to generalise attacking configurations and strategies, maximizing first-contact shot probability. It outperforms baseline optimisation on 3,000+ Premier League corners, suggesting reward-driven tactical discovery over imitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Move beyond reproducing historical patterns in football tactics and discover generalisable, novel configurations and strategies for set-pieces using graph-structured RL.

Method: A graph reinforcement learning architecture processes graph-structured representations of players. A central policy adjusts attacking positions and velocities to maximise first-contact shot probability, generalising to arbitrary starting configurations. Evaluated on 3k Premier League corners with matched inference budgets.

Result: The approach significantly outperforms baseline optimisation under the same inference budgets across the dataset (>3,000 corners), indicating strong practical gains and improved generalisation for set-piece tactics.

Conclusion: Graph RL can shift set-piece analysis from historical evaluation and imitation toward reward-driven tactical discovery, enabling generalisable policies for corner-kick routines that adapt to varying initial configurations.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly employed for the evaluation of football tactics. However, existing approaches focus on characterising historical actions or analyst-specified counterfactual scenarios. In this work, we seek to go beyond the imitation of historically observed patterns towards discovering new generalisable player configurations and strategies. To tackle this, we focus on optimising corner kick routines, and formulate a decision-making problem in which a central policy makes adjustments to attacking player positions and velocities to maximise first contact shot probability. Unlike classic optimisation that solves for isolated setups, we contribute a reinforcement learning architecture operating on graph-structured data that yields a general policy for adjusting arbitrary starting player positions. Evaluated on over 3,000 Premier League corners, our approach strongly outperforms baseline optimisation techniques under matched inference budgets. Our results suggest that graph reinforcement learning can shift set-piece analysis from historical evaluation and imitation towards reward-driven tactical discovery.

</details>


### [322] [End-to-End Subgraph Detection with GraphDETR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06364)
*Dexiong Chen,Till Hendrik Schulz,Karsten Borgwardt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphDETR reframes subgraph detection as end-to-end set prediction using a GNN encoder, learnable query tokens, and a transformer decoder, enabling joint detection in a single forward pass and extending to approximate matching; handles patterns up to 50 nodes in graphs up to 1000 nodes with strong molecular results (AP100 91.2 on ChEMBL).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subgraph isomorphism is NP-complete; traditional combinatorial methods scale poorly and are limited to exact matching. A differentiable, end-to-end approach can handle approximate matching and larger graphs.

Method: Encode the target graph with a graph neural network; use a fixed set of learnable query vectors; decode with a transformer to predict all pattern occurrences jointly; train end-to-end with bipartite matching.

Result: Detects diverse patterns (molecular structures, cycles, cliques, fuzzy patterns) up to 50 nodes in graphs up to 1000 nodes; on ChEMBL, achieves AP100 = 91.2 for molecular functional group detection, predicting the complete set of functional groups per molecule.

Conclusion: GraphDETR provides a scalable, end-to-end differentiable framework for subgraph detection that generalizes beyond exact matching and is effective on chemical graphs, enabling broader pattern discovery in graphs.

Abstract: Subgraph detection seeks to identify whether and where instances of query patterns occur within a larger graph. This problem is fundamental across scientific domains and is closely related to subgraph isomorphism, which is NP-complete, limiting combinatorial approaches to small patterns or moderately sized graphs. We introduce GraphDETR, a deep learning framework that formulates subgraph detection as a set prediction problem, analogous to DETR in object detection. GraphDETR encodes the target graph with a graph neural network, and employs a fixed set of learnable query vectors, decoded via a transformer decoder, to predict all pattern occurrences jointly in a single forward pass. This is enabled by training the model end-to-end with bipartite matching. Unlike traditional combinatorial methods that only solve exact structural matching, GraphDETR naturally extends to approximate matching, enabling detection beyond exact pattern correspondence. Empirically, we show that GraphDETR can detect diverse patterns, such as molecular structures, cycles, cliques, and fuzzy patterns of up to 50 nodes, in target graphs with up to 1000 nodes. We further evaluate on molecular functional group detection over the ChEMBL dataset, where GraphDETR predicts the complete set of functional groups per molecule, achieving a strong performance of $\text{AP}_{100} = 91.2$.

</details>


### [323] [Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator for HEC-RAS 2D Water-Surface Elevation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06385)
*Edward Holmberg,Elias Ioup,Md Meftahul Ferdaus,Mahdi Abdelguerfi,Julian Simeonov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A no-forcing, increment-based learned surrogate (LRFIO) for WSE prediction on native cells in HEC-RAS 2D, achieving large speedups with controlled, selectively added complexity guided by validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable solver-consistent, fast surrogate predictions directly on nonuniform grids to avoid raster remapping errors and reduce compute cost in 2D hydraulic simulations.

Method: Develop LRFIO: calibrate an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories; deploy via closed-form native-cell rollout; establish a base response hierarchy (persistence, global calibrated inertia, segmented response-field inertia); treat segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia as learnable options retained only if justified by validation; use a selector audit to bound complexity; evaluate across four HEC-RAS 2D benchmarks; report rollout times and horizon-normalized speedup.

Result: LRFIO demonstrates adaptive learned complexity across domains; maximum validation regret = 4.30%; deployment rollout times range 0.003–0.242 s; Beaver Bayou horizon-normalized speedup ≈ 2.75×10^4 over HEC-RAS; current native-cell increment serves as a strong predictive scaffold, with added complexity retained only when empirically warranted.

Conclusion: LRFIO provides a solver-conditioned predictive scaffold for 2D hydraulic surrogates; cross-dataset evidence shows adaptive complexity and substantial computational gains; unnecessary complexity should be avoided to preserve efficiency.

Abstract: This article presents a cross-dataset evaluation of learned native-cell surrogate models for solver-consistent water-surface elevation (WSE) prediction in HEC-RAS 2D. To avoid raster remapping error and information-access confounding, surrogates are evaluated directly on the original nonuniform computational cells under an explicit policy that separates static project inputs, current hydraulic state, project-input forcing, calibration-derived quantities, and future solver-output targets. We introduce the Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator (LRFIO), a no-forcing, increment-based learned surrogate that calibrates an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories and deploys the retained operator through closed-form native-cell rollout. LRFIO evaluates a base-case-first response hierarchy consisting of persistence, global calibrated inertia, and segmented response-field inertia. Segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia are treated as learnable modeling choices, with added complexity retained only when validation evidence justifies its cost. Evaluated across four diverse HEC-RAS 2D benchmarks, LRFIO retains different response structures for different domains, demonstrating adaptive learned complexity. The selector audit shows controlled complexity with a maximum validation regret of 4.30%. During deployment, retained rollout times range from 0.003 s to 0.242 s, and the Beaver Bayou measured-solve comparison gives an estimated 2.75 x 10^4 horizon-normalized speedup over HEC-RAS. These results indicate that the current native-cell increment is a strong solver-conditioned predictive scaffold and that added response-field, neural, or spatial complexity should be retained only when empirically justified.

</details>


### [324] [In-Context Multiple Instance Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06458)
*Alexander Möllers,Marvin Sextro,Julius Hense,Gabriel Dernbach,Klaus-Robert Müller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pretraining an in-context Perceiver-style MIL model on synthetic bag data enables zero-shot/few-shot adaptation to new MIL tasks, with single-pass inference, outperforming task-specific supervised baselines across 12 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MIL often suffers in low-label regimes and with overfitting/underfitting in flexible vs rigid models. Synthetic pretraining aims to inject inductive biases and enable rapid adaptation to new bag-based tasks.

Method: Train a Perceiver-style in-context learner on synthetic, bag-structured data generated by multiple generators. Use a mixture of generators to capture complementary inductive biases. At inference, perform a single forward pass with no gradient updates to classify bags. Evaluate on 12 MIL benchmarks, comparing to supervised baselines.

Result: Pretraining on a mixture of synthetic generators yields the best average performance across twelve MIL benchmarks, outperforming supervised baselines requiring task-specific training.

Conclusion: Synthetic pretraining with a mixture of bag-structure generators and a Perceiver-style in-context learner produces a flexible, single-pass MIL classifier that generalizes across tasks better than traditional supervised approaches that need task-specific tuning.

Abstract: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) addresses problems where supervision is available at the level of bags of instances and has been successfully applied in fields ranging from computational pathology to satellite imagery. Nevertheless, existing algorithms struggle in the low-label regime that characterizes many real-world applications. Flexible models overfit and rigid ones fail to adapt to the task at hand. We show that pretraining an in-context learner with a Perceiver-style architecture on synthetic data yields a model that can solve new tasks from a handful of labeled bags. At inference time, classification happens in a single forward pass and requires no gradient updates. We propose and investigate different synthetic data generators for bag-structured data and find that they capture complementary inductive biases. A model pretrained on a mixture of these generators inherits their per-task strengths and achieves the best average performance across twelve MIL benchmarks, outperforming supervised baselines that require task-specific training.

</details>


### [325] [Proper Scoring Rules for Right-Censored Survival Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06393)
*Jef Jonkers,Glenn Van Wallendael,Luc Duchateau,Sofie Van Hoecke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a coherent framework to extend proper scoring rules to right-censored survival data by composing predictive distributions with the censoring mechanism, yielding localized and marginalized scores (including censored versions of CRPS, Brier, pinball, energy score), with propriety guarantees and a sample-based objective (censored engression) for multivariate survival models; empirical results show correct forecast ranking and improved training over naive penalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Right-censoring invalidates standard scoring rules for probabilistic forecasts. A unified, properly scoring framework that respects the censoring mechanism is needed for fair evaluation and training of survival predictions.

Method: Transform the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism to obtain the observed-data law, then apply the corresponding proper score to this induced law. Derive localized scores for fixed censoring times and marginalized scores for random/partial censoring; recover right-censored likelihood and IPCW criteria; extend to censored versions of CRPS, Brier, energy, and pinball; prove propriety under conditional independent censoring and identifiability; introduce censored engression for multivariate modeling.

Result: The framework yields scores that are proper (and strictly proper on the identifiable region) and align with existing censored criteria; forecast rankings under censoring are stable, whereas plug-in weighted scores can reverse rankings; censored engression improves training on censored outcomes in experiments.

Conclusion: A coherent, unifying approach to scoring and training for right-censored survival forecasts, enabling both principled evaluation and effective learning across various censoring regimes and extending to multivariate settings.

Abstract: Proper scoring rules provide a rigorous theoretical basis for the training and evaluation of probabilistic forecasts. However, in the presence of right censoring, the event time is only partially observed, rendering conventional scoring rules inapplicable in their standard form. We propose a framework for proper scoring of right-censored survival outcomes based on a simple idea: first, map the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism, then apply the underlying proper score on the induced observed-data law. This yields localized scores for fixed censoring times and marginalized scores when the censoring time is random or only partially observed. The resulting construction recovers familiar right-censored likelihood and IPCW-type criteria within a coherent framework, while also yielding right-censored versions of the CRPS, pinball loss, Brier score, and energy score. We show that the marginalized score is proper under conditional independent censoring and strictly proper on the identifiable region. The same principle also leads to censored engression, a sample-based learning objective for multivariate right-censored survival modeling. In experiments, our scores correctly rank the oracle forecast across several censoring regimes, whereas forecast-dependent plug-in weighted scores can exhibit ranking reversals. Censored engression likewise substantially improves over naive training on censored outcomes.

</details>


### [326] [The Post-GCN Decade Revisited: Curvature-Stratified Evaluation of Relational Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06397)
*Shuo Wang,Xiangyu Wang,Quanxin Wang,Bailin Wu,Bokui Wang,Shunyang Huang,Boyan Deng,Haonan Liu,Ruiyi Fang,Zhenxiang Xu,Boyu Wang,Zhao Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introducing curvature-stratified evaluation to reveal geometry-dependent performance in relational learning; standard benchmarks hide regime-specific trade-offs; 18 models across 14 datasets; GFMs not always superior to geometry-aligned GNNs; resources released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggregated metrics across heterogeneous datasets assume a uniform underlying structure, causing bias and potentially misleading conclusions about generalization. Intrinsic geometry (curvature) is a latent factor affecting model effectiveness.

Method: Partition datasets by curvature into positive, negative, and near-zero regimes. Evaluate 18 models (GCNs, GFMs, and tabular methods) on 14 datasets. Analyze regime-stability of rankings and cross-regime shifts. Propose a geometry-aware evaluation protocol. Release code, curvature-stratified splits, and tools.

Result: Rankings are stable within curvature regimes but shift across regimes, indicating geometry-dependent transferability. GFMs show diminishing returns in curvature-aligned regimes compared to geometry-aware GNNs.

Conclusion: Standard aggregated benchmarks are insufficient for fair comparisons. A curvature-stratified, geometry-aware evaluation yields more reliable and interpretable assessments. Resources and code are released to support reproducible evaluation.

Abstract: Current evaluation practices in relational learning rely heavily on flat leaderboards that average performance across heterogeneous datasets, implicitly assuming a uniform underlying structure. We show that this assumption introduces systematic bias: it obscures geometry-dependent performance variations and can lead to misleading conclusions about model generalization. In this work, we identify intrinsic geometry as a key latent factor governing model effectiveness. We demonstrate that conventional aggregated metrics mask critical performance trade-offs that only become visible when datasets are stratified by their geometric properties. To address this issue, we introduce a curvature-stratified evaluation framework that partitions datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. Our benchmark evaluates 18 representative models including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), Graph Foundation Models (GFMs), and tabular learning methods across 14 datasets. We find that model rankings are highly stable within each curvature regime but shift significantly across regimes, indicating that performance is fundamentally geometry-dependent rather than universally transferable. Notably, we identify regimes where GFMs offer diminishing returns compared to geometry-aligned GNNs. Based on these findings, we propose a geometry-aware evaluation protocol that yields more reliable and interpretable comparisons than standard aggregated benchmarks. We release all code, curvature-stratified dataset splits, and evaluation tools to support reproducible and rigorous assessment of future relational learning methods. Code and datasets are provided in our project homepage: https://sirbabbage.github.io/CurvBench_HOME/.

</details>


### [327] [Double Preconditioning (DoPr): Optimization for Test-Time Performance, not Validation Loss](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06418)
*Thomas T. Zhang,Alok Shah,Yifei Zhang,Vincent Zhang,Nikolai Matni,Max Simchowitz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduce double-preconditioning (DoPr), combining gradient-wise preconditioning (Adam/Muon) with activation-wise preconditioning (KFAC) to mitigate test-time feedback (TTF) in one-step supervised objectives deployed autoregressively. DoPr improves downstream test-time performance across TTF scenarios, but does not always improve validation loss, prompting reevaluation of evaluation metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time feedback (TTF) arises when training with a one-step objective but deployment follows its own predictions, causing mismatch between training/validation metrics and downstream task performance that grows with sequence length. Existing remedies focus on data, architecture, or objective design; there is a need for optimization-centric approaches to reduce error accumulation.

Method: Propose a new optimization paradigm, double-preconditioning (DoPr), that combines gradient-wise preconditioning (as in Adam/Muon) with activation-wise preconditioning (AP) such as KFAC. Treat AP as a drop-in activation-level intervention to standard gradient-based optimization to improve downstream performance under TTF across autoregressive, generative, and policy-learning settings.

Result: Empirical evidence suggests DoPr yields improvements in downstream test-time performance across a range of TTF settings. However, gains in test-time metrics do not consistently align with improvements in validation loss, highlighting a disjunction between one-step objective optimization and downstream performance.

Conclusion: DoPr represents a promising optimization axis for mitigating error accumulation under TTF and motivates rethinking evaluation -- downstream task metrics may be more informative than validation loss for models trained with one-step objectives; further work is needed to understand when activation-wise preconditioning benefits generalization and how to measure it.

Abstract: Many modern applications of deep learning involve training a neural network via a one-step prediction loss (e.g., $L^2$ regression, cross-entropy), but deploy the network by rolling out along its own predictions. Key examples include autoregressive language modeling, flow-based generative modeling, and robot policy learning. It is well-documented that these settings induce a phenomenon we call test-time feedback (TTF): the mismatch between the training/validation loss and downstream metrics of interest, such as task success rate and generation quality, which grows with task length. While data curation, architecture, and objective design have been proposed to combat train-test shift in TTF settings, this paper proposes optimization as a new design axis to mitigate error accumulation. Specifically, we introduce a new optimization paradigm called double-preconditioning (DoPr) uniquely tailored to the challenges of TTF. DoPr combines gradient-wise preconditioning, as in Adam and Muon, with activation-wise preconditioning (AP), such as in KFAC. We show that the addition of AP yields a drop-in intervention for increasing downstream model performance across a range of TTF settings. Interestingly, these gains in test-time performance do not consistently accompany improvements in validation loss, opening new questions about how to properly evaluate models trained with one-step supervised objectives.

</details>


### [328] [Causal Atlases from Entropic Inference: Bayesian Networks beyond Optimal DAGs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06440)
*Hazhir Aliahmadi,Irina Babayan,Greg van Anders*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Maximum-entropy ensembles yield multiple plausible causal graphs (atlases) from data, highlighting structural ambiguity and artifacts in optimized DAGs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of optimization-based DAG learning that force a single causal graph, potentially masking alternative causal structures and data-driven uncertainty.

Method: Sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of graphs consistent with noisy data from 2- and 20-node linear structural equation models to quantify inherent causal-structure ambiguity.

Result: The entropy-based approach produces an atlas of plausible causal relationships; optimized DAGs may contain artifacts and are not consistently robust across equivalently accurate topologies.

Conclusion: A data-driven atlas approach better represents causal uncertainty than a single optimized DAG, revealing multiple consistent causal maps given the data.

Abstract: Data-driven causal relationship identification is pertinent to advancing understanding of complex systems both within and beyond science. Bayesian networks offer a probabilistic method for modelling generic causal relationships via directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). However, typical techniques for constructing Bayesian networks rely on optimization, which can be ill-suited for learning causal relationships because the underlying data may admit multiple chains of causation. More data-faithful representations of causal relationships would provide frameworks for constructing multiple causal maps that are consistent with the variability that is inherent in underlying data. Here, we show that entropy-based inference generates atlases of plausible causal relationships that are consistent with underlying data. On simulated noisy data of 2- and 20-node linear structural equation models, we sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of graphs that allow us to quantify the inherent structural ambiguity in underlying causal relationships. Our method shows that "optimized" DAGs can contain causal artifacts are not consistent across equivalently accurate topologies.

</details>


### [329] [Event Detection for Parameter-to-KPI Dependency Learning for AI-RAN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06459)
*Christie Djidjev,Nicholas Kaminski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A ML-based event-detection pipeline converts noisy telemetry into binary activity indicators to recover latent parameter-KPI dependencies in AI-RAN; uses synthetic data with planted ground truth; threshold calibration is key.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interdependent AI-driven control functions in AI-RAN/O-RAN require interpretable dependency structures to manage interactions; real interactions are hard to detect in raw data.

Method: Convert continuous traces to binary events via significance-detection; create synthetic closed-loop traffic with planted latent dependencies; evaluate the dependency-recovery pipeline.

Result: The pipeline reliably recovers latent dependencies when signal-to-background separation is adequate; threshold calibration is the main determinant of event-detection quality.

Conclusion: This work provides a foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems.

Abstract: Next-generation wireless networks are expected to rely on multiple concurrent AI-driven control functions that optimize different network objectives simultaneously, particularly in AI-integrated and open radio access network architectures such as AI Radio Access Network (AI-RAN) and Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN). When these functions interact, they can interfere with one another in ways that are difficult to detect from raw network data alone. A key missing piece for managing such interactions is a reliable, interpretable dependency structure that captures which control parameters are actively influencing which network performance outcomes at any given time. This paper focuses on the event-detection step needed to support such dependency learning by converting noisy continuous telemetry into binary indicators of parameter activity and KPI response. The central difficulty is that not every fluctuation in the data reflects a genuine control interaction, so the method must distinguish real parameter-outcome relationships from background variation. Because real AI-RAN traffic traces with known parameter-KPI ground truth are difficult to obtain, we introduce a synthetic closed-loop traffic generator with planted latent dependencies. We use this controlled telemetry to evaluate a machine-learning-based dependency recovery pipeline that formulates the conversion of continuous traces into binary event indicators as a significance-detection problem. Experimental evaluation shows that the proposed pipeline reliably recovers the latent dependency structure from noisy continuous traces when the signal is sufficiently separated from background variation, while highlighting threshold calibration as the key factor controlling event-detection quality. These results constitute a foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems.

</details>


### [330] [PC Layer: Polynomial Weight Preconditioning for Improving LLM Pre-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06470)
*Senmiao Wang,Tiantian Fang,Haoran Zhang,Yushun Zhang,Kunxiang Zhao,Alex Schwing,Ruoyu Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a polynomial preconditioning (PC) layer that parameterizes weights to shape their singular-value spectrum, enabling stable conditioning during LLM training and allowing post-training merge back with no inference overhead. Demonstrates empirical gains over standard transformers on Llama-1B pre-training with AdamW and Muon optimizers, and provides theoretical justification for spectrum-bounded convergence in certain deep linear networks. Code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability and poor conditioning of weight matrices during large-scale transformer training, which can hinder convergence and performance. By actively shaping the singular-value spectrum, the PC layer aims to stabilize optimization without affecting inference efficiency after merge.

Method: Define a PC layer as a weight parameterization via a low-degree polynomial preconditioner applied to weight matrices. This reshapes the singular-value spectrum during training. After training, merge the preconditioned weights back into the original architecture so inference cost remains unchanged. Evaluate on Llama-1B pre-training with AdamW and Muon optimizers. Provide theoretical support by proving that uniformly bounded layer-wise singular values ensure geometric convergence of gradient descent to global minima for certain deep linear networks.

Result: The PC layer achieves improvement over standard transformers in Llama-1B pre-training for both AdamW and Muon optimizers. The training stability from spectrum control is supported by theory showing convergence under bounded singular values in a class of deep linear networks. Code for implementation is provided.

Conclusion: Polynomial preconditioning offers a practical way to stabilize conditioning and control the spectrum during training, with no inference overhead after weight-merge. This approach combines empirical gains with theoretical convergence guarantees for a subset of models, and the authors provide public code for reproducibility.

Abstract: We propose a preconditioning (PC) layer, a weight parameterization via polynomial preconditioner that ensures stable weight conditioning throughout LLM training. The PC module reshapes the singular-value spectrum of weight matrices via low-degree polynomial preconditioning. After training, the preconditioned weights can be merged back into the original architecture, incurring no inference overhead. We demonstrate the advantage of the proposed PC layer over standard transformers in Llama-1B pre-training, for both the AdamW and Muon optimizers. Theoretically, we justify this spectrum-control principle by proving that uniformly bounding each layer's singular values ensures geometric convergence of gradient descent to global minima, for certain deep linear networks. Our code is available at https://github.com/Empath-aln/PC-layer.

</details>


### [331] [RREDCoT: Segment-Level Reward Redistribution for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06475)
*Mykyta Ielanskyi,Kajetan Schweighofer,Lukas Aichberger,Sepp Hochreiter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RREDCoT introduces reward redistribution for Chain-of-Thought (CoT) in RL fine-tuning to reduce variance and improve credit assignment, without extra sample generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Delayed rewards in CoT-based RL (where final answer is verified after the CoT trace) cause high-variance Monte Carlo policy gradients; effective intermediate credit assignment is needed.

Method: Propose RREDCoT (Reward Redistribution for Chain of Thoughts) that uses the model itself to approximate optimal reward redistribution without generating additional samples; explores CoT trace segmentation and state-value estimation; compares against Monte Carlo sampling and attribution methods.

Result: The abstract claims advantages of RREDCoT over MC sampling and other attribution methods and provides analysis on trace segmentation and state-value estimation, though it does not present quantitative results in the abstract.

Conclusion: RREDCoT offers a scalable framework for reward redistribution in CoT RL, highlighting the importance of trace segmentation and value estimation for improved credit assignment while avoiding additional generation.

Abstract: Recent advancements in reasoning language models have been driven by Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning. Most often, these rely on the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm or modifications thereof to steer the models to produce Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces. The final answer can only be verified, and the reward assigned, after the CoT trace is complete, making it a delayed reward problem. GRPO and its modifications correspond to Monte Carlo methods in standard RL, which are known to suffer from high variance. A possible solution to this problem is the redistribution of rewards through credit assignment, where segments of the CoT trace that are important for arriving at the desirable solution are emphasized by assigning a higher reward. While Monte Carlo sampling can be used to provide an unbiased estimate of intermediate state values, its computational overhead makes it unsuitable for train-time credit assignment in long contexts at high granularity. We introduce RREDCoT (Reward REDistribution for Chain of Thoughts), which utilizes the model itself to approximate the optimal reward redistribution without additional generation. We investigate the advantages of our method compared to MC sampling and several attribution methods. We further analyze several aspects relevant to the construction of the redistribution such as segmentation of CoT traces and state value estimation.

</details>


### [332] [Pretraining Recurrent Networks without Recurrence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06479)
*Akarsh Kumar,Phillip Isola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMT reframes RNN training as supervised learning on one-step memory transitions, enabling time-parallel training and stable gradients, and outperforming BPTT on language modeling and pixel sequence modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of standard BPTT: sequential processing, vanishing/exploding gradients, and poor long-range credit assignment in RNNs; enable scalable, time-parallel training for nonlinear RNNs.

Method: Train nonlinear RNNs by predicting the next memory state from the current memory and next input, via a Transformer-based encoder that assigns predictive-state labels (m_t, x_{t+1}) → m_{t+1}. Decouple what to remember from how memory is updated, enabling an O(1) gradient path and no RNN unrolling; train with supervised learning on one-step memory transitions.

Result: SMT outperforms BPTT when pretraining various RNN architectures on tasks such as language modeling and pixel sequence modeling.

Conclusion: SMT enables time-parallel training for nonlinear RNNs, improves handling of long-range dependencies, and could facilitate scaling of temporal-abstraction models by decoupling memory content from its update.

Abstract: Training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) requires assigning credit across long sequences of computations. Standard backpropagation through time (BPTT) addresses this problem poorly: it is sequential in time, limiting parallelism, and suffers from vanishing or exploding gradients, making long-range associations difficult to learn. We propose Supervised Memory Training (SMT), a method for training nonlinear RNNs that sidesteps recurrent credit propagation entirely by reducing RNN training to supervised learning on one-step memory transition labels $(m_t, x_{t+1}) \rightarrow m_{t+1}$. SMT acquires these memory labels by training a Transformer-based encoder on a predictive state objective--retaining only information from the past necessary to predict the future. By decoupling what to remember from how to update memory, SMT enables time-parallel RNN training with a stable $O(1)$ length gradient path between any two tokens--without ever unrolling the RNN. We find that SMT outperforms BPTT when pretraining various RNN architectures on tasks like language modeling and pixel sequence modeling. SMT enables nonlinear RNNs to better capture long-range dependencies and train in parallel, potentially unlocking the scaling of models that build temporal abstractions of past experience.

</details>


### [333] [Regret Minimization with Adaptive Opponents in Repeated Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06486)
*Mingyang Liu,Asuman Ozdaglar,Tiancheng Yu,Kaiqing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret) for adaptive opponents in repeated games; shows sublinear regret under conditions; provides three algorithms; enables learning of subgame-perfect equilibria; experiments show improved cooperation (Stag-Hunt).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard external regret misses adaptive, history-dependent behavior in repeated games. RP-Regret accounts for counterfactual reasoning by comparing realized utility to the best-in-hindsight when all players can respond to history.

Method: Identify necessary conditions for RP-Regret sublinearity with respect to regret comparator variation and the memory of comparator/opponent strategies. Propose three algorithms: (i) optimization-oracle based; (ii) convex, linearized surrogate minimization at each step; (iii) direct RP-Regret minimization when opponents adapt slowly. Analyze non-convexity and provide conditions or algorithms to address it. Show that when all players minimize RP-Regret (or linearized RP-Regret) certain subgame-perfect equilibria can be learned; include experiments.

Result: RP-Regret can be driven sublinearly under stated conditions. The proposed algorithms provide practical approaches to minimize non-convex regret in repeated games with adaptive opponents. If all players run RP-Regret-minimizing procedures, subgame-perfect equilibria become learnable; experiments (e.g., Stag-Hunt) indicate more cooperative outcomes with higher utility when minimizing RP-Regret.

Conclusion: RP-Regret offers a native, game-theoretic regret notion that supports stronger comparators and cooperative outcomes in dynamic, history-dependent settings. It enables equilibrium learning under adaptive opponents, with algorithmic strategies to handle non-convexity; future work includes broader conditions, efficiency, and empirical validation.

Abstract: In this paper, we study regret minimization in repeated games with \emph{adaptive} opponents who can respond based on histories of play. The standard metric of \emph{external regret} in online learning is known to fail to capture such adaptivity. To account for players' counterfactual reasoning, we introduce {\tt Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret)}, a game-theoretic metric that measures the difference between the \emph{realized} and the \emph{best-in-hindsight} accumulated utility when all players can \emph{respond} to the history of play. Compared to existing regret notions in this setting, ours is native to repeated game playing, enabling stronger comparators and opponents with fewer constraints, while maintaining the possibility of finding better equilibria when all players minimize it. We first identify necessary conditions for obtaining {\tt RP-Regret} sublinear in time, on the variation of the player's comparator strategies in the regret definition and on the memories of both the comparator and opponents' strategies. We then study additional conditions and provable algorithms to minimize {\tt RP-Regret}, which is by definition \emph{non-convex} in the strategy space. To address this challenge, we propose three algorithms: (i) one based on an optimization oracle, as assumed in some prior work in online non-convex learning; (ii) one that minimizes a convex and \emph{linearized} surrogate of {\tt RP-Regret} at each iteration; (iii) one that directly minimizes {\tt RP-Regret} when opponents change strategies slowly. Furthermore, when all players can run algorithms to minimize the {\tt RP-Regret} (or its linearized variant), certain subgame perfect equilibria of the repeated game can be learned. We also provide experiments showing that minimizing our regret notions can lead to more cooperative solutions with higher utility in games such as Stag-Hunt.

</details>


### [334] [TailLoR: Protecting Principal Components in Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06494)
*Marius Dragoi,Ioana Pintilie,Alexandra Dragomir,Antonio Barbalau,Florin Brad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TailLoR uses fixed SVD bases U and V from pretrained weights to apply a low-rank update to the singular values, with a soft spectral penalty that shifts updates away from dominant directions, enabling efficient continual learning by routing adaptation into long-tail spectral coordinates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In continual learning, parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods must minimize interference between tasks when updating shared weights. Spectral decomposition offers a fixed reference frame to separate stable, high-sensitivity directions from flexible, low-sensitivity directions. TailLoR aims to reduce forgetting while enabling fine-grained adaptation.

Method: Compute the SVD of the pretrained weight matrix W = USV^T. Treat U and V as fixed reference bases and learn a low-rank update to the singular values (i.e., adjust S via a low-rank perturbation). Introduce a soft spectral penalty that discourages updates aligned with the dominant singular directions (large singular values), thereby routing most adaptation into the long-tail (smaller singular values) coordinates.

Result: The method reduces interference from updates in dominant directions and enables fine-grained adaptation by moving the update into long-tail spectral coordinates, achieving parameter-efficient finetuning suited for continual learning.
