Conclusion: TailLoR provides a spectral-domain parameter-efficient finetuning strategy for continual learning by leveraging fixed pretrained singular bases and a soft penalty to suppress dominance directions, guiding updates to the flexible tail and reducing interference.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient finetuning methods based on spectral decomposition have enabled progress in Continual Learning. In this paper we introduce TailLoR, which utilizes the singular bases U and V of the pre-trained weights as a fixed reference frame to learn a low-rank update applied to the singular value matrix. A soft spectral penalty discourages updates aligned with dominant singular directions, reducing interference while routing fine-grained adaptation into the highly flexible, long-tail spectral coordinates.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [335] [VASO: Formally Verifiable Self-Evolving Skills for Physical AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05395)
*Yunhao Yang,Neel P. Bhatt,Kevin Wang,Samuel Tetteh,Zhangyang Wang,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VASO introduces a formal-verification-guided loop for self-evolving LLM-generated robot skill contracts, achieving high formal-spec compliance with minimal optimization samples by translating verification counterexamples into gradient updates to the skill contract, keeping model weights frozen.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the trust gap in foundation-model-based skills by moving beyond trace-level execution feedback to ensure plans satisfy temporal safety contracts under untested conditions.

Method: Represent each skill with a dual-interface contract: a formal interface mapping robot states/observations/commands to logical propositions for model checking, and a planner-facing interface guiding executable behavior. A model checker filters inconsistent contracts and verifies plans against global/local temporal specifications. When verification fails, counterexamples are converted into textual gradients that update the reusable skill contract while freezing base-model weights.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter tasks shows 97.2% formal-specification compliance with fewer than 100 optimization samples, outperforming execution-feedback, prompt-optimization, and fine-tuning baselines.

Conclusion: VASO is the first framework to couple formal verification with self-evolving LLM-generated robot skills, using formal counterexamples as optimization feedback to update skill contracts rather than tuning model weights or refining one-off plans.

Abstract: Reusable robot skills are becoming the basic units through which embodied agents turn open-ended instructions into long-horizon physical behavior. We argue that, while foundation models have collapsed the cost of creating these skills, the cost of trusting them has not. Existing skill-evolution loops refine skills through execution feedback, unit tests, environment reward, or LLM self-critique, but these signals provide only trace-level evidence: they show that a skill worked on sampled executions, not that skill-induced plans satisfy temporal safety contracts under untested conditions. We introduce VASO, a framework for verification-guided self-evolution of LLM-generated robot skill contracts. In VASO, each skill is represented as a semantic contract with two coupled interfaces: a formal interface that aligns robot states, observations, and control commands with logical propositions for model checking, and a planner-facing interface that guides executable behavior generation. A model checker first filters logically inconsistent skill contracts, then verifies plans induced by the skill against global and local temporal specifications. When verification fails, VASO translates the counterexample trace into a textual gradient that updates the reusable skill contract while keeping foundation-model weights frozen. On Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter tasks, VASO reaches 97.2% formal-specification compliance using fewer than 100 optimization samples, outperforming execution-feedback, prompt-optimization, and fine-tuning baselines. To our knowledge, VASO is the first framework that closes the loop between formal verification and self-evolving LLM-generated skills for physical AI agents: formal counterexamples become optimization feedback for reusable robot skill contracts, rather than merely verifying one-off plans, tuning planner prompts, or fine-tuning model weights.

</details>


### [336] [Efficient Computation of Distance Functions for Navigation Vector Fields in Lie Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05372)
*Vinicius M. Gonçalves,João Baião,Felipe Bartelt,Douglas G. Macharet,Gustavo M. Freitas,Héctor Azpúrua,Luciano C. A. Pimenta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Efficient distance-from-point-to-curve on matrix Lie groups via G-polynomial curves; reduces to a few polynomial root-finding steps, with SE(3) formulas; validated on a manipulator and released as open-source package.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable real-time vector-field-based control on Lie groups, the costly distance and closest-point computations to curves hinder high-frequency control on embedded hardware; a faster method is needed for practical deployments.

Method: Introduce G-polynomial curve representations on matrix Lie groups. Exploit the curve structure to transform the point-to-curve distance problem into a small set of polynomial root-finding computations. Derive practical, closed-form formulas for SE(3). Validate via simulations and experimental robotic-manipulator tests. Provide an open-source computational package.

Result: The approach achieves significant reductions in computation time while maintaining accuracy compared with existing optimization-based distance/closest-point methods; supported by simulations and real-world experiments on a manipulator.

Conclusion: The proposed G-polynomial curve distance computation enables real-time, efficient distance evaluation for vector-field-based control on Lie groups, especially SE(3); the method is practical, accurate, and accompanied by implementable formulas and open-source tooling.

Abstract: Vector-field-based methods are widely used for robot control and are often applied to the path-tracking problem. Some vector field approaches require repeatedly computing the distance between the robot configuration and the curve, as well as the corresponding closest point. Recently, vector fields have been extended to Lie Groups. In this case, this computation can be expensive, especially when performed at high control frequencies on embedded platforms. This paper proposes a method for efficiently computing the distance between a point and a curve represented as what is called a G-polynomial curve, which is a curve representation that generalizes polynomial curves to matrix Lie groups. The proposed approach exploits the structure of these curves to reduce the problem to a small number of polynomial root-finding computations. Simulation results show that the method significantly reduces computation time while maintaining accuracy compared to existing optimization-based approaches. Practical formulas are also provided for the case of the group SE(3), and the method is validated experimentally on a robotic manipulator. The methodology is implemented in a computational package, available online.

</details>


### [337] [MoDex: A Diffusion Policy for Sequential Multi-Object Dexterous Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05407)
*Haofei Lu,Hongjia Liu,Yifei Dong,Florian T. Pokorny,Jens Lundell,Danica Kragic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MoDex is a diffusion-based policy for sequential multi-object grasping with a single dexterous hand, reserving redundant DOFs via an opposition-space conditioning to allow subsequent grasps. Trained in two stages (imitation learning then RL fine-tuning) to aid sim-to-real transfer, it outperforms baselines in both simulation and real hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional dexterous grasping often commits all available DOFs to a single object, leaving no redundancy for future grasps and underutilizing dexterity. The work aims to enable multiple-object grasping without releasing held objects by preserving dexterous redundancy.

Method: A diffusion policy predicts the next gripper pose from observations, conditioned on an opposition-space mask and a point cloud. The opposition space selects which fingers participate in the current grasp, freeing others for future grasps. Training proceeds in two stages: imitation learning on expert demonstrations, followed by reinforcement learning fine-tuning for sim-to-real transfer. Evaluation on a MuJoCo-based Franka Panda with Allegro Hand (sim) and the real robot (real).

Result: MoDex achieves higher success rates than evaluated learning baselines in both simulation and real hardware. Reported improvements range from 2.92% to 17.92% in simulation and 6.67% to 17.78% in the real world.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates effective exploitation of dexterous hand redundancy for sequential grasping and shows promising sim-to-real transfer via staged training. Further work could explore scalability to more objects, broader object distributions, and computational efficiency.

Abstract: This work addresses sequentially grasping multiple objects with a single dexterous hand without releasing those already held. Most dexterous grasping methods commit all of the hand's degrees of freedom to a single object, underutilizing its dexterity and leaving no redundancy for subsequent grasps. The proposed solution, MoDex, is a diffusion policy that predicts the next gripper pose directly from observations, conditioned on an opposition space and point cloud. The opposition space condition specifies which fingers participate in the current grasp, enabling the gripper to use only a subset of its available degrees of freedom while reserving the remaining degrees of freedom for subsequent grasps. To facilitate sim-to-real transfer, MoDex is trained in two stages: first through imitation learning on expert demonstrations, and subsequently through reinforcement learning fine-tuning, which consistently improves success rates over the pre-trained policy. We evaluate MoDex in simulation on a MuJoCo-based Franka Emika Panda robot equipped with an Allegro Hand and on the corresponding real-world hardware platform. Across both simulation and real-world experiments, MoDex achieves higher success rates than the evaluated learning-based baselines, improving performance by 2.92-17.92% and 6.67-17.78%, respectively. Project page: https://modex2026.github.io/.

</details>


### [338] [Learning from Demonstrations over Riemannian Manifolds using Neural ODEs: An Extended Abstract](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05422)
*Diana Cuervo Espinosa,Mahathi Anand,Angela P. Schoellig*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes learning from demonstrations on Riemannian manifolds using neural ODEs to estimate geodesics for natural motion, with decoding to task space for robot deployment; preliminary simulation results and comparisons discussed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between Euclidean LfD assumptions and the curved nature of robot states (positions and orientations) by operating on Riemannian manifolds where geodesics provide natural motion between states.

Method: Represent trajectories on manifolds; numerically estimate geodesics using neural ordinary differential equations; decode geodesics back to the original task space; architecture discussion; simulations; compare with existing geodesic computation methods.

Result: Extended abstract reports initial simulation insights and comparisons to other geodesic computation mechanisms; no detailed quantitative results yet; demonstrates feasibility and potential computational benefits.

Conclusion: Framework is outlined with architecture, initial findings, and identified challenges; future work includes improving geodesic estimation, manifold choices, and robust decoding for practical robot deployment.

Abstract: Learning from demonstratins (LfD) is usually performed over Euclidean spaces, while the robot state, e.g. orientation, naturally evolves over curved spaces. Therefore, to ensure natural, complex motion generation, we investigate learning from demonstrations over Riemannian manifolds that are capable of encoding both position and orientation data. Here, geodesic paths provide for natural motion between two arbitrary points within the manifold. We propose to numerically estimate geodesics via neural ordinary differential equations, mitigating large computational overhead of existing approaches. Finally, these geodesics can be decoded back into the original task space before deploying on the robot. In this extended abstract, we discuss the architecture of our framework, provide some initial insights from our simulation experiments, including comparison to other geodesic computation mechanisms, and discuss the challenges and prospects for future work.

</details>


### [339] [Uncertainty-Aware Adaptive Sensor Fusion for Autonomous Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05437)
*Simegnew Yihunie Alaba,Yuichi Motai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a hybrid ViT+MCNN pipeline with UKF-based VIO, adaptive fusion via learned uncertainty, and an uncertainty-aware loss, achieving state-of-the-art ATE/RPE on KITTI with 155 FPS on A100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve pose estimation accuracy and robustness of VIO under noisy or unreliable sensors by integrating deep learning with probabilistic filtering and uncertainty quantification.

Method: 1) ViT captures temporal IMU dependencies; 2) MCNN learns optical flow cues from visual data; 3) adaptive fusion module weights IMU/visual features using estimated uncertainty; 4) uncertainty-aware loss guides training to account for prediction uncertainty; 5) Unscented Kalman Filter integrates ML outputs for pose estimation.

Result: On KITTI, the approach outperforms baseline VIO methods in Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE). It runs at ~155 FPS on an NVIDIA A100, indicating strong real-time capability and suitability for resource-constrained platforms.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid architecture with uncertainty-aware training shows improved accuracy and robustness for VIO, with efficient inference suitable for autonomous systems operating in challenging environments.

Abstract: This work introduces a hybrid deep learning approach integrated with an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) to enhance pose estimation accuracy in Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) for autonomous navigation. The proposed model employs a Vision Transformer (ViT) network to effectively capture temporal dependencies from inertial measurement unit (IMU) data and utilizes a Multiscale Convolutional Neural Network (MCNN) to learn optical flow-based motion cues from visual data. An adaptive sensor fusion module dynamically weights IMU and visual features by leveraging estimated uncertainty, thus improving robustness in diverse and challenging environmental conditions. Additionally, a novel uncertainty-aware loss function is proposed to explicitly incorporate prediction uncertainty into the learning process, enabling robust and accurate navigation under noisy, incomplete, or unreliable sensor inputs. Comprehensive evaluations of the KITTI dataset demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline approaches, achieving superior performance in terms of Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE). The lightweight and computationally efficient model processes data at 155 FPS on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, making it highly suitable for deployment in resource-constrained autonomous systems.

</details>


### [340] [FlowPRO: Reward-Free Reinforced Fine-Tuning of Flow-Matching VLAs via Proximalized Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05468)
*Yihao Wu,He Zhang,Junbo Tan,Xueqian Wang,Zhengyou Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlowPRO is a reward-free offline fine-tuning framework for flow-matching Vision-Language-Action models, introducing RPRO, a preference-optimization objective with a proximal regularizer to stabilize learning and avoid reward hacking. Data is collected via teleoperation with intervention-and-rollback, then densified via Smooth Interpolation and batch mixing. Empirically, FlowPRO achieves state-leading success on four long-horizon bimanual tasks and ablations validate loss components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the bottleneck of deploying post-training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models on real robots, where reward design and reliable critics are difficult; existing SFT, DAgger rely on failure signals indirectly, while reward-based RL struggles with real-world rewards and critic training.

Method: Propose RPRO (Robotic Flow-matching Proximalized Preference Optimization) as a contrastive optimizer with a proximal regularizer that caps the implicit reward magnitude in a flow-matching action head. Use a teleoperated intervention-and-rollback paradigm to generate paired positive/negative trajectories (τ^w, τ^l) from a single operator action; apply Smooth Interpolation and batch mixing to turn sparse corrections into dense per-state supervision while preserving base policy capabilities.

Result: FlowPRO achieves the highest success rate on four long-horizon bimanual tasks, outperforming four representative baselines; ablations show each loss component contributes to performance.

Conclusion: FlowPRO provides a reward-free offline RL framework for flow-matching VLA models capable of reliable real-robot deployment, with a novel preference-optimization objective and data-augmentation strategy that stabilize learning and mitigate reward-hacking.

Abstract: Post-training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models into policies that can be reliably deployed on real robots remains a major bottleneck. SFT and DAgger exploit failure signals only indirectly, and reward-based RL is bottlenecked by the difficulty of real-world reward design and of training reliable critics. We present FlowPRO, a reward-free offline reinforced fine-tuning framework for flow-matching VLAs. Algorithmically, we propose RPRO (Robotic Flow-matching Proximalized Preference Optimization), a preference-optimization objective tailored to the flow-matching action head of VLA models. RPRO pairs a contrastive optimizer with an explicit proximal regularizer that anchors the absolute magnitude of the implicit reward, thereby eliminating the reward-hacking failure mode of plain Flow-DPO. On the data side, a teleoperated intervention-and-rollback paradigm produces naturally paired positive and negative trajectories $(τ^w, τ^l)$ on a real robot from a single operator action; a Smooth Interpolation procedure, combined with batch mixing, then converts these sparse corrections into dense per-state supervision while preserving the base policy's capabilities. On four long-horizon bimanual tasks, FlowPRO attains the highest success rate, outperforming four representative baselines, and ablations confirm the contribution of each loss component.

</details>


### [341] [Learning Contact Representation for Leg Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05501)
*Emre Girgin,Cagri Kilic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Self-supervised contact detection for legged robots using only joint encoders, removing reliance on force sensors. Probabilistic modeling of stance and swing yields robust odometry; outperforms supervised and baseline probabilistic methods; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Odometry in legged robots traditionally relies on zero foot velocity during stance and often uses ground reaction force sensors. These sensors may be unavailable or unresponsive to disturbances like slip, motivating a sensor-light, robust contact detector.

Method: A self-supervised representation learning framework using standard joint encoder data to learn contact representations and probabilistically model stance and swing phases, without force sensor augmentation.

Result: Experimental results show the proposed detector is effective and superior to supervised methods requiring additional sensors/labels and to baseline probabilistic approaches.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of self-supervised contact detection with minimal sensor assumptions, improving odometry robustness; code is publicly available.

Abstract: The estimation of odometry in legged robots depends on the assumption that the velocity of the foot with respect to the world remains zero during the stance phase. Feedback for the main body velocity is derived from the kinematic serial chain of the feet making accurate leg phase detection is a critical subproblem. A considerable number of studies employ ground reaction force sensors mounted at the tip of the foot to classify, yet these sensors may not be universally available for all legged robots. Additionally, these sensors are often unresponsive to unaccounted disturbances, such as slippage, while the foot remains in contact with the ground. In this study, we propose a self-supervised representation learning framework for contact detection that utilizes the standard sensor set of joint encoders without reliance on force sensor augmentations. We employ learned representations to model the stance and swing phases probabilistically. The experimental results obtained confirm the efficacy of the proposed self-supervised contact detector. Our framework exhibited superior performance in comparison to supervised methods which necessitate sensor set augmentation and labeling, as well as baseline probabilistic approaches. Additionally, we make our code available to the public.

</details>


### [342] [Auditing Demonstration Curation Metrics: Action-Only Scorers Fail on the Structural Defects That Degrade Imitation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05588)
*Aarav Bedi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A controlled audit of seven imitation-learning curation metrics reveals that many metrics fail to identify defects that harm policy performance, especially structural errors. Subtle perturbations are detected by multivariate outlier scores and can recover downstream performance when removed, but structural errors evade action-only metrics and even the best state-trajectory metrics recover only a fraction of the gap. The authors release the testbed and implementations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To validate whether existing demonstration-quality metrics truly identify and filter harmful demonstrations for imitation learning, given that prior validations used heterogeneous data and protocols.

Method: Construct a controlled testbed that injects known defect types into demonstrations. Audit seven curation metrics along two axes: (i) separation of defective vs clean demonstrations, and (ii) whether training a behavior-cloning policy on the curated subset improves task success. Two defect regimes are studied: (a) subtle perturbations (correlated action noise, tremor, truncation) detectable by multivariate outlier scoring, (b) structural errors (wrong action at key moment) invisible to action-only metrics. Evaluate metrics that examine only actions versus those that consider state trajectories.

Result: Subtle perturbations are detectable by multivariate outlier scoring; removing them recovers the downstream gap. Structural errors are invisible to action-only metrics; two metrics are inverted and curating on them can degrade performance. Only state-trajectory-aware metrics detect structural errors, but even the best recovers only about one-third of the downstream gap. High detection accuracy does not guarantee downstream improvement.

Conclusion: Action-only curation metrics are insufficient to reliably improve imitation-learning policies, especially in the presence of structural errors. State-trajectory-based metrics are necessary but still limited; a standardized testbed and transparent implementations are valuable for evaluating and comparing curation metrics.

Abstract: Imitation-learning policies inherit the quality of the demonstrations they are trained on, and a growing set of curation metrics promise to score and filter low-quality demonstrations automatically. These metrics are each validated on different data with different protocols, so it is unclear which of them actually identify the demonstrations that harm a policy. We build a controlled testbed in which demonstration defects are injected with known type, and audit seven curation metrics along two axes: how well each separates defective from clean demonstrations, and whether training a behavior-cloning policy on each metric's curated subset improves task success. We study two defect regimes. Subtle perturbations (correlated action noise, tremor, truncation) are detectable by multivariate outlier scoring and, once removed, recover the full downstream gap. Structural errors, where the demonstration executes a wrong action at a key moment, are invisible to every action-only metric we test, and two of them are inverted: they score defective demonstrations as higher quality and, used for curation, tend to leave the policy at or below the uncurated baseline rather than above it. Only metrics that examine the state trajectory detect structural errors, and even the best of them recovers just a third of the downstream gap. High detection accuracy does not guarantee downstream improvement. We release the testbed and all curation implementations.

</details>


### [343] [Discrete-WAM: Unified Discrete Vision-Action Token Editing for World-Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05645)
*Ziyang Yao,Haochen Liu,Yuncheng Jiang,Zeyu Zhu,Zibin Guo,Jingru Wang,Tianle Liu,Jianwei Cui,Kuiyuan Yang,Hongwei Xie,Jingwei Zhao,Guang Chen,Hangjun Ye*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Discrete-WAM introduces a discrete-aligned vision-action world policy that uses aligned discrete tokens for future states and ego actions, enabling compositional causal reasoning across counterfactual futures via a shared discrete diffusion framework; it achieves competitive autonomous-driving performance and supports controllable generation and counterfactual reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving models either rely on end-to-end state-to-action mappings that learn correlations without explicit action-conditioned dynamics or rely on continuous latent world models that lack a compositional structure for causal reasoning across counterfactual futures. A discrete-aligned, unified framework is proposed to enable causal reasoning and compositional generalization in driving scenarios.

Method: Proposes Discrete-WAM, a unified latent vision-action world policy that aligns future visual states and ego actions as discrete tokens. Builds a shared discrete diffusion framework with unified generative tasks to jointly model world dynamics, world-action policy, and hierarchical decision-enabled policy, enabling compositional reasoning across alternative futures.

Result: Empirical evaluation on large-scale autonomous-driving benchmarks shows competitive performance, with added capabilities for controllable generation and counterfactual reasoning.

Conclusion: The discrete-aligned, diffusion-based World-Action Model provides a principled path toward more reliable decision-making in autonomous driving by enabling compositional causal reasoning and counterfactual planning across diverse scenarios.

Abstract: Autonomous driving requires reasoning about how ego actions shape the evolution of the surrounding world. However, most end-to-end methods rely on direct state-to-action mappings, capturing correlations without explicitly modeling action-conditioned dynamics. Conversely, continuous-latent world models often lack compositional structure for causal reasoning across counterfactual futures. We introduce Discrete-WAM, a unified latent vision-action world policy that represents future visual states and ego actions as aligned discrete tokens, enabling compositional causal reasoning across alternative futures. Built upon this unified discrete alignment, Discrete-WAM establishes a shared discrete diffusion framework with unified generative tasks, jointly formulating world modeling, world-action policy, and hierarchical decision-enabled policy, supporting compositional generalization across diverse driving scenarios. Experiments on large-scale autonomous-driving benchmarks show that Discrete-WAM achieves competitive performance while supporting controllable generation and counterfactual reasoning, offering a principled path toward more reliable decision-making.

</details>


### [344] [Safe Embodied AI for Long-horizon Tasks: A Cross-layer Analysis of Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05660)
*Dabin Kim,Daemin Park,Sangyub Lee,Jinsik Kim,Yeongtak Oh,Jongho Shin,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Survey of safety in long-horizon robotic manipulation, organizing literature by planning-time, policy-time, and execution-time safety, evaluating evidence types, and outlining gaps and future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied AI requires safe long-horizon reasoning and action; failures in the physical world cause harm, damage, and disruption. The literature is fragmented across planning, policy design, and runtime execution, and long-horizon manipulation presents risks like misgrounding, error propagation, drift, and contact hazards. A cross-layer safety perspective is needed.

Method: Structured literature review that categorizes works by intervention locus (planning-time, policy-time, execution-time), assesses strength of evidence (formal guarantees, statistical support, empirical safety heuristics), and clarifies the roles of backbone capability papers, safety mechanisms, and evaluation studies.

Result: Clarifies how safety contributions map to planning-time, policy-time, and execution-time; provides an evidence-type strength assessment; identifies persistent gaps—limited evidence for policy-time safety, weak formal guarantees for contact-rich long-horizon manipulation, immature uncertainty-triggered intervention, and a scarcity of manipulation-specific safety benchmarks.

Conclusion: Calls for cross-layer assurance, better evaluation design, and safer deployment of long-horizon robotic agents, outlining directions for future research across cross-layer safety, evaluation methodology, and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Embodied AI systems are increasingly expected to reason and act over extended horizons in physical environments. This growing capability brings safety to the foreground, because failures in the physical world can harm people, damage objects, and disrupt workplaces. Although safe embodied AI has attracted substantial attention, the literature remains fragmented across planning, policy design, and runtime execution. Long-horizon robotic manipulation is a particularly revealing anchor domain for this problem because semantic misgrounding, subtask-level error propagation, execution drift, and contact-rich physical risk can accumulate within the same closed-loop system. This survey therefore provides a structured review of safety in long-horizon robotic manipulation from an embodied AI perspective. We organize the literature by intervention locus, covering planning-time, policy-time, and execution-time safety, and we analyze the strength of the evidence that each line of work provides, distinguishing formal guarantees, statistical support, and empirical safety heuristics. This framework clarifies the distinct roles of backbone capability papers, direct safety mechanisms, and benchmark or evaluation studies, while exposing where current safety claims are well supported and where they remain indirect. We identify persistent gaps, including limited evidence for policy-time safety, weak formal support for contact-rich long-horizon manipulation, immature uncertainty-triggered intervention, and a shortage of manipulation-specific safety benchmarks. We conclude by outlining research directions for cross-layer assurance, evaluation design, and safer deployment of long-horizon robotic agents in real-world settings.

</details>


### [345] [Preserving Full 6-DOF Actuation Under Abrupt Total Rotor Failures: Passive Fault-Tolerant Flight Control Using a Biaxial-Tilt Hexacopter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05663)
*Yipeng Yang,Yiqiao Tang,Hao Zhang,Jinqi Jiang,Jianfeng He,Rumo Chen,Xinghu Yu,Zhan Li,Huijun Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A passive fault-tolerance framework for a biaxial-tilt overactuated hexacopter that preserves 6-DOF controllability under abrupt rotor failures by extending the AWS metric and implementing two fault-free schemes, validated via simulations and experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional multirotors rapidly lose attainable wrench space under rotor failures, risking loss of 6-DOF control. A biaxial-tilt overactuated hexacopter (BTO) can retain actuation to support stable hover and trajectories when failures occur, enabling passive fault tolerance without relying on fault detection/diagnosis.

Method: 1) Extend inscribed-sphere AWS metric with transient-wrench-jump to assess feasibility under up to three simultaneous rotor failures. 2) Develop two passive schemes without fault detection: (a) controller-layer HOFA controller with a linear extended state observer (LESO) for lumped disturbances, (b) allocator-layer model-reference adaptive control (MRAC) with momentum-based wrench estimation to compensate biases. 3) Compare against uniaxial-tilt and coplanar designs; conduct simulations and flight experiments up to 6-DOF tracking under faults.

Result: Demonstrates stable hovering and 6-DOF trajectory tracking under single and multiple rotor failures. BTO provides larger recovery margins than uniaxial-tilt and coplanar designs. Onboard sensor experiments show robustness under wind, extreme conditions, narrow-frame traversal, and aerial writing.

Conclusion: The BTO architecture with passive control-allocation strategies extends fault-tolerance margins for multirotor flight, enabling reliable 6-DOF control under abrupt rotor failures without fault detection or online optimization, validated by comprehensive simulations and experiments.

Abstract: Conventional multirotors suffer from a rapid collapse of attainable wrench space (AWS) under abrupt total rotor failures, rendering full 6-DOF recovery physically impossible. This paper addresses passive fault-tolerant flight of a biaxial-tilt overactuated hexacopter (BTO) under abrupt total rotor failures that are a priori unknown to the controller. The control design and analysis focus on representative abrupt rotor-failure cases for which the post-failure system remains fully actuated, while no explicit fault detection, isolation, or fault-mode switching is assumed. First, we extend the inscribed-sphere metric of the AWS by incorporating the transient-wrench-jump term, enabling quantitative feasibility assessment under up to three simultaneous rotor failures and benchmarking against uniaxial-tilt and coplanar hexacopters. Second, we develop two computationally efficient passive schemes without relying on fault detection or online optimization. One scheme operates at the controller layer by combining a high-order fully actuated (HOFA) controller with a linear extended state observer (LESO) for lumped-disturbance rejection. The other scheme operates at the allocator layer by using model-reference adaptive control allocation with momentum-based wrench estimation to compensate for control-allocation biases. Simulations and flight experiments validate stable hovering and 6-DOF trajectory tracking under single and multiple rotor failures. Further systematic comparisons confirm that the BTO provides larger recovery margins than uniaxial-tilt and coplanar designs. Additional onboard-sensor-only experiments, including indoor tracking under wind disturbance, outdoor tracking under extreme conditions, narrow-frame traversal, and contact-based aerial writing, further validate the robustness of the proposed framework in complex operational environments.

</details>


### [346] [Dynamic Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery in Robotic Cellular Warehousing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05669)
*Cheng Ren,Ming Li,Xinping Guan,George Q. Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dynamic RXWS: A Dynamic Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery with internal order evolution; introduces two online replanning algorithms based on token passing to handle evolving orders in RCWS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real warehouse operations feature dynamic orders that can gain new SKUs during execution, which static MAPD models cannot handle efficiently or safely.

Method: Two event-triggered online replanning algorithms based on the token passing paradigm: (1) Dynamic Token Passing performs localized replanning upon order updates via add-order decomposition and priority-based token scheduling while ensuring collision-free paths; (2) Cooperative Token Passing allows idle robots to opportunistically assist newly added pickups, further improving system efficiency.

Result: Simulation in Robotic Cellular Warehousing Systems shows substantial reduction in order flowtime compared with static and non-cooperative baselines.

Conclusion: Dynamic and cooperative token-passing replanning effectively address internal order evolution in RCWS, enhancing system-wide performance while maintaining safe, collision-free operation.

Abstract: Robotic Cellular Warehousing Systems (RCWS) give rise to multi-agent pickup and delivery (MAPD) processes in which robots sequentially collect multiple stock-keeping units (SKUs) for each order. Unlike classical MAPD formulations that assume static tasks, real warehouse operations often involve dynamic order evolution, where new SKUs may be appended to an order while it is being executed. Motivated by this practical requirement, this letter formulates the Dynamic Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery problem considering internal order evolution for the first time. Building on the token passing paradigm, we propose two event-triggered online replanning algorithms. The first, Dynamic Token Passing, performs localized replanning upon order updates through add-order decomposition and priority-based token scheduling while preserving collision-free execution. The second, Cooperative Token Passing, further enables idle robots to opportunistically assist newly added pickups, improving system-level efficiency. Simulation results in RCWS environments demonstrate that the proposed methods significantly reduce order flowtime compared with static and non-cooperative baselines.

</details>


### [347] [Accelerating and Scaling MPC-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Humanoid Locomotion and Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05687)
*Junheng Li,Liang Wu,Sergio A. Esteban,Lizhi Yang,Ján Drgoňa,Aaron D. Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Efficient training-time MPC guidance for humanoid robotics via new MPC formulations and a parallel-in-horizon GPU solver (π^nMPC).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrate model predictive control with reinforcement learning to leverage MPC's prediction/constraint handling while enabling robust, scalable learning for humanoid locomotion and manipulation; existing MPC-in-RL approaches incur heavy problem construction and training overhead.

Method: Introduce a centroidal-dynamics MPC reward for ML-guidance during RL training; develop π^nMPC, a parallel-in-horizon, construction-free batched GPU MPC solver operating on time-varying dynamics to reduce memory usage and avoid pre-compilation.

Result: MPC-RL demonstrates superior performance in locomotion and manipulation across various comparative studies and hardware validations; code available at the authors' repository.

Conclusion: Efficient, practical MPC-guided RL for humanoids is achievable with new MPC formulations and a scalable GPU-based solver, enabling improved skills without prohibitive training/setup overhead.

Abstract: In humanoid motion control, model predictive control (MPC) offers physically grounded prediction and constraint handling, while reinforcement learning (RL) enables robust whole-body skills through large-scale simulation. However, using MPC inside RL often requires time-consuming problem construction or excessive training overhead, making such frameworks difficult to justify in practice. This work studies efficient training-time MPC guidance for humanoid locomotion and manipulation, termed MPC-RL. We introduce a centroidal-dynamics MPC reward formulation that leverages guidance from MPC trajectories in training time. To make this practical in massively parallel RL, we develop $π^n$MPC, a parallel-in-horizon and construction-free batched GPU MPC solver that operates directly on time-varying dynamics to avoid high memory usage and pre-compilation. Through a variety of comparative studies and hardware validations, we have found that MPC-RL achieves superior performance in locomotion and manipulation skills. The code base is available at https://github.com/junhengl/mpc-rl.

</details>


### [348] [DexFuture: Hierarchical Future-State Visuomotor Targeting for Bimanual Dexterous Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05699)
*Runfa Blark Li,Kuang-Ting Tu,Nikola Raicevic,Dwait Bhatt,Xinshuang Liu,Keito Suzuki,Ki Myung Brian Lee,Nikolay Atanasov,Truong Nguyen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical DexFuture framework combines a high-level future-state predictor with a low-level policy to enable fast, dynamic, near-oracle bimanual tool use without privileged demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of high-dimensional hand-tool-object dynamics, reliance on demonstrations for future references, and slow planning in action-conditioned world models, by generating dynamically consistent future targets without privileged states.

Method: A two-level architecture: (1) a horizon-conditioned transformer-based Future-State Visuomotor Target Predictor that outputs structured visuomotor embeddings and multi-step target trajectories from egocentric RGB, proprioception, and history; (2) a Target-Conditioned per-link transformer-based policy that tracks these targets. The system decouples coarse future reference generation from fine-grained control and semantic prediction from high-frequency execution.

Result: On OakInk2 bimanual tool-use tasks, DexFuture achieves ~90% of the privileged-oracle performance, vs ~7% for a no-reference policy; runs at 60 Hz, about 250× faster than DexWM-style CEM planning with a future action-conditioned world model.

Conclusion: A scalable, fast, hierarchical framework that decouples planning and control, delivering strong performance close to oracle supervision and enabling high-frequency execution for complex bimanual manipulation; future work may explore generalization to more tasks and robustness to visual/history noise.

Abstract: Bimanual dexterous tool use remains challenging for robots due to high-dimensional hand configurations and complex hand-tool-object dynamics and contact. Most existing control policies depend on future configuration references provided from demonstrations, while future action-conditioned world models require slow online planning over high-dimensional action sequences. A significant challenge is generating a dynamically consistent future reference trajectory without relying on privileged states from demonstrations or slow counterfactual planning. We propose DexFuture, a hierarchical system that couples a high-level Future-State Visuomotor Target Predictor with a low-level Target-Conditioned Structured Dexterous Policy. Conditioned on egocentric RGB, proprioceptive and geometric history, the high-level predictor constructs structured hand-tool-object visuomotor embeddings and uses a horizon-conditioned transformer to generate a multi-step future target trajectory. Then, the low-level policy tracks them with a target-conditioned per-link transformer. This hierarchy decouples coarse future reference generation from fine-grained action control, and slow long-horizon semantic prediction from high-frequency execution. On OakInk2 bimanual tool-use tasks, DexFuture achieves 90% of the privileged-oracle performance, compared to 7% for a no-reference policy. DexFuture operates at 60 Hz, approximately 250 times faster than DexWM-style Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) planning with a future action-conditioned world model.

</details>


### [349] [PiL-World: A Chunk-Wise World Model for VLA Policy-in-the-Loop Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05773)
*Chong Ma,Taiyi Su,Jian Zhu,Jianjun Zhang,Zitai Huang,Yi Xu,Hanli Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PiL-World enables closed-loop policy-in-the-loop VLA evaluation by using a chunk-wise world model that predicts multi-view future observations conditioned on the VLA rollout. It integrates action-derived visual cues and latent task histories, learns from both successes and failures, and avoids real robot execution during evaluation. It markedly improves fidelity of offline VLA evaluation in real dual-arm tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing world models for robot action evaluation are mostly open-loop, using pre-collected action trajectories, which cannot faithfully support closed-loop VLA evaluation where each action chunk depends on the observed outcome of the previous step. This creates a large gap between predicted and real performance.

Method: Propose PiL-World, a chunk-wise world model that, given the current observation and the action trajectory rolled out by a VLA policy, generates multi-view future observations consistent with the rollout and compatible with policy inputs. It alternates between VLA inference and world-model prediction, uses head-view motion-derived visual control, and latent histories encoding task context to condition video generation and jointly predict complementary views. It also learns from failed trajectories to better cover the distribution of real executions.

Result: PiL-World yields high fidelity rollouts that align with real robot executions across three real dual-arm manipulation tasks. It reduces the gap between real-world VLA success rates and closed-loop evaluation estimates from 63.2% to 12.0%.

Conclusion: PiL-World enables accurate, closed-loop evaluation of VLA policies without real-robot execution at every step, enhancing offline policy evaluation by improving rollout fidelity through action-conditioned video generation and context-aware latent histories, and benefiting from learning on failed trajectories.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies operate in a closed loop in real-world robot tasks: a robot observes the scene, executes an action chunk, and conditions its next decision on the resulting observation. However, most existing world models for robot action evaluation are limited to open-loop prediction along pre-collected action trajectories. This prevents them from supporting closed-loop VLA evaluation, where each action chunk must be conditioned on the observation generated by the previous execution. To address this gap, we propose PiL-World, a chunk-wise world model designed for policy-in-the-loop VLA evaluation. Given the current observation and the action trajectory rolled out by a VLA policy, PiL-World generates multi-view future observations that are consistent with the VLA rollout and match the image inputs required by the policy. By alternating between VLA inference and world-model prediction, PiL-World enables closed-loop evaluation without real robot execution at every step. To improve rollout fidelity, PiL-World conditions video generation on action-derived visual control from head-view robot motion and latent histories that encode task execution context, while jointly predicting complementary multi-view observations. Beyond successful teleoperated demonstrations, it also learns from failed execution trajectories, helping the imagined rollouts better match the distribution of real policy executions. We evaluate PiL-World on three real dual-arm manipulation tasks. PiL-World generates imagined rollouts that are highly consistent with real robot executions. More importantly, compared with the baseline, it reduces the error between VLA success rates measured in real-world rollouts and those estimated through closed-loop world-model evaluation from 63.2% to 12.0%.

</details>


### [350] [Visuotactile and Explicitly Force-Controlled Robotic Ultrasound for Abdominal Volumetric Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05848)
*Adrian Piedra,R Brooke Jeffrey,Oussama Khatib*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A robotic ultrasound system autonomously acquires 3D abdominal scans by learning from expert data, fusing stereo vision, touch feedback, and force control; it performs two scan paths and achieves imaging quality comparable to experts while enabling 3D volume acquisition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce operator dependency and enhance diagnostic potential by developing an autonomous, adaptable ultrasound system that combines perception, force control, and expert-established scanning strategies.

Method: Record expert radiologists’ freehand transducer motion, applied forces, and strategies; replay expert data to reproduce scans; build 3D abdomen topography using stereo vision; refine rib cage boundary with stiffness measurements; plan two scan trajectories (beneath the rib cage, and across soft tissue); control a 7-DOF torque-controlled manipulator in closed-loop force control to maintain probe contact.

Result: The system achieves high-quality ultrasound imaging comparable to expert scans, dynamically adapts to patient-specific topographies, and enables 3D volume acquisition surpassing expert capabilities.

Conclusion: Integrating expert knowledge with perception-based autonomy and physical reasoning can improve diagnostic performance in robotic ultrasound and enable richer volumetric data for analysis.

Abstract: In this paper, we present a robotic ultrasound acquisition system that integrates stereo vision, touch-based feedback, and expert-informed strategies to perform autonomous and adaptive abdominal scans. The system records freehand motion and force data from expert radiologists, creating a framework to capture transducer motion, applied forces, and anatomical scanning strategies. This expert data is replayed to replicate characteristic scans with the robot, forming a foundation for further autonomous capabilities. Using stereo vision, the system generates three-dimensional topography maps of the patient's abdomen, which are refined through stiffness measurements at key points to delineate the rib cage boundary. These combined techniques enable the robot to execute two distinct scanning paths: an upward-angled sweep beneath the rib cage to visualize structures near the upper abdomen and a perpendicular sweep across soft tissue regions. A compliant, torque-controlled seven degree-of-freedom robotic manipulator is controlled to maintain consistent probe contact through closed-loop force control over the varied anatomical surfaces. Physical experiments demonstrate that the system achieves high-quality imaging comparable to expert scans while dynamically adapting to patient-specific topographies. Furthermore, the robotic system surpasses expert capabilities by enabling three-dimensional volume acquisition, which enhances diagnostic potential and provides volumetric data for advanced analyses. This work highlights the integration of expert knowledge into autonomous robotic systems and underscores the potential of combining perception-based autonomy with physical reasoning for enhanced diagnostic performance.

</details>


### [351] [LadderMan: Learning Humanoid Perceptive Ladder Climbing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05873)
*Siheng Zhao,Yuanhang Zhang,Ziqi Lu,Pieter Abbeel,Rocky Duan,Koushil Sreenath,Yue Wang,C. Karen Liu,Guanya Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LadderMan presents a two-stage learning pipeline for humanoid ladder climbing: learn multiple climbing experts from a single reference motion via hybrid motion tracking, then distill them into a unified depth-based visuomotor policy using imitation and reinforcement learning; bridges sim-to-real with vision foundation models and adds a dual-agent manipulation policy for on-ladder tasks, achieving zero-shot transfer to real hardware across diverse ladders and manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Climbing ladders is highly challenging for humanoid robots due to sparse footholds/handholds, complex whole-body coordination, and perception/control sensitivity; a robust, generalizable approach is needed for real-world deployment.

Method: Stage 1: hybrid motion tracking to learn multiple climbing experts from one reference ladder motion. Stage 2: distill experts into a unified depth-based visuomotor climbing policy using hybrid imitation and reinforcement learning. Real-world deployment aided by vision foundation models to reduce sim-to-real gap in depth perception. A separate dual-agent manipulation policy enables stable on-ladder manipulation via teleoperation.

Result: Demonstrates robust ladder climbing across diverse ladder geometries, zero-shot transfer to real hardware, and support for various manipulation tasks under ladder constraints; reports successful real-world deployment and provides video demonstrations.

Conclusion: LadderMan offers a unified framework that combines imitation and reinforcement learning with depth-based sensing and teleoperation-enabled manipulation to robustly handle constrained ladder scenarios, enabling generalization across ladder geometries and successful sim-to-real transfer; future work may address data efficiency, long-term durability, and more complex manipulation on ladders.

Abstract: Humanoid robots hold great promise for operating in human-centered environments, yet ladder climbing remains one of the most challenging tasks due to sparse footholds and handholds, complex whole-body coordination, and sensitivity to perception and control errors. We present \textbf{LadderMan}, a unified system that enables humanoid robots to robustly climb diverse ladders and perform manipulation under such constrained conditions. Our climbing policy is built on a scalable two-stage learning pipeline, where we use hybrid motion tracking to learn multiple climbing experts from a single reference motion, and distill these experts into a unified depth-based visuomotor climbing policy via hybrid imitation and reinforcement learning. To enable real-world deployment, we leverage vision foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap in depth perception. Building on the learned climbing policy, we further train a separate manipulation policy using a dual-agent formulation, allowing stable on-ladder manipulation via teleoperation. Experiments demonstrate that LadderMan achieves robust ladder climbing across a wide range of geometries, successfully transfers to real-world hardware in a zero-shot manner, and supports various manipulation tasks under challenging ladder constraints. Video results are available at https://ladderman-robot.github.io .

</details>


### [352] [TAGA: Terrain-aware Active Gaze Learning for Generalizable Agile Humanoid Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05880)
*Peizhuo Li,Hongyi Li,Mingfeng Fan,Fangzhou Xu,Shuhao Liao,Yuxuan Ma,Zicheng Zeng,Ze Wang,Yongbin Jin,Yuhong Cao,Hongtao Wang,Guillaume Sartoretti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Terrain-aware Active Gaze (TAGA) uses reinforcement learning to learn where to look on height scans to guide humanoid locomotion, improving data efficiency and real-world robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve wide perceptual coverage and precise local geometry understanding under tight onboard compute, inspired by how humans selectively gaze at relevant terrain during locomotion.

Method: Fuse visual input, proprioception, and motion commands. Train an attention-based policy that selectively attends to informative regions of height scans for the downstream network. Gaze behavior emerges via reinforcement learning without extra supervision. Reduces observation density while preserving task-relevant information.

Result: Policy demonstrates robust simulation-to-hardware transfer, including terrain-aware foothold selection, elevated-platform traversal, competitive sparse-foothold traversal, and a 1.2 m real-world gap traversal—the largest reported for perceptive humanoid locomotion. Maintains stability under perceptual disturbances and environmental interference.

Conclusion: Active gaze learning via RL yields data-efficient, generalizable perceptive locomotion on diverse terrains; TAGA enables fine-grained perception under onboard constraints and scales to larger terrains and challenging scenarios.

Abstract: Agile humanoid locomotion across diverse challenging terrain demands both wide perceptual coverage and precise local geometry understanding. Motivated by the way humans selectively look at relevant terrain during locomotion, we introduce TAGA, a Terrain-aware Active Gaze learning framework for Attention-based humanoid control. By fusing vision, proprioception, and motion commands, our framework guides the model to learn anticipatory cues and actively attend to specific areas of the height scan, selectively using these informative regions for the downstream network. This adaptively increases the information density of observations under tight onboard computational constraints, thus enabling fine-grained perceptive locomotion over larger-scale terrains. We find that such gaze behaviors can naturally emerge through reinforcement learning alone, without requiring additional supervision or explicit guidance, significantly improve training efficiency. As a result, the trained policy demonstrates robust and generalizable locomotion in simulation and on hardware, including reliable terrain-aware foothold selection, elevated-platform traversal, competitive sparse-foothold traversal, and the largest reported real-world gap traversal distance of 1.2m among perceptive humanoid locomotion systems, while maintaining stability under severe perceptual disturbances and environmental interference.

</details>


### [353] [A Novel Method with Encoder-Decoder for Cross-Sensor Adaptation in Surface Shape Sensing with Sparse Strain Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05903)
*Shuo Wang,Heng Luo,Dian Jin,Xiaoming Tao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Encoder-decoder surface shape sensing with sparse strain sensors uses meta-learning and few-shot adaptation to enable cross-sensor transfer; achieves ~4.0 mm error with <5% labeled data and adaptation <1s; improves over 23 mm error without adaptation and 20 min training; >65% more points within 5 mm error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensor-array performance varies due to intrinsic differences or installation, causing inconsistent shape sensing. Large data requirements and per-array retraining increase cost and delay; cross-sensor transfer desired to reduce labeling, data collection, and computation.

Method: Encoder–decoder architecture for surface shape sensing with sparse strain sensors, augmented with meta-learning and few-shot adaptation to enable adaptation across different sensor array groups (cross-sensor adaptation).

Result: After cross-sensor adaptation, a new sensor array achieves ~4.0 mm sensing error using <5% newly labeled data with adaptation time <1 s; significant improvements over baseline (23.0 mm without adaptation, 20 min data collection to train a new model). The proportion of points with error <5 mm increases by >65%.

Conclusion: The approach substantially reduces data labeling, training time, and computational cost for surface shape sensing and shows broad potential for soft robotics and wearables.

Abstract: Performance variations in sensor arrays, caused by intrinsic differences or installation conditions, can lead to inconsistent results during shape sensing. To obtain accurate results, a large amount of data is usually required, and a separate model must be retrained for each sensor array, thereby increasing the cost and time of data acquisition, transmission, and computation. To address this issue, this work proposes an encoder-decoder architecture for surface shape sensing based on sparse strain sensors and further incorporates meta-learning and few-shot adaptation strategies to enable adaptation across different groups of sensor arrays. Experimental results demonstrate that, after the cross-sensor adaptation, a newly deployed sensor array achieves a sensing error of approximately 4.0 mm relying on less than 5.0% newly labeled data and requiring an adaptation time of under 1 second, which represents a substantial improvement from 23.0 mm error without adaptation and 20-minute data collection time required to train a new model. Moreover, the number of points with errors below 5.0 mm increased by more than 65.0%. These results indicate that the proposed method can substantially reduce the cost and training burden of surface shape sensing, and it has broad potential applications in soft robotics and wearable devices.

</details>


### [354] [Learning of Robot Safety Policies via Adversarial Synthetic Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05952)
*Nikolai Dorofeev,Alexey Odinokov,Rostislav Yavorskiy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adversarial, agentic gamification for hazard-informed safety learning in robots via Red Team–Blue Team scenario generation to reveal edge cases and embed safety into Physical AI; currently outlining problem formulation and architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust robot safety in complex real-world environments is hard; random sims or manual enumeration miss high-risk edge cases; an adversarial, learning-based approach can systematically uncover hazards and guide policy improvement.

Method: Model scenario generation as an adversarial loop between a Red Team (hazard explorations) and a Blue Team (policy refinement). Integrate classical risk modeling with modern learning to iteratively discover critical failures and adapt safety policies; described as an ongoing, scalable architecture.

Result: Framework enables efficient discovery of high-risk edge cases not easily found by random simulation; ongoing work with a focus on problem formulation and proposed architecture rather than empirical results.

Conclusion: Offers a scalable, principled direction for embedding safety into Physical AI systems in complex environments; requires empirical validation and evaluation against baselines, with future work focusing on implementation and deployment details.

Abstract: In this work, we propose an agentic gamification framework for hazard-informed learning of robot safety policies through synthetic scenarios. We model scenario generation as an adversarial game between two agents: a Red Team that explores the space of potential failures by constructing hazardous situations, and a Blue Team that incrementally refines safety policies to prevent them. This iterative process enables efficient discovery of high-risk edge cases that are unlikely to be captured through random simulation or manual enumeration. By combining classical risk modeling with adversarial scenario generation and modern learning paradigms, this work provides a scalable pathway for embedding safety into Physical AI systems operating in complex real-world environments. The paper describes ongoing work. The contribution is a problem formulation and a proposed solution architecture.

</details>


### [355] [Towards a Data Flywheel for Embodied Intelligence in Logistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05960)
*Anlan Yu,Zaishu Chen,Zhiqing Hong,Daqing Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A data-centric framework for industrial embodied intelligence in logistics using a logistics data flywheel, World Models for supervision, and WM-DAgger for robust imitation learning; extends to large-scale multimodal data alignment for continual policy improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scalability challenge of data-efficient, robust embodied policies in industry by turning daily operations into reusable data assets and enabling long-tail manipulation under real-world distribution shifts.

Method: Construct a logistics data flywheel that converts daily operations into reusable data assets; employ World Models to generate reliable supervision for long-tail parcel manipulation; introduce WM-DAgger to synthesize out-of-distribution recovery data for robust imitation learning; plan to align large-scale multimodal data (labeled demonstrations, unlabeled videos, system logs) for policy learning and continuous feedback.

Result: WM-DAgger demonstrates synthesis of out-of-distribution recovery data to improve robustness in imitation learning.

Conclusion: The framework presents a viable, scalable path for industrial embodied intelligence, with World Models and data aggregation as core components; ongoing work targets scaling with multimodal data and continual system improvement.

Abstract: Embodied intelligence is moving from laboratory demonstrations toward industrial deployment, with the logistics industry serving as a key application scenario. Learning-based policies offer a promising path beyond traditional perception-planning-control pipelines, but their scalability depends on how embodied data can be collected, organized, and reused. This research studies a data-centric framework for industrial embodied intelligence by constructing a logistics data flywheel. Our framework converts daily operations into reusable data assets, uses World Models to generate reliable supervision for long-tail parcel manipulation, and feeds deployment feedback back into policy improvement. As an initial result, \textit{WM-DAgger} introduces a World-Model-based data aggregation framework that synthesizes out-of-distribution recovery data for robust imitation learning. Building on this result, ongoing work explores how large-scale in-the-wild multimodal data, including labeled human demonstrations, unlabeled operational videos, and system-level robot logs, can be aligned for policy learning and transformed into feedback for continual system improvement.

</details>


### [356] [World-Language-Action Model for Unified World Modeling, Language Reasoning, and Action Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05979)
*Yi Yang,Zhihong Liu,Siqi Kou,Yiyang Chen,Yanzhe Hu,Jianbo Zhou,Boyuan Zhao,Zhijie Wei,Xiao Xia,Xueqi Li,Pengfei Liu,Zhijie Deng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Autoregressive embodied model unifying world modeling and language reasoning (WLA) to predict next states and actions from instructions, images, and robot states; achieves fast inference and state-of-the-art long-horizon multitask performance, with cross-embodiment learning potential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge world modeling and natural-language reasoning for embodied AI to enable robust long-horizon control and scalable learning across embodiments, including learning from unannotated cross-embodiment videos.

Method: Autoregressive Transformer backbone predicting next state that includes semantic textual intention and fine-grained dynamics. World Expert supervises physical dynamics via a world-modeling objective; Action Expert handles state-action mapping. Meta-queries allow world prediction to implicitly influence action generation; test-time scaling by toggling world prediction. WLA-0 prototype has 2B parameters and 40 ms inference on RTX 5090.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art multi-task and long-horizon learning: 92.94% success on RoboTwin2.0 Clean and 56.5% on RMBench. 40 ms per inference. Demonstrates potential to learn novel tasks from cross-embodiment robot videos without action annotations.

Conclusion: WLA offers a unified, scalable framework that fuses world modeling and language reasoning for efficient, long-horizon robot control and cross-embodiment learning; future work includes broader task coverage, ablations, and safety considerations.

Abstract: We propose world-language-action (WLA) models as a new class of embodied foundation models. WLA takes textual instructions, images, and robot states as inputs to jointly predict textual subtasks, subgoal images, and robot actions, conjoining the \emph{world modeling interface} to learn from extensive egocentric videos as in the world-action model (WAM) and the \emph{language reasoning} capacities to solve complex long-horizon tasks as in vision-language-action (VLA) models. At the core of WLA lies an \emph{autoregressive (AR)} Transformer backbone, instead of a bidirectional diffusion Transformer as in WAMs, to predict the \emph{next state}, comprising the \emph{semantic-level} textual intention and complementary \emph{fine-grained} physical dynamics. The physical dynamics are supervised by the world modeling objective based on a dedicated World Expert, and are leveraged to ease the characterization of the state-action correlation for the Action Expert. WLA leverages meta-queries to make the world prediction \emph{implicitly} impact the action generation so that the former can be disabled during inference. The world prediction can also be activated to enable test-time scaling for improved robot control. Our WLA-0 prototype, with 2B active parameters, achieves 40 ms per inference on an NVIDIA RTX 5090. Evaluations across simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that WLA-0 achieves state-of-the-art multi-task and long-horizon learning abilities, e.g., 92.94\% success rate on RoboTwin2.0 Clean and 56.5\% success rate on RMBench. WLA-0 also holds the promise to learn novel tasks directly from \emph{cross-embodiment robot videos} without action annotations.

</details>


### [357] [Merging model-based control with multi-agent reinforcement learning for multi-agent cooperative teaming strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06011)
*Christian Llanes,Spencer W. Jensen,Samuel Coogan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A multi-agent actor-critic MPC framework (MA-AC-MPC) that fuses MARL with MPC for safe, dynamically-feasible actions in cooperative tasks; hardware demonstrations show 100% landing success in a heterogeneous drone-rover setting, vs 60% with MA-AC-MLP, and robustness across environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety and real-time feasibility in MARL for multi-agent tasks; leverage MPC's robust short-horizon planning to enforce safety and dynamic feasibility while MARL handles long-horizon cooperative policy learning.

Method: Extend actor-critic MPC to multi-agent setting (MA-AC-MPC); compare to MA-AC-MLP; evaluate in multi-agent pursuit-evasion and a heterogeneous drone-rover landing task; pursuers use augmented proportional navigation as an advanced control law; hardware experiments show robustness.

Result: MA-AC-MPC demonstrates improved performance and safety; in hardware for landing task, 100% success vs 60% for MA-AC-MLP; robust across environments; evader strategy comparison indicates behavior dif- ferences favoring MA-AC-MPC.

Conclusion: MA-AC-MPC is a promising integration of MARL and MPC for safe, dynamically feasible multi-agent control; encourages further research into scalability, theoretical guarantees, and broader hardware validations.

Abstract: In this work, we propose a framework that combines multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with model-based control to achieve safe, dynamically feasible actions in cooperative multi-agent tasks. Multi-agent reinforcement learning provides the advantage of learning cooperative policies for multi-agent teams from discrete non-differentiable rewards in a long planning horizon. Model-predictive control is robust and offers safe, dynamically feasible actions in a fast replanning framework for short horizons. We propose an algorithm that extends actor-critic model predictive control for MARL which we refer to as multi-agent actor-critic model predictive control (MA-AC-MPC). We demonstrate the capabilities of this algorithm by applying it to a multi-agent pursuit-evasion scenario. Specifically, we compare the evader team's strategy using the MA-AC-MPC model and a multi-layer perceptron model (MA-AC-MLP). The pursuer team uses augmented proportional navigation as it is accepted as an advanced adversarial control law. We also provide an example with a heterogeneous environment where a drone and omni-wheeled rover cooperate to achieve repeatable and successful landing with 100% success rate in hardware for MA-AC-MPC compared to 60% for MA-AC-MLP. We demonstrate the robustness of the proposed MA-AC-MPC algorithm in hardware for both environments.

</details>


### [358] [RealDexUMI: A Wearable Universal Manipulation Interface for Dexterous Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06033)
*Chaoyi Xu,Yixuan Jiang,Jiahui Huan,Yuhui Fu,Haoyu Zhou,Weitian Yuan,Jiayi Yu,Wanpeng Zhang,Haoqi Yuan,Zongqing Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A wearable universal manipulation interface RealDexUMI enables deployable dexterous manipulation by mapping a palm-side teleoperation glove to a shared end-effector with in-hand sensing, achieving zero-gap data across collection/deployment; 88.75% average success over eight real-robot tasks with cross-embodiment transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To collect dexterous manipulation data that preserves fine hand-object interactions while remaining deployable, avoiding retargeting losses, embodiment conversions, or costly robot-specific teleoperation.

Method: Introduce RealDexUMI: a wearable system with a shared dexterous end-effector module combining a lightweight dexterous hand, in-hand vision, and fingertip tactile sensing. A palm-side isomorphic teleoperation glove maps human finger inputs to robot-hand joints, enabling real-time, retargeting-free, intuitive control. The system provides zero-gap end-effector data with matched in-hand observations, tactile signals, contacts, and hand actions between collection and deployment. Evaluated on eight real-robot tasks spanning fine-grained, contact-rich, long-horizon, and bimanual manipulation.

Result: Policies trained on RealDexUMI data achieve an average success rate of 88.75% across eight tasks, generalize to unseen initial poses, and transfer across three embodiments.

Conclusion: RealDexUMI demonstrates scalable, intuitive, and transferable dexterous manipulation data collection, yielding deployable policies with cross-embodiment transfer and robustness to pose variation.

Abstract: Learning dexterous manipulation requires demonstrations that preserve fine hand-object interactions while remaining executable at deployment. Existing pipelines either lose deployable dexterity through retargeting or embodiment conversion, or rely on robot-specific teleoperation that is costly to scale and often lacks intuitive, contact-aware control for dexterous data collection. We present RealDexUMI, a wearable universal manipulation interface built around a shared dexterous end-effector module that integrates a lightweight dexterous hand, in-hand vision, and fingertip tactile sensing. A palm-side isomorphic teleoperation glove maps human finger inputs to robot-hand joint commands, enabling real-time, retargeting-free, intuitive, and precise hand control. The shared hand and sensing modules yield zero-gap end-effector data, with matched in-hand observations, tactile signals, contacts, and hand actions between collection and deployment. Across eight real-robot tasks spanning fine-grained, contact-rich, long-horizon, and bimanual manipulation, policies trained on RealDexUMI data achieve an average success rate of 88.75%, generalize to unseen initial poses, and transfer across three embodiments. Website: https://research.beingbeyond.com/realdexumi

</details>


### [359] [Gotta Grow Fast: Design and Benchmarking of a Tip Mount for High-Speed Vine Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06040)
*Antonio Alvarez Valdivia,Robert Reeve,Ankush Dhawan,Ciera McFarland,Chad Council,Margaret McGuinness,Nathaniel Hanson*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Triangular roller tip mount enables consistent tip-eversion and sensor integration in soft growing vine robots by reducing internal resistance during growth; introduced a benchmarking testbed to measure tail tension, and demonstrated lowest tension and repeatability across variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensorized tip mounting in continuously growing soft robots is hindered by material turnover at the tip, internal layer friction, added mass, and constriction, which impede reliable eversion and sensor performance.

Method: Iterative failure analysis to refine a triangular roller tip mount; validation on TPU-coated ripstop nylon vine robots; development of a custom testbed that isolates tip mounting effects by measuring tail tension during eversion; comparative experiments against prior designs.

Result: The triangular roller mount yields the lowest tail tension and the most repeatable growth; enables consistent eversion with tip-mounted sensors; CAD for the mount and testbed provided to enable replication and benchmarking.

Conclusion: Presents a validated tip-mount design and a repeatable benchmarking framework to advance sensor/tool integration in soft growing robots.

Abstract: Soft, growing vine robots extend through tip eversion, a mechanism that enables navigation through cluttered environments. However, integrating cameras and other sensors at the tip is uniquely challenging because the material forming the tip is constantly renewed as the robot grows. This continual material turnover, combined with friction between internal layers, added tip weight, and fabric constriction, complicates sensor and tool mounting. These limitations hinder the deployment of vine robots for inspection and search tasks, where rapid growth while carrying tip-mounted sensors is essential. In this work, we present a triangular roller tip mount that reduces internal resistance during growth by rolling rather than sliding against the robot body. The design was refined through iterative failure analysis, enabling, for the first time, consistent eversion on a TPU-coated ripstop nylon vine robot. To quantitatively evaluate mount performance, we introduce a custom testbed that isolates tip mounting effects by measuring tail tension during eversion. Comparative experiments across multiple mount variants, including prior designs, show that our triangular roller mount achieves the lowest tail tension and most repeatable growth performance. These results establish both a validated tip mount design and a repeatable benchmarking framework for advancing sensor and tool integration in soft growing robots. CAD for the mount and testbed is available at: https://sprout-mitll.github.io/tip_mounts/.

</details>


### [360] [Sample-efficient Low-level Motion Planning for Robotic Manipulation Tasks via Zero-shot Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06041)
*Yuanzhi He,Victor Romero-Cano,José J. Patiño,Juan David Hernández,William Sawtell,Gualtiero Colombo*

Main category: cs.RO
