TL;DR: An iCEM-based motion planning framework (iCEM+TL) combines Transfer Learning and Reward Redesign to improve planning performance for complex tasks like stacking and shelf placement, achieving up to 23% higher success rate in simulation and validated on a real Franka Emika robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of iCEM in handling complex, real-time manipulation tasks that involve stacking, sliding, and shelf placement, where training time and model complexity hamper performance.

Method: Introduce iCEM+TL by transferring key iCEM parameters from simpler upstream tasks to guide downstream tasks, and apply Reward Redesign (RR) via task decomposition tailored to stacking and shelf placement.

Result: Simulation shows up to 23% improvement in success rate; real-world validation on a Franka Emika robot confirms practical feasibility for deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed iCEM+TL framework with TL and RR enhances performance for complex manipulation tasks and demonstrates potential for real-world robotic applications, indicating effective knowledge reuse and task-specific reward shaping.

Abstract: As robotic systems become more sophisticated, the growing complexity of their motion planning models and the longer training times pose substantial challenges. Evolutionary algorithms such as the Sample-efficient Cross-Entropy Method (iCEM) have recently demonstrated promising potential for low-level real-time planning by leveraging efficient knowledge reuse strategies to improve performance. Although effective in many control tasks, iCEM's performance can be constrained in more complex scenarios, particularly those requiring stacking, sliding, and shelf placement. In this work, we propose a novel iCEM+TL framework that explicitly leverages Transfer Learning (TL), where key iCEM parameters are transferred from simpler upstream tasks to guide more complex downstream tasks. Additionally, we applied Reward Redesign (RR) through task decomposition for stacking objects and shelf placement to optimize task-specific performance. Results from the simulation show that our framework achieves success rate improvements of up to 23%. The framework is further validated on a real Franka Emika robot in a stacking task, demonstrating its practical feasibility for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [361] [L-SDPPO: Policy Optimization of Spiking Diffusion Policy for Intra-vehicular Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06049)
*Liwen Zhang,Dong Zhou,Guanghui Sun,Yifei Zheng,Yuhui Hu,Kaihong Ouyang,Zuoquan Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces L-SDPPO, a low-energy intra-vehicular manipulation framework using a Spiking Diffusion Policy optimized via reinforcement learning, with a state-dependent latency injection mechanism to regulate input timing; achieves higher success rates and lower energy consumption than state-of-the-art methods on five intra-vehicular tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the dual challenge in spacecraft robotics of robust control for complex multimodal actions under microgravity, where objects drift unpredictably and energy budgets are tight; diffusion policies model complex actions but are energy-intensive, and perception of dynamic spatiotemporal features is limited.

Method: Propose L-SDPPO that optimizes a Spiking Diffusion Policy (SDP) with reinforcement learning; introduce SDLI (state-dependent latency injection) to mimic biological neural delays for dynamic timing of inputs; evaluate on five representative intra-vehicular tasks (e.g., hatch opening, precision container capping).

Result: Empirically, the method achieves higher success rates and lower energy consumption compared to state-of-the-art robotic manipulation methods across the tested tasks; demonstrates viability of the proposed approach in intra-vehicular manipulation.

Conclusion: L-SDPPO with SDP and SDLI provides a viable, energy-efficient solution for intra-vehicular robotic manipulation under microgravity; further work could address generalization, real-hardware deployment, and comprehensive ablations.

Abstract: Intra-vehicular robots in spacecraft help reduce astronaut workload and improve mission efficiency. Recent research focuses on using deep learning methods to achieve the acute control required for operations in these complex environments. However, objects exhibit unpredictable, unconstrained drift without gravitational damping. These factors demand robustness against complex multimodal action distributions. Diffusion policies (DP) can model these complex actions, but their iterative sampling process consumes too much energy for the limited power budgets of spacecraft. We therefore propose a low-energy intra-vehicular robotic manipulation framework, L-SDPPO, in which the Spiking Diffusion Policy (SDP) is optimized with a reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm. Furthermore, to address the insufficient perception of dynamic spatiotemporal features in microgravity, we propose the statedependent latency injection (SDLI) mechanism, which mimics biological neural delays to dynamically regulate the timing of input information. Evaluation on five representative intra-vehicular daily tasks (e.g., hatch opening and precision container capping) shows that our method consistently achieves higher success rates and lower energy consumption, compared to the state-of-the-art robotic manipulation methods. These results demonstrate our method is a viable intra-vehicular robotic manipulation method.

</details>


### [362] [A Conversational Framework for Human-Robot Collaborative Manipulation with Distributed Generative AI models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06061)
*Arash Ghasemzadeh Kakroudi,Roel Pieters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A distributed ROS 2-based framework that integrates LLMs and VLMs for end-to-end human-robot manipulation with operator-confirmed execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable flexible, robust human-robot collaboration in manipulation by unifying language understanding, visual grounding, and motion planning/execution across distributed hardware while preserving a responsive control loop and safety through operator oversight.

Method: Separate ROS 2 nodes for language understanding, visual grounding, orchestration, and motion execution. From free-form user commands, generate structured action requests for pick, place, and handover. A VLM returns image-space targets that are converted to metric robot-frame goals using depth and calibration. A web dashboard displays intermediate intent and grounding overlays (pixel, depth, robot-frame) and requires explicit operator confirmation before any motion. Experiments on a Franka FR3 platform evaluate end-to-end task reliability and latency under increasing scene ambiguity and compare alternative LLM/VLM configurations. Code and documentation are available at github.com/cogrob-tuni/franka-llm.

Result: End-to-end evaluation of task reliability and latency as scene ambiguity increases; comparative assessment of different LLM/VLM configurations within the same processing pipeline.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of a distributed, operator-verified framework for human-robot manipulation that leverages LLMs and VLMs with ROS 2, and provides actionable code and documentation for replication.

Abstract: This paper presents a distributed conversational framework for human-robot collaborative manipulation that integrates local language and vision-language models (VLMs) with a Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2)-based execution stack. Language understanding, visual grounding, orchestration, and motion execution run as separate ROS 2 nodes, enabling flexible deployment across distributed hardware while maintaining a responsive control loop. From free-form user commands, the system generates structured action requests for pick, place, and handover. It uses a VLM to return image-space targets, which are converted into metric robot-frame goals using depth and calibration. A web dashboard exposes intermediate intent and grounding overlays (pixel, depth, and robot-frame) and requires explicit operator confirmation before any motion is executed. Experiments on a Franka FR3 platform evaluate end-to-end task reliability and latency under increasing working table scene ambiguity and compare alternative LLM/VLM configurations in the same pipeline. Code and full documentation are available at [github.com/cogrob-tuni/franka-llm](https://github.com/cogrob-tuni/franka-llm).

</details>


### [363] [3D Underwater Path Planning via Generative Flow Field Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06077)
*Zachary Cooper-Baldock,Paulo E. Santos,Russell S. A. Brinkworth,Karl Sammut*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: cGAN-based surrogates (PatchGAN and 2D3DGAN-SA) predict 128^3 wake-field volumes from scalar inputs to accelerate 3D path planning for AUV launch/recovery, achieving microsecond inference and recovering 45-60% of CFD benefits, enabling edge-device deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CFD via RANS accurately characterizes complex 3D propeller-wake but is too slow for onboard planning; need fast, accurate surrogates to enable energy-efficient, safe LAR operations.

Method: Two cGAN architectures with a hierarchical pipeline synthesize full 128^3 wake-field volumes from scalar conditions; integrated into a 3D energy-weighted A* path planning framework; evaluated under four knowledge levels across ~19,800 trajectories and 550 flow conditions.

Result: Inference times ~28-146 microseconds; full CFD knowledge yields 5.7-12.5% energy savings and up to 77.8% fewer high-velocity wake-core encounters; cGAN surrogates recover ~45-60% of CFD benefits; demonstrable downstream value in 3D maritime robotics.

Conclusion: First systematic quantification of downstream planning value of cGAN-predicted hydrodynamic fields in 3D maritime robotics; viable edge-deployable surrogate for RANS in LAR planning; opens path for further accuracy and uncertainty improvements.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) launch and recovery (LAR) into the hull of an advancing host platform requires traversal of a complex, three-dimensional propeller wake whose hydrodynamic structure cannot be characterised by a uniform current model. High-fidelity Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations resolve this structure with sufficient accuracy for path planning, but their computational cost renders them impractical for onboard use. We address this gap by integrating two conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) architectures -- a regularised PatchGAN and a 2D3DGAN with self-attention -- as drop-in replacements for RANS CFD data within a three-dimensional, energy-weighted A* path planning framework. Both generators are driven by a hierarchical pipeline that synthesises full $128^3$ voxel flow field volumes from scalar operating condition inputs alone, with end-to-end inference times of approximately 28-146 $μ$s, compared to hours for a single RANS computation. We benchmark all four environmental knowledge levels: uniform current, ground-truth CFD, PatchGAN, and 2D3DGAN~SA across 19,800 independently generated trajectories spanning 550 distinct flow conditions. Full CFD wake knowledge reduces energy expenditure by 5.7-12.5% and high-velocity wake-core encounters by up to 77.8% relative to uniform-current planning, with both benefits scaling with operating severity. The cGAN surrogates recover approximately 45-60% of the CFD energy benefit and high-velocity cell avoidance benefit while operating at inference speeds compatible with edge device use. These results provide the first systematic quantification of the downstream path planning value of cGAN-predicted hydrodynamic fields in a three-dimensional maritime robotics application.

</details>


### [364] [Towards Realistic 3D Sonar Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06130)
*Youssef Attia,Davide Costa,Francesco Wanderlingh,Filippo Campagnaro,Enrico Simetti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modular, acoustically-grounded 3D sonar simulation framework integrated into NVIDIA Isaac Sim, with hardware-in-the-loop validation and a roadmap to fully acoustics-driven sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current geometry-based sonar renderings fail to capture fundamental acoustic phenomena (refraction, multipath, phase-dependent signal formation), hindering realistic perception and algorithm development in underwater robotics.

Method: Develop a volumetric 3D sonar model, GPU-accelerated rendering, physically grounded acoustic propagation, integration into Isaac Sim; model inspired by Water Linked 3D-15; build within underwater simulation; hardware-in-the-loop with FastLIO2 SLAM on Jetson Orin Nano; sensor fusion with synthetic 3D sonar, DVL, IMU, pressure.

Result: Validation via hardware-in-the-loop; qualitative comparison with harbor sheet-pile inspections; characterizes remaining sim-to-real gap; provides roadmap toward acoustics-driven volumetric sensing.

Conclusion: The work advances toward fully acoustics-driven volumetric sonar sensing in underwater robotics and identifies practical steps to close the sim-to-real gap.

Abstract: As underwater robotics research increasingly addresses complex 3D perception and autonomous navigation, the fidelity of sonar simulation has become a key factor in algorithm development. Current simulation frameworks typically rely on geometry-driven rendering, approximating 3D sonar as an underwater equivalent to LiDAR, which fails to account for fundamental acoustic phenomena such as refraction, multi-path interference, and phase-dependent signal formation. This paper proposes a modular architecture for realistic 3D sonar simulation that integrates GPU-accelerated graphics engines with physically grounded acoustic propagation principles. We implement a volumetric 3D sonar model within the NVIDIA Isaac Sim environment, modeled after the Water Linked 3D-15 sensor, and integrate it into a comprehensive underwater simulation framework. The system is validated through a hardware-in-the-loop configuration, where a modified FastLIO2 SLAM pipeline, executed on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, performs sensor fusion using synthetic 3D sonar, DVL, IMU, and pressure data. Finally, a qualitative comparison between simulated outputs and real-world data from harbor sheet-pile inspections is provided, characterizing the remaining sim-to-real gap and establishing a roadmap toward fully acoustics-driven volumetric sensing.

</details>


### [365] [MotionDisco: Motion Discovery for Extreme Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06139)
*Ilyass Taouil,Michal Ciebelski,Shafeef Omar,Haizhou Zhao,Angela Dai,Aaron M. Johnson,Majid Khadiv*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LLM-guided evolutionary search discovers long-horizon, contact-rich humanoid loco-manipulation motions from scratch, without teleoperation or motion retargeting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the combinatorial growth of contact interactions and task horizon in humanoid loco-manipulation and enable autonomous discovery of novel skills without human demonstrations.

Method: Couple a large language model-guided evolutionary search over interaction sequences with an efficient sequential kinodynamic trajectory optimizer and pruning strategy; ablations validate effectiveness; subsequently train RL trackers to deploy motions on real humanoids.

Result: Discovery of successful whole-body trajectories across challenging long-horizon tasks; extensive ablations show the benefit of LLM guidance; ability to transfer motions to a real humanoid via RL-tracked policies; supplementary videos provided.

Conclusion: First work to discover and deploy long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation skills entirely through automated evolutionary search.

Abstract: We present MotionDisco, a framework that discovers contact-rich, long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation motions from scratch, without relying on teleoperation or motion retargeting from human demonstrations. This is challenging because the space of possible contact interactions grows combinatorially with the task horizon and the number of objects in the scene. MotionDisco enables rapid discovery of novel motions by coupling a large language model (LLM) guided evolutionary search over sequences of interactions with an efficient sequential kinodynamic trajectory optimizer and pruning strategy, enabling the rapid discovery of novel skills. Through extensive ablation studies, we show that our LLM-guided search discovers successful whole-body trajectories across several challenging long-horizon tasks. Finally, by training reinforcement learning tracking policies on the discovered trajectories, we transfer the motions to a real humanoid robot. This is the first work to discover and deploy long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation skills entirely through automated evolutionary search. Supplementary videos of the experiments are available at: https://youtu.be/DHiVz34QYlw.

</details>


### [366] [AffordanceVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Model Empowering Action Generation through Affordance-Aware Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06155)
*Qize Yu,Jiadi You,Yuran Wang,Jiaqi Liang,Bowen Ping,Yang Tian,Yue Chen,Minghong Cai,Zeying Gong,Ruihai Wu,Yinchuan Li,Junwei Liang,Yingcong Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AffordanceVLA introduces a structured, intermediate representation of affordances to bridge perception, language, and action in robotic manipulation. It uses three components—Which2Act, Where2Act, and How2Act—within a Mixture-of-Transformer, trained with a progressive curriculum and data augmentation, achieving strong results in sim and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the misalignment between vision-language model semantics and embodied control by introducing task-oriented affordance forecasts as an intermediate layer that is spatially grounded and action-conditioned.

Method: 1) Which2Act: object-centric grounding via visual latent prediction to suppress distractions; 2) Where2Act: 2D interaction localization via affordance map estimation; 3) How2Act: 3D geometric reasoning to guide manipulation policies. These are integrated in a Mixture-of-Transformer with specialized experts. A three-stage training strategy with a progressive data curriculum and an automated data augmentation pipeline is used to compensate for scarce dense affordance labels.

Result: Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings show strong performance across diverse manipulation scenarios.

Conclusion: AffordanceVLA provides spatially grounded, semantically conditioned, and action-coupled intermediate representations that effectively bridge vision, language, and action, enabling more robust and precise manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models leverage the rich world knowledge of pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to enable instruction-following robotic manipulation. However, the structural mismatch between VLM semantic spaces and embodied control policies often hinders the learning of precise perception--action mappings. To address this challenge, we propose \textbf{AffordanceVLA}, a unified framework that introduces structured affordance forecasting as a task-oriented intermediate representation to establish a more precise and robust perception--action mapping. Specifically, we progressively model manipulation priors through three complementary components: 1) \textbf{Which2Act} for object-centric grounding via visual latent prediction to suppress distractions; 2) \textbf{Where2Act} for 2D interaction localization via affordance map estimation; and 3) \textbf{How2Act} for 3D geometric reasoning to guide manipulation policies. These affordance cues provide spatially grounded, semantically conditioned, and action-coupled intermediate representations, thereby naturally bridging vision, language and action. We integrate these modules into a Mixture-of-Transformer (MoT) architecture with specialized experts and train the model using a three-stage training strategy with a progressive data curriculum. To overcome the scarcity of dense affordance labels in robotic datasets, we also develop a robust automated data augmentation pipeline. Extensive experiments on simulation and real-world demonstrate that AffordanceVLA achieves strong performance across diverse manipulation scenarios.

</details>


### [367] [ActiveMimic: Egocentric Video Pretraining with Active Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06194)
*Xingyao Lin,Guojin Zhong,Tianyi Lu,Ziyi Ye,Yichen Zhu,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ActiveMimic introduces a pretraining framework for robot learning from egocentric human video by recovering synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn camera, modeling camera motion as a viewpoint action, and jointly learning active perception and manipulation before adapting to a target robot; it outperforms baselines trained on human video and matches state-of-the-art robot-data pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a persistent transfer gap: pretraining on egocentric human video underperforms compared with pretraining on robot data. The missing signal is active perception, manifested as camera motion/ viewpoint changes during manipulation, which is treated as noise by standard pipelines.

Method: 1) Extract synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn RGB camera; 2) Model camera motion as a viewpoint action; 3) Jointly learn active perception and manipulation from in-the-wild egocentric video; 4) Adapt the learned representations to a target robot.

Result: Empirical studies across tasks with diverse active-perception demands show ActiveMimic surpasses baselines pretrained on human video and matches state-of-the-art models pretrained on robot data.

Conclusion: Active perception signals originate from egocentric human video pretraining rather than robot-specific fine-tuning, confirming active perception as the key to unlocking egocentric human video for robot pretraining.

Abstract: Egocentric human video offers a scalable alternative to robot data for pretraining, yet models pretrained on such video consistently underperform those pretrained on robot data. We attribute this gap to a missing signal, the active perception behavior in egocentric videos, where humans continuously reposition their viewpoint during manipulation, inducing camera motion that standard pipelines treat as noise. To address this, we present ActiveMimic, a pretraining framework that recovers synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn RGB camera, models camera motion as a viewpoint action, and jointly learns active perception and manipulation from in-the-wild egocentric human video before adapting to a target robot. Empirically, real-world experiments across tasks with diverse active perception demands show that ActiveMimic consistently surpasses baselines pretrained on human video and matches state-of-the-art models pretrained on robot data. Further analysis provides evidence that active perception capability originates from egocentric human video pretraining rather than robot-specific fine-tuning, confirming active perception as the key to unlocking egocentric human video for robot pretraining.

</details>


### [368] [TAM: Torque Adaptation Module for Robust Motion Transfer in Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06218)
*Dongwon Son,Florian Shkurti,Jason Lee,Naman Shah,Beomjoon Kim,Dieter Fox*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TAM is a transferable torque adaptation module that sits between the policy controller and the robot, using proprioceptive history to compute residual torque. It enables zero-shot transfer across robots and action spaces by training in randomized simulation and requiring no real-robot data, improving robustness in dynamic, contact-rich manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Policy transfer across robots and payloads suffers from sim-to-real gaps and differing dynamics, especially in contact-rich manipulation where small timing differences cause failures. Domain randomization or system identification either hurts performance or demands robot-specific data. A reusable, history-based torque adaptor could decouple policy training from robot-specific dynamics.

Method: Introduce the Torque Adaptation Module (TAM) consisting of a history encoder that embeds proprioceptive history into a latent state and a torque adaptor that outputs residual torque corrections. TAM sits between the low-level controller and the robot torque interface, making it agnostic to the policy action space and enabling same weights to adapt policies with different action representations. TAM is trained entirely in randomized simulation with multi-robot pretraining and a robot-specific fine-tuning step, without real-robot data. Evaluation is zero-shot on a Franka Panda with diverse tasks (vision-based box pushing from RL, a BC-based flip policy, and an MPC ball-on-plate).

Result: TAM improves zero-shot real-robot execution relative to online system identification and RMA baselines, enabling robust performance across dynamic manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: A proprioception-driven torque adaptor can decouple policy learning from robot specifics, allowing cross-robot and cross-policy transfer without policy-domain randomization and with minimal real-robot data, thereby enhancing robustness in dynamic manipulation.

Abstract: A policy tuned for one robot often behaves differently on another, whether due to the sim-to-real gap, unknown payloads, or the differing dynamics of two instances of the same robot. In contact-rich, dynamic manipulation, even small motion discrepancies can result in failure to track reference motion, since they disrupt the timing and modes of contact. Common remedies, such as domain randomization or system identification, either produce overly conservative task policies or require data that must be recollected for each robot or payload. We introduce the Torque Adaptation Module (TAM), a learned module that adapts the torque commands sent to the robot to match the behavior of an ideal robot. TAM operates between the low-level controller that tracks the policy's actions and the robot's torque interface. It includes a history encoder that embeds proprioceptive history into a latent state and a torque adaptor that computes residual torque corrections. Because TAM depends only on proprioceptive history and not on policy observations, or the action space, the same TAM weights can be reused to adapt policies with different action spaces (joint targets, end-effector targets, or direct torques). The policies themselves do not need to be trained with domain randomization of robot parameters. Instead, we offload the need for domain randomization to TAM by training it entirely in randomized simulation, using multi-robot pretraining followed by a robot-specific fine-tuning step that still requires no real-robot data. We evaluate TAM zero-shot on a real Franka Panda robot across dynamic manipulation tasks that include a vision-based box pushing policy (from RL), a flip policy (from BC), and an MPC ball-on-plate balancing. Our experiments show that TAM improves zero-shot real-robot execution compared to online system identification and RMA baselines and enables robust dynamic manipulation performance.

</details>


### [369] [CLEAR: Cognition and Latent Evaluation for Adaptive Routing in End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06219)
*Yining Xing,Zehong Ke,Zhiyuan Liu,Yanbo Jiang,Wenhao Yu,Jianqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CLEAR delivers ultra-fast, multi-modal autonomous driving planning by replacing slow diffusion sampling with a single-step latent drift in a VAE, guided by semantic reasoning and an adaptive scheduler. It uses Drive-JEPA as visual encoder and fine-tuned Qwen-3.5 for scene-aware states to select trajectories, achieving strong NAVSIM v1 performance without dense annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based planning captures diverse driving behaviors but incurs high latency unsuitable for safety-critical real-time deployment. There is a need for fast, high-fidelity, multi-modal planning with semantic awareness.

Method: - Visual encoder: Drive-JEPA. - Replace multi-step denoising with a single-step conditional drift in a VAE latent space, with a conditioning coefficient α to balance diversity and precision. - Scene-aware hidden states extracted by fully fine-tuning Qwen-3.5–0.8B on driving QA pairs. - Adaptive Scheduler selects α and sample count N from predefined schemes. - Cross-attention scorer selects the best trajectory among candidates. - No reliance on dense geometric annotations or iterative sampling.

Result: On NAVSIM v1, CLEAR achieves a state-of-the-art PDMS of 93.7, demonstrating that high-fidelity, multi-modal planning can be performed efficiently.

Conclusion: High-fidelity, fast, multi-modal autonomous driving planning is achievable by integrating fast latent planning with deep semantic reasoning and adaptive control, reducing latency without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving models often struggle to balance multi-modal maneuver generation with real-time inference constraints. While diffusion models successfully capture diverse driving behaviors, their iterative denoising process incurs unacceptable latency for safety-critical deployment. To address this, we propose CLEAR (Cognition and Latent Evaluation for Adaptive Routing), a framework that combines ultra-fast generative planning with deep semantic reasoning. CLEAR employs Drive-JEPA as the visual encoder and replaces the multi-step denoising chain with a single-step conditional drift in a VAE latent space, introducing a conditioning coefficient to balance diversity and expert precision. Meanwhile, we fully fine-tune Qwen~3.5~0.8B on driving QA pairs to extract scene-aware hidden states. These states guide both an Adaptive Scheduler, which selects the conditioning coefficient $α$ and sample count $N$ from a discrete set of predefined schemes, and a cross-attention scorer that selects the optimal trajectory from candidates. On the NAVSIM v1 benchmark, CLEAR achieves a state-of-the-art PDMS of 93.7. Our results demonstrate that high-fidelity, multi-modal planning can be executed efficiently without dense geometric annotations or iterative sampling.

</details>


### [370] [MPCoT: Reward-Guided Multi-Path Latent Reasoning for Test-Time Scalable Vision-Language-Action](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06245)
*Boyang Zhang,Lianlei Shan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MPCoT enables multi-path latent reasoning for vision-language-action tasks without extra reasoning tokens, improving long-horizon performance while preserving the original action interface; it uses M initial hypotheses, K refinement steps, and a train-time path-preference objective to align latent paths with execution quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-pass action decoding in VLA policies struggles with long-horizon uncertainty. Explicit chain-of-thought can help but adds latency and a cumbersome interface. The work aims to provide deeper latent reasoning with configurable depth-width while keeping a zero-token interface.

Method: Initialize M hypotheses, refine them for K weight-tied steps, then softly aggregate before action decoding. Train with a path-preference objective evaluating branches by expert-action consistency, world-model/VLM progress, and success feedback. Preserve the original 8-step action interface, generate zero reasoning tokens, and expose inference controls (K, M). Evaluate under matched LIBERO and CALVIN protocols.

Result: On LIBERO and CALVIN, MPCoT improves long-horizon performance. Ablations indicate beneficial effects from increased depth (K) and width (M), the value of confidence-weighted aggregation, and reward-guided path supervision in aligning latent paths with downstream execution quality.

Conclusion: MPCoT provides deeper latent reasoning without token latency, preserves the standard action interface, and offers tunable depth and width to balance deliberation and responsiveness, achieving improved long-horizon control under established benchmarks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies remain brittle in long-horizon and high-uncertainty control, where one-pass action decoding provides limited inference-time deliberation. Explicit chain-of-thought can increase reasoning depth, but introduces token latency and an indirect text-to-action interface. We propose MPCoT, a reward-guided multi-path latent reasoning framework that initializes $M$ hypotheses, refines them for K weight-tied steps, and softly aggregates them before action decoding. A training-only path-preference objective evaluates candidate action branches with expert-action consistency, world-model/VLM-based progress, and success feedback to align the latent path scorer with downstream execution quality. MPCoT preserves the original 8-step action interface, generates zero reasoning tokens, and exposes configurable inference controls (K,M). Under matched protocols on LIBERO and CALVIN, MPCoT improves long-horizon performance, with ablations confirming depth-width effects, confidence-weighted aggregation, and reward-guided path supervision.

</details>


### [371] [Breaking Time: A Fully Gaussian Framework for Distributed and Continuous-Time SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06250)
*Davide Ceriola,Simone Ferrari,Luca Di Giammarino,Leonardo Brizi,Giorgio Grisetti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: G-solver is a fully Gaussian, distributed continuous-time SLAM framework that fuses heterogeneous, asynchronous sensor data using Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) with Gaussian Process (GP) motion priors, enabling probabilistic trajectory interpolation and scalable multi-camera coupling without heavy synchronization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of fusing heterogeneous, asynchronous sensor streams in continuous-time SLAM and to enable scalable, decentralized inference with principled trajectory interpolation and learnable hyperparameters.

Method: A fully Gaussian GBP-based solver (G-solver) that uses Gaussian Process motion priors to represent the trajectory and provide probabilistic interpolation; GBP performs distributed message passing for scalable inference; extends naturally to multi-camera setups without specialized synchronization.

Result: Demonstrates accurate and stable trajectory estimation on synthetic and real data, including rolling shutter and distributed multi-camera scenarios, with runtimes comparable to existing continuous-time methods; an open-source implementation is released.

Conclusion: The framework offers a principled, scalable, and synchronization-free approach to continuous-time SLAM for heterogeneous sensor suites, leveraging GP priors for interpolation and GBP for distributed inference, suitable for multi-camera systems.

Abstract: Continuous-time SLAM provides a principled framework for fusing heterogeneous sensors while estimating smooth trajectories, and is particularly well-suited for handling heterogeneous, asynchronous sensor streams with non-uniform readout patterns, such as rolling shutter cameras, LiDAR scanners, radar sweeps, or event-based sensors. In this work, we introduce G-solver, a fully Gaussian and distributed framework that combines Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) with Gaussian Process (GP) motion priors for continuous-time trajectory estimation. Our GP model provides a probabilistic representation of the trajectory, enabling consistent interpolation and the use of data-driven hyperparameters, while GBP offers a scalable message-passing formulation well-suited for decentralized settings. The resulting solver naturally extends to multi-camera scenarios without specialized synchronization or engineering effort. We evaluate the approach on synthetic and real data, including rolling shutter and distributed multi-camera optimization, demonstrating accurate and stable estimation with runtimes comparable to existing continuous-time methods. An open-source implementation is released.

</details>


### [372] [RadiusFPS: Efficient Farthest Point Sampling on CPUs and GPUs via Spherical Voxel Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06255)
*Ziyang Yu,Xiang Li,Qiong Chang,Jun Miyazaki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RadiusFPS accelerates FPS via spherical voxel pruning and a GPU warp-level implementation, achieving up to 2.5x speedup with comparable accuracy on standard benchmarks, enabling faster end-to-end robotic perception pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) provides uniform geometric coverage but is computationally expensive at sensor-scale point counts. This bottleneck hinders real-time robotics where compute and latency budgets are tight.

Method: RadiusFPS introduces spherical voxel indexing to derive conservative geometric bounds that prune redundant distance computations per iteration, plus a coordinate-wise point-skip test to skip residual updates while preserving the standard FPS update rule and tie-breaking policy. RadiusFPS-G fuses voxel selection, pruning, and distance updates into warp-level GPU kernels to maximize memory coalescing and eliminate global-memory round-trips.

Result: RadiusFPS-G achieves up to 2.5× speedup over GPU-based FPS on indoor (S3DIS, ScanNet) and outdoor (SemanticKITTI) benchmarks, matches or exceeds QuickFPS among evaluated methods, uses about half the GPU memory, and maintains comparable segmentation accuracy. When paired with the learning-based FastPoint sampler in an end-to-end pipeline, it attains the fastest inference among evaluated configurations.

Conclusion: RadiusFPS makes FPS-style sampling practical for latency- and memory-constrained robotic perception by delivering substantial speedups with minimal accuracy loss, enabling faster end-to-end pipelines.

Abstract: Point clouds are a primary sensory representation for robotic perception, underpinning LiDAR-based autonomous driving, simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), and navigation. Within these pipelines, Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) is the most well-known downsampling operator, as its uniform coverage preserves the geometric structure on which downstream perception relies. However, the large time complexity of classical FPS scales poorly with the million-point-per-second rates of modern 3D sensors, making it a dominant latency bottleneck that conflicts with the real-time and limited onboard compute budgets of robotic systems. Therefore, we propose RadiusFPS, an FPS acceleration framework based on spherical voxel pruning that preserves the standard FPS update rule under the same initialization and tie-breaking policy. By indexing the point cloud with spherical voxels, RadiusFPS derives a conservative geometric bound that prunes redundant distance computations in each iteration, complemented by a coordinate-wise point-skip test that removes residual updates. We further introduce RadiusFPS-G, a warp-level GPU implementation that fuses voxel selection, pruning, and distance update into memory-coalesced kernels, eliminating costly global-memory round-trips. On indoor (S3DIS, ScanNet) and outdoor LiDAR (SemanticKITTI) benchmarks, RadiusFPS-G attains up to 2.5x speedup over GPU-based FPS and matches or exceeds QuickFPS among the evaluated methods while using roughly half its GPU memory, with comparable segmentation accuracy. When coupled with the learning-based FastPoint sampler, the resulting pipeline achieves the fastest End-to-End inference among all evaluated configurations. These properties make high-quality FPS-style sampling practical for latency- and memory-constrained robotic vision.

</details>


### [373] [Multi-Resolution Tactile Imitation Learning for Contact-Rich Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06281)
*Rickmer Krohn,Erik Helmut,Niklas Funk,Jan Peters,Vignesh Prasad,Georgia Chalvatzaki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MiTaS fuses heterogeneous tactile sensors at different temporal resolutions (RGB, GelSight Mini, Evetac) via modality-specific convolutional stems and transformer fusion to drive a flow-matching policy for contact-rich manipulation, achieving 80% average success across five tasks and outperforming vision-only (31%) and vision-tactile (54%) baselines; co-training with multi-tactile data yields further gains; analysis confirms sensor contributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the underexplored potential of combining heterogeneous tactile sensors operating at different temporal resolutions to improve manipulation learning in contact-rich tasks.

Method: A multi-branch architecture with modality-specific CNN stems for each sensor stream, followed by transformer-based fusion to create a unified multi-resolution tactile representation; this representation conditions a flow-matching policy trained via imitation learning; inputs include RGB, GelSight Mini, and high-frequency Evetac event data; includes ablations and attention analysis; evaluated on five tasks and includes a co-training setup.

Result: MiTaS achieves an average success rate of 80% across tasks, outperforming vision-only (31%) and visual-tactile (54%) baselines. Co-training a visuo-tactile model with multi-tactile data yields >10% gains in some tasks without access to Evetac at evaluation. Sensor-reading and attention analyses highlight the contribution of different sensors during task execution.

Conclusion: Integrating multi-resolution tactile information via modality-specific processing and transformer fusion significantly enhances imitation-learning-based manipulation under contact-rich conditions, with clear sensor contributions and potential for further gains through co-training and detailed analyses.

Abstract: Touch sensing is beneficial for solving a wide variety of manipulation tasks. While there exists a wide range of tactile sensors with different properties, exploiting the fusion of multiple heterogeneous tactile sensors to improve manipulation learning remains underexplored. We present Multi-Resolution Tactile Sensing (MiTaS), a representation framework that leverages multiple tactile sensors operating at different temporal resolutions in order to solve complex contact-rich manipulation tasks. We propose a novel architecture using modality-specific convolutional stems and transformer-based fusion that effectively fuses information from an RGB camera stream, a vision-based GelSight Mini sensor and a high-frequency event-based Evetac sensor. This multi-sensor representation then conditions a flow-matching policy for solving downstream tasks. Experimental results across five contact-rich manipulation tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of multi-resolution tactile features in imitation learning. MiTaS achieves an average success rate of 80 %, while vision-only (31 %) and visual-tactile (54 %) baselines cannot solve the task reliably. Co-training a visuo-tactile model with multi-tactile data boosts performance by over 10 \% in certain tasks, without having access to the Evetac sensor during policy evaluation. A detailed sensor-reading and attention analysis reveals the importance of different sensors throughout task execution, validating our multi-resolution tactile sensing approach. Project Page: http://mitas-touch.github.io.

</details>


### [374] [Attitude-Aided Linear Calibration of Triaxial Accelerometers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06308)
*Yongqiang Yu,Tian Huang,Yipeng Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Attitude-aided CAL (ALAC) offers a fast, linear calibration for triaxial MEMS accelerometers by using orientation data to form a unified error matrix, enabling closed-form LS estimation with only five measurements and online extension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on costly reference setups and nonlinear optimization; enable accurate, real-time calibration for low-cost or self-calibrating MEMS IMUs.

Method: Construct a Combined Error Matrix (CEM) to model sensor errors; perform linear least-squares estimation to jointly estimate bias and gravity; decompose the CEM to recover scale, non-orthogonality, and alignment rotations. Under static gravity, solve a constrained homogeneous least-squares (CHLS) problem in closed form. Requires five arbitrarily oriented measurements; supports recursive online calibration.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a stationary robot-mounted accelerometer and a quasi-static public IMU trajectory shows ALAC outperforming reference-based and online baselines in both offline and online modes, robust to sensor noise; matches iterative self-calibration under filtered data and surpasses all baselines on raw measurements.

Conclusion: ALAC provides a robust, practical calibration framework for MEMS-based inertial platforms, especially suitable for low-cost IMUs and online calibration, with effective estimation of bias, gravity, scale, non-orthogonality, and alignment.

Abstract: Triaxial MEMS accelerometers are widely used for inertial sensing, navigation, and sensor fusion, but existing calibration methods often rely on costly reference setups or nonlinear iterative optimization, limiting their efficiency and applicability to low-cost or self-calibrating systems. We present attitude-aided linear accelerometer calibration (ALAC), a method that operates on any platform providing orientation information, such as turntables, robotic arms, or inertial measurement units. ALAC constructs a combined error matrix (CEM) to represent sensor errors in a unified calibration model and enables linear least-squares estimation. The bias and gravity vector are jointly estimated, implicitly accounting for platform misalignment, and matrix decomposition of the CEM recovers scale, non-orthogonality, and alignment rotation parameters. Under static gravity, calibration is formulated as a constrained homogeneous least-squares (CHLS) problem and solved in closed form using standard linear algebra. Only five arbitrarily oriented measurements are required, and a recursive extension supports online or in-field calibration. Experiments on a stationary robot-mounted accelerometer and a quasi-static public IMU trajectory show that ALAC, in both offline and online modes, outperforms reference-based and online baselines in accuracy and robustness to sensor noise. On the same dataset, it matches iterative self-calibration under filtered conditions and surpasses all evaluated baselines on raw measurements. These results demonstrate a robust and practical calibration scheme for MEMS-based inertial platforms, especially low-cost IMUs and online calibration scenarios.

</details>


### [375] [Meridian: Metric-Semantic Primitive Matching for Cross-View Geo-Localization Beyond Urban Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06312)
*Mason Peterson,Qingyuan Li,Yixuan Jia,Fernando Cladera,Carlos Nieto-Granda,Camillo Jose Taylor,Jonathan P. How*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Meridian enables training-free global localization in GNSS-denied environments by cross-domain matching of metric-semantic primitives between aerial imagery and ground RGB-D data, supported by robust submap pose consistency metrics and outlier-rejecting pose graph optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable global localization in GNSS-denied, natural environments is challenging due to reliance on environment-specific models, repetitive geometries, and featureless terrain. Overhead aerial imagery offers cross-domain cues, but existing methods struggle in unstructured outdoor areas and require area-specific training.

Method: Introduce Meridian, which matches high-level metric-semantic primitives across aerial images and ground RGB-D data. Develop novel consistency metrics to estimate a distribution over robot submap poses and to reject outlier hypotheses within a robust pose graph optimization framework for accurate trajectory estimation. Importantly, no training or fine-tuning is performed on area-specific data.

Result: Localization is demonstrated across diverse settings (autonomous driving dataset, park/campus, wilderness camp) with an average optimized trajectory error of 2.4 m over 19 km of ground traversal.

Conclusion: Meridian generalizes across environments without area-specific training, enabling reliable GNSS-denied global localization in unstructured outdoor terrain and supporting improved task planning and safety in aerial-ground robotics.

Abstract: Successful robot automation requires accurate global localization to support repeatability, task planning, goal specification, and safe operation. However, reliable localization in GNSS-denied environments remains an open problem. Overhead aerial imagery offers a promising solution, but existing approaches primarily target structured urban environments and have been rarely demonstrated in unstructured natural terrain. Limitations of the state-of-the-art include a reliance on models trained for specific environments, as well as difficulty handling repetitive geometries and featureless landscapes commonly found in natural outdoor areas. To overcome these challenges, we present Meridian, a method for matching high-level metric-semantic primitives across aerial images and ground robot RGB-D camera data that achieves accurate global localization and generalizes well across diverse environments, all without any training or algorithmic fine-tuning on area-specific data. We formulate novel consistency metrics to estimate a distribution over robot submap poses and to reject outlier hypotheses in a robust pose graph optimization step for accurate robot trajectory estimation. We demonstrate that our algorithm can localize a ground robot across a wide variety of environments, including an autonomous driving dataset, a park and campus area, and a wilderness camp, with an average optimized trajectory error of 2.4 m over 19 km of ground traversal.

</details>


### [376] [VOLT: Vision and Language Trajectory Segmentation for Faster-than-Demonstration Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06323)
*Robert Ramirez Sanchez,Daniel J. Evans,Dylan P. Losey,Siddarth Jain*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A method to accelerate robotic execution by selectively downsampling demonstrations using a vision-and-language segmentation (VOLT) approach, enabling faster-than-demonstration policies without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans often perform tasks faster than robots can learn to execute; uniform downsampling loses critical slow, precise segments. There is a need for a policy that can run faster while preserving accuracy, by identifying which parts of a task require careful execution.

Method: Introduce VOLT, a vision-and-language trajectory segmentation method that analyzes video demonstrations to segment actions into fast and slow components using contextual cues. It then downsamples only the segments that can be accelerated and leaves others intact, providing reformatted trajectories compatible with standard imitation learning approaches (e.g., diffusion policies).

Result: The approach yields faster task execution with strong performance. Segmentation quality is crucial; poorly identified acceleration regions can lead to overly cautious or unreliable policies. VOLT outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives in enabling faster execution while maintaining performance.

Conclusion: Selective acceleration via accurate segmentation is an effective strategy for learning faster-than-demonstrations. VOLT demonstrates that combining vision-language understanding with trajectory segmentation can produce restartable, reliable, and faster imitation-learned policies.

Abstract: Humans often take longer to demonstrate a task than a robot would need to execute it. Rather than learning to replicate the demonstration at the same pace, many industrial and practical applications require robots to perform tasks as quickly as possible. In this paper, we investigate several hypotheses for learning policies that operate faster-than-demonstrations. Our experiments show that the most effective strategy is to downsample recorded demonstrations and train the robot's policy on this accelerated data. However, uniformly downsampling an entire trajectory can be problematic. Some parts of a task can be safely sped up (e.g., unconstrained motion), while others demand slower, more precise motion (e.g., object interactions or fine manipulation). To address this challenge, we introduce VOLT, a vision-and-language trajectory segmentation method that reasons over video demonstrations, and leverages contextual cues to determine when acceleration is appropriate and when careful precision is required. VOLT identifies segments where slow, deliberate motion is necessary, then selectively downsamples the remaining segments. The resulting reformatted trajectories can be used with standard imitation learning approaches, such as diffusion policies. Our results highlight that segmentation quality is critical -- baseline methods often misidentify when acceleration is possible, leading to overly cautious or unreliable policies. Compared to state-of-the-art alternatives, VOLT allows robots to execute tasks faster while maintaining strong performance.

</details>


### [377] [Waypoints Matter: A Systematic Study for Sampling-Based Trajectory Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06366)
*Josep M. Barbera,Antonio Artuñedo,Jorge Villagra*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Waypoint placement is the dominant design variable for sampling-based autonomous driving planners; across 449 configs and 5 maps, nominal inter-waypoint spacing ds mainly drives performance; uniform spacing often matches or surpasses RDP* and curvature-based strategies; curvature helps slightly on complex roads under certain weights; RDP* never beats uniform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how waypoint placement, treated as a design variable, impacts planner feasibility and reliability, filling a gap where placement effects are underexplored.

Method: Compare three placement strategies (uniform spacing, augmented RDP variant RDP*, curvature-conditioned allocation) across 449 configurations and five CommonRoad maps; fix trajectory primitive and candidate budget; evaluate performance.

Result: ds is the primary performance driver; large differences due to placement; uniform spacing with well-tuned ds matches or outperforms RDP* and curvature variant; curvature variant offers small gains on geometrically complex roads under reliability-first and balanced weights; RDP* never outperforms uniform.

Conclusion: Treat ds as the dominant tuning parameter for waypoint placement; geometry-aware strategies should be reserved for curvature-rich corridors where feasibility limits mobility; focus on ds optimization before exploring complex placement heuristics.

Abstract: Real-time autonomous driving commonly relies on sampling-based trajectory planners that link candidate trajectories to target waypoints along the road centerline. The placement of these waypoints directly impacts both the existence and quality of feasible trajectories. Yet, its effect on planner performance remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we treat waypoint placement as a first-class design variable. We hold the trajectory primitive and candidate budget fixed, and systematically sweep three placement strategies (uniform spacing, an augmented Ramer-Douglas-Peucker variant (RDP*), and a novel curvature-conditioned allocation) across 449 configurations and five CommonRoad maps of increasing geometric complexity. Our results show that the nominal inter-waypoint spacing $d_s$ is the primary performance driver, with large differences in planner reliability attributed to placement alone. Uniform sampling at a well-tuned spacing matches or surpasses both RDP* and the centered curvature variant. The curvature variant offers a small but consistent advantage on geometrically complex roads under reliability-first and balanced weightings, while RDP* never outperforms uniform sampling. These findings suggest that $d_s$ should be treated as the dominant tuning parameter, with geometry-aware strategies reserved for curvature-rich corridors where feasibility is the limiting factor.

</details>


### [378] [Ensuring Interaction Safety in Multitask Exoskeleton Control: A Simulation-Trained Variable Impedance Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06370)
*Muyuan Ma,Houcheng Li,Haotian Zhai,Lijun Han,Xinpan Meng,Xiuze Xia,Long Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Simulation-trained variable-impedance control for exoskeletons with Lyapunov-based safety, using PPO-generated motion data and a dual-modality policy for nine tasks, achieving reduced metabolic cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptation to diverse tasks and interaction safety are critical challenges in wearable exoskeletons; a data-driven approach with stability guarantees is needed.

Method: 1) Create a simulation-based exoskeleton-human motion data pipeline; 2) Use PPO to synthesize muscle activations while the exoskeleton compensates biological joint torques; 3) Train a dual-modality policy (semantic instructions + proprioceptive history) to predict reference trajectories and variable impedance gains for nine tasks; 4) Impose Lyapunov-based stability constraint to bound stiffness variations, ensuring asymptotic stability of the coupled system.

Result: Experimentally, the framework reduces metabolic cost in real-world tasks relative to standard baselines, indicating effective multitask control and safety guarantees in practice.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates feasibility of safe, multitask exoskeleton control with formal stability bounds, supporting broader adoption and extension to varied tasks.

Abstract: Wearable exoskeletons can augment human phys ical capabilities during complex activities. However, ensuring adaptation across diverse tasks while guaranteeing interaction safety remains a critical challenge. To address this, a simulation trained variable impedance control approach with stability guarantees is proposed. First, a simulation-based human exoskeleton motion data generation pipeline is established, utilizing Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to synthesize human muscle activations while the exoskeleton provides direct compensation for human biological joint torques. Subsequently, the generated dataset is used to train a dual modality policy that fuses semantic instructions with proprioceptive history, enabling the prediction of reference trajectories and variable impedance gains for nine different motion tasks. To guarantee safety, the network outputs are constrained by a stability criterion derived from Lyapunov stability theory, which bounds stiffness variations to ensure the asymptotic stability of the coupled system. Experimental results indicate that the proposed framework reduces metabolic cost in real-world scenarios com pared with standard baseline methods. These findings suggest the feasibility of the proposed framework for safe, multitask exoskeleton control.

</details>


### [379] [RiskFlow: Fast and Faithful Safety-Critical Traffic Scenario Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06423)
*Qi Lan,Yining Tang,Yu Shen,Yi Zhou,Yuhao Wei,Jie Li,Guofa Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RiskFlow enables efficient, closed-loop multi-agent traffic generation by learning a finite-interval average velocity field that maps Gaussian action sequences to accelerations and yaw-rates in one pass, with test-time output-space guidance and vehicle-dynamics reconstruction for realistic, adversarially capable scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address computational burden and artifact accumulation in diffusion-based closed-loop generation, which causes jitter, abnormal accelerations, and off-road behavior in long rollouts.

Method: Train a finite-interval average velocity field to transform action sequences; use Jacobian-vector-product (JVP) objective for stable training; at test time apply output-space guidance to steer critical agents toward risky interactions and regularize off-road behavior; reconstruct physically feasible trajectories via vehicle dynamics.

Result: Empirical evaluation on nuScenes with tbsim closed-loop setup shows improved realism vs baselines while preserving safety-critical generation and substantially faster inference.

Conclusion: RiskFlow offers a diffusion-free, efficient, controllable framework for safety-critical traffic scenario generation, achieving favorable realism–adversariality trade-offs and scalable evaluation.

Abstract: Safety-critical traffic scenario generation is essential for evaluating autonomous driving systems under rare but high-risk interactions. Existing diffusion-based methods offer strong controllability in closed-loop generation, but their iterative denoising process is computationally expensive and may accumulate sampling and guidance errors over long rollouts, causing unrealistic motion artifacts such as jitter, abnormal acceleration, and off-road behavior. To address these issues, we propose RiskFlow, a closed-loop safety-critical multi-agent traffic generation framework that formulates future trajectory generation as transport in the action space. Instead of relying on iterative denoising, RiskFlow learns an average velocity field over a finite interval to transform Gaussian action sequences into future acceleration and yaw-rate commands with a single forward pass, using a JVP-based objective for efficient and stable training. At test time, RiskFlow applies output-space guidance to the generated actions, steering selected critical agents toward risky interactions while regularizing off-road behavior, and reconstructs physically feasible trajectories through vehicle dynamics. Experiments on nuScenes with tbsim closed-loop evaluation show that RiskFlow achieves a strong adversariality-realism trade-off across multi-agent and long-horizon settings. Compared with representative baselines, RiskFlow consistently improves realism while maintaining competitive safety-critical generation capability, and substantially reduces inference time for evaluation.

</details>


### [380] [Flow-based Policy Adaptation without Policy Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06461)
*Luzhe Sun,Jingtian Ji,Haoran Chen,Jiawei Zhou,Matthew R. Walter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GLOVES uses flow-based adaptation to correct non-expert actions in real time by mapping actions toward an expert distribution, with an intervention gate that preserves agent intent and only intervenes on anomalous actions. It learns from limited demonstrations and stitches local expert patterns for robust shared-control across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To leverage pretrained policies, foundation models, or human operators while avoiding suboptimal, noisy, or misaligned actions, by enabling selective, action-level adaptation rather than full autonomy.

Method: Train flow-based models to transport actions toward an expert action distribution. Use reverse flow evaluation as an in-distribution score to gate interventions. Pass through actions consistent with the expert distribution; correct anomalous or out-of-distribution actions. Learn local expert action patterns from limited demonstrations and stitch them during execution to provide a lightweight shared-control module.

Result: Demonstrates improved task success and robustness through selective adaptation while preserving agent intent; provides an intrinsic in-distribution score for gating; requires only limited expert supervision; demonstrated across tasks/environments; code and demos available.

Conclusion: GLOVES offers a principled, low-overhead shared-control framework for robust action adaptation that can be applied across tasks and domains with minimal supervision.

Abstract: Leveraging prior knowledge from pretrained policies, foundation models, or human operators offers an efficient alternative to learning robot skills from scratch. However, these agents often provide actions that are suboptimal, noisy, or misaligned with task-specific expert behavior. We propose GLOVES, a family of flow-based adaptation methods that correct non-expert actions by transporting them toward an expert action distribution. Rather than replacing agentic control with full autonomy, GLOVES performs selective action-level adaptation, improving task success while preserving agent intent. The learned flow also provides a natural in-distribution scoring mechanism through reverse flow evaluation. We use this signal as an intervention gate: actions that appear consistent with the expert distribution are passed through unchanged, while anomalous or out-of-distribution (OOD) actions are corrected. In this way, assistance is only provided when necessary. GLOVES requires only limited expert supervision, using a small number of demonstrations or reusable successful skill segments. By learning local expert action patterns and stitching them during execution, GLOVES provides a lightweight shared-control module for robust action adaptation across tasks and environments. Code and demos are available at ripl.github.io/GLOVES_web.

</details>


### [381] [TempoVLA: Learning Speed-Controllable Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06491)
*Dong Jing,Jingchen Nie,Tianqi Zhang,Jiaqi Liu,Huaxiu Yao,Zhiwu Lu,Mingyu Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TempoVLA is a controllable-speed Vision-Language-Action model that uses data-side Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) and a model-side speed conditioning mechanism to enable dynamic, bidirectional execution speed in robot manipulation, validated in simulation and on real tasks and extensible via large multimodal models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language-Action models typically inherit a fixed execution speed from demonstrations; deceleration/control of speed is underexplored. Since the magnitude of predicted actions correlates with movement speed, explicit speed control offers a direct, practical lever for adapting motion to risk phases (low-risk transit vs high-risk contact).

Method: Two coupled components: (1) Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) on the data side to re-time demonstrations to any target speed by merging or splitting actions while preserving motion semantics; (2) A model-side conditioning mechanism that feeds the target speed into the policy. The approach can cooperate with a large multimodal model to achieve dynamic speed control.

Result: VSTA achieves the requested speed with negligible motion error. TempoVLA enables flexible speed control in both directions; VSTA also boosts the default 1× performance by better data utilization. Real-world and simulation experiments demonstrate dynamic speed control, accelerating in low-risk phases and decelerating for high-risk ones, with synergistic benefits when combined with large multimodal models.

Conclusion: TempoVLA demonstrates that explicit speed conditioning in VLAs allows controllable, context-adaptive robot manipulation. The data-augmentation (VSTA) and conditioning components are effective individually and jointly, enabling safe, efficient operation and offering a path to further gains via multimodal model integration.

Abstract: Robot manipulation alternates between low-risk transit phases that call for fast execution and high-risk contact stages that demand slow, precise motion. Yet existing Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) only inherit a single fixed speed from training demonstrations. Prior efforts to accelerate VLAs through model compression, KV-cache reuse, or reinforcement learning only shift the policy from one fixed speed to another, and leave deceleration almost unexplored. We observe that the magnitude of each predicted action already governs how fast the robot moves, opening a direct route to controllable execution speed. We turn this observation into TempoVLA, a single VLA whose execution speed is controlled by an explicit condition. TempoVLA combines two coupled components. (1) A data-side Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) that re-times demonstration to any target speed by merging or splitting actions while preserving its motion semantics. (2) A model-side conditioning mechanism that feeds the speed to the policy. Statistics show that VSTA reaches the requested speed with negligible motion error. Experiments in simulation and on real-world tasks demonstrate that TempoVLA achieves flexible speed control in both directions, while VSTA additionally boosts the default $1\times$ performance via better data utilization. Furthermore, by cooperating with a large multimodal model, TempoVLA realizes dynamic speed control, accelerating through low-risk phases and decelerating for high-risk ones.

</details>


### [382] [HANDOFF: Humanoid Agentic Task-Space Whole-Body Control via Distilled Complementary Teachers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06493)
*Lizhi Yang,Junheng Li,Nehar Poddar,Yiling Hou,Gio Huh,Robert Griffin,Georgia Gkioxari,Aaron Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Handoff presents HANDOFF, a single humanoid whole-body controller that uses an explicit, modular interface bridging planning and control, distilled from three specialists via multi-teacher KL distillation into a context-gated mixture-of-experts, enabling robust, language-driven manipulation on a real robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current whole-body controllers require dense kinematic/spatial references that planners struggle to produce. An intuitive, explicit interface is needed to make planning and control more decoupled, generalizable, and expressive for diverse manipulation skills.

Method: Introduce HANDOFF as a single controller that follows an explicit interface. Distill a mixture-of-experts student from three specialists—whole-body motion tracking with safety-filtered data, locomotion, and fall-recovery—using multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme. Evaluate on Unitree G1 for velocity tracking, manipulation workspace, and robustness. Demonstrate hardware feasibility with multiple natural-language-driven task roll-outs powered by a VLM-driven agentic planner without task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.

Result: HANDOFF matches state-of-the-art velocity tracking on the Unitree G1 and offers one of the largest robust manipulation workspaces among published results. Hardware demonstrations with natural-language-driven tasks show practical feasibility, driven by a VLM-based planner without task-specific data or fine-tuning.

Conclusion: A compact, explicit interface for whole-body control enables modular, robust manipulation through a context-gated mixture-of-experts, validated on hardware and compatible with language-driven planning. The approach decouples planning from control, broadening the manipulative capabilities of humanoid robots in real-world settings.

Abstract: For a humanoid robot to be deployed in the real world, the choice of command space (i.e., the interface between task planning and whole-body control) is crucial. Existing whole-body controllers typically demand dense kinematic or spatial references that planners struggle to synthesize from task semantics. We instead propose a compact, explicit interface that is intuitive, general, modular, and expressive enough for diverse manipulation skills. To this end, we introduce HANDOFF, a single humanoid whole-body controller that follows this interface and is distilled via multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme into a mixture-of-experts student from three complementary specialists: whole-body motion tracking with safety-filtered data, locomotion, and fall-recovery. On the Unitree G1, HANDOFF matches state-of-the-art velocity tracking and offers one of the largest robust manipulation workspaces. We further demonstrate hardware feasibility through multiple natural-language-driven task roll-outs, powered by a VLM-driven agentic planner with no task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.

</details>


### [383] [Ask-to-Clarify: Resolving Instruction Ambiguity through Multi-turn Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.15061)
*Xingyao Lin,Xinghao Zhu,Tianyi Lu,Sicheng Xie,Hui Zhang,Xipeng Qiu,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes Ask-to-Clarify framework for VLA-based embodied agents enabling multi-turn clarification dialogue before action; improves collaboration and action generation via a two-component architecture and a two-stage training regime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world instructions are often ambiguous and require back-and-forth communication; current VLAs mostly operate in one-way instruction-to-action, limiting effective collaboration with humans.

Method: Two-component framework: (1) a visual-language model (VLM) for collaboration to handle ambiguity through dialogue; (2) a diffusion-based action module that generates low-level actions. A connection module produces diffusion conditions from VLM outputs to adjust observations by instructions. A two-stage knowledge-insulation training: first fine-tune the collaboration component on ambiguity-solving dialogue data, then integrate the action component while freezing the collaboration component. Inference uses a signal detector to switch between asking questions and executing actions.

Result: Evaluated on eight real-world tasks; the approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art VLAs.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a viable path toward collaborative embodied agents by enabling clarified, two-way interactions before action execution, with a training strategy that preserves interaction capabilities while enabling end-to-end action generation.

Abstract: The ultimate goal of embodied agents is to create collaborators that can interact with humans, not mere executors that passively follow instructions. This requires agents to communicate, coordinate, and adapt their actions based on human feedback. Recently, advances in VLAs have offered a path toward this goal. However, most current VLA-based embodied agents operate in a one-way mode: they receive an instruction and execute it without feedback. This approach fails in real-world scenarios where instructions are often ambiguous. In this paper, we address this problem with the Ask-to-Clarify framework. Our framework first resolves ambiguous instructions by asking questions in a multi-turn dialogue. Then it generates low-level actions end-to-end. Specifically, the Ask-to-Clarify framework consists of two components, one VLM for collaboration and one diffusion for action. We also introduce a connection module that generates conditions for the diffusion based on the output of the VLM. This module adjusts the observation by instructions to create reliable conditions. We train our framework with a two-stage knowledge-insulation strategy. First, we fine-tune the collaboration component using ambiguity-solving dialogue data to handle ambiguity. Then, we integrate the action component while freezing the collaboration one. This preserves the interaction abilities while fine-tuning the diffusion to generate actions. The training strategy guarantees our framework can first ask questions, then generate actions. During inference, a signal detector functions as a router that helps our framework switch between asking questions and taking actions. We evaluate the Ask-to-Clarify framework in 8 real-world tasks, where it outperforms existing state-of-the-art VLAs. The results suggest that our proposed framework, along with the training strategy, provides a path toward collaborative embodied agents.

</details>


### [384] [OLIVE: Online Low-Rank Incremental Learning for Efficient Adaptive Exoskeletons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05234)
*Dong Liu,Yanxuan Yu,Ben Lengerich,Tony Geng,Ying Nian Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OLIVE enables online, parameter-efficient personalization of exoskeleton control by modeling the adaptive policy as a low-rank residual update to a pretrained base controller, updated via reward-shaped policy gradient from on-body sensors, with contextual gating and a dynamic rank scheduler to handle diverse terrains; achieves robust gait improvements with low update cost and low latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static gait policies fail to adapt to real-world dynamics and individual user variation; need an online, efficient adaptation method that preserves stability.

Method: Decompose the adaptive component dW = A_t B_t^T with rank r << min(d,k); online updates via reward-shaped policy gradient using EMG/IMU/vibration; gating to modulate personalization; dynamic rank scheduler adjusts rank based on terrain complexity; maintain pretrained W_0 as base controller.

Result: Empirical gains of +13pp gait smoothness, +22pp effort reduction, +15pp motion stability over strongest baseline; convergence in ~1,800 steps; end-to-end latency ~7.4 ms; code available at provided URL.

Conclusion: OLIVE delivers efficient, adaptable exoskeleton control with a stable base policy, enabling robust performance across flat walking, stairs, slopes, and uneven terrain by updating only a small, low-rank residual informed by sensory feedback.

Abstract: Wearable exoskeleton systems hold promise for restoring mobility in individuals with physical impairments, yet most existing controllers rely on static gait policies that lack the ability to adapt to dynamic real-world environments or individual user characteristics. We present \olive (\underline{O}nline \underline{L}ow-rank \underline{I}ncremental Learning for Efficient Adapti\underline{ve} Exoskeletons), a parameter-efficient online adaptation framework that continuously personalizes exoskeleton control during deployment. \olive decomposes the adaptive component of the control policy into a low-rank residual form~$\dW = \At\Bt^\top$ with rank~$r!\ll!\min(d,k)$, reducing online update cost from $\mathcal{O}(dk)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r(d{+}k))$ while preserving the stability of a pretrained base controller~$\Wz$. Parameters are updated via a reward-shaped policy gradient driven purely by on-body sensor feedback (EMG, IMU, vibration), eliminating dependence on offline reference trajectories. A gating mechanism modulates the strength of personalization based on contextual state, and a dynamic rank scheduler adapts the update dimensionality to terrain complexity -- allocating minimal capacity on simple flat terrain and expanding to higher-rank updates on demanding uneven surfaces -- enabling robust performance across diverse activities: flat walking, stair navigation, slopes, and uneven terrain. Experiments on the wearable platform demonstrate that \olive achieves +13, +22, and +15 percentage-point improvements in gait smoothness, effort reduction, and motion stability over the strongest baseline, converging within $\sim$1{,}800 walking steps at 7.4,ms end-to-end latency. Our code implementation is available at https://github.com/FastLM/OLIVE.

</details>


### [385] [A New Quaternion-Joint Cable-Driven Redundant Manipulator Configuration and its Control Through FABRIK and Residual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05236)
*Tanapath Pornthisan,Thanapat Kemthong,Thanyapisit Kangsathien,Pasut Aranchaiya,Paulo Garcia,Viboon Sangveraphunsiri*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A 4-segment, 8-joint cable-driven manipulator with quaternion joints extends workspace at lower hardware cost; Residual Reinforcement Learning significantly outperforms FABRIK (3 orders of magnitude in pose accuracy) with a simplified control pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust path-traversing in obstructed workspaces; reduce motor count per DOF via quaternion joints; address complex kinematics and fabrication non-idealities.

Method: Design and analyze a 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator; apply Residual Reinforcement Learning to control; describe FABRIK-based control and learning approach; compare performance.

Result: Wider workspace and lower hardware cost than prior designs; residual RL outperforms FABRIK by three orders of magnitude in positional and orientational accuracy; simpler control implementation.

Conclusion: The configuration is more workspace-efficient; residual RL is a superior control strategy for this class of manipulators; methodology aids design and control for novel quaternion-joint manipulators.

Abstract: Robotic arms capable of traversing arbitrary spatial paths, especially in highly obstructed workspaces, are highly desired across several industries. Quaternion-joints have recently empowered a specific class of robotic arms -- cable-driven redundant manipulators -- beyond its prior capabilities. Specifically, quaternion-joints reduce the number of required motors per degree of freedom, paving the way for more compact solutions.An ongoing challenge is that the complexity of the kinematic model of quaternion joints challenges a priori decisions on manipulator configurations and imposes higher computational demands on the control system and its non-linearities amplify all discrepancies between design and physical artifact arising from fabrication imprecision. Here we show a that a 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator can achieve a broader workspace than extant configurations, at lower hardware cost, and that Residual Reinforcement Learning outperforms extant state-of-the-art methods -- specifically, the FABRIK algorithm -- on the control of such manipulator. Our results show that this configuration is more workspace-effective than prior designs, and that Residual Reinforcement Learning outperforms FABRIK by three orders of magnitude on positional and orientational accuracy, effecting precise control of the novel 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator. Additionally, the control implementation is simpler: we describe the complete FABRIK process for control and corresponding learning implementation. Our methodology is applicable to the design of new systems, providing designers with further tools for the development of this class of manipulators and corresponding control systems for novel configurations.

</details>
